definition: A group of persons constitutes a community if and only if (i) they inhabit a common geographical area, (ii) they interact with one another on a regular basis, and (iii) they share a set of customs or practices.
conditions: inhabit a common geographical area
interact with one another regularly
share a set of customs or practices
counterexample: The Jewish diaspora of mediaeval Europe comprised persons scattered across many countries who rarely, if ever, met one another face to face. They shared religious customs, a liturgical language, and a strong sense of collective identity, yet they did not inhabit a common geographical area nor interact regularly. The first and second conditions fail, yet they were universally regarded as a community.
missing_condition: The definition requires geographical co-presence and regular interaction, but a community may be sustained by shared identity, tradition, and symbolic bonds across great distances without physical proximity.
passage: One might propose that a group of persons constitutes a community if and only if they inhabit a common geographical area, they interact with one another on a regular basis, and they share a set of customs or practices. This definition captures the village, the town, the urban neighbourhood: in each case, persons live near one another, meet in the course of daily life, and observe common practices. Yet the definition proves too narrow when one considers dispersed communities. The Jewish diaspora of mediaeval Europe is a case in point. Its members were scattered across the kingdoms and principalities of the continent, separated by hundreds of miles, different languages, and often hostile borders. A Jew in Toledo might never meet a Jew in Cracow. Regular interaction in any direct sense was impossible for the great majority. Yet they shared religious customs, a liturgical language, a body of sacred texts, and a profound sense of collective belonging. No historian would deny that they constituted a community. The first and second conditions fail, yet the community persists. The definition has mistaken geographical proximity and face-to-face interaction — features of the local community — for essential features of community as such.

definition: A group of persons constitutes a community if and only if (i) membership is voluntary, (ii) the members recognise one another as fellow members, and (iii) the group pursues a common good.
conditions: membership is voluntary
members recognise one another as fellow members
group pursues a common good
counterexample: A person born into a small rural village inherits membership in that community without any act of volition. He is recognised by the villagers and participates in the common life of the place, yet he did not choose to belong; he was born into it. The first condition fails, yet the village is a paradigmatic community.
missing_condition: The definition demands voluntary entry, but many of the most significant communities — the village, the nation, the linguistic group — are joined by birth rather than by choice.
passage: It is sometimes held that a group of persons constitutes a community if and only if membership is voluntary, the members recognise one another as fellow members, and the group pursues a common good. This account captures certain communities well: a guild of craftsmen, a religious brotherhood, a philosophical society. In each case the members have chosen to join, they know one another as fellows, and they work towards a shared end. Yet the definition encounters a difficulty with communities into which persons are born. Consider a small rural village in which generations of families have lived side by side. A child born in that village is a member of the community from the moment of his birth. He is recognised by the other villagers, he participates in the festivals and labours of the place, and the village pursues its common good — the maintenance of the church, the upkeep of the roads, the management of the common land. But his membership was not voluntary; he did not choose to belong. He was born there, as his parents were before him. The first condition fails. The definition has taken a feature of elective associations — voluntary entry — and imposed it upon community as such, when in truth many of the deepest communities are those one is born into rather than those one chooses to join.

definition: A practice constitutes a tradition if and only if (i) it has been transmitted from one generation to the next over a considerable period, (ii) the practitioners are conscious of its antiquity, and (iii) it remains substantially unchanged across generations.
conditions: transmitted across generations over a considerable period
practitioners conscious of its antiquity
remains substantially unchanged across generations
counterexample: The celebration of Christmas trees in England became widespread only in the mid-nineteenth century, following the example of the Royal household. Within a single generation it was embraced as a cherished tradition, yet it had not been transmitted over a considerable period, and its practitioners in 1860 could scarcely have been conscious of great antiquity. The first and second conditions fail, yet the practice was already spoken of as a tradition.
missing_condition: The definition requires long transmission and consciousness of antiquity, but traditions may be rapidly adopted and experienced as traditional by virtue of their ritual character, their association with important occasions, and their felt connection to communal identity.
passage: A natural account holds that a practice constitutes a tradition if and only if it has been transmitted from one generation to the next over a considerable period, the practitioners are conscious of its antiquity, and it remains substantially unchanged across generations. This definition captures the paradigm cases: the celebration of harvest festivals in English villages, the recitation of ancestral genealogies in certain cultures, the observance of ancient religious rites. Each has been handed down over centuries, each is felt to be ancient, and each retains a recognisable form. Yet consider the Christmas tree. The practice of decorating an evergreen tree at Christmas became widespread in England only after the eighteen-forties, when the Royal household popularised the custom. Within scarcely a generation, the Christmas tree was embraced across the country and spoken of as a cherished tradition. But in 1860, the practice had not been transmitted over a considerable period — it was barely twenty years old in most households. And the practitioners could not have been conscious of great antiquity, for the custom was palpably recent. The first and second conditions fail. Yet the Christmas tree was already experienced and described as a tradition. The definition has insisted upon temporal depth, when a practice may acquire the character of a tradition through its ritual significance and its association with communal life, even when its actual history is brief.

definition: A practice constitutes a tradition if and only if (i) it is performed in the same manner each time it occurs, (ii) it serves a social function recognised by the community, and (iii) deviation from the practice is met with disapproval.
conditions: performed in the same manner each time
serves a recognised social function
deviation met with disapproval
counterexample: The tradition of jazz improvisation in New Orleans requires that each performance differ from the last; the musicians are expected to depart from any fixed arrangement. The tradition serves a recognised social function and deviation from the norm of improvisation would indeed be frowned upon, yet the practice is not performed in the same manner each time. The first condition fails, yet jazz improvisation is a tradition of the highest order.
missing_condition: The definition equates tradition with invariant repetition, but some traditions consist precisely in the expectation of creative variation within a recognised framework.
passage: One might define a tradition as a practice that is performed in the same manner each time it occurs, serves a social function recognised by the community, and is such that deviation from it is met with disapproval. This definition captures certain traditions well: the fixed liturgy of a church service, the prescribed steps of a ceremonial dance, the unvarying words of a toast at a formal dinner. In each case the practice is repeated identically, it serves a recognised purpose, and departures from the established form are censured. Yet consider the tradition of jazz improvisation as practised in New Orleans. The essence of the tradition is that each performance must differ from every other. The musicians take a familiar melody and transform it through spontaneous invention; to play the same solo twice would be to violate the very spirit of the art. The tradition serves a recognised social function — it is central to the musical culture of the city and to the identity of its practitioners. And deviation from the expectation of improvisation would indeed be met with disapproval. But the practice is emphatically not performed in the same manner each time. The first condition fails. The definition has mistaken the rigidity of certain traditions for an essential feature of tradition as such, overlooking those traditions whose vitality lies in creative variation rather than in faithful repetition.

definition: A pattern of behaviour constitutes a custom if and only if (i) it is practised by the majority of a given society, (ii) it is transmitted by imitation rather than by formal instruction, and (iii) it carries no legal sanction for non-observance.
conditions: practised by the majority of the society
transmitted by imitation rather than formal instruction
carries no legal sanction for non-observance
counterexample: In many societies, the custom of wearing mourning dress after a bereavement is observed by only a minority of the population at any given time — namely, those who have recently lost a relative. It is not practised by the majority. Yet it is universally recognised as a custom and is transmitted informally. The first condition fails, yet mourning dress is a paradigmatic custom.
missing_condition: The definition requires majority practice, but a custom need only be recognised and observed by those to whom it applies in the relevant circumstances; it need not be actively practised by most members of the society at any given moment.
passage: A plausible account holds that a pattern of behaviour constitutes a custom if and only if it is practised by the majority of a given society, it is transmitted by imitation rather than by formal instruction, and it carries no legal sanction for non-observance. This definition captures familiar customs: shaking hands upon meeting, removing one's hat indoors, sending cards at Christmas. Each is practised by most people, learned informally, and not enforced by law. Yet consider the custom of wearing mourning dress after a bereavement. In British society, this custom was long observed with great solemnity: the bereaved wore black for a prescribed period, the depth and duration of mourning varying according to the closeness of the relationship. The custom was transmitted by imitation and social expectation, and there was no legal penalty for disregarding it. But at any given moment, only a small minority of the population — those who had recently suffered a loss — were actually practising the custom. The majority were not in mourning. The first condition fails. Yet no one would deny that wearing mourning dress was a custom of English society. The definition has confused the universality of a custom's recognition with the universality of its active practice, when in truth a custom may apply only in particular circumstances and be practised only by those to whom those circumstances pertain.

definition: A pattern of behaviour constitutes a custom if and only if (i) it has persisted for a long period, (ii) its practitioners can articulate the reason for its observance, and (iii) it is specific to a particular culture or locality.
conditions: has persisted for a long period
practitioners can articulate the reason for its observance
specific to a particular culture or locality
counterexample: The custom of applauding at the end of a performance is observed in virtually every culture around the world. It has persisted for centuries and its practitioners can generally offer a reason for it. Yet it is not specific to a particular culture or locality; it is near-universal. The third condition fails, yet applause is plainly a custom.
missing_condition: The definition restricts custom to the culturally particular, but some customs are so widespread as to be practically universal, and their universality does not deprive them of their customary character.
passage: One might propose that a pattern of behaviour constitutes a custom if and only if it has persisted for a long period, its practitioners can articulate the reason for its observance, and it is specific to a particular culture or locality. This definition captures many customs admirably: the siesta in Mediterranean countries, the tea ceremony in Japan, the handshake in Western Europe. Each has persisted over time, each has a recognised rationale, and each is associated with a particular culture. Yet consider the custom of applauding at the conclusion of a musical or theatrical performance. Audiences in London, in Vienna, in Tokyo, in Buenos Aires, and in virtually every city in which public performances are given express their appreciation by clapping their hands. The practice has persisted for centuries, and most people can articulate the reason: applause signals approval and gratitude. But the custom is not specific to any particular culture or locality; it is near-universal. The third condition fails. The definition has assumed that customs are culturally local, when in fact some customs are so fundamental to human social intercourse that they transcend cultural boundaries. Universality does not strip a practice of its customary character; it merely shows that the definition has drawn the boundaries too narrowly.

definition: A social arrangement constitutes an institution if and only if (i) it is formally organised with explicit rules, (ii) it persists beyond the tenure of any individual member, and (iii) it fulfils a recognised social function.
conditions: formally organised with explicit rules
persists beyond any individual member
fulfils a recognised social function
counterexample: The institution of marriage in many societies has no formal organisation in the bureaucratic sense — there is no central body administering it, no written constitution governing its internal life. It persists beyond any individual and fulfils a profound social function, but in its customary form it lacks the formal organisational structure that the definition requires. The first condition fails, yet marriage is among the most fundamental of all social institutions.
missing_condition: The definition demands formal organisation with explicit rules, but many institutions — marriage, hospitality, gift-giving — are constituted by norms, expectations, and shared understandings rather than by formal organisation.
passage: A familiar account holds that a social arrangement constitutes an institution if and only if it is formally organised with explicit rules, it persists beyond the tenure of any individual member, and it fulfils a recognised social function. This definition captures many prominent institutions: the university, the bank, the parliament. Each is formally organised, each endures beyond the lives of its members, and each serves a recognised social purpose. Yet consider the institution of marriage as it exists in a great many societies. Marriage persists across generations; it is not tied to any particular couple. It fulfils social functions of the highest importance: the regulation of kinship, the rearing of children, the ordering of property and inheritance. But in its customary form, marriage has no formal organisation in the bureaucratic sense. There is no central administrative body, no written charter, no office-bearers. The norms of marriage are conveyed by custom, expectation, and informal social pressure rather than by an explicit rulebook. The first condition fails. The definition has modelled the concept of institution upon the bureaucratic organisation, overlooking the vast range of institutions — marriage, hospitality, mourning, gift-giving — that are constituted by shared norms and expectations rather than by formal rules and organisational charts.

definition: A social arrangement constitutes an institution if and only if (i) it was deliberately established for a stated purpose, (ii) it has a defined membership, and (iii) it exercises authority over its members.
conditions: deliberately established for a stated purpose
has a defined membership
exercises authority over its members
counterexample: Language is a social institution par excellence, yet no language was deliberately established for a stated purpose. English, French, and Chinese arose through gradual, unplanned processes over centuries. Language has no defined membership — anyone may learn and speak it — and it exercises no authority over its speakers in the way that a parliament governs its members. All three conditions fail, yet language is indisputably an institution.
missing_condition: The definition conceives of institutions as deliberately created bodies with formal boundaries, but some of the most important institutions — language, money, the market — arose spontaneously and lack defined membership or authoritative control.
passage: One might define an institution as a social arrangement that was deliberately established for a stated purpose, has a defined membership, and exercises authority over its members. This definition captures the paradigm cases of institutional creation: the founding of a university by royal charter, the establishment of a hospital by an act of parliament, the incorporation of a trading company. Each was deliberately established, each has a defined membership, and each exercises authority. Yet consider language. Language is, by any reasonable account, a social institution of the first importance. It shapes thought, mediates all social interaction, and persists across generations. But no language was deliberately established for a stated purpose. English was not founded; it grew. It has no defined membership: anyone who learns it may speak it, and no authority admits or excludes speakers. And language exercises no authority over its speakers in the formal sense; one may speak ungrammatically without sanction from any institutional body. All three conditions fail. Yet to deny that language is an institution would be absurd. The definition has taken the deliberately founded organisation as the model of all institutions, neglecting the great class of institutions that arise spontaneously through the accumulated practices of countless individuals over long periods of time.

definition: A people possesses a culture if and only if (i) they share a common language, (ii) they produce distinctive works of art, and (iii) they transmit their way of life to subsequent generations through deliberate instruction.
conditions: share a common language
produce distinctive works of art
transmit way of life through deliberate instruction
counterexample: The Swiss share no single common language — they speak German, French, Italian, and Romansch — yet they possess a recognisable culture with distinctive customs, political traditions, and social practices. The first condition fails, yet Swiss culture is a well-established reality.
missing_condition: The definition makes a shared language essential, but a culture may be sustained by common political institutions, shared customs, and a collective sense of identity even where no single language is spoken by all.
passage: It is sometimes held that a people possesses a culture if and only if they share a common language, they produce distinctive works of art, and they transmit their way of life to subsequent generations through deliberate instruction. This definition captures many familiar cases: French culture, with its common language, its literary and artistic achievements, and its conscious transmission through education; Japanese culture, similarly unified by language and sustained by deliberate pedagogy. Yet the case of Switzerland presents a difficulty. The Swiss speak four languages — German in the north and east, French in the west, Italian in the south, and Romansch in certain Alpine valleys. There is no single common language shared by all Swiss citizens. Yet Switzerland possesses a recognisable culture: a tradition of political neutrality, a distinctive form of direct democracy, characteristic customs of civic life, and a strong sense of national identity. The Swiss transmit their way of life to their children, and they produce works of art and craft that are identifiably Swiss. The first condition fails, yet the culture is real. The definition has elevated a shared language to the status of a necessary condition, when in truth a culture may be constituted and sustained by common institutions, customs, and a collective identity that transcend linguistic boundaries.

definition: A people possesses a culture if and only if (i) their practices are stable over time, (ii) they are distinguishable from neighbouring peoples by their customs, and (iii) their culture is internally homogeneous.
conditions: practices stable over time
distinguishable from neighbours by customs
culture internally homogeneous
counterexample: Indian culture encompasses an extraordinary diversity of languages, religions, customs, and artistic traditions. The practices of a Tamil Brahmin differ markedly from those of a Punjabi Sikh. The culture is emphatically not internally homogeneous. The third condition fails, yet Indian culture is a meaningful and widely recognised entity.
missing_condition: The definition requires internal homogeneity, but many of the world's richest cultures are characterised by internal diversity rather than uniformity, held together by overarching themes and shared reference points rather than by sameness.
passage: One might propose that a people possesses a culture if and only if their practices are stable over time, they are distinguishable from neighbouring peoples by their customs, and their culture is internally homogeneous. This definition captures small, isolated communities well: a tribal group with uniform customs, a monastic order with a single rule of life. Yet it encounters grave difficulty when applied to large and complex civilisations. Consider Indian culture. The subcontinent is home to hundreds of languages, numerous religions, and an extraordinary diversity of customs and practices. The dietary habits, marriage rituals, artistic traditions, and social arrangements of a Tamil Brahmin in Madras differ profoundly from those of a Punjabi Sikh in Amritsar. Regional variation is not marginal but fundamental. The culture is emphatically not internally homogeneous. The third condition fails. Yet Indian culture is a meaningful and widely recognised entity — a civilisation with identifiable themes, shared reference points, and a continuity that stretches back thousands of years. The definition has demanded uniformity where what exists is unity in diversity: a culture held together not by sameness of practice but by overarching traditions, shared texts, and a common civilisational inheritance that accommodates enormous internal variation.

definition: A rule of conduct constitutes a social norm if and only if (i) it is widely observed within the relevant group, (ii) violation of it provokes a negative response from other group members, and (iii) the rule can be explicitly stated by those who observe it.
conditions: widely observed within the group
violation provokes negative response
rule can be explicitly stated by observers
counterexample: The norms governing conversational distance — how close one stands to an interlocutor — are widely observed and their violation provokes discomfort or annoyance. Yet most people cannot explicitly state the rule. They observe the norm unconsciously; if asked, they would struggle to articulate the precise distance that constitutes the boundary. The third condition fails, yet conversational distance is governed by a genuine social norm.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the norm be articulable, but many social norms operate below the level of conscious awareness, governing behaviour through habit and unreflective social attunement rather than through explicit rules.
passage: A plausible account holds that a rule of conduct constitutes a social norm if and only if it is widely observed within the relevant group, its violation provokes a negative response from other group members, and the rule can be explicitly stated by those who observe it. This definition captures many norms effectively: the norm against queue-jumping, the norm of punctuality, the norm of reciprocating a greeting. Each is widely observed, each is enforced by social disapproval, and each can be stated in words. Yet consider the norms governing conversational distance — the physical space one maintains when speaking to another person. These norms are widely observed: in English society, one typically stands at arm's length from an interlocutor. Their violation provokes a clear negative response: if a stranger stands too close, one feels discomfort and steps back; if he stands too far away, the conversation feels cold and strained. But most people cannot explicitly state the rule. They do not know the distance in feet or inches; they observe the norm without being able to articulate it. If pressed, they might say only that one should stand at a 'comfortable' distance — which is circular rather than informative. The third condition fails. The definition has assumed that norms are consciously held principles, when in truth many of the most powerful social norms operate beneath the level of explicit awareness, governing behaviour through habit and tacit social understanding.

definition: A rule of conduct constitutes a social norm if and only if (i) it serves the collective welfare of the group, (ii) it is obeyed out of a sense of obligation rather than self-interest, and (iii) it applies equally to all members of the group.
conditions: serves the collective welfare
obeyed out of obligation rather than self-interest
applies equally to all members
counterexample: The norm of deference to elders, observed in many societies, does not apply equally to all members: it imposes obligations on the young whilst conferring privileges on the old. The third condition fails, yet deference to elders is a social norm of great antiquity and force.
missing_condition: The definition requires equal application, but many norms are asymmetric by design, imposing different obligations on different persons according to age, sex, rank, or role.
passage: One might hold that a rule of conduct constitutes a social norm if and only if it serves the collective welfare of the group, it is obeyed out of a sense of obligation rather than self-interest, and it applies equally to all members of the group. This definition captures egalitarian norms well: the norm of honesty, the norm of mutual aid, the norm of fair dealing. Each serves the collective good, each is felt as an obligation, and each applies to everyone alike. Yet many norms are not egalitarian in their application. Consider the norm of deference to elders, which is observed with great seriousness in many societies around the world. The young are expected to yield to the old in matters of precedence, to speak respectfully, and to defer to the judgement of their seniors. This norm serves a social function — the preservation of social order and the honouring of experience — and it is obeyed out of a sense of duty. But it does not apply equally to all: it imposes obligations upon the young whilst conferring privileges upon the old. A young man must defer; an old man is deferred to. The third condition fails. The definition has projected the structure of egalitarian norms upon norms generally, when in truth many of the most deeply rooted social norms are deliberately and necessarily asymmetric, assigning different duties to persons in different positions.

definition: An interaction constitutes cooperation if and only if (i) two or more agents act together towards a shared goal, (ii) each agent is aware of the others' participation, and (iii) each agent benefits from the joint activity.
conditions: two or more agents act towards a shared goal
each agent aware of others' participation
each agent benefits from joint activity
counterexample: In wartime, soldiers may cooperate in a dangerous assault from which some of them will certainly not return. Each soldier acts towards the shared goal and is aware of her comrades' participation, yet the soldier who is killed does not benefit from the joint activity. The third condition fails for that individual, yet her participation was genuine cooperation.
missing_condition: The definition requires that each participant benefit, but cooperation may involve sacrifice: an agent may cooperate towards a goal from which they expect no personal benefit, or from which they may even suffer harm.
passage: A natural account holds that an interaction constitutes cooperation if and only if two or more agents act together towards a shared goal, each agent is aware of the others' participation, and each agent benefits from the joint activity. This definition captures many everyday cases: farmers who cooperate to bring in the harvest, merchants who cooperate to organise a market, neighbours who cooperate to build a wall. In each case the participants share a goal, know of one another's involvement, and derive a benefit. Yet consider a military assault in wartime. A company of soldiers advances together upon a fortified position. They share a goal — the capture of the position — and each is acutely aware of her comrades' participation. Their action is coordinated, purposeful, and directed towards a common end. But some of them will be killed. The soldier who falls does not benefit from the joint activity; she loses everything. The third condition fails for her. Yet no one would deny that her participation was cooperative: she acted in concert with her comrades towards a shared objective. The definition has made mutual benefit a necessary condition of cooperation, when in truth cooperation sometimes demands sacrifice, and the willingness to bear costs — even the ultimate cost — for the sake of a shared purpose is among the highest forms of cooperative action.

definition: An interaction constitutes cooperation if and only if (i) the agents communicate their intentions to one another, (ii) they coordinate their actions by explicit agreement, and (iii) the resulting outcome could not have been achieved by any one agent alone.
conditions: agents communicate their intentions
coordinate by explicit agreement
outcome unachievable by any one agent alone
counterexample: Two strangers pushing a stalled motorcar out of an intersection cooperate effectively without any explicit agreement. They see the need, take up positions at the rear of the vehicle, and push together. There is no verbal communication and no prior agreement; the cooperation arises spontaneously from the shared perception of the situation. The second condition fails, yet the interaction is cooperative.
missing_condition: The definition requires explicit agreement, but cooperation often arises tacitly from mutual recognition of a shared situation, without any communication of intentions or negotiation of terms.
passage: One might define cooperation as an interaction in which the agents communicate their intentions to one another, they coordinate their actions by explicit agreement, and the resulting outcome could not have been achieved by any one agent alone. This definition captures the planned cooperative venture: a business partnership, a treaty between nations, a jointly authored book. In each case, the parties discuss their aims, agree upon a plan, and accomplish together what neither could accomplish alone. Yet cooperation is not always so deliberate. Consider two strangers who happen upon a motorcar stalled in the middle of a busy intersection. Without a word, both move to the rear of the vehicle and begin to push. They adjust their efforts to one another's pace, they strain together, and the car rolls clear of the road. The outcome — the removal of the obstruction — could not have been achieved by either alone, but there was no communication of intentions and no explicit agreement. The cooperation arose spontaneously from the shared perception of a need and a tacit understanding of what was required. The second condition fails. The definition has taken the explicitly negotiated partnership as the model of all cooperation, overlooking the vast domain of tacit cooperation in which agents coordinate their actions through mutual awareness and shared understanding, without the formality of verbal agreement.

definition: A person trusts another if and only if (i) they believe the other will act in their interest, (ii) this belief is based upon evidence of the other's past reliability, and (iii) they are willing to make themselves vulnerable to the other's actions.
conditions: believes other will act in their interest
belief based on evidence of past reliability
willing to make themselves vulnerable
counterexample: An infant trusts its mother from the earliest days of life, before it has any evidence of past reliability. The infant has had no opportunity to assess its mother's track record; its trust is instinctive rather than evidential. The second condition fails, yet the infant's trust is perhaps the most fundamental form of trust there is.
missing_condition: The definition grounds trust in evidential assessment of reliability, but the most basic forms of trust — an infant's trust in its mother, a person's trust in a stranger who offers help — arise prior to and independently of any evidence of past performance.
passage: A familiar account holds that a person trusts another if and only if she believes the other will act in her interest, this belief is based upon evidence of the other's past reliability, and she is willing to make herself vulnerable to the other's actions. This definition captures many instances of trust: one trusts one's solicitor because he has handled one's affairs competently in the past; one trusts a tradesman because he has consistently delivered good work. The evidential basis of trust is clear. Yet consider the trust of an infant in its mother. From the earliest days of life, the infant relies upon its mother for nourishment, warmth, and protection. It turns to her in distress and is comforted by her presence. This reliance has every mark of trust: the infant is utterly vulnerable, and it behaves as though it expects its mother to act in its interest. But the infant has no evidence of past reliability. It has not assessed its mother's track record and formed a considered judgement. Its trust is not based upon evidence at all; it is instinctive, pre-rational, and immediate. The second condition fails. The definition has modelled trust upon the mature, reflective trust of adults who have accumulated experience, overlooking the primal trust that precedes all evidence and constitutes the foundation upon which later, more considered forms of trust are built.

definition: A person trusts another if and only if (i) they have a positive expectation of the other's behaviour, (ii) the trust is reciprocal — the other trusts their in return, and (iii) the relationship is sustained over a period of time.
conditions: positive expectation of other's behaviour
trust is reciprocal
relationship sustained over time
counterexample: A patient trusts her surgeon to perform an operation competently, yet the surgeon may have no occasion to trust the patient in return; the relationship is not reciprocal. Moreover, the trust may exist only for the duration of a single procedure, not over a sustained period. The second and third conditions fail, yet the patient's trust in the surgeon is genuine and important.
missing_condition: The definition requires reciprocity and duration, but trust may be one-directional and momentary — a patient trusts a surgeon they have never met, in a relationship that lasts only hours.
passage: One might propose that a person trusts another if and only if she has a positive expectation of the other's behaviour, the trust is reciprocal, and the relationship is sustained over a period of time. This definition captures the trust between friends, between long-standing business partners, and between spouses: in each case the trust runs in both directions and has been built up over years. Yet consider a patient who places herself in the hands of a surgeon she has never met. She trusts him to perform the operation competently, to exercise due care, and not to cause unnecessary harm. Her trust is genuine — she is making herself profoundly vulnerable — and she has a positive expectation of his behaviour. But the trust is not reciprocal: the surgeon has no particular need to trust the patient, and the patient has done nothing to earn the surgeon's trust. Moreover, the relationship may last only a few hours — the duration of the consultation and the operation — and may never be renewed. The second and third conditions fail. The definition has taken the long-standing mutual trust of close relationships as the model of all trust, neglecting the common and important cases in which trust is one-directional, situational, and brief.

definition: A person is alienated if and only if (i) they are estranged from the product of them labour, (ii) they experience their work as unfulfilling, and (iii) their condition is the result of economic exploitation.
conditions: estranged from product of them labour
experiences work as unfulfilling
condition results from economic exploitation
counterexample: A retired scholar who has withdrawn from all social life may feel profoundly alienated — estranged from his community, purposeless, and disconnected from the world around him. He is not engaged in labour, there is no product from which to be estranged, and his condition is not the result of economic exploitation but of social isolation. All three conditions fail, yet his alienation is real and deeply felt.
missing_condition: The definition ties alienation exclusively to the labour process and economic exploitation, but alienation may arise from social isolation, cultural displacement, or the loss of meaningful connection to others, quite independently of economic conditions.
passage: A well-known account holds that a person is alienated if and only if she is estranged from the product of her labour, she experiences her work as unfulfilling, and her condition is the result of economic exploitation. This definition captures the alienation of the factory worker who produces goods she will never own and performs tasks that offer no satisfaction — the paradigm case in nineteenth-century social thought. Yet alienation is a broader phenomenon than this account allows. Consider a retired scholar who, upon leaving the university, withdraws into solitude. He has no labour from which to be estranged, no employer who exploits him, and no product that is taken from him. Yet he feels profoundly alienated: disconnected from the colleagues who once gave his life meaning, purposeless in the absence of his vocation, estranged from a society whose concerns he no longer shares. His alienation is social and existential rather than economic. It arises not from exploitation but from isolation — from the severing of the bonds that once connected him to a community of fellow enquirers. All three conditions of the definition fail, yet the phenomenon is real and recognisable. The definition has reduced alienation to its economic species, overlooking the forms of alienation that arise from social disconnection, cultural displacement, and the loss of meaning in the absence of any economic cause.

definition: A person is alienated if and only if (i) they feel themselves to be an outsider within them own society, (ii) this feeling is caused by the society's failure to include them, and (iii) they desire to be included.
conditions: feels themselves to be an outsider
feeling caused by society's failure to include them
desires to be included
counterexample: A monk who has voluntarily withdrawn from the world to live in contemplative solitude may feel himself an outsider, yet his estrangement is not caused by society's failure to include him — he has excluded himself by choice — and he does not desire to be included. The second and third conditions fail, yet the monk's detachment from ordinary social life is a recognisable form of alienation, albeit a chosen one.
missing_condition: The definition assumes alienation is involuntary and caused by exclusion, but a person may become alienated through deliberate withdrawal, finding that the distance they have chosen produces an estrangement they did not fully anticipate.
passage: One might hold that a person is alienated if and only if she feels herself to be an outsider within her own society, this feeling is caused by the society's failure to include her, and she desires to be included. This account captures the alienation of the excluded: the immigrant who is denied acceptance, the poor man who is shut out of civic life. In each case the person feels estranged, the cause is social exclusion, and the desire for inclusion is evident. Yet consider a monk who voluntarily enters a cloistered order, renouncing the comforts and connexions of ordinary life. He lives apart from society, observes a rule of silence, and devotes himself to prayer and contemplation. Over the years he comes to feel profoundly estranged from the world beyond the monastery walls — its concerns seem alien, its pleasures incomprehensible, its values foreign to his own. He is an outsider. But his estrangement was not caused by society's failure to include him; he chose to withdraw. And he does not desire to be included; he has renounced the world deliberately. The second and third conditions fail. Yet the monk's detachment from ordinary social existence is a recognisable form of alienation — one that may grow beyond what he intended and produce a sense of displacement that he did not foresee. The definition has restricted alienation to the involuntary, overlooking the possibility that chosen withdrawal may itself engender estrangement.

definition: A political arrangement rests upon a social contract if and only if (i) the members of the society have explicitly consented to its terms, (ii) the terms are mutually agreed upon by all parties, and (iii) the contract establishes the obligations of both the governed and the governing.
conditions: members have explicitly consented to its terms
terms mutually agreed upon by all parties
contract establishes obligations of governed and governing
counterexample: The subjects of the English Crown after the Glorious Revolution of 1688 were widely held to live under a constitutional settlement that established mutual obligations between Crown and Parliament. Yet the vast majority of subjects — women, the unpropertied, the inhabitants of distant colonies — never explicitly consented to any terms and were not parties to any agreement. The first and second conditions fail for most of the population, yet the settlement was regarded as a social contract.
missing_condition: The definition requires explicit, universal consent, but the social contract has traditionally been understood as resting upon tacit consent, hypothetical agreement, or the acceptance implied by continued residence, rather than upon the actual, explicit consent of every member.
passage: A familiar account holds that a political arrangement rests upon a social contract if and only if the members of the society have explicitly consented to its terms, the terms are mutually agreed upon by all parties, and the contract establishes the obligations of both the governed and the governing. This definition captures the idealised picture of contractual government: free and equal persons convene, negotiate terms, agree upon a framework, and bind themselves and their rulers to its provisions. Yet the history of actual political settlements tells a different story. Consider the constitutional arrangement that followed the Glorious Revolution of 1688. The Bill of Rights and the Act of Settlement established the powers and limits of the Crown, the authority of Parliament, and the liberties of the subject. This settlement was widely celebrated as embodying the spirit of a social contract. But the vast majority of English subjects never explicitly consented to any terms. Women had no political voice whatever. The unpropertied — the great majority of men — had no vote and no seat at any table of negotiation. The inhabitants of the colonies were bound by arrangements in which they had taken no part. The first and second conditions fail for all of these persons. The definition requires a form of consent that has never obtained in any actual polity. The concept of the social contract, as it has been understood by its most distinguished advocates, rests not upon explicit universal consent but upon tacit acceptance, hypothetical agreement, or the obligations implied by continued residence within the commonwealth.

definition: A political arrangement rests upon a social contract if and only if (i) it was established at a determinate moment in history, (ii) its terms are recorded in a founding document, and (iii) subsequent generations are bound by the original agreement.
conditions: established at a determinate moment in history
terms recorded in a founding document
subsequent generations bound by original agreement
counterexample: The unwritten constitution of the United Kingdom has no founding document and was not established at a single determinate moment. It evolved over centuries through statute, convention, and judicial decision. Yet it is frequently described as embodying a social contract between the Crown, Parliament, and the people. The first and second conditions fail, yet the arrangement functions as a social contract.
missing_condition: The definition demands a datable founding and a written charter, but a social contract may be constituted by an evolving web of conventions, statutes, and mutual expectations that develop gradually and are never codified in a single document.
passage: One might propose that a political arrangement rests upon a social contract if and only if it was established at a determinate moment in history, its terms are recorded in a founding document, and subsequent generations are bound by the original agreement. This definition fits certain cases well: the American Constitution was adopted at a specific moment, its terms are recorded in a written document, and subsequent generations are governed by its provisions. Yet consider the constitutional arrangements of the United Kingdom. There is no single founding document. The Magna Carta, the Petition of Right, the Bill of Rights, the Parliament Acts, and a vast body of convention and judicial precedent together compose the constitution, but no one of these is the founding charter of the state. The constitution was not established at a determinate moment; it evolved over many centuries, acquiring its present shape through the gradual accumulation of statutes, customs, and understandings. The first and second conditions fail. Yet the British constitutional arrangement is frequently and reasonably described as embodying a social contract — an understanding between Crown, Parliament, and people regarding their respective rights and obligations. The definition has identified the social contract with its most formal and explicit expression, overlooking the possibility that a social contract may consist in an evolving, unwritten web of mutual expectations and constitutional conventions that have never been reduced to a single text.

definition: An interaction constitutes cooperation if and only if (i) the agents share a common goal, (ii) each agent voluntarily chooses to participate, and (iii) the agents trust one another to fulfil their respective roles.
conditions: agents share a common goal
each agent voluntarily chooses to participate
agents trust one another to fulfil roles
counterexample: Prisoners of war forced to labour together on a construction project share a common goal — completing the task so as to avoid punishment — and they may cooperate with great efficiency. But their participation is not voluntary; it is coerced. The second condition fails, yet the prisoners' joint action is genuine cooperation, albeit cooperation born of compulsion rather than free choice.
missing_condition: The definition requires voluntariness, but cooperation may arise under coercion; what is essential is coordinated joint action towards a shared end, not the freedom of the agents' initial decision to participate.
passage: A plausible account holds that an interaction constitutes cooperation if and only if the agents share a common goal, each agent voluntarily chooses to participate, and the agents trust one another to fulfil their respective roles. This definition captures the cooperative ventures of free agents: a team of craftsmen building a house, a group of scholars compiling an encyclopaedia. In each case the goal is shared, the participation is willing, and there is mutual trust. Yet consider prisoners of war compelled to labour together on a railway or a fortification. They share a common goal — the completion of the project, so as to avoid punishment or to secure better conditions. They coordinate their efforts, divide the tasks, and support one another in the work. Their joint action has all the hallmarks of cooperation. But their participation is not voluntary; it is coerced by the threat of violence. They did not choose to cooperate; they were forced to. The second condition fails. Yet the cooperation is real — the prisoners accomplish together what none could accomplish alone, and they do so through coordinated, goal-directed action. The definition has made voluntariness a necessary condition, confusing the auspices under which cooperation arises with the nature of cooperation itself.

definition: A person trusts another if and only if (i) they have formed a judgement of the other's character, (ii) the judgement is favourable, and (iii) they act upon the judgement by relying upon the other in some matter of importance.
conditions: formed a judgement of the other's character
judgement is favourable
acts upon judgement by relying on the other
counterexample: A traveller who asks a passer-by for directions trusts the stranger to answer honestly, yet she has formed no judgement of his character. She knows nothing about him. She relies upon a general presumption of good will among strangers rather than upon any assessment of his individual qualities. The first condition fails, yet her reliance upon the stranger constitutes trust.
missing_condition: The definition grounds trust in character assessment, but much ordinary trust rests upon a default presumption of good will extended to strangers, requiring no prior judgement of the individual's character.
passage: It is sometimes held that a person trusts another if and only if she has formed a judgement of the other's character, the judgement is favourable, and she acts upon the judgement by relying upon the other in some matter of importance. This definition captures the trust that arises from acquaintance: one trusts a friend because one has observed his honesty over many years, or one trusts an employee because one has assessed her competence and integrity. The judgement of character precedes and justifies the reliance. Yet consider a traveller in an unfamiliar city who stops a passer-by to ask for directions. She trusts the stranger to answer honestly and to the best of his knowledge. She acts upon his answer, turning left rather than right, and arrives at her destination. Her reliance upon the stranger is genuine trust — she has made herself vulnerable to his potential dishonesty or error. But she has formed no judgement of his character. She has never seen him before; she knows nothing of his honesty, his competence, or his disposition. Her trust rests not upon an assessment of the individual but upon a general presumption that strangers, on the whole, will answer a simple question honestly. The first condition fails. The definition has taken the considered trust of long acquaintance as the model of all trust, overlooking the pervasive background trust that enables strangers to interact and upon which much of social life depends.

definition: A rule of conduct constitutes a social norm if and only if (i) it promotes social harmony, (ii) it is endorsed by the community as a whole, and (iii) it has persisted without significant change over a long period.
conditions: promotes social harmony
endorsed by community as a whole
persisted without significant change
counterexample: The norm of duelling among the European aristocracy persisted for centuries and was endorsed by the class to which it applied, yet it did not promote social harmony — it resulted in injury and death. It was, moreover, condemned by the Church and by many legal authorities. The first condition fails, yet duelling was a genuine and powerful social norm within its milieu.
missing_condition: The definition assumes that norms serve harmonious ends, but some deeply entrenched norms are destructive, and their persistence depends not upon their contribution to harmony but upon the social pressures and conceptions of honour that sustain them.
passage: One might define a social norm as a rule of conduct that promotes social harmony, is endorsed by the community as a whole, and has persisted without significant change over a long period. This definition captures many familiar norms: the norm of politeness, the norm of fair dealing, the norm of respect for one's elders. Each promotes harmony, each is broadly endorsed, and each has endured. Yet consider the norm of duelling among the European aristocracy. From the sixteenth century to the nineteenth, gentlemen settled disputes of honour by fighting with swords or pistols, often to the death. The norm was powerful: a gentleman who refused a challenge risked social ruin. It was endorsed within the aristocratic class and persisted for some three hundred years. But it did not promote social harmony. It resulted in the injury and death of countless men, often over trivial slights. It was condemned by the Church, prohibited by law, and deplored by many thoughtful persons as barbarous. The first condition fails entirely. The definition has assumed that social norms are benign instruments of harmony, but the norm of duelling was a destructive practice sustained by conceptions of honour and by the severe social penalties attached to its non-observance. A norm may be deeply entrenched and widely followed without promoting the common good.

definition: A group of persons constitutes a community if and only if (i) its members are united by bonds of affection, (ii) each member knows every other member personally, and (iii) the group is small enough that all members can assemble in one place.
conditions: members united by bonds of affection
each member knows every other personally
group small enough to assemble in one place
counterexample: The Roman Catholic Church has been described as a community of faith for nearly two thousand years. Its members number in the hundreds of millions; no member knows every other personally, and the whole body could never assemble in one place. Yet the Church is a community — bound by shared belief, liturgy, and a sense of mutual belonging. The second and third conditions fail, yet the community is real.
missing_condition: The definition restricts community to the face-to-face group, but large-scale communities may be sustained by shared symbols, rituals, and an imagined fellowship that transcends personal acquaintance.
passage: A tempting account holds that a group of persons constitutes a community if and only if its members are united by bonds of affection, each member knows every other member personally, and the group is small enough that all members can assemble in one place. This definition captures the intimate community: the small village, the close-knit congregation, the circle of friends. In each case the members know one another, care for one another, and can gather together. Yet consider the Roman Catholic Church. For nearly two thousand years it has been described as a community — the community of the faithful, the body of Christ. Its members share a creed, a liturgy, a calendar of observances, and a profound sense of belonging to a single fellowship. But the Church numbers hundreds of millions of members across every continent. No member knows every other personally; the Pope himself has met only a tiny fraction of his flock. And the whole body could never assemble in one place — no building and no city could contain it. The second and third conditions fail entirely. Yet the Church is a community in a deep and important sense: its members are bound together by shared symbols, rituals, and a conviction of spiritual fellowship that transcends personal acquaintance. The definition has modelled community upon the small, face-to-face group, excluding the great communities that are sustained by what one might call an imagined but nonetheless real bond of common membership.

definition: A political arrangement rests upon a social contract if and only if (i) the parties to the contract are rational, self-interested individuals, (ii) the contract is designed to maximise the welfare of each party, and (iii) no party would rationally prefer the state of nature to the contracted arrangement.
conditions: parties are rational self-interested individuals
contract maximises welfare of each party
no party would rationally prefer state of nature
counterexample: A community of religious dissenters who establish a colony governed by scriptural law may be said to have entered a social contract, yet their motivations are not self-interested in the ordinary sense — they seek to honour divine commandments and to live in accordance with their faith. The contract is not designed to maximise individual welfare but to realise a vision of the godly society. The first and second conditions fail, yet the arrangement is contractual.
missing_condition: The definition presupposes rational self-interest as the sole motive, but social contracts may be animated by religious conviction, communal solidarity, or moral idealism rather than by the maximisation of individual welfare.
passage: One might hold that a political arrangement rests upon a social contract if and only if the parties to the contract are rational, self-interested individuals, the contract is designed to maximise the welfare of each party, and no party would rationally prefer the state of nature to the contracted arrangement. This definition captures the contractarian picture in its most austere form: solitary individuals, calculating their advantage, agree to submit to a common authority because each judges that the benefits of civil society outweigh the costs. Yet consider the Puritan settlers who established colonies in New England in the seventeenth century. They drew up covenants and compacts — the Mayflower Compact of 1620 is the most famous — which established the terms of their common life. These were social contracts in a meaningful sense. But the parties were not motivated by rational self-interest as ordinarily understood. They sought to build a community governed by scriptural law, to honour God, and to live in accordance with their faith. The contract was not designed to maximise individual welfare but to realise a vision of the righteous society, in which the good of the soul took precedence over material advantage. The first and second conditions fail. The definition has imported a particular psychological assumption — that persons are self-interested maximisers — into the very concept of the social contract, excluding the contracts of those who are moved by faith, duty, or communal aspiration.

definition: A group G constitutes a community if and only if (i) the members of G share a bounded geographical locality, (ii) the members of G engage in regular face-to-face interaction, and (iii) the members of G possess a shared set of customs and traditions that are transmitted across generations.
conditions: the members of G share a bounded geographical locality
the members of G engage in regular face-to-face interaction
the members of G possess a shared set of customs and traditions that are transmitted across generations
counterexample: Consider the case of a dispersed religious order whose members are scattered across several continents. The monks of this order share no bounded geographical locality; they reside in monasteries separated by thousands of miles. They do not engage in regular face-to-face interaction, meeting perhaps once a decade at a general chapter. Yet they share a profound sense of common identity, follow the same rule of life, observe the same liturgical calendar, and regard one another as brothers bound by solemn vows. They constitute a community in any ordinary sense of the term.
missing_condition: The definition requires geographical proximity and face-to-face interaction as necessary conditions, yet communities may be constituted by shared commitments, practices, and bonds of mutual recognition that persist across great distances. What is wanted is a condition pertaining to the mutual recognition of membership and shared obligation, rather than the merely spatial requirement of co-location.
passage: One might propose that a group constitutes a community if and only if its members share a bounded geographical locality, engage in regular face-to-face interaction, and possess a shared set of customs and traditions transmitted across generations. The definition has considerable initial plausibility; one thinks at once of the English village, the Scottish clan settled upon its ancestral lands, or the tightly knit parish of a market town. In each of these cases the members occupy a common territory, see one another daily, and participate in customs — harvest festivals, local fairs, patterns of mutual aid — that have been handed down from generation to generation. The three conditions appear to identify what is essential to communal life. Yet one must attend to the dispersed religious order. The Benedictines, let us say, maintain monasteries in France, Italy, Brazil, and the Philippines. The monks of these several houses share no bounded locality; they are separated by oceans. They do not meet face to face with any regularity; a monk in Normandy may never encounter his brother in Manila. Yet the order is a community, and its members regard it as such. They follow the same Rule, observe the same hours of prayer, submit to the same vows of stability, obedience, and conversion of life. They correspond, they pray for one another, and when one house suffers misfortune the others rally to its aid. The bonds of obligation, shared practice, and mutual recognition that unite them are no less real for being exercised at a distance. The definition, in requiring geographical co-location and face-to-face interaction, mistakes the conditions that happen to characterise one familiar form of community for conditions that are necessary to community as such.

definition: A group G constitutes a community if and only if (i) the members of G are bound by ties of kinship or descent, (ii) the members of G share a common language or dialect, and (iii) the members of G collectively own or steward a common resource such as land or water.
conditions: the members of G are bound by ties of kinship or descent
the members of G share a common language or dialect
the members of G collectively own or steward a common resource such as land or water
counterexample: Consider the community of scholars that formed around a great mediaeval university. The masters and students of such an institution were drawn from many nations and spoke many tongues; Latin served as their common medium of instruction, yet they were not bound by kinship, nor did they share a dialect in any ordinary sense. They collectively owned no land or common resource. Yet they constituted a community of remarkable cohesion, bound by shared intellectual purposes, common statutes, and mutual obligations of teaching and learning.
missing_condition: The definition conflates community with clan or tribal organisation. It neglects the possibility that communities may be constituted by shared purposes and voluntarily assumed obligations rather than by descent, linguistic homogeneity, or collective ownership of material resources.
passage: One might propose that a group constitutes a community if and only if its members are bound by ties of kinship or descent, share a common language or dialect, and collectively own or steward a common resource such as land or water. The definition captures something important about many traditional forms of communal life. The Highland clan, for instance, was united by real or fictive kinship, spoke Gaelic, and held its lands in common under the chief. The agricultural village of mediaeval England was similarly bound by ties of family, shared the local dialect, and managed common fields and pastures through collective arrangements. One might suppose that these three conditions identify the essential structure of any genuine community. Yet one must attend to the mediaeval university. The community of masters and students at Bologna or Paris was drawn from across Europe; an Englishman, a Provençal, a Saxon, and a Lombard might sit together in the same lecture hall. They were not bound by kinship; they came from different families, different regions, different nations. They did not share a common dialect; they spoke to one another in Latin precisely because their native tongues were mutually unintelligible. And they collectively owned no common resource — the university's property, such as it was, belonged to the corporation as a legal entity, not to the members as co-stewards of ancestral land. Yet the university was a community, and was recognised as such by its members and by the wider society. Its members were bound by shared intellectual purposes, by common statutes and oaths, by the mutual obligations of teaching and learning. The definition, in requiring kinship, linguistic homogeneity, and collective resource ownership, identifies features that are characteristic of one historically important form of community — the kin-based territorial group — but that are not necessary conditions of community as such.

definition: A practice P constitutes a tradition if and only if (i) P has been continuously performed for at least three generations, (ii) P is transmitted from elders to the young through explicit instruction, and (iii) the practitioners of P can articulate its origin and the reasons for its performance.
conditions: P has been continuously performed for at least three generations
P is transmitted from elders to the young through explicit instruction
the practitioners of P can articulate its origin and the reasons for its performance
counterexample: Consider the tradition of a particular style of bread-making in a rural district. The women of the district have baked their bread in a distinctive fashion for as long as anyone can remember, shaping the loaves in a peculiar manner and scoring them with a characteristic pattern. The practice is transmitted not through explicit instruction but through imitation; daughters watch their mothers and learn by doing, without formal teaching. Moreover, no one in the district can articulate the origin of the practice or offer reasons for the particular shape and scoring pattern. When asked, they say simply that it has always been done so. Yet it is plainly a tradition.
missing_condition: The definition requires explicit instruction and articulable origins, yet many traditions are transmitted through imitation and habituation rather than through deliberate teaching, and their origins are lost beyond the reach of memory. What is wanted is a broader account of transmission that encompasses tacit, non-discursive modes of cultural continuity.
passage: One might propose that a practice constitutes a tradition if and only if it has been continuously performed for at least three generations, it is transmitted from elders to the young through explicit instruction, and its practitioners can articulate its origin and the reasons for its performance. The definition has a certain appeal. One thinks of the Jewish Passover seder, in which the youngest child is explicitly instructed to ask why this night is different from all other nights, and the elder recounts the story of the Exodus that gives the ceremony its meaning. The practice has been maintained for many generations, it is transmitted through deliberate teaching, and its practitioners can articulate both its origin and its rationale with considerable precision. Yet one must attend to the case of the local bread-making tradition. In a certain rural district, the women bake their bread in a distinctive fashion — the loaves are shaped in a particular way, scored with a characteristic cross-hatched pattern, and baked at a particular temperature. The practice has been maintained for generations beyond counting. But it is not transmitted through explicit instruction; there is no lesson, no catechism of bread-making, no elder who sits the young woman down and explains the method. The daughter learns by standing beside her mother and watching, by handling the dough herself under her mother's eye, by absorbing the technique through repeated imitation. And no one in the district can articulate the origin of the practice or the reasons for the particular shape and scoring. When pressed, the women shrug and say that their mothers did it so, and their grandmothers before them. The practice is transmitted tacitly, through imitation and habituation, and its origins are lost. Yet it is a tradition in any reasonable sense of the word. The definition, in requiring explicit instruction and articulable origins, mistakes the features of a particular class of self-conscious, discursive traditions for features that all traditions must possess.

definition: A practice P constitutes a tradition if and only if (i) P is performed in the same manner on each occasion, without variation, (ii) P serves a recognisable social function such as marking a rite of passage, and (iii) P involves the use of special objects, garments, or symbols set apart from everyday use.
conditions: P is performed in the same manner on each occasion, without variation
P serves a recognisable social function such as marking a rite of passage
P involves the use of special objects, garments, or symbols set apart from everyday use
counterexample: Consider the tradition of storytelling around the evening fire in an oral culture. The stories told are traditional — they belong to the common inheritance of the group and have been passed down through generations — yet each telling varies. The storyteller embroiders, abbreviates, adapts the tale to the audience and the occasion. The practice serves no single recognisable social function such as marking a rite of passage; it is performed for entertainment, instruction, and the sheer pleasure of narrative. And it involves no special objects or garments; the storyteller sits in ordinary clothes by an ordinary fire. Yet storytelling of this kind is among the oldest and most pervasive of human traditions.
missing_condition: The definition requires invariance, a specific social function, and the use of special objects, yet traditions may be characterised by creative variation within continuity, may serve diffuse or multiple purposes, and may require no material apparatus whatsoever. The definition confuses the features of ritual tradition with the features of tradition as such.
passage: One might propose that a practice constitutes a tradition if and only if it is performed in the same manner on each occasion without variation, it serves a recognisable social function such as marking a rite of passage, and it involves the use of special objects, garments, or symbols set apart from everyday use. The definition is not without plausibility. The coronation ceremony of an English monarch, for instance, follows a prescribed order that has remained largely unchanged for centuries, serves the manifest social function of marking the accession to the throne, and involves the use of sacred objects — the crown, the orb, the sceptre — that are kept apart from common use. One might suppose that these three conditions capture what it is for a practice to be traditional. Yet one must attend to the tradition of oral storytelling. In societies without writing, the telling of stories around the evening fire is a tradition of immense antiquity and cultural importance. The stories themselves are traditional — they belong to the shared inheritance of the group and are passed from one generation to the next. But the practice is not performed in the same manner on each occasion. Every telling is different; the storyteller adapts the tale to the audience, elaborates certain episodes, abbreviates others, introduces topical allusions, and responds to the reactions of the listeners. The practice does not serve a single recognisable function; it entertains, it instructs, it preserves the group's history, it gives pleasure, and it binds the listeners together in a shared imaginative experience. And it involves no special objects, garments, or symbols; the storyteller wears ordinary clothes and sits by an ordinary fire. Yet oral storytelling is as genuine a tradition as any coronation. The definition mistakes the rigid, ceremonial, materially elaborate tradition for the only kind of tradition there is, and in doing so excludes some of the most fundamental and widespread traditional practices known to human culture.

definition: A pattern of behaviour B constitutes a custom if and only if (i) B is practised by a majority of the members of a given society, (ii) B is regarded by its practitioners as obligatory, and (iii) deviation from B is met with formal punishment or sanction.
conditions: B is practised by a majority of the members of a given society
B is regarded by its practitioners as obligatory
deviation from B is met with formal punishment or sanction
counterexample: Consider the custom of tipping a waiter after a meal in a restaurant. The practice is widespread and is regarded by most as something one ought to do. Yet deviation from the custom is not met with formal punishment or sanction. No law compels tipping; no tribunal imposes a fine upon the person who leaves nothing. The consequence of failing to tip is merely a certain social awkwardness, a disapproving glance from the waiter, perhaps a sense of mild discomfort in the person who has departed from the custom. The third condition fails, yet tipping is plainly a custom.
missing_condition: The definition requires formal punishment for deviation, yet many customs are enforced only through informal social pressure — disapproval, awkwardness, gossip, or mild ostracism — rather than through any formal mechanism. What is needed is a broader notion of social enforcement that encompasses informal as well as formal sanctions.
passage: One might propose that a pattern of behaviour constitutes a custom if and only if it is practised by a majority of the members of a given society, is regarded by its practitioners as obligatory, and deviation from it is met with formal punishment or sanction. The definition is initially attractive. One thinks of the customs of mourning in Victorian England, where the wearing of black for a prescribed period was practised by the majority, was regarded as a solemn obligation, and where failure to observe the custom could result in social censure of a quasi-formal character — exclusion from polite society, withdrawal of invitations, public remarks of a reproving nature. The three conditions appear to be satisfied, and one might suppose that they identify what is essential to any custom. Yet one must attend to the custom of tipping. In many societies it is customary to leave a gratuity for the waiter after a meal in a restaurant. The practice is widespread; the majority of diners observe it. It is regarded, at least loosely, as something one ought to do; the person who does not tip feels that he has failed to discharge a social obligation, however minor. But deviation from the custom is not met with formal punishment or sanction. There is no law that requires tipping; no court will fine the person who leaves the restaurant without adding a gratuity. The waiter may frown, the diner's companions may raise an eyebrow, and the person himself may feel a twinge of social discomfort, but these are informal, diffuse responses, not formal sanctions. The third condition fails entirely. Yet tipping is a custom — it is one of the most familiar customs of modern social life. The definition, in requiring formal punishment for deviation, mistakes the features of those customs that have hardened into quasi-legal obligations for features that all customs must possess.

definition: A pattern of behaviour B constitutes a custom if and only if (i) B is performed at fixed, recurring intervals — daily, weekly, or annually, (ii) B has a discernible origin in a specific historical event or decree, and (iii) B is associated with a particular social class or occupational group.
conditions: B is performed at fixed, recurring intervals — daily, weekly, or annually
B has a discernible origin in a specific historical event or decree
B is associated with a particular social class or occupational group
counterexample: Consider the custom of shaking hands upon meeting an acquaintance. The practice is not performed at fixed intervals; it occurs whenever two acquaintances happen to meet, which may be at any time and with no regularity whatsoever. Its origin is lost in antiquity — no specific historical event or decree can be cited as its source. And it is not associated with any particular social class or occupational group; persons of every station shake hands. Yet the handshake is one of the most universal and deeply rooted customs of Western social life.
missing_condition: The definition requires temporal regularity, traceable origins, and class specificity, yet customs may be triggered by contingent social encounters rather than by fixed schedules, may have origins that are wholly untraceable, and may be practised across all social strata without distinction. The definition confuses the features of calendrical and class-specific customs with those of customs in general.
