passage: One might propose that a pattern of behaviour constitutes a custom if and only if it is performed at fixed, recurring intervals, has a discernible origin in a specific historical event or decree, and is associated with a particular social class or occupational group. The definition is not wholly implausible. One thinks of the custom of the Lord Mayor's Show in London, performed annually on a fixed date, traceable to a specific charter of the thirteenth century, and associated with the mercantile and civic class. The three conditions are satisfied, and one might take this as evidence that they capture what is essential to custom. Yet one must attend to the handshake. The custom of shaking hands upon meeting an acquaintance is one of the most pervasive and deeply entrenched customs of Western social life. It is practised by persons of every class, from the labourer to the lord; it is not associated with any particular occupational group or social stratum. It is not performed at fixed intervals; it occurs whenever two acquaintances happen to meet, which may be several times in a single day or not for months. And its origin is lost beyond the reach of historical enquiry; no specific event or decree can be cited as the moment at which the custom began. Various conjectures have been offered — that it originated as a gesture of peace, showing that the hand held no weapon — but these are speculations, not established historical facts. The handshake satisfies none of the three conditions, yet it is a custom of the most genuine and deeply rooted kind. The definition, drawn from a particular type of formal, calendrical, class-bound custom, mistakes the features of this type for the features of custom as such, and thereby excludes some of the most fundamental and widespread customs of social life.

definition: An arrangement A constitutes a social institution if and only if (i) A is formally codified in written rules or statutes, (ii) A is administered by designated officers who hold their positions through formal appointment, and (iii) A occupies a permanent physical location such as a building or complex of buildings.
conditions: A is formally codified in written rules or statutes
A is administered by designated officers who hold their positions through formal appointment
A occupies a permanent physical location such as a building or complex of buildings
counterexample: Consider the institution of the market in a pre-literate society. The market assembles at a customary place — perhaps a crossroads or a riverbank — on customary days, and buyers and sellers exchange goods according to customary prices and procedures. Yet the institution is not formally codified in written rules; the society has no writing. It is not administered by designated officers holding formal appointments; the market governs itself through the habitual compliance of its participants. And it does not occupy a permanent building; the market-place is an open area that serves other purposes on non-market days. Yet the market is an institution — one of the most fundamental institutions of social life.
missing_condition: The definition requires formal codification, appointed officers, and a permanent physical location, yet institutions may exist and persist through customary practice, informal governance, and the habitual convergence of participants at a place that has no permanent institutional character. What is wanted is a broader account that recognises institutions constituted by stable patterns of expectation and conduct rather than by formal apparatus alone.
passage: One might propose that an arrangement constitutes a social institution if and only if it is formally codified in written rules or statutes, is administered by designated officers holding their positions through formal appointment, and occupies a permanent physical location. The definition has considerable plausibility when one considers such institutions as the Bank of England — governed by its charter, administered by its governors, and housed in Threadneedle Street — or the University of Oxford, with its statutes, its appointed officers, and its ancient colleges. One might suppose that formal codification, appointed administration, and physical permanence are essential to institutional existence. Yet one must attend to the market in a pre-literate society. In many societies that possess no writing, the market is nonetheless an institution of central importance. Buyers and sellers assemble at a customary place — a crossroads, a riverbank, a clearing — on customary days, and they exchange goods according to customary prices and procedures that are understood by all participants. The institution is not codified in any written document, for the society has no script. No designated officers administer it; the market operates through the habitual compliance of its participants, who know what is expected and act accordingly. And the market occupies no permanent building; the place of assembly is an open space that reverts to other uses on non-market days. Yet the market is an institution. It is a stable, enduring arrangement that structures the economic life of the society, that is recognised by its members as a distinct social entity, and that persists across generations. The definition, in requiring written codification, formal officers, and a permanent building, identifies features that characterise certain modern, bureaucratic institutions but that are not necessary conditions of institutional existence as such.

definition: An arrangement A constitutes a social institution if and only if (i) A was deliberately established at an identifiable moment by an act of founding, (ii) A has a defined membership with criteria for admission and exclusion, and (iii) A possesses a hierarchical internal structure with distinct levels of authority.
conditions: A was deliberately established at an identifiable moment by an act of founding
A has a defined membership with criteria for admission and exclusion
A possesses a hierarchical internal structure with distinct levels of authority
counterexample: Consider the institution of language. A natural language such as English is an institution in the fullest sense: it structures human interaction, imposes obligations of conformity upon its speakers, and persists across centuries. Yet no one deliberately founded the English language at an identifiable moment. It has no defined membership with criteria for admission and exclusion; anyone who speaks it is, in some sense, a participant, and there is no authority empowered to admit or exclude speakers. And it possesses no hierarchical internal structure with distinct levels of authority; no person or body governs English by right.
missing_condition: The definition requires deliberate founding, defined membership, and hierarchy, yet some of the most fundamental social institutions — language, money, the family — arose through gradual, unplanned processes of social evolution rather than through acts of deliberate creation, and they operate without formal membership criteria or hierarchical governance.
passage: One might propose that an arrangement constitutes a social institution if and only if it was deliberately established at an identifiable moment by an act of founding, has a defined membership with criteria for admission and exclusion, and possesses a hierarchical internal structure with distinct levels of authority. The definition captures something true of many institutions. The Royal Society was founded in 1660 by a specific act of incorporation; it has a defined membership, with fellows elected according to established criteria; and it possesses a hierarchical structure, with a president, council, and officers of various ranks. One might take these features to be essential to institutional existence. Yet one must attend to the institution of language. The English language is, in a perfectly natural sense, a social institution. It structures the interactions of millions of persons; it imposes upon its speakers obligations of conformity — one must use words in roughly the way others use them if one is to be understood; and it persists across centuries, outlasting any individual speaker. But it was not deliberately established at any identifiable moment. No founding charter was drawn up for the English language; it emerged gradually, over centuries, through the slow accumulation of changes in pronunciation, grammar, and vocabulary. It has no defined membership; there are no criteria for admission to or exclusion from the class of English speakers. Anyone who speaks the language participates in the institution, and no authority is empowered to deny participation. And it has no hierarchical structure; no person or body governs the English language by right of office. Grammarians may prescribe, but their authority is advisory, not governmental. The definition mistakes the features of deliberately created, formally organised institutions for features that are necessary to all institutions, and thereby excludes some of the most fundamental institutions of human social life.

definition: A society S possesses a culture if and only if (i) S has produced enduring works of art, literature, or architecture, (ii) S maintains formal institutions of learning dedicated to the preservation and transmission of these works, and (iii) S draws a recognised distinction between an educated élite and the general populace.
conditions: S has produced enduring works of art, literature, or architecture
S maintains formal institutions of learning dedicated to the preservation and transmission of these works
S draws a recognised distinction between an educated élite and the general populace
counterexample: Consider a nomadic people of the Central Asian steppe who have produced no permanent architecture, who possess no written literature, and who maintain no formal institutions of learning. Yet they possess a rich culture: an elaborate oral poetry, a complex system of kinship obligations, a distinctive cuisine, a tradition of horsemanship, and a body of musical practice that is deeply admired by neighbouring peoples. They draw no distinction between an educated élite and the general populace; cultural participation is universal. All three conditions fail, yet the society is manifestly cultured.
missing_condition: The definition identifies culture with the products and institutions of literate, sedentary civilisation, and thereby excludes the cultures of non-literate and nomadic peoples. What is wanted is a broader conception that encompasses the total pattern of a society's shared practices, meanings, and expressive forms, whether or not these are materialised in permanent works or preserved through formal institutions.
passage: One might propose that a society possesses a culture if and only if it has produced enduring works of art, literature, or architecture, maintains formal institutions of learning dedicated to the preservation and transmission of these works, and draws a recognised distinction between an educated élite and the general populace. The definition has a certain plausibility when one considers the great civilisations of antiquity. Classical Athens produced the Parthenon, the tragedies of Sophocles, the dialogues of Plato; it maintained schools of philosophy; and it drew a clear distinction between the educated citizen and the unlettered. One might suppose that these features are constitutive of culture as such. Yet one must attend to the nomadic peoples of the Central Asian steppe. These peoples produced no permanent architecture — their dwellings were portable felt tents, assembled and disassembled as the herds moved. They possessed no written literature in the narrow sense; their stories, genealogies, and songs were transmitted orally. They maintained no formal institutions of learning — no academies, no schools, no libraries. And they drew no recognised distinction between an educated élite and the populace at large; every member of the group participated in the cultural life of the whole. Yet these peoples possessed cultures of remarkable richness and complexity. Their oral poetry was elaborate and highly structured; their systems of kinship obligation were intricate and precisely articulated; their traditions of horsemanship, falconry, and textile work constituted bodies of practical skill transmitted with great care from generation to generation. The definition, in requiring permanent works, formal institutions, and an educated élite, identifies culture with the particular form it takes in literate, sedentary civilisations, and thereby excludes the cultures of peoples whose cultural achievements are no less real for being embodied in transient materials and transmitted through oral and practical rather than written and institutional means.

definition: A society S possesses a unified culture if and only if (i) all members of S share the same religious or cosmological commitments, (ii) all members of S participate in the same festivals and ceremonies, and (iii) all members of S regard the same historical narratives as authoritative accounts of their collective past.
conditions: all members of S share the same religious or cosmological commitments
all members of S participate in the same festivals and ceremonies
all members of S regard the same historical narratives as authoritative accounts of their collective past
counterexample: Consider the culture of the Ottoman Empire at its zenith. Muslims, Christians, and Jews lived within a single political and social order, holding different religious commitments, celebrating different festivals, and regarding different historical narratives as authoritative. Yet the Ottoman Empire possessed a unified culture in a meaningful sense — a shared idiom of architecture, cuisine, music, and social courtesy that cut across confessional lines and gave the empire its distinctive cultural character.
missing_condition: The definition requires unanimity of religious commitment, ceremonial participation, and historical narrative, yet a unified culture may encompass significant internal diversity in all three respects. What is wanted is a condition pertaining to shared practical idioms and mutual intelligibility of social conduct rather than uniformity of conviction.
passage: One might propose that a society possesses a unified culture if and only if all its members share the same religious or cosmological commitments, participate in the same festivals and ceremonies, and regard the same historical narratives as authoritative accounts of their collective past. The definition has a certain attraction when one considers a small, homogeneous society — the Iceland of the saga period, perhaps, where all free men worshipped the same gods, gathered at the same Althing, and shared the same body of genealogical and historical lore. One might suppose that such unanimity is what constitutes cultural unity. Yet one must attend to the Ottoman Empire. At the height of its power in the sixteenth century, the Empire comprised Muslims, Orthodox Christians, Armenian Christians, Jews, and others, each holding different religious commitments and celebrating different festivals. The Muslim observed Ramadan; the Christian observed Lent; the Jew observed Passover. Their authoritative historical narratives diverged fundamentally — the Muslim looked to the life of the Prophet, the Christian to the Gospels, the Jew to the Torah and the Talmud. Yet the Ottoman Empire possessed a unified culture in a perfectly intelligible sense. There was a shared idiom of social life — a common cuisine, a common musical tradition that drew upon the modes and instruments of several peoples, a common architectural vocabulary, and a common code of social courtesy and hospitality that transcended confessional boundaries. The definition, in requiring unanimity of religious commitment, ceremony, and historical narrative, identifies cultural unity with a degree of homogeneity that is rarely found in complex societies and is not, in any case, necessary for the existence of a genuine shared culture.

definition: A rule R constitutes a social norm if and only if (i) R is explicitly stated and known to all members of the relevant group, (ii) compliance with R is motivated by fear of punishment, and (iii) R was established by a recognised authority such as a chief, council, or legislature.
conditions: R is explicitly stated and known to all members of the relevant group
compliance with R is motivated by fear of punishment
R was established by a recognised authority such as a chief, council, or legislature
counterexample: Consider the norm of queuing — of waiting one's turn in a line rather than pushing to the front. This norm is not explicitly stated in any code or proclamation; no authority ever decreed that one must queue. Compliance is motivated not primarily by fear of punishment but by a sense of fairness and the desire to avoid the disapproval of others. Yet queuing is a social norm of considerable force, observed with remarkable consistency in many societies, and its violation provokes genuine indignation.
missing_condition: The definition requires explicit statement, fear of punishment as the motivating force, and establishment by authority, yet many of the most powerful social norms are tacit, are sustained by internalised sentiments of fairness or propriety rather than by fear, and arose through spontaneous convergence rather than authoritative decree.
passage: One might propose that a rule constitutes a social norm if and only if it is explicitly stated and known to all members of the relevant group, compliance with it is motivated by fear of punishment, and it was established by a recognised authority. The definition has some plausibility when one considers the norms of a disciplined military regiment, where rules are explicitly promulgated, compliance is enforced by the threat of punishment, and the rules are established by the commanding officer. One might suppose that these features are essential to any social norm. Yet one must attend to the norm of queuing. In many societies — the English case is perhaps the most frequently remarked upon — it is a firm social norm that persons waiting for a service must form an orderly line and must be served in the order of their arrival. This norm is nowhere explicitly stated; one searches in vain for a statute or proclamation that establishes it. No recognised authority decreed that persons must queue. The norm arose, so far as anyone can tell, through a gradual, spontaneous convergence of practice — through the repeated experience that an orderly line is fairer and more efficient than a disordered crowd. And compliance is not motivated primarily by fear of punishment. There is no formal penalty for pushing to the front of a queue. What motivates compliance is a complex mixture of internalised fairness, fellow-feeling, and the desire to avoid the disapproval and indignation of others. The person who pushes in feels not that he risks punishment but that he has done something that is, in a simple and direct sense, unfair. The definition, in requiring explicit statement, fear of punishment, and authoritative establishment, identifies the features of formally promulgated, coercively enforced rules and mistakes them for the features of social norms as such.

definition: A rule R constitutes a social norm if and only if (i) R prescribes a specific, determinate action in a specific type of situation, (ii) R admits of no exceptions or contextual variation, and (iii) R applies uniformly to all members of the society regardless of their role or status.
conditions: R prescribes a specific, determinate action in a specific type of situation
R admits of no exceptions or contextual variation
R applies uniformly to all members of the society regardless of their role or status
counterexample: Consider the norm of hospitality as observed in many Mediterranean and Middle Eastern societies. The norm requires that a host provide food, shelter, and protection to a guest, but it does not prescribe a specific, determinate action; the host must exercise judgement about what the situation requires — how much food, what kind of shelter, how long the guest may stay. The norm admits of contextual variation; hospitality to a stranger differs in degree and kind from hospitality to a kinsman. And it does not apply uniformly regardless of role; the obligations of the host differ from those of the guest. Yet hospitality is among the most powerful and deeply entrenched social norms in human history.
missing_condition: The definition requires specificity, exceptionlessness, and uniform application, yet many of the most important social norms are open-textured, context-sensitive, and role-differentiated. What is wanted is a conception of norms that allows for the exercise of practical judgement within a framework of shared expectations.
passage: One might propose that a rule constitutes a social norm if and only if it prescribes a specific, determinate action in a specific type of situation, admits of no exceptions or contextual variation, and applies uniformly to all members of the society regardless of role or status. The definition has a certain clarity and rigour. One thinks of a simple norm such as the rule against theft: do not take what belongs to another. The prescribed action is specific — abstention from taking; the rule appears to admit of no exceptions; and it applies to everyone alike. One might suppose that all social norms share these features. Yet one must attend to the norm of hospitality. In many societies — those of the Mediterranean, the Middle East, the Central Asian steppe — the norm of hospitality is among the most fundamental and sacred of social obligations. A host must provide food, shelter, and protection to a guest. But the norm does not prescribe a specific, determinate action; what hospitality requires varies with the circumstances. The host must exercise practical judgement: a weary traveller arriving at nightfall requires different provision from a neighbour who calls in the afternoon. The norm admits of abundant contextual variation; the hospitality owed to a stranger is different in character and degree from that owed to a kinsman, and the hospitality expected of a wealthy man differs from that expected of a poor one. And the norm does not apply uniformly regardless of role; the obligations of the host and the obligations of the guest are quite different — the guest, for instance, must not abuse the host's generosity or overstay his welcome. The definition, in requiring specificity, exceptionlessness, and uniformity, captures the features of simple prohibitive rules and mistakes them for features that all social norms must possess.

definition: An interaction I constitutes cooperation if and only if (i) the parties to I have explicitly agreed upon a common goal, (ii) each party contributes an equal share of effort towards the achievement of that goal, and (iii) the parties are motivated by the expectation of mutual material benefit.
conditions: the parties to I have explicitly agreed upon a common goal
each party contributes an equal share of effort towards the achievement of that goal
the parties are motivated by the expectation of mutual material benefit
counterexample: Consider the case of two strangers who find themselves attempting to free a cart that has become stuck in a muddy road. Neither has explicitly agreed upon a common goal; they simply see the situation and begin to push. One is stronger and contributes a greater share of physical effort than the other. Neither expects material benefit; the cart belongs to a third party, and the two strangers will go their separate ways once the cart is freed. Yet their joint action is a clear instance of cooperation.
missing_condition: The definition requires explicit agreement, equal contribution, and the expectation of material benefit, yet cooperation may arise spontaneously without explicit agreement, may involve unequal contributions, and may be motivated by sympathy, duty, or simple fellow-feeling rather than by the prospect of material gain.
passage: One might propose that an interaction constitutes cooperation if and only if the parties have explicitly agreed upon a common goal, each contributes an equal share of effort, and the parties are motivated by the expectation of mutual material benefit. The definition has some plausibility when applied to commercial partnerships: two merchants agree to pool their capital for a joint venture, each investing an equal sum, and both expect to profit from the arrangement. One might suppose that these features — explicit agreement, equal contribution, mutual material benefit — are essential to cooperation as such. Yet one must attend to the case of the stuck cart. Two strangers, travelling in opposite directions along a country road, come upon a cart that has sunk to its axles in mud. Without a word of explicit agreement, both set their shoulders to the cart and push. One is a large man and contributes the greater part of the physical effort; the other, slighter of build, does what he can. The cart belongs to a farmer who is not present; neither stranger has any expectation of material reward. They push because the situation presents itself as one that calls for joint effort, and because it would be unnatural, even shameful, to walk past. Their interaction is plainly cooperative — they are working together to achieve a result that neither could achieve alone. Yet the three conditions of the definition all fail. There is no explicit agreement; the contributions are unequal; and neither party expects material benefit. The definition mistakes the features of calculated, contractual cooperation for the features of cooperation as such, and thereby excludes the spontaneous, asymmetric, and disinterested forms of joint action that are among the most common instances of cooperative behaviour in everyday life.

definition: An interaction I constitutes cooperation if and only if (i) the parties to I are aware of one another's contributions and can observe one another's actions, (ii) the parties to I act simultaneously or in close temporal proximity, and (iii) the parties to I can communicate with one another during the course of the interaction.
conditions: the parties to I are aware of one another's contributions and can observe one another's actions
the parties to I act simultaneously or in close temporal proximity
the parties to I can communicate with one another during the course of the interaction
counterexample: Consider the cooperation involved in the construction of a mediaeval cathedral. The stonemasons who carved the foundations in 1220 and the glaziers who installed the windows in 1280 were cooperating in the creation of a single great work, yet they were separated by sixty years. They could not observe one another's actions; they could not communicate; and the earlier workers could not have been aware of the specific contributions the later ones would make. Yet the cathedral is a cooperative achievement, and each generation's labour presupposed and built upon the work of those who came before.
missing_condition: The definition requires mutual awareness, temporal proximity, and communication, yet cooperation may extend across generations, with participants separated by decades or centuries, unable to observe or communicate with one another. What is wanted is a broader account of cooperation that encompasses temporally extended joint endeavours sustained by shared purposes and inherited plans.
passage: One might propose that an interaction constitutes cooperation if and only if the parties are aware of one another's contributions and can observe one another's actions, act simultaneously or in close temporal proximity, and can communicate with one another during the interaction. The definition is plausible when applied to a team of rowers pulling together on a river — each can see the others, they stroke in unison, and they can call out instructions and encouragement. One might suppose that mutual visibility, temporal simultaneity, and communication are essential to cooperative action. Yet one must attend to the building of a mediaeval cathedral. The great cathedrals of Europe were cooperative enterprises of extraordinary ambition, yet they were built over periods spanning generations. The stonemasons who laid the foundations of Chartres cathedral in one century did not live to see the glaziers who installed the rose windows in the next. The earlier workers could not observe the actions of the later ones; they could not communicate with them; and they were separated by decades of intervening time. Yet their labour was cooperative. Each generation worked according to a shared plan, bequeathed by the original master builder and refined by his successors; each generation's contribution presupposed and built upon the work of its predecessors; and the finished cathedral was a joint achievement to which all had contributed. The definition, in requiring mutual awareness, temporal proximity, and communication, captures the features of cooperation as it occurs between persons who are present to one another at the same moment, and mistakes these features for features that cooperation must always possess.

definition: A person S trusts a person T if and only if (i) S has had repeated positive interactions with T in the past, (ii) S possesses specific evidence of T's reliability based on T's prior conduct, and (iii) S has made a conscious, deliberate decision to rely upon T.
conditions: S has had repeated positive interactions with T in the past
S possesses specific evidence of T's reliability based on T's prior conduct
S has made a conscious, deliberate decision to rely upon T
counterexample: Consider a young child who trusts its mother. The infant, from its earliest days, rests in its mother's arms with a tranquillity that bespeaks the deepest trust. Yet the infant has not had repeated past interactions of the kind the definition envisages — it has barely begun to interact at all. It possesses no specific evidence of reliability based on prior conduct; it has not assessed its mother's track record. And it has made no conscious, deliberate decision to rely upon her; the trust is immediate, unreflective, and apparently innate. Yet it is trust, and of the most profound kind.
missing_condition: The definition requires a history of interaction, evidence of reliability, and conscious decision, yet the most fundamental form of trust — the trust of the infant for its mother — is prior to all experience, evidence, and deliberation. What is wanted is an account that recognises a primitive, pre-rational mode of trust grounded in the vulnerability of the one who trusts and the responsiveness of the one who is trusted.
passage: One might propose that a person trusts another if and only if the first person has had repeated positive interactions with the second, possesses specific evidence of the second's reliability based on prior conduct, and has made a conscious, deliberate decision to rely upon the second. The definition has considerable plausibility in the domain of commercial and professional relations. A merchant trusts a supplier because they have traded successfully on many occasions; the merchant can cite specific instances of the supplier's reliability; and the merchant's decision to extend credit is a conscious, calculated act of reliance. One might suppose that these features are essential to trust wherever it occurs. Yet one must attend to the trust of the infant. A newborn child, placed in its mother's arms, rests there with a tranquillity that bespeaks a trust of the most complete and unconditional character. The child does not stiffen or withdraw; it relaxes, it sleeps, it turns towards the warmth and heartbeat of the mother with an instinctive confidence. Yet the child has not had repeated positive interactions with the mother in any sense that the definition can accommodate — the child's life has only just begun. It possesses no specific evidence of the mother's reliability; it has not observed her conduct and drawn conclusions from it. And it has made no conscious decision to trust; the infant is incapable of deliberation of any kind. The trust is immediate, unreflective, and, as it were, given in advance of all experience. The definition, in requiring past interactions, evidence, and conscious decision, captures the features of mature, considered trust between adults and mistakes them for features that are necessary to trust as such, thereby excluding the most primitive and perhaps the most fundamental form of trust known to human life.

definition: A person S trusts a person T if and only if (i) S and T share a common group membership or social identity, (ii) S expects T to act in S's interest because of this shared identity, and (iii) S would feel betrayed rather than merely disappointed if T failed to act in S's interest.
conditions: S and T share a common group membership or social identity
S expects T to act in S's interest because of this shared identity
S would feel betrayed rather than merely disappointed if T failed to act in S's interest
counterexample: Consider the trust that a patient places in a surgeon whom the patient has never met and with whom the patient shares no common group membership or social identity. The patient is a shopkeeper from a small town; the surgeon is a specialist at a distant hospital. They belong to different social worlds. Yet the patient trusts the surgeon to perform the operation competently and with due care. The patient expects competent treatment not because of shared identity but because of the surgeon's professional role and training.
missing_condition: The definition requires shared group membership and identity-based expectations, yet trust may be extended to strangers on the basis of institutional roles, professional competence, and the normative expectations attaching to those roles. What is wanted is an account that accommodates trust in strangers who occupy positions of responsibility.
passage: One might propose that a person trusts another if and only if they share a common group membership or social identity, the first expects the second to act in the first's interest because of this shared identity, and the first would feel betrayed rather than merely disappointed if the second failed to do so. The definition captures something important about trust within close-knit groups. The members of a family, a clan, or a regiment trust one another in part because of their shared membership, and the violation of that trust is experienced not as mere disappointment but as betrayal — a rupture of the bond that membership creates. One might suppose that shared identity is the ground of all trust. Yet one must attend to the patient and the surgeon. A shopkeeper from a small market town requires an operation and is referred to a specialist at a large hospital in a distant city. The shopkeeper and the surgeon have never met; they share no common group membership, no social identity, no ties of kinship, locality, or profession. They are, in the fullest sense, strangers. Yet the shopkeeper trusts the surgeon. He submits to the anaesthetic, permits the surgeon to open his body with a knife, and rests in the confidence that the operation will be performed competently and with due care. The shopkeeper's expectation of competent treatment is not based on shared identity; it is based on the surgeon's institutional role, professional training, and the normative expectations that attach to the practice of medicine. The definition, in requiring shared group membership as a condition of trust, captures the features of particularistic, identity-based trust and mistakes them for features that all trust must possess, thereby excluding the impersonal, role-based trust that is indispensable to the functioning of complex modern societies.

definition: A person S is alienated if and only if (i) S is separated from the means of productive labour, (ii) S performs repetitive, mechanical work that affords no scope for creativity, and (iii) S is employed by another person or entity and works for a wage.
conditions: S is separated from the means of productive labour
S performs repetitive, mechanical work that affords no scope for creativity
S is employed by another person or entity and works for a wage
counterexample: Consider a wealthy landowner who possesses extensive means of production, employs others rather than being employed, and performs no repetitive labour whatsoever. Yet this landowner may be deeply alienated — estranged from his neighbours, disconnected from the traditions of his community, unable to find meaning or purpose in his life of leisure. He feels himself a stranger in his own district, cut off from the human fellowship that once sustained the landed class. All three conditions fail, yet he is alienated.
missing_condition: The definition identifies alienation exclusively with the condition of the wage-labourer separated from the means of production, yet alienation may afflict persons of any economic station. What is wanted is a broader account that encompasses estrangement from community, from meaningful social relations, and from a sense of purpose, regardless of one's relation to productive labour.
passage: One might propose that a person is alienated if and only if the person is separated from the means of productive labour, performs repetitive and mechanical work, and is employed by another for a wage. The definition is not without foundation; it captures something important about the condition of the factory worker in an industrial economy, who owns no tools or materials, performs the same narrow operation hour after hour, and labours for a wage set by the employer. The estrangement of such a worker from the product of his labour, from the process of production, and from his own creative capacities is a real and significant form of alienation. One might suppose that these economic conditions are essential to alienation as such. Yet one must attend to the alienated landowner. Consider a gentleman who possesses a large estate, fertile lands, and extensive means of production. He employs tenants and labourers; he is not employed by anyone. He performs no repetitive work; his days are spent in leisure. Yet he is alienated. He feels himself a stranger among his neighbours; the ties of mutual obligation and shared endeavour that once bound the landowner to his community have dissolved. He takes no part in the life of the parish; he finds no meaning in the management of his estates; he drifts through his days in a condition of aimless detachment. His alienation is not economic but social and spiritual — an estrangement from community, from purpose, and from the web of human relations that give life its significance. The definition, in locating alienation exclusively in the economic condition of the wage-labourer, identifies one important species of alienation but mistakes it for the genus.

definition: A person S is alienated if and only if (i) S is geographically displaced from the place of S's birth or upbringing, (ii) S lives among persons whose language and customs are foreign to S, and (iii) S desires to return to the place of origin but is prevented from doing so.
conditions: S is geographically displaced from the place of S's birth or upbringing
S lives among persons whose language and customs are foreign to S
S desires to return to the place of origin but is prevented from doing so
counterexample: Consider a person who has lived in the same village all his life, who speaks the local dialect, and who has no desire to be anywhere else. Yet the village has been transformed around him; its old ways have vanished, its customs have been abandoned, its familiar shops and gathering-places have been replaced by establishments that serve strangers passing through. The man has not moved, but the social world he inhabited has dissolved. He is alienated — estranged from a community that no longer exists — though he has never left the place of his birth.
missing_condition: The definition identifies alienation with geographical displacement and the desire to return, yet a person may be alienated without moving at all, if the social world that gave life its meaning is destroyed or transformed beyond recognition. What is wanted is a condition pertaining to the loss of the social world in which one felt at home, whether that loss is caused by one's own displacement or by the transformation of one's surroundings.
passage: One might propose that a person is alienated if and only if the person is geographically displaced from the place of birth or upbringing, lives among persons whose language and customs are foreign, and desires to return to the place of origin but is prevented from doing so. The definition captures something real about the condition of the exile. One thinks of the communities of displaced persons who, cast out of their homelands by war or persecution, live among strangers whose tongue they cannot speak and whose customs they cannot share, longing for a return that circumstances forbid. The alienation of such persons is genuine and poignant. One might suppose that displacement, cultural foreignness, and thwarted longing for return are the essential conditions of alienation. Yet one must attend to the man who has never left his village. He was born there; he has lived there all his life; he speaks the local dialect; and he has no desire to be elsewhere. But over the course of his lifetime the village has been transformed. The old families have moved away; the farms have been sold to distant owners; the public house where men gathered in the evenings has closed; the church where he was baptised holds services only once a month for a dwindling congregation. The customs he knew as a boy — the harvest supper, the village fête, the practice of neighbours helping one another with the hay — have vanished. He is surrounded by newcomers whose ways are strange to him, not because he has entered their world but because they have entered his. He is alienated — profoundly estranged from the social world around him — yet he has not moved an inch. The definition, in requiring geographical displacement and the desire to return, mistakes the alienation of the exile for the only form of alienation there is, and thereby overlooks the alienation of the person who remains in place while the place itself is transformed beyond recognition.

definition: A political arrangement A constitutes a social contract if and only if (i) A was established by an explicit act of collective agreement among the members of the society, (ii) the terms of A are recorded in a written document accessible to all members, and (iii) every member of the society voluntarily consented to A at the time of its establishment.
conditions: A was established by an explicit act of collective agreement among the members of the society
the terms of A are recorded in a written document accessible to all members
every member of the society voluntarily consented to A at the time of its establishment
counterexample: Consider the unwritten constitution of England. The political arrangements of the English state were not established by a single act of collective agreement; they evolved over centuries through custom, precedent, judicial decision, and occasional statute. The terms are not recorded in a single written document; they are dispersed across Magna Carta, the Bill of Rights, numerous Acts of Parliament, and an immense body of convention and practice. And no moment can be identified at which every member of the society voluntarily consented. Yet the English constitution embodies a form of social contract — an implicit, evolving understanding between governors and governed regarding the terms of political authority.
missing_condition: The definition requires an explicit founding act, a written document, and universal voluntary consent, yet the social contract may be implicit, unwritten, and sustained by tacit acquiescence and ongoing practice rather than by a single act of unanimous agreement.
passage: One might propose that a political arrangement constitutes a social contract if and only if it was established by an explicit act of collective agreement, its terms are recorded in a written document accessible to all, and every member of the society voluntarily consented at the time of its establishment. The definition has a certain appeal when one considers the founding of the American republic, where delegates met in convention, debated and drafted a written constitution, and the document was submitted to the states for ratification. One might suppose that explicit agreement, written terms, and voluntary consent are the essential elements of any social contract. Yet one must attend to the English constitution. The political arrangements of England were not established by a single act of agreement. They grew gradually, over many centuries, through the accretion of custom, precedent, and statute. There is no single written document that sets forth the terms of the English constitution; the relevant provisions are scattered across Magna Carta, the Petition of Right, the Bill of Rights of 1689, the Act of Settlement, the Parliament Acts, and a vast body of unwritten convention. And there was no moment at which every member of English society voluntarily consented to the arrangement. The constitution evolved; each generation inherited it from the last and modified it, often imperceptibly, through practice and interpretation. Yet the English constitution embodies a social contract in a perfectly intelligible sense — an understanding, implicit and evolving, between the crown and the people, between Parliament and the electorate, regarding the terms upon which political authority is exercised and the limits that constrain it. The definition, in requiring explicit agreement, a written document, and universal consent, captures the features of one particular model of constitutional founding and mistakes them for features that any social contract must possess.

definition: A political arrangement A rests upon a social contract if and only if (i) the parties to A are free and equal individuals acting in their own self-interest, (ii) A is the product of rational calculation aimed at maximising each party's individual advantage, and (iii) compliance with A is motivated solely by the recognition that defection would leave each party worse off.
conditions: the parties to A are free and equal individuals acting in their own self-interest
A is the product of rational calculation aimed at maximising each party's individual advantage
compliance with A is motivated solely by the recognition that defection would leave each party worse off
counterexample: Consider the social compact of a religious community that settles a new territory. The members of the community agree to govern themselves according to certain principles, but they do not act as free and equal individuals maximising self-interest. They act as members of a congregation, bound by shared religious obligations, and the terms of their compact are determined not by rational calculation of advantage but by their understanding of divine commandment. Compliance is motivated not by the fear that defection would leave them worse off but by a sense of sacred duty and mutual love.
missing_condition: The definition requires self-interested rational maximisation and fear of disadvantage as the sole motivations, yet social contracts may be grounded in shared moral or religious commitments, in bonds of mutual affection, and in a sense of sacred or communal obligation that is not reducible to individual advantage.
passage: One might propose that a political arrangement rests upon a social contract if and only if the parties are free and equal individuals acting in their own self-interest, the arrangement is the product of rational calculation aimed at maximising individual advantage, and compliance is motivated solely by the recognition that defection would leave each party worse off. The definition articulates a familiar model of the social contract as a bargain among rational egoists. One can see its appeal: if individuals in a state of nature calculate that cooperation serves their interests better than conflict, they may agree to submit to common rules, and the resulting arrangement can be understood as a contract grounded in mutual advantage. Yet one must attend to the religious compact. When a community of devout believers settles a new territory — one thinks of the Pilgrim Fathers and the Mayflower Compact of 1620 — they enter into an agreement to govern themselves, and this agreement has the character of a social contract. But the parties do not act as self-interested individuals maximising personal advantage. They act as members of a congregation, bound to one another by ties of faith and mutual obligation. The terms of their compact are not the product of rational calculation aimed at individual advantage; they are determined by the community's understanding of its duties before God. And compliance is not motivated solely by the recognition that defection would leave each party worse off; it is motivated by a sense of sacred duty, by love of the brethren, and by the conviction that the compact is not merely a prudential arrangement but a covenant with moral and spiritual authority. The definition, in requiring self-interest, rational maximisation, and fear of disadvantage, captures one model of the social contract and mistakes it for the only possible one.

definition: An interaction I constitutes cooperation if and only if (i) the parties to I share the same ultimate objective, (ii) the outcome of I benefits all parties equally, and (iii) each party could identify, in advance, the specific benefit that the interaction would yield.
conditions: the parties to I share the same ultimate objective
the outcome of I benefits all parties equally
each party could identify, in advance, the specific benefit that the interaction would yield
counterexample: Consider the cooperation between a farmer and a blacksmith in a small rural community. The farmer brings grain to market; the blacksmith forges tools. They cooperate through exchange: the farmer supplies the blacksmith with food, the blacksmith supplies the farmer with ploughshares. Their ultimate objectives differ — the farmer wishes to cultivate his land, the blacksmith wishes to practise his trade. The benefits are not equal in any measurable sense; they are incommensurable. Yet their interaction is cooperative, for each depends upon the other and each contributes to the other's flourishing.
missing_condition: The definition requires shared objectives, equal benefit, and identifiable gains, yet cooperation frequently unites parties with different objectives who benefit in incommensurable ways that could not have been precisely specified in advance. What is wanted is an account of cooperation that recognises the productive complementarity of differing purposes.
passage: One might propose that an interaction constitutes cooperation if and only if the parties share the same ultimate objective, the outcome benefits all parties equally, and each party could identify in advance the specific benefit the interaction would yield. The definition has some plausibility in the case of two labourers jointly lifting a heavy stone — both wish to move the stone, both benefit equally from its removal, and both can foresee the result. One might suppose that shared objectives, equal benefit, and foreseeable gains are essential to cooperation. Yet one must attend to the case of the farmer and the blacksmith. In a small rural community, the farmer and the blacksmith cooperate through the exchange of goods and services. The farmer brings the blacksmith grain and meat; the blacksmith forges the farmer ploughshares and horseshoes. Their interaction is cooperative in the fullest sense — each depends upon the other, each contributes to the other's livelihood, and the community as a whole is sustained by their mutual service. But they do not share the same ultimate objective. The farmer's objective is to cultivate his land and bring in his harvest; the blacksmith's objective is to practise his trade and maintain his forge. These purposes are different, and neither party has adopted the other's purpose as his own. The benefits are not equal in any measurable sense; one cannot compare the value of bread to the value of a ploughshare except through the rough medium of customary exchange. And neither party could have identified, in advance, the precise benefit the interaction would yield, for the harvest is uncertain and the need for tools varies from season to season. The definition mistakes the features of simple, symmetric, foreseeable cooperation for the features of cooperation as such, and thereby excludes the most common and economically important form of cooperative interaction — the complementary exchange of different goods and services between parties with different purposes.

definition: A person S trusts a person T if and only if (i) S is confident that T will act honestly, (ii) S's confidence is based upon T's known moral character, and (iii) S has personally observed T acting honestly in situations where dishonesty would have been advantageous to T.
conditions: S is confident that T will act honestly
S's confidence is based upon T's known moral character
S has personally observed T acting honestly in situations where dishonesty would have been advantageous to T
counterexample: Consider the trust that a depositor places in a bank. The depositor is confident that the bank will honour its obligations, but this confidence is not based upon the bank's moral character — a bank is not the sort of entity that possesses moral character in the ordinary sense. The depositor has not personally observed the bank resisting the temptation to dishonesty. The depositor's trust rests upon the institutional structure of banking: government regulation, deposit insurance, auditing requirements, and the bank's reputational interest in maintaining public confidence. Yet the trust is genuine.
missing_condition: The definition requires confidence in moral character based on personal observation, yet trust is frequently placed in institutions rather than in persons, and it may rest upon structural and regulatory safeguards rather than upon any assessment of moral character. What is wanted is an account that encompasses institutional trust alongside personal trust.
passage: One might propose that a person trusts another if and only if the first is confident that the second will act honestly, this confidence is based upon the second's known moral character, and the first has personally observed the second acting honestly in situations where dishonesty would have been advantageous. The definition captures something important about trust between individuals who know one another well. One thinks of the trust between old friends who have witnessed one another's conduct over many years and have seen one another resist temptations to dishonesty. Such trust is grounded in the direct personal assessment of moral character, and it is among the most secure forms of trust that exist. One might suppose that all trust shares this structure. Yet one must attend to the trust that a depositor places in a bank. A man deposits his savings in a bank and trusts that the bank will return them upon demand. His trust is genuine — he rests easy in the confidence that his money is safe. But the bank is not a person possessing moral character; it is an institutional entity. The depositor has not personally observed the bank acting honestly in situations where dishonesty would have been advantageous; he has, in all likelihood, never observed the internal workings of the bank at all. His confidence rests upon a different foundation entirely: upon the regulatory structure of the banking system, upon the existence of deposit insurance, upon the auditing requirements to which the bank is subject, and upon the reputational interest that the bank has in maintaining public confidence. The trust is not personal but institutional; it is grounded not in the assessment of character but in the assessment of structural safeguards. The definition, in requiring confidence in moral character based on personal observation, captures the features of interpersonal trust and mistakes them for the features of trust in general.

definition: A rule R constitutes a social norm if and only if (i) R has remained unchanged for at least two generations, (ii) R is regarded by its adherents as reflecting the natural or proper order of things, and (iii) R is defended by its adherents when challenged, with reasons that appeal to tradition or precedent.
conditions: R has remained unchanged for at least two generations
R is regarded by its adherents as reflecting the natural or proper order of things
R is defended by its adherents when challenged, with reasons that appeal to tradition or precedent
counterexample: Consider the norm of applauding at the conclusion of a public lecture. In many academic settings, this norm emerged within living memory; it has not persisted unchanged for two generations. Its adherents do not regard it as reflecting the natural order of things; they recognise it as a mere convention. And when challenged, they would not defend it by appeal to tradition but would offer practical reasons — that applause signals appreciation and provides feedback to the speaker. Yet the norm is real and widely observed; the person who sits in conspicuous silence at the end of a lecture violates a social expectation.
missing_condition: The definition requires antiquity, perceived naturalness, and defence by appeal to tradition, yet social norms may be of recent origin, may be recognised by their adherents as conventional rather than natural, and may be defended on practical rather than traditional grounds. What is wanted is an account that recognises the normative force of shared expectations regardless of their age or perceived naturalness.
passage: One might propose that a rule constitutes a social norm if and only if it has remained unchanged for at least two generations, is regarded by its adherents as reflecting the natural or proper order of things, and is defended, when challenged, by appeal to tradition or precedent. The definition has considerable plausibility when applied to the deeply entrenched norms of traditional societies — the norm of primogeniture, for instance, which persisted for centuries, was regarded as natural and divinely ordained, and was defended by appeal to ancestral custom. One might suppose that antiquity, perceived naturalness, and traditional vindication are the hallmarks of a genuine social norm. Yet one must attend to the norm of applauding after a public lecture. In many academic and cultural settings, it is a firm norm that the audience applauds when the speaker concludes. The person who sits in conspicuous silence, arms folded, while the rest of the audience claps, violates a real social expectation and may attract puzzled or disapproving glances. Yet the norm has not persisted unchanged for two generations; it arose, in its present form, within the living memory of persons still active in academic life, and its precise conventions — when to begin, how long to continue, whether to stand — have shifted noticeably over time. Its adherents do not regard it as reflecting the natural order of things; they recognise it as a convention, a practice that might easily have been otherwise. And when challenged, they would not defend it by appeal to tradition but by appeal to practical considerations: that applause is a courteous signal of appreciation, that it provides useful feedback, that it marks the formal conclusion of the event. The definition mistakes the features of ancient, deeply naturalised norms for features that all social norms must possess.

definition: A group G constitutes a community if and only if (i) the members of G are roughly equal in wealth and social standing, (ii) the members of G make decisions collectively through open deliberation, and (iii) no member of G exercises authority over any other member.
conditions: the members of G are roughly equal in wealth and social standing
the members of G make decisions collectively through open deliberation
no member of G exercises authority over any other member
counterexample: Consider the feudal village of mediaeval England. The lord of the manor, the parish priest, the freeholders, the villeins, and the landless labourers were grossly unequal in wealth and standing. Decisions were not made through open deliberation among equals but through the exercise of the lord's authority, tempered by custom. The lord exercised considerable authority over the villeins. Yet the feudal village was a community — its members were bound by ties of mutual obligation, shared in common fields and festivals, and possessed a strong sense of collective identity.
missing_condition: The definition requires equality, collective deliberation, and the absence of authority, yet communities may be hierarchical, may make decisions through authoritative direction rather than open deliberation, and may be bound together by ties of mutual obligation and shared identity that persist across sharp inequalities of wealth and power.
passage: One might propose that a group constitutes a community if and only if its members are roughly equal in wealth and social standing, make decisions collectively through open deliberation, and no member exercises authority over any other. The definition has an appealing democratic character; one thinks of the New England town meeting, where citizens of roughly equal standing gather to debate and decide the affairs of the town by common consent. One might suppose that equality, deliberation, and the absence of hierarchy are constitutive of genuine community. Yet one must attend to the feudal village. The mediaeval English village was a community of the most robust and enduring kind. Its members — the lord, the priest, the freeholders, the villeins, the cottagers — were bound together by an intricate web of mutual obligations, shared in the labour of the common fields, celebrated the same festivals, worshipped in the same church, and possessed a strong and vivid sense of collective identity. But they were not equal in wealth or standing; the gulf between the lord and the villein was enormous. Decisions were not made through open deliberation among equals; the lord held her manorial court, and her authority, though tempered by custom and the expectations of her tenants, was real and consequential. The lord exercised authority over the villeins in matters of considerable importance — the terms of their tenure, the allocation of strips in the common fields, the settlement of disputes. All three conditions of the definition fail. Yet the feudal village was a community, and its members experienced it as such. The definition mistakes the features of one particular — and historically unusual — form of egalitarian community for the features of community as such.

definition: A person S is alienated from a society Y if and only if (i) S actively rejects the values and practices of Y, (ii) S refuses to participate in the social life of Y, and (iii) S can articulate reasons for the rejection and non-participation.
conditions: S actively rejects the values and practices of Y
S refuses to participate in the social life of Y
S can articulate reasons for the rejection and non-participation
counterexample: Consider a retired clerk who has lived in the same town all his life. He does not reject the values of his society; he holds them sincerely. He does not refuse to participate in social life; he attends church, votes, and greets his neighbours. Yet he feels profoundly alienated — a pervasive sense of disconnection, of going through the motions without engagement, of being present in body but absent in spirit. He cannot articulate reasons for this feeling; when asked, he says only that something is missing, that the world feels distant and unreal. All three conditions fail, yet the alienation is genuine and deeply felt.
missing_condition: The definition requires active rejection, refusal to participate, and articulable reasons, yet alienation may manifest as a diffuse, inarticulate sense of disconnection that coexists with outward conformity and sincere endorsement of social values. What is wanted is an account that encompasses the passive, felt, and often inexplicable dimension of alienation alongside its active, deliberate forms.
passage: One might propose that a person is alienated from a society if and only if the person actively rejects its values and practices, refuses to participate in its social life, and can articulate reasons for the rejection and non-participation. The definition captures something real about the alienation of the conscious dissenter — the political radical, the religious nonconformist, the artist who repudiates bourgeois society and can explain, at length, why its values are hollow and its customs oppressive. Such persons are alienated, and their alienation takes the form of articulate, principled rejection. One might suppose that this is what alienation essentially is. Yet one must attend to the retired clerk. He has lived in the same town all his life. He holds the values of his society sincerely — he believes in decency, hard work, and the importance of community. He participates in social life; he attends church on Sunday, votes at every election, greets his neighbours in the street. He has not rejected anything; he has not refused anything. Yet he is alienated. A pervasive sense of disconnection hangs over his days — a feeling that he is going through the motions of a life that no longer engages him, that the social world around him has become distant and somehow unreal. He cannot articulate reasons for this feeling; when pressed, he can only say that something is missing, that things are not as they once were, that the world feels hollow. His alienation is not a matter of rejection or refusal; it is a felt condition, a quality of experience, a diffuse and inarticulate sense that the fabric of social meaning has worn thin. The definition, in requiring active rejection, non-participation, and articulable reasons, captures the alienation of the rebel and mistakes it for the only form of alienation there is.

definition: A practice P constitutes a tradition if and only if (i) P has been transmitted from one generation to the next over a substantial period of time, (ii) P is performed in conscious imitation of the manner in which it was performed by previous generations, and (iii) the participants in P are able to identify the origin of P in a particular founding act or founding figure.
conditions: P has been transmitted from one generation to the next over a substantial period of time
P is performed in conscious imitation of previous generations
the participants can identify the origin of P in a particular founding act or figure
counterexample: Consider the common law of England. The common law is, by universal acknowledgement, a tradition — perhaps the most venerable legal tradition in the Western world. It has been transmitted from generation to generation over many centuries, and each generation of lawyers and judges has received it from its predecessors. But the common law was not performed in conscious imitation of a founding act, for it has no identifiable founding act. No legislator decreed it; no single figure established it. It grew up gradually, through the accumulation of judicial decisions, local customs, and procedural practices, without any moment at which it was consciously inaugurated. The third condition fails: the participants cannot point to an origin. Yet the common law is a tradition.
missing_condition: The definition requires an identifiable origin in a founding act or figure, but many traditions have no such origin; they arise gradually and imperceptibly from the accumulated practices of many persons over many generations. What is wanted is an account that allows for traditions of anonymous and diffuse origin.
passage: One might propose that a practice constitutes a tradition if and only if it has been transmitted from one generation to the next over a substantial period, it is performed in conscious imitation of the manner of previous generations, and the participants are able to identify the origin of the practice in a particular founding act or founding figure. The definition captures certain cases well. The Passover seder is a tradition that has been transmitted across generations, is performed in conscious imitation of its earlier observance, and its origin is identified in the Exodus from Egypt. The Olympic Games, revived in 1896, trace their tradition to a founding act in ancient Greece. One might suppose that the identifiability of origins is essential to the concept of a tradition. Yet one must consider the common law of England. The common law is a tradition in the fullest and most serious sense of the term. It has been transmitted from generation to generation for the better part of a millennium; each new generation of lawyers, barristers, and judges receives it from its predecessors through the study of decided cases, the practice of the courts, and the immersion in a professional culture of great antiquity. But the common law has no identifiable founding act and no founding figure. It was not established by any legislator or decreed by any sovereign. It grew up gradually, through the slow accumulation of judicial decisions, local customs, and procedural conventions, over centuries during which no single person or body consciously inaugurated it. The earliest practitioners of the common law did not think of themselves as founding a tradition; they were simply settling disputes according to the customs of their locality. The third condition of the definition fails entirely. The definition has taken a feature of certain conspicuous traditions — those that are associated with a memorable founding event or a revered founder — and treated it as a necessary condition of tradition as such, when in truth many of the most deeply rooted traditions in human life — the common law, the conventions of hospitality, the customs of mourning, the practices of agriculture — arose anonymously and imperceptibly, without any founding act that could be identified or any founding figure who could be named.

definition: A person P is alienated from an activity A if and only if (i) P is compelled to perform A by external forces — economic necessity, legal obligation, or social pressure — rather than by any inner desire, (ii) P derives no satisfaction or fulfilment from A, and (iii) P regards the product of A as belonging to someone other than themselves — that is, P has no proprietary relation to the fruits of their labour.
conditions: P is compelled to perform A by external forces rather than by inner desire
P derives no satisfaction or fulfilment from A
P regards the product of A as belonging to someone other than themselves
counterexample: Consider a devoted schoolteacher in a poorly funded school who is underpaid and burdened with administrative tasks imposed by the educational authorities. He is, in a significant sense, compelled by economic necessity to continue in a post that offers little financial reward, and the reports and forms he must complete are demanded by external bureaucratic forces. Yet he is not alienated from the activity of teaching. He derives deep satisfaction from the progress of his pupils, from the illumination of a difficult point, from the sense that he is forming minds and characters. And the product of his labour — the education of the children — he regards as his own achievement, even though it brings him neither wealth nor ownership of any tangible thing. The first condition is partly satisfied, but the second and third fail. The teacher is constrained but not alienated.
missing_condition: The definition identifies alienation with the conjunction of external compulsion, absence of satisfaction, and dispossession of the product, but a person may be subject to external compulsion and yet remain unalienated if the activity itself is experienced as meaningful. What is wanted is an account that distinguishes between external constraint, which is compatible with meaningful engagement, and alienation proper, which involves a deeper estrangement from the activity and its purpose.
passage: One might hold that a person is alienated from an activity if and only if the person is compelled to perform it by external forces — economic necessity, legal obligation, or social pressure — rather than by inner desire, derives no satisfaction or fulfilment from it, and regards its product as belonging to someone other than himself. The definition captures a familiar picture of the factory worker who labours not because he wishes to but because he must eat, who finds nothing in his work to engage his faculties or reward his spirit, and who sees the goods he produces carried away to enrich another. The three conditions seem to capture the essence of his predicament. Yet one must consider the case of a devoted schoolteacher in a school that is poorly funded and heavily burdened with bureaucratic requirements. The teacher is, in a significant sense, compelled by economic necessity to remain in his post; he has a family to support and no ready alternative employment. The administrative tasks that consume an ever-larger portion of his time — the completion of forms, the writing of reports, the attendance at meetings called by the educational authorities — are imposed upon him by external forces and serve purposes that are not his own. In these respects, the first condition of the definition is satisfied. But the teacher is not alienated from the activity of teaching. When he stands before his class and explains a difficult theorem, or reads aloud a passage of poetry and watches comprehension dawn upon a young face, he experiences a satisfaction that is among the deepest rewards of human life. The education of his pupils is, in his own estimation, his achievement — the product of his skill, his patience, and his devotion — even though it brings him no material ownership and no financial return commensurate with its value. The second and third conditions fail. The teacher is constrained by circumstances, but he is not estranged from his work; the activity is experienced as profoundly meaningful despite the conditions under which it is performed. The definition has confused the external circumstances that may accompany alienation with alienation itself, overlooking the possibility that a person may be subject to economic compulsion and bureaucratic imposition and yet remain, in the most important sense, unalienated, because the activity engages his deepest capacities and its fruits are recognised as his own.

definition: A group of persons G constitutes a community if and only if (i) the members of G occupy a common geographical territory, (ii) the members of G interact with one another on a regular basis in the course of their daily lives, and (iii) the members of G share a common language and a common set of customs.
conditions: the members occupy a common geographical territory
the members interact regularly in daily life
the members share a common language and customs
counterexample: Consider the community of mathematicians. Mathematicians in Oxford, in Goettingen, in Princeton, and in Moscow regard themselves as members of a single community. They read one another's papers, attend one another's lectures, correspond regularly, and share a set of norms — standards of proof, conventions of exposition, habits of collaboration — that are recognisably common to the discipline. They speak of 'the mathematical community' without any sense of strain. Yet they do not occupy a common geographical territory; they are scattered across the globe. They do not interact regularly in the course of daily life; they may meet only at annual conferences or through correspondence. The first and second conditions fail, yet the group is a community.
missing_condition: The definition restricts community to groups that share territory and daily interaction, but communities may be constituted by shared intellectual commitments, shared norms, and regular communication across great distances. What is wanted is an account that accommodates non-territorial communities.
passage: One might propose that a group of persons constitutes a community if and only if the members occupy a common geographical territory, interact with one another regularly in the course of daily life, and share a common language and a common set of customs. The definition captures the village community, the neighbourhood, the small town — cases in which territorial proximity, daily interaction, and shared customs create a recognisable social unit. The inhabitants of a rural parish in England occupy the same territory, encounter one another at the market, the church, and the public house, and share the customs, festivals, and dialect of the locality. They form a community in the fullest sense. Yet one must consider the community of mathematicians. Mathematicians in Oxford, in Goettingen, in Princeton, and in Moscow regard themselves as members of a single community, and the usage is not metaphorical. They read one another's published work, correspond upon problems of mutual interest, attend international congresses, and collaborate across national and linguistic boundaries. They share a set of norms that are as binding as any village custom — standards of rigorous proof, conventions of mathematical notation, habits of anonymous refereeing, traditions of acknowledging priority. A mathematician in Tokyo has more in common, in respect of professional norms and intellectual habits, with a mathematician in Cambridge than with his next-door neighbour. Yet the members of this community do not occupy a common geographical territory. They are scattered across the surface of the globe, separated by thousands of miles. They do not interact with one another regularly in the course of daily life; many of them meet face to face only at annual or biennial conferences, and their regular communication is carried on by letter and by the printed page. The first and second conditions of the definition fail. The definition has taken the features of the geographically bounded, face-to-face community and elevated them to necessary conditions of community as such, when in truth some of the most vital and cohesive communities in modern life are non-territorial, united not by proximity but by shared commitments, shared standards, and regular communication across distance.

definition: A group of persons G constitutes a community if and only if (i) the members of G reside in the same geographical area, (ii) the members of G interact with one another on a regular basis, and (iii) the members of G share a set of customs, values, or traditions that distinguish them from persons outside the group.
conditions: the members reside in the same geographical area
the members interact with one another regularly
the members share customs, values, or traditions distinguishing them from outsiders
