counterexample: Consider the community of mathematicians scattered across many countries. They do not reside in the same geographical area; they live in London, Paris, Princeton, Tokyo, and scores of other cities. Yet they constitute a community in a meaningful sense. They share a set of intellectual values — rigour, elegance, generality — and a body of customs: the conventions of proof, the etiquette of attribution, the traditions of the seminar and the conference. They interact regularly, though not by meeting in the street but by exchanging papers, attending conferences, and corresponding. They recognise one another as members of a common undertaking. The first condition fails — they are geographically dispersed — yet they are a community.
missing_condition: The definition requires geographical co-residence, but many genuine communities — scholarly communities, diaspora communities, communities of faith — are dispersed across wide geographical areas and bound together by shared practices, values, or commitments rather than by proximity. What is wanted is an account that recognises communities of shared purpose as well as communities of shared place.
passage: One might hold that a group of persons constitutes a community if and only if the members reside in the same geographical area, interact with one another regularly, and share a set of customs, values, or traditions that distinguish them from outsiders. The definition captures many paradigmatic communities. A village in the English countryside is a community: its residents live in the same locale, they meet at the church, the public house, and the village green, and they share customs — the harvest festival, the cricket match, the parish meeting — that bind them together and distinguish them from the inhabitants of the next village. Yet one must consider the community of mathematicians. These persons are scattered across the globe. They live in cities and university towns on every continent, separated by thousands of miles of land and sea. The first condition of the definition — geographical co-residence — fails entirely. Yet they constitute a community in a sense that is neither metaphorical nor attenuated. They share a body of intellectual values: a commitment to rigour, a taste for elegance, a reverence for generality and depth. They share customs: the conventions of mathematical proof, the etiquette of citation and attribution, the traditions of the seminar, the conference, and the visiting lectureship. They interact regularly, though their interactions take forms quite different from the face-to-face meetings of villagers: they exchange papers, referee one another's work, collaborate across national boundaries, and gather at international congresses. They recognise one another as fellows in a common enterprise, and the bonds that unite them are as strong and as defining as any geographical tie. The definition has modelled the concept of community upon the village — the small, geographically bounded group whose members live side by side — and has made geographical co-residence a necessary condition, overlooking the many communities that are bound together not by proximity but by shared practice, shared purpose, and shared intellectual or spiritual commitment.

definition: A practice P constitutes a tradition if and only if (i) P has been performed continuously — that is, in every generation — for at least three generations, (ii) P is transmitted from one generation to the next by explicit instruction, and (iii) P is performed in a manner substantially unchanged from its original form.
conditions: P has been performed continuously for at least three generations
P is transmitted by explicit instruction
P is performed in a manner substantially unchanged from its original form
counterexample: Consider the tradition of jazz improvisation in American music. Jazz improvisation has been practised for over a century and across many generations of musicians. It is transmitted from one generation to the next — but not primarily by explicit instruction. The young musician learns to improvise by listening to recordings, by sitting in with experienced players, by absorbing the idiom through years of immersion in the music. Formal instruction plays a part, but the essential transmission is by example, by listening, and by practice. Moreover, jazz improvisation has changed enormously from its original form: the improvisations of the early New Orleans musicians bear little resemblance to the complex harmonic explorations of later generations. The second and third conditions fail, yet jazz improvisation is a tradition — one of the richest and most vital in American culture.
missing_condition: The definition requires explicit instruction and substantial continuity of form, but many traditions are transmitted by informal means — imitation, immersion, apprenticeship — and evolve continuously. What is wanted is an account that allows tradition to encompass practices that change over time whilst retaining a recognisable continuity of spirit or purpose.
passage: One might propose that a practice constitutes a tradition if and only if it has been performed continuously for at least three generations, it is transmitted by explicit instruction, and it is performed in a manner substantially unchanged from its original form. The definition captures certain familiar examples. The Latin Mass, as practised in the Roman Church for many centuries, was performed continuously, transmitted by explicit instruction in seminaries and schools, and maintained in a form that changed little from one century to the next. The military drill of a regiment may be transmitted by formal instruction and performed in a fixed manner over many generations. Yet one must consider the tradition of jazz improvisation. Jazz has been practised in America for more than a century. Each generation of musicians has taken up the art from its predecessors, and the continuity of the practice from the early years of the twentieth century to the present day is not in doubt. It is a tradition in the fullest sense — musicians speak of the jazz tradition, of the tradition of the blues, of the tradition of the jam session. But jazz improvisation is not transmitted primarily by explicit instruction. The young musician does not learn to improvise by sitting in a classroom and being told the rules. He learns by listening — to recordings, to live performances, to the playing of his elders. He learns by sitting in: by playing alongside experienced musicians who do not explain what they are doing but simply do it, leaving the younger player to absorb the idiom by immersion. And jazz improvisation has not been performed in a manner substantially unchanged from its original form. The improvisations of the earliest jazz musicians in New Orleans — based on simple harmonies, collective improvisation, and the blues scale — bear little resemblance to the intricate, harmonically adventurous solos of later players. The music has evolved continuously, each generation transforming the tradition it inherited. The second and third conditions of the definition fail. The definition has modelled tradition upon practices that are fixed and formally transmitted, overlooking the possibility that a tradition may be transmitted informally and may evolve dramatically whilst retaining a recognisable continuity of spirit and purpose.

definition: An arrangement A constitutes a social institution if and only if (i) A is established by explicit agreement among the members of a society — that is, by a founding charter, a statute, or a comparable formal act, (ii) A possesses a defined organisational structure with designated roles and offices, and (iii) A is governed by codified rules that are available for inspection by its members.
conditions: A is established by explicit agreement — a charter, statute, or comparable formal act
A possesses a defined organisational structure with designated roles
A is governed by codified rules available for inspection
counterexample: Consider the institution of marriage as it has existed in most human societies throughout history. Marriage is a social institution — perhaps the most fundamental institution of all. Yet in the vast majority of societies, marriage was not established by explicit agreement among the members of society. There was no founding charter of marriage, no statute that created it, no formal act by which the members of the community agreed to institute the practice. Marriage arose gradually, organically, from the customs and needs of communal life. It possesses no organisational structure in the sense of designated roles and offices beyond the roles of husband and wife themselves; there is no president of marriage, no secretary, no board of directors. And for most of human history, the rules governing marriage were not codified but customary — embedded in the practices and expectations of the community rather than written down in any document. All three conditions fail, yet marriage is an institution.
missing_condition: The definition requires formal establishment, organisational hierarchy, and codified rules, but many of the most important social institutions — marriage, language, the market, the family — arose gradually from custom rather than from any deliberate act of foundation. What is wanted is an account that encompasses both formal organisations and informal, customary institutions.
passage: One might hold that an arrangement constitutes a social institution if and only if it is established by explicit agreement — a charter, a statute, or a comparable formal act — it possesses a defined organisational structure with designated roles and offices, and it is governed by codified rules available for inspection. The definition captures certain paradigmatic institutions. A university is established by a charter; it has a defined structure — a chancellor, a senate, faculties, departments — and it is governed by codified statutes and regulations. A parliament is established by constitutional enactment; it has designated roles — speaker, members, committees — and it operates under standing orders that any member may consult. Yet one must consider the institution of marriage. Marriage is, by universal recognition, a social institution — indeed, it is often called the oldest and most fundamental institution of human society. But it was not established by explicit agreement among the members of any society. There is no charter of marriage, no statute that created it, no moment at which the members of a community came together and agreed to institute the practice of marrying. Marriage arose gradually, from the customs and needs of communal life, over thousands of years. It possesses no organisational structure in the sense of designated offices and roles beyond those of husband and wife; there is no committee of marriage, no board, no elected officers. And for the greater part of human history, the rules governing marriage were not codified. They were customary — embedded in the expectations, the prohibitions, and the ceremonial practices of the community — and were transmitted from one generation to the next not by written documents but by example, by gossip, and by the pressure of communal opinion. All three conditions of the definition fail. The definition has modelled the concept of a social institution upon the formal organisation — the university, the parliament, the corporation — and has mistaken the features of that species for the features of the genus, overlooking the vast class of informal, customary institutions that arise from the practices of communal life and that shape human existence more deeply than any charter or statute.

definition: A pattern of behaviour B is a custom if and only if (i) B is practised by the majority of the members of a community, (ii) B has been practised for a substantial period of time — at least two generations, and (iii) the members of the community can articulate the reason for practising B.
conditions: B is practised by the majority of the community's members
B has been practised for at least two generations
the community's members can articulate the reason for practising B
counterexample: Consider the custom of shaking hands upon meeting. This is a custom in the fullest sense: it is practised by the majority of members of many Western communities and has been practised for generations. Yet if one asks a person why he shakes hands upon meeting, he is very likely unable to articulate a reason. He may say 'It is what one does,' or 'I was brought up to do it,' but he will not be able to explain why the gesture exists — what purpose it serves, what rationale lies behind it. The origin and reason of the custom are lost in the mists of time; it is practised not because its reason is understood but because it is habitual. The third condition fails, yet hand-shaking is a custom.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the practitioners be able to articulate the reason for the custom, but many customs are practised unreflectively, out of habit and social expectation, without any conscious awareness of their origin or purpose. What is wanted is an account that recognises the role of unreflective habit and social conformity in sustaining customs.
passage: One might propose that a pattern of behaviour is a custom if and only if it is practised by the majority of the community's members, it has been practised for at least two generations, and the members of the community can articulate the reason for practising it. The definition has a certain rationalist appeal. It suggests that customs are sustained by the understanding of their practitioners — that people do what they do because they know why they do it, and that this understanding is part of what makes a practice a custom rather than a mere coincidence. Yet one must consider the custom of shaking hands upon meeting. This practice is observed by the vast majority of members of many Western communities. It has been practised for generations — centuries, indeed. It is a custom in the fullest and most ordinary sense of the term. But if one stops a man in the street and asks him why he shakes hands upon meeting an acquaintance, he will, in all likelihood, be unable to give a reason. He may say that it is the done thing, that it would be rude not to, that he has always done it, that everyone does it. He will not be able to explain the origin or the rationale of the gesture. Historians may offer conjectures — that it originated as a demonstration that one carried no weapon, or as a sign of good faith in the sealing of an agreement — but the man in the street knows none of this. He shakes hands because that is what one does; the practice is habitual, automatic, unreflective. The third condition of the definition fails. The definition has assumed that the persistence of a custom depends upon the conscious understanding of its practitioners, when in truth many of the most deeply rooted customs persist precisely because they are unreflective — because they have passed from the domain of conscious choice into the domain of automatic habit, and are performed not because their reason is understood but because not to perform them would feel strange, rude, or wrong.

definition: A rule R is a social norm if and only if (i) R prescribes or prohibits a specific type of behaviour, (ii) R is endorsed by the majority of the members of the community in which it operates, and (iii) violations of R are punished by formal sanctions — that is, by penalties imposed by an authoritative body such as a court, a council, or a committee.
conditions: R prescribes or prohibits a specific type of behaviour
R is endorsed by the majority of the community
violations of R are punished by formal sanctions imposed by an authoritative body
counterexample: Consider the norm against queue-jumping. In British society, the expectation that one will wait one's turn in a queue is deeply felt and widely endorsed. A person who pushes to the front of a queue violates this norm, and the violation is met with disapproval: cold looks, pointed remarks, occasionally a sharp rebuke. But there is no formal sanction. No court will hear a case of queue-jumping; no council will impose a penalty; no committee will deliberate upon the offence. The consequences of violation are entirely informal — social disapproval, loss of esteem, the discomfort of being glared at by one's fellow citizens. The third condition fails, yet the norm against queue-jumping is as powerful and as widely observed as many norms enforced by formal sanctions.
missing_condition: The definition requires formal sanctions, but many of the most powerful social norms are enforced entirely by informal means — disapproval, ostracism, gossip, and the withdrawal of social esteem. What is wanted is an account that recognises informal enforcement as sufficient to sustain a norm.
passage: One might hold that a rule is a social norm if and only if it prescribes or prohibits a specific type of behaviour, it is endorsed by the majority of the community, and violations are punished by formal sanctions imposed by an authoritative body. The definition captures certain norms well. The prohibition against theft is a social norm: it prohibits a specific behaviour, it is endorsed by the community, and violations are punished by courts with fines or imprisonment. The prohibition against perjury similarly satisfies all three conditions. Yet one must consider the norm against queue-jumping as it operates in British society. The expectation that one will wait one's turn in a queue is deeply embedded in British social life. It prescribes a specific behaviour — joining the back of the queue and advancing in order — and it is endorsed by the overwhelming majority of the community. A person who violates the norm — who pushes past those who have been waiting and takes a place he has not earned — is met with immediate and unambiguous disapproval. Fellow queuers fix him with cold stares; someone may say 'Excuse me, there is a queue'; the general atmosphere of censure is unmistakable. The norm is powerful, and it is widely observed; queue-jumping is rare in Britain, and when it occurs it provokes genuine indignation. But no formal sanction is imposed. There is no law against queue-jumping, no penalty prescribed by statute, no court that will hear a complaint. The disapproval of one's fellows is the only consequence — and it is entirely sufficient to enforce the norm. The third condition of the definition fails. The definition has modelled social norms upon legal rules, and has required the apparatus of formal enforcement — courts, councils, authorised penalties — as a necessary condition, when in truth the most pervasive and deeply felt norms of social life are enforced not by official bodies but by the informal, diffuse, and immensely effective pressure of communal disapproval.

definition: Two or more persons cooperate if and only if (i) each person acts with the intention of achieving a shared goal, (ii) each person is aware that the others are also acting towards the same goal, and (iii) the persons coordinate their actions by means of explicit communication — that is, by verbal agreement, written plan, or formal signal.
conditions: each person acts with the intention of achieving a shared goal
each person is aware that the others are also acting towards the same goal
the persons coordinate by means of explicit communication
counterexample: Consider two strangers who arrive simultaneously at a narrow doorway from opposite sides. Without a word being spoken, one steps back and gestures the other through; the second nods and passes. They have cooperated — each has acted with the intention of achieving the shared goal of navigating the doorway without collision, each was aware that the other was acting towards the same end, and the passage was accomplished smoothly. But they did not coordinate by means of explicit communication. No word was spoken; no plan was agreed upon; no formal signal was exchanged. The stepping-back, the gesture, and the nod are all tacit, spontaneous, and unpremeditated. The third condition fails, yet cooperation has plainly occurred.
missing_condition: The definition requires explicit communication, but much cooperation is accomplished through tacit understanding, gesture, and the silent reading of intentions. What is wanted is an account that encompasses both explicit and tacit coordination.
passage: One might propose that two or more persons cooperate if and only if each acts with the intention of achieving a shared goal, each is aware that the others are acting towards the same goal, and the persons coordinate their actions by means of explicit communication — verbal agreement, written plan, or formal signal. The definition captures many clear instances of cooperation. When a team of builders constructs a house, they share the goal of completing the building, each is aware of the others' contributions, and they coordinate by discussing the plan, assigning tasks, and signalling to one another as the work proceeds. Yet one must consider the case of two strangers who arrive at a narrow doorway from opposite sides at the same moment. Each sees the other; each recognises that if both attempt to pass at once, they will collide or at best wedge themselves uncomfortably in the frame. Without a word, one of them steps back and makes a small gesture with his hand — an open palm, a slight movement towards the doorway — that indicates 'After you.' The other nods, passes through, and they part. This transaction is accomplished in a few seconds, and it is cooperation of the most natural and unremarkable kind. Each person acted with the intention of achieving a shared goal — the smooth navigation of the doorway. Each was aware that the other was acting towards the same end. But no explicit communication took place. No words were exchanged; no plan was agreed upon; no formal signal was given. The stepping-back and the gesture were spontaneous, tacit, and unrehearsed, arising from a mutual understanding of the situation that required no verbalisation. The third condition of the definition fails. The definition has modelled cooperation upon the planned, articulate coordination of a work team or a military unit and has overlooked the vast territory of tacit cooperation — the silent adjustments, the unspoken accommodations, the spontaneous yieldings — by which human beings manage their interactions a hundred times a day without exchanging a single word.

definition: A person P trusts another person Q if and only if (i) P believes that Q will act in P's interest in a given matter, (ii) P has evidence that Q has acted reliably in the past — that is, P's belief is based upon a track record of Q's behaviour, and (iii) P and Q have a personal relationship — that is, they know one another individually and have interacted directly.
conditions: P believes Q will act in P's interest
P's belief is based on evidence of Q's reliable past behaviour
P and Q have a personal relationship involving direct interaction
counterexample: Consider the trust that a person places in a surgeon whom she has never met. She is told that she requires an operation; a surgeon is assigned to her case; she has never spoken to this surgeon, knows nothing of his character, and has no personal relationship with him whatever. Yet she trusts him. She allows him to render her unconscious and to open her body with a knife — an act of trust as profound as any in human experience. Her trust is not based upon a personal track record; she has never observed the surgeon at work. It is based upon the institution — the hospital, the medical profession, the system of training and certification that ensures that a person who bears the title 'surgeon' has met certain standards of competence. The second and third conditions fail, yet the trust is genuine and deep.
missing_condition: The definition requires a personal relationship and an observed track record, but trust may be placed in strangers on the basis of institutional guarantees — professional credentials, reputational systems, and the norms of an occupation. What is wanted is an account that encompasses institutional as well as personal trust.
passage: One might hold that a person trusts another if and only if the first believes the second will act in her interest, this belief is based upon evidence of the second person's reliable past behaviour, and the two have a personal relationship involving direct interaction. The definition captures many familiar instances of trust. A man trusts his business partner because he has observed the partner's integrity over many years of collaboration; the trust is grounded in a personal relationship and a long track record. A woman trusts her neighbour to water her plants whilst she is away, on the basis of years of neighbourly acquaintance and observed reliability. Yet one must consider the case of a patient who is about to undergo surgery. The surgeon is a stranger. The patient has never met him before; she has been assigned to his care by the hospital, and their first encounter is in the consulting room the day before the operation. She knows nothing of his character, has never observed his work, and has no personal relationship with him. Yet she trusts him. She signs the consent form; she allows herself to be anaesthetised; she places her body, and perhaps her life, in the hands of a man she does not know. This is trust of the most consequential kind. It is not based upon a track record of the surgeon's behaviour; the patient has no such track record to consult. It is based upon the institution — upon the assurance that the hospital has credentialled the surgeon, that the medical profession has trained and examined him, that a system of oversight exists to ensure a minimal standard of competence. The second and third conditions of the definition fail. The definition has modelled trust upon the personal, face-to-face relationships of small communities, in which trust is built up gradually through repeated interaction and direct observation, and has overlooked the vast domain of institutional trust — the trust that modern persons place in strangers every day, on the basis of professional credentials, organisational reputations, and the norms of institutions they have never inspected.

definition: A person P is alienated from an activity A if and only if (i) P is compelled to engage in A by external necessity — that is, P does not choose A freely, (ii) P derives no satisfaction or fulfilment from A, and (iii) the product or result of A is appropriated by another person or institution, so that P does not benefit from what P has produced.
conditions: P is compelled to engage in A by external necessity
P derives no satisfaction or fulfilment from A
the product of A is appropriated by another
counterexample: Consider a wealthy amateur painter who paints solely for his own pleasure. He is under no compulsion to paint; he does so freely, in his leisure hours, because he finds it agreeable. The paintings remain in his possession; no one appropriates them. Yet he may be alienated from the activity of painting in a recognisable sense. He paints mechanically, following fashionable styles without conviction, producing competent but uninspired canvases that express nothing of his own sensibility. He paints not because the activity engages his deepest capacities but because painting is what persons of his class are expected to do. He goes through the motions, but the activity does not touch him. The first and third conditions fail — he is not compelled and no one appropriates his work — yet there is a genuine alienation from the activity: a disconnection between the person and what he does.
missing_condition: The definition locates alienation in external compulsion and material appropriation, but a person may be alienated from an activity that is freely chosen and whose products are retained, if the activity fails to engage the person's authentic capacities and sensibility. What is wanted is an account that recognises the possibility of alienation rooted in inauthenticity rather than in economic exploitation.
passage: One might propose that a person is alienated from an activity if and only if the person is compelled to engage in the activity by external necessity, derives no satisfaction from it, and the product is appropriated by another. The definition captures an important and familiar form of alienation. The factory worker who stands at an assembly line all day, performing a repetitive task not because he finds it rewarding but because he must earn a wage, and whose product is taken by the factory owner — this worker is alienated in precisely the sense the definition describes. Yet one must consider the case of a wealthy amateur painter. This man is under no compulsion whatever to paint. He has ample private means and paints in his leisure hours purely because it is what he has always done. No external necessity drives him to the easel; he could stop tomorrow without suffering any material consequence. And the paintings remain his own; they hang in his house or are given to friends; no employer or institution appropriates them. The first and third conditions fail outright. Yet the painter may be alienated from his painting in a sense that is, upon reflection, perfectly recognisable. He paints mechanically, without conviction or engagement. He follows the fashionable styles of the day not because they express anything that matters to him but because he supposes it is what a person of cultivation ought to paint. He produces canvases of technical competence but no feeling. He goes through the motions of the artistic life — the studio, the palette, the easel — without any of the absorption, the struggle, the intensity that characterise authentic creative work. There is a disconnection between the man and his activity; the painting does not engage his deepest capacities, does not draw upon his genuine sensibility, does not satisfy the creative impulse that, one supposes, originally led him to take up the brush. The definition, by locating alienation exclusively in external compulsion and material appropriation, has overlooked the possibility of alienation through inauthenticity — the alienation of a person who is free and unexploited but who nevertheless fails to find in his activity the engagement and self-expression that would make it genuinely his own.

definition: A political arrangement A is legitimated by a social contract if and only if (i) the members of the society have explicitly consented to A — that is, each member has performed an act of consent such as voting, signing, or swearing an oath, (ii) the consent was given at a determinate moment in time — there is a point at which the contract was entered into, and (iii) the members were free to refuse — that is, the consent was not obtained by coercion or deception.
conditions: the members have explicitly consented — by voting, signing, or swearing an oath
the consent was given at a determinate moment in time
the members were free to refuse
counterexample: Consider the political arrangements under which most persons in established democracies actually live. A citizen of Britain, let us say, has never explicitly consented to the arrangements by which he is governed. He has not signed a contract, sworn an oath of allegiance, or performed any other act that could be described as explicit consent. He was born into the political order, grew up within it, and lives under its laws — but at no determinate moment did he agree to it. He votes, perhaps, but voting is not consent to the political order as a whole; it is a choice among candidates within an order that one has never been asked to endorse or reject. And his freedom to refuse is, at best, nominal; he can emigrate, but this is a drastic and burdensome option that bears little resemblance to the genuine freedom to decline a contract. All three conditions fail, yet many theorists hold that the citizen stands in a relation to the state that is aptly described in contractual terms.
missing_condition: The definition requires explicit consent at a determinate moment, but the legitimacy that social contract theory seeks to capture may rest upon tacit consent, hypothetical agreement, or the ongoing voluntary participation of citizens in social and political life. What is wanted is an account that does not reduce the social contract to an actual historical event of explicit agreement.
passage: One might hold that a political arrangement is legitimated by a social contract if and only if the members of the society have explicitly consented to it, the consent was given at a determinate moment, and the members were free to refuse. The definition models political legitimacy upon the private contract — the agreement between two parties, entered into freely and at a specific time, by which each undertakes certain obligations. The analogy has a long history in political philosophy. Yet one must consider the situation of an ordinary citizen in an established democracy. A man born in Britain in the twentieth century lives under a political order that he has never consented to in any explicit fashion. He has not signed a document, sworn an oath, or performed any other act that constitutes explicit agreement to the constitutional arrangements under which he lives. He was born into the political order as he was born into the climate; it was there before him and will be there after him. At no determinate moment — no specific day, no particular occasion — was he asked whether he consented to the arrangements by which he is governed, and at no moment did he give or withhold such consent. He votes in elections, but voting is not consent to the political order as a whole; it is a choice among candidates and parties within an order whose fundamental structure is not on the ballot. And his freedom to refuse is, at best, a theoretical possibility. He can emigrate, certainly, but emigration is a drastic step involving the abandonment of home, family, livelihood, and everything familiar, and it leads only to another political order to which he has equally not consented. The option of emigration is not the genuine freedom to decline a contract; it is more nearly the freedom of a drowning man to choose between two lifeboats. All three conditions of the definition fail. The definition has taken the features of private, voluntary, explicit agreement and imposed them upon the political relation, which is in most cases not voluntary, not explicit, and not datable to any particular moment, yet which may nevertheless be aptly described in contractual terms if the contract is understood not as an actual historical event but as a principle of legitimacy — a standard by which political arrangements are assessed.

definition: A group of persons G possesses a culture if and only if (i) the members of G share a common language, (ii) the members of G share a common religion or system of metaphysical beliefs, and (iii) the members of G produce and appreciate a distinctive body of artistic works — literature, music, painting, and the like.
conditions: the members share a common language
the members share a common religion or metaphysical system
the members produce and appreciate a distinctive body of artistic works
counterexample: Consider modern Switzerland. The Swiss possess a culture — a recognisable national identity, shared customs, shared political traditions, a shared sense of belonging to a common national community. Yet the Swiss do not share a common language; they speak four national languages — German, French, Italian, and Romansh — and the linguistic boundaries run deep, dividing the country into distinct regions. They do not share a common religion; the population is divided between Catholics and Protestants, with a growing number of persons who profess no religion at all. The first two conditions fail outright, yet Switzerland possesses a culture — a way of life, a set of shared institutions and customs, a national character — that is as distinctive and as recognisable as that of any linguistically and religiously homogeneous nation.
missing_condition: The definition requires linguistic and religious homogeneity, but many genuine cultures are multilingual and multi-religious. What is wanted is an account that locates culture in shared practices, institutions, and forms of life rather than in the uniformity of language or belief.
passage: One might propose that a group of persons possesses a culture if and only if the members share a common language, share a common religion or system of metaphysical beliefs, and produce and appreciate a distinctive body of artistic works. The definition has a certain nineteenth-century flavour; it conjures the image of the nation-state as a community united by language, faith, and art. And it captures certain cases well. The culture of classical Athens might be described in these terms: the Athenians shared the Greek language, shared a common religion centred upon the Olympian gods, and produced a body of literary and artistic works — the tragedies, the comedies, the sculpture, the architecture — that remains distinctive and recognisable to this day. Yet one must consider modern Switzerland. The Swiss are a people with a strong and distinctive national culture — shared customs, shared political traditions, a commitment to neutrality and federalism, a recognisable national character that travellers and observers have remarked upon for centuries. But they do not share a common language. The Swiss Confederation encompasses four linguistic communities: the German-speaking majority, the French-speaking west, the Italian-speaking south, and the small Romansh-speaking region in the east. The linguistic boundaries are sharp and consequential; a citizen of Zurich and a citizen of Geneva do not speak the same language at home. Nor do the Swiss share a common religion. The population is divided between Catholics and Protestants — a division that has shaped Swiss history profoundly — and in recent decades a growing number of citizens profess no religious affiliation whatever. The first two conditions of the definition fail. Yet Swiss culture is real. The customs of the Alpine communities, the traditions of direct democracy, the institutions of federalism, the culture of precision and reliability in commerce and manufacture — these constitute a form of life that is as distinctive and as coherent as any national culture on earth. The definition has equated culture with linguistic and religious uniformity, overlooking the possibility that a culture may be constituted by shared practices, shared institutions, and a shared sense of common belonging, even in the absence of a single language or a single faith.

definition: A group of persons G constitutes a community if and only if (i) the members of G reside in a common geographical area, (ii) the members of G interact with one another on a regular basis, and (iii) the members of G share a set of interests, values, or concerns that bind them together.
conditions: Members reside in a common geographical area
Members interact regularly
Members share interests, values, or concerns
counterexample: Consider a group of scholars scattered across several countries who correspond regularly, read and comment upon one another's work, share a common set of intellectual concerns, and regard themselves as belonging to the same intellectual community. The members interact regularly and share interests and values, but they do not reside in a common geographical area — they may be separated by thousands of miles. Yet we should not hesitate to call them a community: the community of analytic philosophers, or the community of ornithologists, or the community of medieval historians. The first condition is not met, yet the group is plainly a community.
missing_condition: The definition is too restrictive in requiring geographical proximity. It should require instead that the members be connected by sustained patterns of interaction and mutual recognition, whether or not they occupy a common physical space.
passage: It is natural to suppose that a community consists of persons who reside in a common geographical area, interact regularly, and share a set of interests, values, or concerns. This definition captures the intuitive picture of a community as a village, a neighbourhood, or a town — a group of people who live near one another, see one another often, and are bound together by common cares. The geographical condition provides the spatial basis for interaction, and the condition of shared interests gives the group its coherence and identity. Yet the definition is, one might observe, too closely modelled on the pre-modern village to accommodate the full range of communities that exist in the modern world. Consider a group of scholars specialising in a particular field of inquiry, scattered across several countries and even continents. They correspond regularly by letter or other means; they read and respond to one another's publications; they share a common set of intellectual concerns and a common vocabulary for addressing them; and they regard themselves, and are regarded by others, as a community. Yet they do not reside in a common geographical area. The definition excludes them. What the definition has failed to capture is the possibility of community without proximity — the possibility that sustained patterns of interaction and mutual recognition may constitute a community in the absence of geographical co-location.

definition: A practice or body of thought T is a tradition if and only if (i) T has been handed down from generation to generation over a considerable period of time, (ii) T is accepted by its inheritors on the authority of the past — because it has always been done or thought thus, and (iii) T remains unchanged in its essential features throughout the period of its transmission.
conditions: T has been handed down over generations
T is accepted on the authority of the past
T remains unchanged in its essential features
counterexample: Consider the tradition of English common law. It has been handed down over many centuries, and it is accepted partly on the authority of precedent — because earlier courts decided thus. Yet common law has not remained unchanged in its essential features: it has evolved continuously, adapting to new circumstances, absorbing new principles, and overturning earlier rulings. The tradition of common law is a tradition precisely of gradual modification and adaptation, not of static preservation. The third condition is not met, yet common law is a paradigmatic tradition.
missing_condition: The definition errs in requiring that a tradition remain unchanged. Many of the most vital traditions are traditions of gradual adaptation and reinterpretation. The definition should require only continuity of transmission and acknowledgement of the past, not invariance of content.
passage: One might define a tradition as a practice or body of thought that has been handed down over generations, accepted by its inheritors on the authority of the past, and unchanged in its essential features throughout the period of its transmission. The first two conditions are relatively uncontroversial: they capture the ideas of intergenerational transmission and of deference to what has gone before. The third condition is intended to ensure that a tradition retains its identity across time — that the practice handed down today is recognisably the same practice that was handed down a century ago. Yet one might observe that this third condition, far from capturing the nature of tradition, actually misdescribes it. Consider the tradition of English common law, which has been transmitted from generation to generation of jurists for many centuries. Each generation of jurists receives the tradition from its predecessors and passes it on to its successors. The authority of precedent — the principle that earlier decisions should guide later ones — is central to the tradition. Yet common law has not remained unchanged: it has evolved continuously, absorbing new principles, responding to new circumstances, and overruling earlier decisions when they no longer serve justice. The tradition is a tradition of adaptive development, not of static preservation. The definition requires a condition of unchangingness that is absent from, and indeed contrary to, the character of many living traditions.

definition: A rule R is a social norm if and only if (i) R prescribes or prohibits a type of behaviour within a community, (ii) the majority of the community's members conform to R, and (iii) violations of R are met with disapproval, censure, or punishment by other members of the community.
conditions: R prescribes or prohibits a type of behaviour
The majority of the community conforms to R
Violations are met with disapproval or punishment
counterexample: Consider the norm against bribery in a society where bribery is, in fact, widely practised. The rule prohibiting bribery prescribes a type of behaviour; violations, when they come to light, are met with disapproval and sometimes with legal punishment; yet the majority of the community does not conform — bribery is pervasive and routine. The second condition is not met, yet we should wish to say that the norm against bribery exists in that society. The norm persists as a standard against which behaviour is judged, even though compliance is rare.
missing_condition: The definition conflates the existence of a norm with widespread compliance. A norm may exist as a recognised standard of behaviour even when the majority fails to conform. The definition needs a condition addressing the recognised authority of the rule rather than mere behavioural conformity.
passage: A natural proposal holds that a social norm is a rule that prescribes or prohibits a type of behaviour, is conformed to by the majority of the community, and is enforced by disapproval or punishment when violated. This definition captures the standard sociological picture of norms as behavioural regularities sustained by sanctions. The prescriptive condition ensures that we are dealing with a rule rather than a mere description of what people happen to do. The majority-conformity condition ensures that the rule is actually followed, not merely proclaimed. And the sanctions condition distinguishes genuine norms from idle recommendations. Yet the definition encounters difficulties when one considers societies in which a norm exists but is widely flouted. Consider a society in which bribery is formally prohibited and publicly condemned, yet in practice pervasive. The prohibition prescribes a type of behaviour; violations, when they are exposed, are met with censure and sometimes with legal penalty; yet the majority does not conform — bribery is routine and expected. The second condition is not met. But we should wish to say that the norm against bribery exists in that society, for it is the standard by which behaviour is judged and the principle to which people appeal when condemning corrupt practices. The definition has confused the existence of a norm with compliance, when in fact a norm may exist as a recognised standard even in the absence of widespread conformity.

definition: A group of agents G engages in cooperation if and only if (i) each member of G acts in a way that contributes to a common goal, (ii) each member of G is aware that the other members are similarly contributing, and (iii) each member of G acts voluntarily — no member is compelled by force or threat.
conditions: Each member acts in a way that contributes to a common goal
Each member is aware that others are similarly contributing
Each member acts voluntarily
counterexample: Consider a group of prisoners who are required by their warden to dig a trench. Each prisoner digs — contributing to the common goal of completing the trench. Each prisoner is aware that the others are similarly digging. Yet no prisoner acts voluntarily — each is compelled by the threat of punishment. We should nevertheless wish to say that the prisoners are cooperating: they are coordinating their efforts toward a shared task, dividing the labour, and adjusting their actions in light of what the others are doing. The cooperation may be coerced, but it is cooperation nonetheless.
missing_condition: The definition is too restrictive in requiring voluntariness. Cooperation can occur under coercion — what is essential is the coordination of actions toward a common goal, not the freedom of the agents who coordinate. The voluntariness condition should be replaced by a condition requiring mutual adjustment of actions.
passage: One might propose that cooperation occurs when each member of a group acts to contribute to a common goal, is aware that the others are doing the same, and acts voluntarily. This definition captures the paradigmatic cases of cooperative activity: friends working together to build a shed, colleagues collaborating on a project, neighbours joining forces to clear a road after a storm. In each case, individuals contribute to a shared objective, they are aware of one another's contributions, and they participate of their own free will. The requirement of voluntariness is intended to distinguish cooperation from mere coordinated labour imposed from without. Yet one might observe that the requirement is too strong. Consider a group of prisoners set to work by their warden on a common task — digging a trench, let us say. Each prisoner digs, contributing to the completion of the trench. Each is aware that the others are doing the same. But none acts voluntarily — each is compelled by the threat of punishment. Nevertheless, the prisoners cooperate: they divide the labour, adjust their actions in light of what the others are doing, and coordinate their efforts to accomplish the task efficiently. The cooperation is coerced, but it is cooperation all the same. The definition has confused a desirable feature of cooperation — that it be free — with a necessary condition, when what is truly essential is the coordination of actions toward a shared objective.

definition: A person S trusts a person R if and only if (i) S expects that R will perform some action A or refrain from some action B, (ii) S is vulnerable to R — that is, S stands to be harmed if R fails to meet S's expectation, and (iii) S has evidence, based on R's past behaviour, that R will meet the expectation.
conditions: S expects R to perform or refrain from a certain action
S is vulnerable to R
S has evidence from R's past behaviour supporting the expectation
counterexample: Consider a young child who trusts a stranger who speaks to her kindly. The child expects the stranger to be helpful; the child is vulnerable — she could be harmed if the stranger is malicious; but the child has no evidence based on the stranger's past behaviour, since she has never encountered this person before. The third condition is not satisfied, yet the child's attitude is plainly one of trust. Trust can be extended in the absence of evidence, on the basis of a general disposition to trust, or on the basis of cues — a kind voice, a reassuring manner — that fall short of an evidential record of past behaviour.
missing_condition: The definition is too demanding in requiring an evidential basis in past behaviour. Trust is sometimes extended in the absence of such evidence — on the basis of general goodwill, perceived character, or even a hopeful disposition. The definition needs to accommodate trust that is not grounded in a track record.
passage: A plausible analysis of trust holds that one person trusts another if and only if the first expects the second to act in a certain way, is vulnerable to the second's failing to do so, and has evidence from the second's past behaviour that the expectation will be met. These conditions capture a common and important form of trust — the trust one places in a person whose reliability has been demonstrated over time. The expectation condition identifies the content of the trust; the vulnerability condition explains why trust matters — where there is nothing at stake, there is nothing to trust; and the evidence condition ensures that the trust is rationally grounded rather than merely wishful. Yet one might observe that trust, in many of its most characteristic forms, outruns the evidence. Consider a young child who approaches a stranger and takes the stranger's offered hand. The child expects kindness; the child is vulnerable; but the child has no evidence of the stranger's past behaviour. The child's trust is not irrational — it is a natural feature of childhood — but it is not based on a track record. Similarly, one might trust a new acquaintance on the basis of a kind voice or an open manner, without any evidence of past reliability. The definition has captured one species of trust — earned trust — but has excluded the trust that is extended as a default disposition, a leap of faith, or a response to perceived character rather than proven conduct.

definition: A person S is alienated from an activity A if and only if (i) S engages in A regularly, (ii) S derives no satisfaction or sense of meaning from A, and (iii) S has no control over the manner in which A is performed — the conditions and character of A are determined by others.
conditions: S engages in A regularly
S derives no satisfaction or meaning from A
S has no control over the manner in which A is performed
counterexample: Consider a skilled craftsman who works independently — a potter, say, who owns his own workshop, selects his own clay, designs his own forms, and sets his own hours. He engages in his craft regularly; he has full control over the manner of its performance. Yet he may derive no satisfaction from it — the market demands uniform, unimaginative pieces that bore him, and the financial pressures of his trade have drained his work of meaning. The third condition is not satisfied — he has complete control — yet he is alienated from his craft. His alienation consists not in external domination but in the felt absence of meaning.
missing_condition: The definition is too restrictive in requiring external domination as a condition of alienation. A person may be alienated from an activity even when they control its performance, if the activity has lost its meaning or connection to them deeper purposes. The definition needs to recognise that alienation can be internal as well as structural.
passage: One might define alienation from an activity as a condition in which a person engages regularly in the activity, derives no satisfaction from it, and has no control over how it is performed. This definition captures an important and familiar form of alienation — the alienation of the factory worker who spends his days performing a repetitive task designed by others, under conditions set by others, for purposes that are not his own. The regularity condition ensures that we are dealing with a sustained condition rather than a momentary dissatisfaction; the absence of satisfaction captures the subjective dimension of alienation; and the absence of control captures its structural dimension — the sense that the activity is imposed from without. Yet one might observe that alienation can arise even in the absence of external control. Consider a skilled potter who owns her workshop, selects her materials, and determines every aspect of her craft. She has full control. Yet the market demands uniform, unimaginative work, and financial necessity has forced her into a routine of producing pieces she despises. She derives no satisfaction from her work; it has become meaningless to her. She is alienated, yet the third condition of the definition is not met. Her alienation is internal — a matter of meaning rather than of control. The definition has identified one important source of alienation — domination by others — but has failed to recognise that alienation may have its roots in the agent's own relation to the significance of her activity.

definition: An arrangement A constitutes a social contract if and only if (i) A is an agreement — explicit or tacit — among the members of a group to abide by certain rules of conduct, (ii) each member agrees to A because doing so is in that member's self-interest, and (iii) the rules specified by A are enforced by a common authority to which the members have consented.
conditions: A is an agreement among members to abide by rules of conduct
Each member agrees because it is in their self-interest
The rules are enforced by a consented-to common authority
counterexample: Consider a group of neighbouring farmers who, over generations, have developed an unspoken understanding about the use of a shared water source. No one agreed explicitly or tacitly to any rules — the practices simply evolved. Some farmers participate not out of self-interest but out of a sense of obligation to the community. And there is no common authority that enforces the rules — compliance is maintained by mutual expectation and social pressure rather than by any empowered body. Yet the arrangement has many of the features of a social contract: it governs conduct, it is mutually beneficial, and it sustains a cooperative order. None of the three conditions is straightforwardly met, yet the arrangement serves the function the definition is intended to capture.
missing_condition: The definition is too narrowly modelled on the contractarian tradition and fails to account for cooperative arrangements that arise through gradual social evolution rather than agreement, that are sustained by motivations other than self-interest, and that are enforced by diffuse social pressure rather than a common authority.
passage: One might hold that a social contract is an agreement among the members of a group to abide by certain rules of conduct, entered into because each member judges it to be in her own interest, and enforced by a common authority to which the members have consented. This definition captures the essentials of the contractarian picture: rational individuals, recognising the advantages of cooperation, agree to constrain their conduct in exchange for the assurance that others will do the same, and they establish an authority to enforce the agreement. It is a powerful and influential picture, and it illuminates much about the structure of political obligation. Yet one might observe that the definition is too closely tied to the model of deliberate agreement to accommodate the full range of cooperative arrangements that serve the function of a social contract. Consider a group of neighbouring farmers who share a common water source. Over generations, an unspoken understanding has developed concerning who may draw water when, how much may be taken, and how disputes are to be settled. No one entered into an agreement; the practices simply grew up. Some farmers comply not out of self-interest but out of a sense of duty to their neighbours. And there is no common authority that enforces the understanding — compliance rests on mutual expectation and the diffuse pressure of community opinion. Yet the arrangement functions much as a social contract is supposed to function: it sustains order, enables cooperation, and distributes a shared resource. The definition has captured one way in which cooperative arrangements may arise — through deliberate agreement — but has failed to recognise that they may also emerge through the gradual, unplanned evolution of social practice.

definition: A set of practices, beliefs, and artefacts C constitutes a culture if and only if (i) C is shared by the members of a distinct social group, (ii) C is transmitted from generation to generation through socialisation and education, and (iii) C forms a coherent, integrated whole — the practices, beliefs, and artefacts are mutually reinforcing and internally consistent.
conditions: C is shared by the members of a distinct social group
C is transmitted from generation to generation
C forms a coherent, integrated whole
counterexample: Consider the culture of a large, modern, pluralistic society — contemporary Britain, for instance. There is a recognisable set of practices, beliefs, and artefacts that may be called British culture. These are shared, to varying degrees, by the members of the society and transmitted across generations. Yet British culture is manifestly not a coherent, integrated whole: it contains internal tensions, contradictions, and competing traditions — between urban and rural ways of life, between secular and religious orientations, between cosmopolitan and insular impulses. The third condition is not satisfied, yet we should not hesitate to speak of British culture.
missing_condition: The definition requires an unrealistic degree of internal coherence. Actual cultures are typically characterised by internal diversity, tension, and contestation. The definition should require only that the practices and beliefs be recognisably connected by common historical origins and overlapping participation, not that they form a seamless whole.
passage: One might define a culture as a set of practices, beliefs, and artefacts shared by a distinct social group, transmitted across generations, and forming a coherent, integrated whole. The first condition identifies culture as a collective phenomenon — not the possession of an individual but of a group. The second captures the diachronic dimension — culture persists through time by being handed down from parents to children, from teachers to pupils. And the third condition is intended to ensure that we are dealing with a genuine culture rather than a miscellaneous collection of unrelated habits. The practices, beliefs, and artefacts of a culture, on this view, hang together: the religious practices are consistent with the moral beliefs, the artefacts express the aesthetic values, and the whole forms an intelligible unity. Yet one might observe that actual cultures rarely display the degree of coherence that this condition demands. Consider the culture of a large, modern, pluralistic society. The practices, beliefs, and artefacts of such a society are not internally consistent — they are rife with tensions, contradictions, and competing influences. Religious traditions coexist uneasily with secular institutions; cosmopolitan attitudes jostle against parochial ones; ancient customs persist alongside modern innovations. The society's culture is not an integrated whole but a contested and heterogeneous assemblage. Yet we do not for that reason deny that the society has a culture. The definition, it appears, requires a condition of coherence that the facts of cultural life do not sustain.

definition: A person or body B has authority over a group G if and only if (i) B issues directives that prescribe or prohibit actions by members of G, (ii) members of G generally comply with B's directives, and (iii) B has the capacity to impose sanctions upon members of G who fail to comply.
conditions: B issues directives prescribing or prohibiting actions
Members of G generally comply with B's directives
B can impose sanctions on non-compliant members
counterexample: Consider a widely respected elder in a small community who offers guidance and counsel but issues no directives, has no capacity to impose sanctions, and whose influence rests entirely upon the respect in which she is held. When she speaks, others listen and often follow her advice, but her influence proceeds from moral standing rather than from any power to command or punish. She has no authority in the sense defined, yet she has authority in a perfectly intelligible sense — moral authority, the authority of wisdom and character. The definition captures coercive authority but fails to accommodate the authority that rests upon respect, expertise, or moral standing.
missing_condition: The definition is too narrowly tied to the model of coercive or institutional authority and fails to recognise forms of authority — moral, intellectual, charismatic — that rest upon respect, expertise, or personal qualities rather than upon the power to issue directives and impose sanctions.
passage: A natural analysis holds that a person or body has authority over a group if and only if that person or body issues directives, the group generally complies, and the authority has the capacity to impose sanctions on those who do not comply. This definition captures a familiar form of authority — the authority of the state, the military commander, the employer. In each case, directives are issued, compliance is expected and generally forthcoming, and sanctions await those who fail to obey. The capacity to sanction is what gives the authority its teeth, and the general compliance of the group is what distinguishes genuine authority from the mere pretension to it. Yet one might observe that the definition, by focusing on directives and sanctions, fails to capture a form of authority that is at least as important: the authority that rests upon respect, wisdom, or moral standing. Consider a widely respected elder in a small community — a person of exceptional judgement and character to whom others turn for guidance. She issues no directives; she has no capacity to impose sanctions; she simply offers counsel, and others follow it because they respect her. She has authority — everyone in the community would say so — but her authority is of a kind that the definition cannot accommodate. The definition has extracted its conditions from the paradigm of coercive authority and has thereby excluded the authority of the sage, the expert, and the moral exemplar.

definition: A distribution of goods D within a society is just if and only if (i) D gives to each member what that member has earned through their or their own effort, (ii) D treats all members equally — no member receives more or less on account of morally irrelevant features such as birth, rank, or connexion, and (iii) the rules governing D are publicly known and consistently applied.
conditions: D gives each member what they have earned through effort
D treats all members equally regardless of morally irrelevant features
The rules of D are publicly known and consistently applied
counterexample: Consider a society in which a person is born with a severe disability that prevents him from working. He has earned nothing through his own effort, and the first condition would therefore assign him nothing. The rules are publicly known and consistently applied; everyone is treated equally in the formal sense that no one receives goods on the basis of birth or connexion. Yet we should hesitate to call a distribution just that leaves a person who is unable to work through no fault of his own with nothing at all. The first condition, taken strictly, excludes any provision for those who cannot earn — the very young, the very old, and the disabled.
missing_condition: The definition fails to include a condition addressing need. A just distribution must attend not only to what persons have earned but also to what they require in order to live a minimally decent life, particularly when their inability to earn is not of their own making.
passage: One might propose that a distribution of goods is just if and only if it gives each person what she has earned through her own effort, treats all persons equally regardless of morally irrelevant features, and is governed by rules that are publicly known and consistently applied. This definition has considerable appeal: it embodies the principle that reward should be proportional to effort, it insists upon the moral equality of all members of the society, and it demands the rule of law rather than arbitrary discretion. A society that distributed its goods in accordance with these three principles would, it seems, have a strong claim to justice. Yet one might observe that the definition, for all its appeal, is blind to a dimension of justice that most persons would regard as fundamental. Consider a person who is born with a severe disability that renders him permanently unable to work. He has earned nothing through his own effort — not through any laziness or moral failing, but because the capacity for effort is not available to him. The first condition would therefore assign him nothing. The second condition is satisfied — he is not discriminated against on the basis of birth or rank, but this is cold comfort when the result is that he receives nothing at all. We should hesitate to call just a distribution that leaves those who are unable to earn through no fault of their own without any provision whatsoever. The definition has captured the principle of desert but has failed to include the principle of need.

definition: A group of persons G constitutes a community if and only if (i) the members of G share a common geographical location — they live in the same place, (ii) the members of G interact regularly with one another in the course of daily life, and (iii) the members of G share a common language.
conditions: The members share a common geographical location
The members interact regularly in daily life
The members share a common language
counterexample: Consider the community of mathematicians working on the Langlands programme across universities in Paris, Princeton, Chicago, and Tokyo. These individuals share no common geographical location — they are spread across different continents. They may interact only occasionally in daily life, meeting primarily at conferences or through written correspondence. Yet they share a deep sense of common purpose, mutual recognition, and intellectual solidarity. They refer to themselves as a community, and the broader academic world recognises them as one. The first and second conditions fail. Yet this group constitutes a community in every meaningful sense of the word — bound together by shared intellectual commitments, common standards of rigour, and mutual respect.
missing_condition: The definition requires geographical co-location and daily interaction, but many genuine communities are constituted by shared purposes, values, and commitments rather than by proximity. A condition requiring shared ends and mutual recognition, rather than spatial adjacency, would be more adequate.
passage: One might propose that a group of persons constitutes a community if and only if its members share a common geographical location, interact regularly in the course of daily life, and share a common language. This definition captures the features of the village, the neighbourhood, and the small town — the paradigmatic communities of traditional social thought. The geographical condition places the members in a shared space; the interaction condition ensures that they are not merely co-located strangers but active participants in a shared life; and the language condition provides the medium through which communal life is conducted. Yet one might observe that these conditions, though they describe one form of community admirably, are too narrow for the concept as it is actually employed. Consider the community of mathematicians working on the Langlands programme — a deep and far-reaching set of conjectures linking number theory and representation theory. These mathematicians are dispersed across universities on four continents. They do not share a geographical location; they may see one another only at annual conferences. And while most communicate in English, their common bond is not a natural language but a shared body of problems, methods, and standards. Yet this group is recognised, both by its members and by the wider academic world, as a genuine community. Its members feel a sense of belonging, mutual obligation, and shared purpose that rivals anything found in a geographical neighbourhood. The definition mistakes the features of the traditional village for the essence of community, overlooking the possibility that shared intellectual commitments and mutual recognition can bind persons into a community as effectively as shared geography.

definition: A practice or set of practices P constitutes a tradition if and only if (i) P has been transmitted from one generation to the next over a significant period of time, (ii) the participants in P regard it as having been handed down from their forebears, and (iii) P has remained unchanged throughout its history — that is, the practice as performed today is identical to the practice as performed at its origin.
conditions: P has been transmitted across generations over a significant period
Participants regard P as handed down from forebears
P has remained unchanged throughout its history
counterexample: Consider the tradition of the English common law. The common law has been transmitted from one generation of jurists to the next for over eight centuries. Its practitioners regard it as a tradition inherited from their predecessors, tracing its lineage back to the medieval courts. But the common law has not remained unchanged; it has been continuously adapted, reinterpreted, and expanded. Doctrines that were settled in the thirteenth century have been overturned or modified in the twentieth; new categories of liability have been created; old principles have been applied to circumstances — motor cars, telecommunications, aviation — that their originators could not have imagined. The third condition fails entirely. Yet the English common law is among the most venerable and widely cited traditions in the world.
missing_condition: The definition requires that a tradition remain unchanged, but the most enduring traditions are precisely those that have adapted over time while preserving a recognisable continuity of purpose and identity. What is needed is a condition of continuity rather than identity — the requirement that each generation's version of the practice be recognisably connected to its predecessor, not that it be identical.
passage: One might propose that a practice constitutes a tradition if and only if it has been transmitted across generations over a significant period, its participants regard it as inherited from their forebears, and it has remained unchanged throughout its history. The first two conditions capture the essential temporal and attitudinal features of tradition: a tradition is old, and it is felt to be old by those who practise it. The third condition adds a requirement of constancy — the tradition is the same practice, performed in the same way, as it has always been. Yet one might observe that this third condition, though it expresses a common idealisation, is satisfied by virtually no actual tradition. Consider the English common law, which traces its lineage back over eight centuries to the courts of Henry II. The common law has been transmitted from one generation of judges and advocates to the next; its practitioners regard themselves as custodians of a venerable inheritance. But the common law has changed profoundly. Doctrines of negligence, contract, and criminal liability have been created, modified, and sometimes abandoned. Principles developed for a feudal agricultural society have been adapted to an industrial and now a post-industrial one. The practice as performed today bears only a family resemblance to the practice of the twelfth century. The third condition fails, yet no one would deny that the common law is a tradition. The definition confuses tradition with frozen repetition. What is needed is not a condition of identity across time but a condition of recognisable continuity — the requirement that each generation's practice be connected to and developed from its predecessor, even as it changes in response to new circumstances.

definition: A pattern of behaviour B within a social group G constitutes a custom if and only if (i) B is practised by the majority of the members of G, (ii) B is perceived by the members of G as obligatory — that is, failure to practise B is regarded as a violation of social norms, and (iii) B has an identifiable origin — that is, the members of G can point to a specific event, decree, or decision that established B.
conditions: B is practised by the majority of G
B is perceived as obligatory by the members of G
B has an identifiable origin in a specific event or decision
counterexample: Consider the custom of shaking hands upon greeting in Western societies. The handshake is practised by the majority. It is perceived as obligatory in many social contexts — to refuse a proffered handshake is felt as a slight, an insult, or at least a conspicuous breach of manners. But no one can point to a specific event, decree, or decision that established the custom. Its origins are lost in the mists of antiquity; various theories attribute it to medieval knights, to ancient Romans, or to even older practices, but no definitive origin has ever been identified. The third condition fails. Yet the handshake is among the most deeply embedded customs of Western social life.
missing_condition: The definition requires an identifiable origin, but most customs arise gradually, through the slow accumulation of habitual behaviour, without any founding moment. A condition acknowledging that customs may emerge spontaneously from repeated social interaction, rather than being instituted by a determinate act, is needed.
passage: One might propose that a pattern of behaviour constitutes a custom if and only if it is practised by the majority of a social group, is perceived as obligatory by its members, and has an identifiable origin in a specific event or decision. The first two conditions capture the social character of custom: it is widespread and normatively binding. The third condition adds a historical requirement — the custom must be traceable to a determinate beginning, an act of institution. Yet one might observe that this third condition is at odds with the nature of most customs as they are actually found. Consider the custom of shaking hands upon greeting. The handshake is practised by the great majority of adults in Western societies. It is perceived as normatively binding — to refuse a proffered hand is regarded as rude, hostile, or at best eccentric. But no one can identify the specific event or decision that established the custom. Its origins are immemorial; scholars have proposed various theories — that it originated as a gesture of peace, showing that the hand held no weapon — but none can be verified, and the practitioners of the custom are entirely ignorant of its genesis. The third condition fails. Yet the handshake is as firmly established a custom as any in Western culture. The definition has confused customs with edicts. Edicts have identifiable origins; customs, characteristically, do not. They arise gradually, through the slow accretion of habitual behaviour, and their beginnings are typically invisible even to those who practise them most faithfully.

definition: A social arrangement A constitutes an institution if and only if (i) A is governed by a formal set of written rules or regulations, (ii) A has designated officers or functionaries who administer its operations, and (iii) A occupies a permanent physical location — a building or complex of buildings — at which its activities are conducted.
conditions: A is governed by formal written rules
A has designated officers or functionaries
A occupies a permanent physical location
counterexample: Consider the institution of the market — the system by which goods and services are exchanged through the mechanism of supply and demand.
