The market is governed not by a single set of formal written rules but by the accumulated customs of commerce, supplemented by a patchwork of legislation that varies from jurisdiction to jurisdiction. It has no single set of designated officers; it is administered by no one in particular, emerging from the independent actions of countless buyers and sellers. And it occupies no permanent physical location; market transactions occur in shops, on streets, in homes, and increasingly through electronic networks that exist nowhere in physical space. All three conditions fail. Yet the market is universally recognised as one of the fundamental institutions of human social life.
missing_condition: The definition is modelled on formal bureaucratic organisations and excludes the vast class of informal, emergent, and decentralised institutions — markets, languages, systems of etiquette — that structure social life without formal rules, designated officers, or permanent locations. A condition requiring only that the arrangement impose stable, recognised patterns of behaviour on its participants would be more adequate.
passage: One might propose that a social arrangement constitutes an institution if and only if it is governed by formal written rules, has designated officers, and occupies a permanent physical location. This definition captures the features of many familiar institutions — the university, the court of law, the hospital — quite well. Each has written rules, appointed officials, and a physical home. Yet one might observe that the definition is shaped by the model of the formal bureaucratic organisation and fails to accommodate institutions of a very different kind. Consider the market — the system by which goods and services are exchanged through supply and demand. The market is one of the most important institutions in human history, shaping the organisation of labour, the distribution of wealth, and the structure of entire societies. But it is not governed by a single set of formal written rules; the customs of commerce have evolved over centuries and are supplemented by diverse and sometimes contradictory legislation. It has no designated officers; it is administered by no central authority but emerges from the independent decisions of millions of buyers and sellers. And it occupies no permanent physical location; transactions occur in shops, on street corners, in living rooms, and increasingly in the ethereal medium of electronic networks. All three conditions fail, yet the market is as plainly an institution as the Bank of England. The definition mistakes the outward trappings of formal organisations for the essence of institutionality. What makes something an institution is not its possession of written rules and physical premises but its capacity to impose stable, recognised patterns of behaviour upon the persons who participate in it.

definition: An activity A involving two or more agents constitutes cooperation if and only if (i) each agent intends to contribute to a shared goal, (ii) each agent is aware of the other agents' contributions and adjusts their own behaviour accordingly, and (iii) each agent acts voluntarily — that is, no agent is coerced into participating.
conditions: Each agent intends to contribute to a shared goal
Each agent is aware of and adjusts to the others' contributions
Each agent acts voluntarily without coercion
counterexample: Consider two prisoners in adjacent cells who coordinate an escape. Each intends to contribute to the shared goal of freedom. Each is aware of the other's actions and adjusts accordingly — one distracts the guard while the other works on the lock. But their cooperation arises from the most extreme duress: they are imprisoned, and the alternative to escape is continued captivity. One might argue that their participation is not voluntary in the relevant sense — they cooperate not from free choice but from desperate necessity. The third condition is, at the very least, doubtful. Yet their activity is a paradigmatic instance of cooperation — coordinated action towards a shared end, with mutual awareness and adjustment.
missing_condition: The definition requires voluntariness, but many of the most significant instances of human cooperation — wartime alliances, emergency responses, cooperation among prisoners or among persons in dire circumstances — arise from necessity rather than free choice. A condition requiring only that the agents act intentionally and with mutual coordination, regardless of the constraints under which they act, would be more adequate.
passage: One might propose that an activity involving two or more agents constitutes cooperation if and only if each agent intends to contribute to a shared goal, each is aware of the others' contributions and adjusts their own behaviour accordingly, and each acts voluntarily. The shared-goal condition distinguishes cooperation from mere parallel action; the mutual-awareness condition captures the interactive, responsive character of cooperative behaviour; and the voluntariness condition ensures that what looks like cooperation is not in fact coerced compliance. Yet one might observe that the voluntariness condition, though it captures an ideal, excludes many of the most compelling instances of human cooperation. Consider two prisoners in adjacent cells who coordinate an elaborate escape. Each intends to contribute to the shared goal of freedom. Each is exquisitely aware of the other's actions and adjusts their own with care — one creating a distraction at a precisely timed moment while the other works on the lock. But their cooperation arises from the most extreme constraint: they are imprisoned, and the alternative to cooperative escape is continued captivity. Their participation is driven not by free choice but by desperate necessity. The voluntariness condition is doubtful at best. Yet the prisoners' activity is, by any reasonable standard, a paradigmatic instance of cooperation — coordinated, goal-directed, mutually responsive joint action. The definition confuses an ideal feature of cooperative arrangements — that they be freely entered into — with a necessary condition of cooperation as such. What is essential is the mutual coordination of intentional action towards a shared end, not the absence of all external constraint.

definition: A person A trusts a person B if and only if (i) A believes that B will act in A's interest, (ii) A has had prior positive interactions with B that provide a basis for this belief, and (iii) A feels no anxiety about B's future behaviour.
conditions: A believes B will act in A's interest
A has had prior positive interactions with B
A feels no anxiety about B's future behaviour
counterexample: Consider a patient who trusts a surgeon she has never met, on the basis of the surgeon's qualifications, the hospital's reputation, and the recommendation of her general practitioner. The patient believes the surgeon will act in her interest. But she has had no prior interactions with the surgeon whatsoever — they have never met, and she knows nothing of the surgeon's character beyond the institutional credentials. Moreover, she feels considerable anxiety about the surgery and the surgeon's performance. The second and third conditions fail. Yet the patient trusts the surgeon; she is placing her life in the surgeon's hands on the basis of institutional guarantees and professional credentials, and this is plainly a form of trust.
missing_condition: The definition requires prior personal interaction and the absence of anxiety, but much trust is institutional — placed in strangers on the basis of credentials, reputation, and institutional safeguards — and much trust is accompanied by anxiety rather than serene confidence. A condition acknowledging that trust may be grounded in institutional structures and that it often coexists with considerable vulnerability and concern would be more adequate.
passage: One might propose that a person trusts another if and only if the first believes the second will act in the first person's interest, the first has had prior positive interactions with the second, and the first feels no anxiety about the second's future behaviour. This definition captures the features of trust between intimate friends or longstanding associates: it is grounded in experience, it is confident, and it is free of worry. The prior-interaction condition provides an evidential basis for trust; the anxiety condition captures the psychological ease that trust is supposed to bring. Yet one might observe that these conditions are too restrictive for the concept of trust as it actually operates in social life. Consider a patient who entrusts herself to a surgeon she has never met. The patient believes the surgeon will act in her interest — she would not consent to the operation otherwise. But she has had no prior interactions with the surgeon; her trust is based entirely on the surgeon's institutional credentials, the hospital's reputation, and the recommendation of her general practitioner. And she is far from free of anxiety; she is profoundly nervous, as any person facing surgery might be. Both the second and the third conditions fail. Yet the patient trusts the surgeon in the fullest and most consequential sense: she places her life in the surgeon's hands. The definition has been shaped by the model of personal trust between intimates and has overlooked the vast domain of institutional trust — the trust we place in strangers on the basis of credentials, qualifications, and the institutional structures that vouch for their competence and integrity.

definition: A person A is alienated from an activity or institution X if and only if (i) A participates in X involuntarily — that is, A would not participate if free to choose otherwise, (ii) A derives no satisfaction or meaning from participation in X, and (iii) A is conscious of the discrepancy between X as it is and X as it might be — that is, A has a conception of a better alternative.
conditions: A participates in X involuntarily
A derives no satisfaction or meaning from X
A is conscious of a better alternative
counterexample: Consider a skilled carpenter who works in a furniture factory. The carpenter participates voluntarily — there is no compulsion, and alternative employment is available. The carpenter even derives a certain satisfaction from the precision and quality of the individual pieces. Yet the carpenter is alienated from the work, for the design decisions are made by others, the products are sold under a brand name that erases the craftsman's identity, and the rhythm and purpose of the work are dictated by the factory's production schedule rather than by the carpenter's own judgement. The first two conditions fail, yet the carpenter experiences alienation — a sense that the activity, though freely chosen and not wholly unsatisfying, fails to engage the full range of the carpenter's capacities and does not belong to the carpenter in any meaningful sense.
missing_condition: The definition requires involuntary participation and total absence of satisfaction, but alienation may be experienced even by those who participate voluntarily and derive partial satisfaction, when the activity fails to engage their capacities fully or when the product of their labour is appropriated by others. A condition addressing the relation between the worker and the product — whether the worker controls, owns, and is recognised in the product of labour — is needed.
passage: One might propose that a person is alienated from an activity or institution if and only if the person participates involuntarily, derives no satisfaction or meaning from it, and is conscious of a better alternative. The involuntariness condition captures the sense of compulsion; the lack of satisfaction captures the experiential emptiness; and the consciousness of alternatives captures the critical perspective that distinguishes alienation from mere habit. Yet one might observe that these conditions, taken jointly, describe only the most extreme form of alienation and miss the subtler and more pervasive forms. Consider a skilled carpenter who works in a furniture factory. The carpenter is not compelled to work there; alternative employment exists, and the carpenter has chosen this position freely. The carpenter even derives a certain satisfaction from the precision of the joinery and the quality of the finished pieces. But the carpenter does not design the furniture — that is done by others. The products are sold under a brand name that effaces the craftsman's identity. And the pace and purpose of the work are dictated by the factory's production schedule, not by the carpenter's own judgement or creative impulse. The first two conditions fail, yet the carpenter is alienated — not in the sense of being unwillingly enslaved to hateful labour, but in the sense that the work does not fully engage the carpenter's capacities, that the product of the carpenter's skill belongs to someone else, and that the carpenter's identity as a maker is invisible in the finished product. The definition captures alienation in its most dramatic form but misses the more common and perhaps more insidious forms — the alienation of the competent worker whose labour is freely given and partially satisfying but whose creative agency is nevertheless diminished and whose products are appropriated.

definition: A rule R is a social norm within a group G if and only if (i) the majority of the members of G conform to R, (ii) the members of G believe that the majority of members conform to R, and (iii) violations of R are punished by formal sanctions — fines, penalties, or legal consequences imposed by an authority.
conditions: The majority of G conforms to R
Members of G believe that the majority conforms
Violations are punished by formal sanctions
counterexample: Consider the norm of queuing — waiting one's turn in an orderly line — in British society. The majority of people queue. People believe that the majority queue. But violations of the queuing norm are not punished by formal sanctions; there is no fine for pushing to the front, no legal penalty for failing to wait one's turn. The punishment is entirely informal: disapproving looks, audible tuts, pointed remarks, and social ostracism. The third condition fails. Yet the queuing norm is among the most powerful and widely observed social norms in British life, enforced not by law but by the weight of collective disapproval.
missing_condition: The definition requires formal sanctions, but many of the most effective social norms are enforced entirely through informal means — disapproval, gossip, social exclusion, and the withdrawal of esteem. A condition acknowledging that norm-enforcement may be informal as well as formal is needed.
passage: One might propose that a rule is a social norm within a group if and only if the majority of members conform to it, the members believe that the majority conforms, and violations are punished by formal sanctions. The first two conditions capture the behavioural and cognitive dimensions of norms: a norm is a rule that people follow and that people believe others follow. The third condition captures the enforcement dimension: norms are backed by sanctions. This definition works well for norms that are also codified in law — the norms against theft, assault, and fraud, which are enforced by fines, imprisonment, and other legal penalties. Yet one might observe that the formal-sanction condition is too narrow for the vast majority of social norms. Consider the British norm of queuing. The British queue with remarkable discipline — in shops, at bus stops, at post offices. People believe that others queue, and conformity is expected. But there is no formal sanction for failing to queue; no authority imposes a fine on the person who pushes to the front. The sanction is entirely informal: the disapproving stare, the audible tut, the muttered remark, and — in extreme cases — social ostracism. These informal sanctions are extraordinarily effective; the queuing norm is one of the most reliably observed norms in British social life. The definition requires formal enforcement, but the most pervasive and deeply felt social norms — norms of politeness, dress, table manners, and conversational propriety — are enforced not by law but by the subtler and often more potent mechanisms of informal social pressure.

definition: A political arrangement P is grounded in a social contract if and only if (i) the members of the society subject to P have explicitly consented to be governed by P, (ii) the consent was given freely and without coercion, and (iii) the consent was given at a specific historical moment — that is, there is a determinate event at which the contract was formed.
conditions: Members have explicitly consented to be governed by P
The consent was given freely and without coercion
The consent was given at a specific historical moment
counterexample: Consider the political arrangements of any modern democratic state — say, the United Kingdom. No living citizen of the United Kingdom has explicitly consented to be governed by its constitutional arrangements. The constitution, such as it is, evolved over centuries through a series of statutes, conventions, and judicial decisions; there was no single moment of explicit consent. And many citizens were born into the arrangement, inheriting their political obligations along with their nationality, without any act of free consent. All three conditions fail. Yet the political arrangements of the United Kingdom are widely regarded as legitimate, and the language of social contract is regularly invoked to describe and justify them.
missing_condition: The definition requires explicit, historically datable consent, but no actual political arrangement has ever been grounded in such consent. What is needed is a notion of tacit or hypothetical consent — the idea that the arrangement is one that rational persons would consent to, or that continued participation implies a form of acquiescence — rather than a requirement of explicit historical agreement.
passage: One might propose that a political arrangement is grounded in a social contract if and only if the members of the society have explicitly consented to be governed by it, the consent was freely given, and the consent occurred at a specific historical moment. This definition embodies the most literal reading of the social-contract tradition: the state rests on an actual agreement among its members, freely entered into at a determinate time. The explicit-consent condition ensures that the contract is not a fiction; the freedom condition ensures that the consent is genuine; and the historical-moment condition anchors the contract in reality rather than in hypothesis. Yet one might observe that no actual political arrangement satisfies these conditions. Consider the United Kingdom. No living Briton has explicitly consented to the constitutional arrangements under which she lives. There was no founding moment at which the British polity was established by mutual agreement; the constitution evolved over centuries through statute, convention, and judicial decision. And the citizens who are born into the arrangement inherit their political obligations without any act of free consent — they are bound by the arrangements of their ancestors. All three conditions fail, yet the United Kingdom is governed, and its governance is widely regarded as legitimate. The definition takes the social-contract metaphor literally and demands what has never existed: an actual, explicit, freely given, historically datable act of political consent. What the tradition has generally meant by 'social contract' is something weaker and more subtle — a standard of legitimacy, a hypothetical agreement that rational persons would endorse, or an implicit consent expressed through continued participation in political life.

definition: A set of practices, beliefs, and artefacts C constitutes the culture of a group G if and only if (i) C is shared by all members of G — every member participates in the same practices, holds the same beliefs, and uses the same artefacts, (ii) C is transmitted from the older members of G to the younger through explicit instruction, and (iii) C is unique to G — no other group possesses the same set of practices, beliefs, and artefacts.
conditions: C is shared by all members of G
C is transmitted through explicit instruction
C is unique to G
counterexample: Consider British culture. It is not shared by all Britons in the same way — a Cornish farmer, a Glaswegian shipbuilder, a London financier, and a Welsh poet participate in overlapping but distinct sets of practices, hold varying beliefs, and use different artefacts. Much of British culture is transmitted not through explicit instruction but through immersion, imitation, and the slow absorption of customs and attitudes that no one formally teaches. And British culture is not wholly unique — it shares its language with dozens of other nations, many of its literary and musical traditions with other European cultures, and many of its political institutions with its former colonies. All three conditions fail. Yet no one would deny that there is such a thing as British culture.
missing_condition: The definition requires unanimity, explicit transmission, and uniqueness, but actual cultures are internally diverse, largely transmitted through implicit socialisation, and share many elements with neighbouring cultures. A condition requiring only a recognisable pattern of shared practices and dispositions, transmitted by any means and overlapping with the cultures of other groups, would be more adequate.
passage: One might propose that a set of practices, beliefs, and artefacts constitutes the culture of a group if and only if it is shared by all members, transmitted from elders to juniors through explicit instruction, and unique to the group. The unanimity condition ensures that culture is genuinely shared; the transmission condition captures the intergenerational character of culture; and the uniqueness condition gives each culture a distinct identity. Yet one might observe that these conditions are far too demanding. Consider British culture. It is not shared uniformly by all Britons; a Highland shepherd and a City broker participate in markedly different practices and hold markedly different attitudes, yet both are recognisably British. Much of British culture is transmitted not through explicit instruction but through immersion — through growing up in a particular landscape, absorbing a particular sense of humour, developing particular expectations about the weather, the queue, the cup of tea. And British culture is far from unique: it shares its language with dozens of nations, many of its literary traditions with the broader European tradition, and many of its political forms with the Commonwealth nations that inherited them. All three conditions fail, yet British culture is plainly real and recognisable. The definition demands a degree of homogeneity, explicitness, and uniqueness that no actual culture possesses. What is needed is a more modest and realistic account: a culture is a recognisable pattern of shared practices, dispositions, and sensibilities, transmitted by any means — explicit or implicit, formal or informal — and overlapping freely with the cultures of neighbouring groups.

definition: A group G constitutes a community if and only if (i) the members of G share a common geographical location, (ii) the members of G engage in regular face-to-face interaction, and (iii) the members of G recognise themselves as belonging to G.
conditions: Members share a common geographical location
Members engage in regular face-to-face interaction
Members recognise themselves as belonging to the group
counterexample: Consider a diaspora — a people scattered across many countries who nevertheless maintain a vivid sense of common identity, observe shared customs, and recognise themselves as members of one community. The Armenian diaspora, for instance, spans continents. Its members do not share a geographical location and do not, in many cases, engage in regular face-to-face interaction. Yet they maintain cultural practices, collective memory, and a powerful sense of belonging. The first two conditions are not met, yet few would deny that this constitutes a community.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for communities constituted by shared identity, cultural practice, and collective memory rather than by physical co-presence, and thereby excludes diasporic, religious, and other geographically dispersed communities.
passage: One might propose that a community is a group sharing a common location, engaging in regular face-to-face interaction, and recognising its own membership. This analysis captures the village, the urban neighbourhood, and the small-town congregation. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is furnished by the phenomenon of diaspora. Consider the Armenian people, dispersed by the upheavals of the early twentieth century across Europe, the Americas, and the Middle East. The members of the Armenian diaspora do not share a common geographical location; they are separated by thousands of miles. Many have never met one another and do not engage in regular face-to-face interaction. Yet they maintain shared cultural practices — language, liturgy, cuisine, commemorative observance — and a powerful sense of belonging to a single community. They call themselves a community, and they are recognised as one by others. Two of the three conditions in the definition are plainly unsatisfied, yet it would be perverse to deny that the Armenian diaspora constitutes a community. What the definition overlooks is the possibility that community may be constituted by shared identity, cultural practice, and collective memory rather than by geographical proximity and physical interaction.

definition: A practice P constitutes a tradition in a society S if and only if (i) P has been transmitted from one generation to the next within S, (ii) P has been maintained without substantial alteration, and (iii) P is valued by the members of S as a link to their past.
conditions: P has been transmitted across generations
P has been maintained without substantial alteration
P is valued as a link to the past
counterexample: Consider the tradition of the British coronation. The ceremony has been transmitted from generation to generation and is valued as a link to the past. Yet it has undergone substantial alteration over the centuries: the language has changed from Latin to English, entire sections have been added or removed, the regalia have been modified, and the role of the Church has shifted. The second condition is not satisfied, for the ceremony has been repeatedly and substantially altered. Yet it is indisputably a tradition — indeed, one of the most celebrated traditions in British public life.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for traditions that evolve substantially over time while retaining a continuous line of descent and a perceived connection to the past, thereby confusing fidelity of transmission with rigid sameness.
passage: A common analysis holds that a tradition is a practice transmitted across generations, maintained without substantial alteration, and valued as a connection to the past. This captures the image of tradition as a stable inheritance — the same songs, the same rites, the same customs passed down unchanged. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by the coronation ceremony of the British monarch. The coronation is unquestionably a tradition of the highest order. It has been transmitted across many generations, stretching back to the medieval period and beyond. It is valued, indeed revered, as a link to the nation's past. But it has not been maintained without substantial alteration. The language of the ceremony has shifted from Latin to English; entire portions have been added, curtailed, or rearranged; the order of precedence among participants has changed; and the theological significance of certain acts has been reinterpreted. Each generation has modified the ceremony to suit its own sensibilities while claiming continuity with what came before. The definition, by requiring that a tradition be maintained without substantial alteration, would exclude the coronation — a consequence that reveals the inadequacy of the condition. What is needed is a recognition that traditions may evolve considerably while remaining traditions, provided they retain a continuous line of descent and a perceived connection to the past.

definition: A pattern of behaviour B in a society S constitutes a custom if and only if (i) B is widely practised among the members of S, (ii) B is not enforced by explicit legal sanction, and (iii) deviation from B is met with social disapproval.
conditions: B is widely practised among the members of S
B is not enforced by explicit legal sanction
Deviation from B is met with social disapproval
counterexample: Consider the custom of tipping waiters in a restaurant. In many societies, tipping is widely practised and is not legally mandated. But in some cases, deviation from tipping is met not with disapproval but with indifference — particularly in establishments where tipping is common but not strongly expected. A diner who leaves no tip may receive no adverse reaction at all. The third condition is not satisfied, yet tipping remains, by any ordinary reckoning, a social custom.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate customs that are widely followed but whose violation elicits no social disapproval, suggesting that the force of a custom may reside in habitual conformity rather than in the threat of censure.
passage: One might analyse a custom as a widely practised pattern of behaviour, not legally enforced, whose violation occasions social disapproval. This captures many clear cases: the custom of shaking hands upon introduction is widely practised, carries no legal mandate, and its omission may prompt puzzlement or offence. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises with the custom of tipping. In many countries, tipping waiters is widely practised and is not required by law. But the reaction to a failure to tip varies greatly. In some establishments, the diner who leaves no gratuity provokes visible displeasure; in others, the omission passes without remark. There are restaurants in which tipping is practised by nearly everyone yet in which no one pays attention to whether a particular diner tips or not. The custom persists not because of the threat of disapproval but because of habit, imitation, and a diffuse sense that tipping is 'the done thing.' The definition's third condition — that deviation be met with social disapproval — is too strong. Customs may persist through force of habit and conformity alone, without any mechanism of censure. A more adequate account would need to recognise that the binding force of custom may reside in mere regularity rather than in the prospect of social sanction.

definition: An arrangement A in a society S constitutes an institution if and only if (i) A consists of a set of rules or norms that govern the behaviour of the members of S in a particular domain, (ii) A is formally codified in documents, charters, or constitutions, and (iii) A persists beyond the tenure of any particular individual who occupies a role within it.
conditions: A consists of rules or norms governing behaviour in a particular domain
A is formally codified
A persists beyond any individual's tenure
counterexample: Consider the institution of queuing in British society. There are unwritten rules governing who stands where, how the queue is formed, and what constitutes an illegitimate attempt to advance one's position. These norms govern behaviour in the specific domain of waiting for services. The practice persists across generations. Yet it is nowhere formally codified — there is no charter of queuing, no written constitution of the queue. The second condition is not met, yet queuing is widely and rightly regarded as one of the most characteristically British institutions.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for institutions that operate through informal, unwritten norms rather than formal codification, and thereby excludes many of the most deeply entrenched social institutions.
passage: A familiar analysis holds that an institution is a set of rules governing behaviour in some domain, formally codified, and persisting beyond any individual's tenure. This captures the paradigmatic cases: Parliament, the judiciary, the Bank of England. Each consists of rules, each is formally codified, and each persists long after any particular officeholder has departed. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is furnished by what one might call the institution of the queue. In British society, there are well-understood norms governing the formation and maintenance of queues: one joins at the back, one does not push ahead, and one awaits one's turn with patience. These norms govern behaviour in a particular domain — the domain of waiting for goods, services, or entry. The practice persists across generations; it has endured for as long as anyone can remember. But it is nowhere formally codified. There is no written charter of the queue, no statutory instrument governing the order in which persons are served. The norms exist in practice and in the expectations of participants, not in any document. If formal codification is a necessary condition of being an institution, the queue must be excluded — a conclusion that few students of British social life would accept. What the definition overlooks is that some of the most powerful institutions in a society operate through informal, unwritten norms that are all the more binding for never having been set down.

definition: A group G possesses a culture C if and only if (i) the members of G share a set of beliefs, values, and practices, (ii) C is transmitted from older to younger members through deliberate instruction, and (iii) C distinguishes G from other groups.
conditions: Members share beliefs, values, and practices
C is transmitted through deliberate instruction
C distinguishes G from other groups
counterexample: Consider the dietary habits, gestures, and unspoken assumptions about personal space that characterise a national group. These are shared among the members and serve to distinguish the group from others. Yet many of these features are not transmitted through deliberate instruction; they are acquired by imitation, osmosis, and unconscious absorption in the course of growing up. No one teaches a child the precise distance at which to stand when conversing; the child simply learns it by being immersed in the practice. The second condition is not satisfied, yet these features are manifestly part of the group's culture.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate elements of culture that are transmitted not through deliberate instruction but through unconscious imitation, habituation, and immersion, which together account for a vast proportion of cultural transmission.
passage: One might propose that a group possesses a culture if its members share beliefs, values, and practices; if these are transmitted through deliberate instruction from older to younger members; and if the shared complex distinguishes the group from others. This analysis accommodates certain prominent aspects of culture: religious instruction, the teaching of a national language, the explicit passing down of moral precepts. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when we consider those elements of culture that are transmitted without anyone meaning to transmit them. Consider the unspoken conventions governing personal space in a particular society — the distance at which one stands when conversing, the manner in which one averts the gaze or holds the body. These conventions are shared by the members of the society and distinguish them from members of other societies. But they are not taught through deliberate instruction. No parent sits a child down and explains the proper distance for conversation. Rather, the child absorbs the convention through immersion — by observing, imitating, and being corrected in the course of daily life. The transmission is unconscious, gradual, and largely invisible to both teacher and learner. A definition that requires deliberate instruction will miss this enormous domain of cultural life, capturing only what is explicitly taught while overlooking the far larger territory of what is tacitly acquired.

definition: A rule R operative in a society S constitutes a social norm if and only if (i) R prescribes or proscribes a type of behaviour for the members of S, (ii) the majority of members of S conform to R, and (iii) members of S expect one another to conform to R.
conditions: R prescribes or proscribes a type of behaviour
The majority of members conform to R
Members expect one another to conform to R
counterexample: Consider the norm against littering in a large city where, despite the norm, a great many people do litter. The rule proscribes littering. Members of the society expect one another not to litter — they express disapproval when they see it done. Yet in certain neighbourhoods, the majority of people do in fact litter; conformity falls below fifty per cent. The second condition is not met, yet the norm persists — it continues to be invoked, to generate expectations, and to serve as a standard against which behaviour is judged.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for norms that persist as genuine standards of behaviour even when majority compliance has broken down, suggesting that the force of a norm does not depend upon majority conformity but upon the persistence of the expectation and the practice of invoking the norm as a standard.
passage: One might analyse a social norm as a rule prescribing or proscribing behaviour, to which the majority conforms, and which members expect one another to follow. This analysis captures many straightforward cases: the norm of greeting an acquaintance upon meeting is widely followed and widely expected. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by the norm against littering in a large city. The norm proscribes the discarding of rubbish in public places. Members of the community expect one another to refrain from littering — they condemn it in conversation, they support anti-litter campaigns, and they express displeasure when they witness it. Yet in certain areas of the city, the majority of inhabitants do in fact litter. Rubbish accumulates in the streets despite the existence of the norm. The second condition — majority conformity — is not satisfied. Yet it would be odd to say that the norm does not exist, or that it has ceased to be a norm. It continues to generate expectations, to serve as a basis for criticism, and to inform the judgments of residents about how people ought to behave. What the definition overlooks is that a norm may persist as a genuine social force even when it is widely violated, provided the expectation of compliance and the practice of censure remain in place. Majority conformity is a common accompaniment of norms, but it is not a necessary condition.

definition: An interaction between agents A and B constitutes cooperation if and only if (i) A and B act jointly toward a shared goal, (ii) A and B are each aware that the other is contributing to the shared goal, and (iii) A and B each benefit from the joint action.
conditions: A and B act jointly toward a shared goal
A and B are each aware of the other's contribution
A and B each benefit from the joint action
counterexample: Consider two rival shopkeepers on the same street who both place their wares on the pavement each morning, thereby attracting foot traffic that benefits them both. Each is aware that the other's display contributes to the flow of customers. They share the goal of a busy commercial street. And each benefits. All three conditions appear to be met. Yet neither shopkeeper regards herself as cooperating with the other; they are competitors who happen to benefit from one another's parallel activities. The 'joint action' is coincidental rather than coordinated.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the agents' actions be coordinated through communication, agreement, or mutual adjustment, rather than merely parallel activities that happen to produce a shared benefit.
passage: A natural analysis of cooperation holds that it consists in joint action toward a shared goal, mutual awareness of the other's contribution, and mutual benefit. One might observe that this definition captures the paradigmatic case: two persons carrying a heavy table through a doorway act jointly, are aware of each other's effort, and both benefit from getting the table moved. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises in the commercial world. Consider two rival shopkeepers on the same street, each of whom places an attractive display of wares upon the pavement each morning. The combined effect is to draw foot traffic to the street, from which both shopkeepers benefit. Each is aware that the other's display contributes to the flow of customers. They share, in a sense, the goal of a thriving commercial street. And each benefits from the other's activity. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Yet neither shopkeeper is cooperating with the other. They are rivals, pursuing their own interests in parallel. The mutual benefit is a fortunate by-product of independent competitive activity, not the result of coordinated effort. What the definition lacks is a condition requiring genuine coordination — communication, agreement, or deliberate mutual adjustment of action — to distinguish cooperation from the mere coincidence of parallel self-interested behaviour.

definition: A person A trusts a person B with respect to a matter M if and only if (i) A believes that B will act competently and in good faith with respect to M, (ii) A voluntarily makes themselves vulnerable to B's actions regarding M, and (iii) A has reasons — whether evidential or testimonial — for them belief in B's reliability.
conditions: A believes B will act competently and in good faith
A voluntarily makes themselves vulnerable to B's actions
A has reasons for believing in B's reliability
counterexample: Consider a small child who falls asleep in a stranger's arms during a long train journey. The child has no reasons — evidential or testimonial — for believing in the stranger's reliability. The child has not deliberated about the matter at all. Yet there is a sense in which the child trusts the stranger: she relaxes into vulnerability in the stranger's care, with no apprehension or reserve. All the phenomenological marks of trust are present — the ease, the unconcern, the surrender of vigilance — but the third condition is unsatisfied.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate the primitive, pre-rational trust exhibited by very young children, which is not grounded in reasons or evidence but in an unreflective disposition to be at ease in another's care.
passage: One might analyse trust as a combination of belief in another's competence and good faith, voluntary vulnerability, and the possession of reasons for the belief. This analysis captures the trust one places in a solicitor retained on recommendation, or in a surgeon whose qualifications one has verified. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is furnished by the trust of a small child. Consider an infant on a long train journey who, overcome by weariness, falls asleep in the arms of a stranger seated beside her parent. The child has not assessed the stranger's credentials. She possesses no evidence, no testimonials, no reasons of any sort for believing the stranger to be reliable. She has not deliberated. Yet all the phenomenological hallmarks of trust are present: the relaxation of the body, the absence of apprehension, the voluntary surrender of vigilance. It appears that the child trusts the stranger in a genuine sense. If we require that trust be grounded in reasons, this episode is excluded — but its exclusion seems to misrepresent the phenomenon. There is a primitive, pre-rational form of trust that children exhibit before they are capable of reasoning about reliability, and the definition, geared to the deliberative trust of adults, fails to accommodate it.

definition: A person A is alienated from an activity X if and only if (i) A engages in X not for its own sake but under external compulsion or economic necessity, (ii) A exercises no creative control over the manner in which X is performed, and (iii) A derives no satisfaction or fulfilment from X.
conditions: A engages in X under compulsion or necessity rather than for its own sake
A exercises no creative control over the manner of performance
A derives no satisfaction or fulfilment from X
counterexample: Consider a factory worker who assembles electronic components according to a rigid protocol, exercises no creative control, and works solely for the wage. Yet suppose this worker derives genuine satisfaction from the social bonds formed with colleagues on the assembly line — the camaraderie, the shared jokes, the mutual support. She is satisfied, in a sense, by the activity, even though the satisfaction derives from the social context rather than from the productive work itself. The third condition is ambiguously met, for she derives satisfaction from the activity broadly construed though not from its productive content.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish between satisfaction derived from the intrinsic character of the productive activity and satisfaction derived from the social circumstances that accompany it, thereby obscuring the specific alienation from productive labour that the concept is intended to capture.
passage: A natural analysis of alienation might hold that a person is alienated from an activity if she engages in it under compulsion, exercises no creative control over it, and derives no satisfaction from it. This definition captures the paradigmatic case of the assembly-line worker who performs a repetitive task for a wage, follows a fixed procedure, and finds the work soul-destroying. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when we attend to the social dimensions of work. Consider a worker in an electronics factory who assembles components according to a rigid, minute protocol. She works solely for the wage; she would not assemble components if she were not paid. She exercises no creative control over the process. The first two conditions are clearly met. But she takes genuine pleasure in the companionship of her fellow workers — the conversations, the mutual aid, the sense of belonging to a team. She looks forward to coming to work each day, not because of the work itself but because of the human bonds it occasions. In one sense, she derives satisfaction from the activity; in another, the satisfaction has nothing to do with the productive content of the activity. The definition cannot distinguish between these two senses of satisfaction, and its third condition is consequently indeterminate. What is needed is a more precise specification of the kind of satisfaction whose absence constitutes alienation — satisfaction in the creative and productive character of the work itself, not in the social conditions that merely accompany it.

definition: An arrangement A between individuals constitutes a social contract if and only if (i) the individuals consent to limit certain of their natural liberties, (ii) they do so in exchange for the benefits of social order and mutual protection, and (iii) the arrangement is entered into voluntarily by all parties.
conditions: Individuals consent to limit certain natural liberties
They do so in exchange for social order and mutual protection
The arrangement is entered into voluntarily by all parties
counterexample: Consider the situation of a child born into an existing political society. The child has not consented to any limitation of her liberties; she has entered no agreement, voluntary or otherwise. She has exchanged nothing for anything. Yet she is bound by the laws and norms of the society into which she was born — she is taxed, conscripted, educated, and governed. If the social contract requires the voluntary consent of all parties, no person born into an existing society is a party to the contract, and the concept loses its application to all but the founding generation.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for the binding of subsequent generations who have not consented, suggesting that the concept of a social contract cannot rest upon actual voluntary consent but must appeal to some notion of tacit, hypothetical, or constructive consent.
passage: One might analyse a social contract as a voluntary arrangement in which individuals consent to limit their natural liberties in exchange for social order and mutual protection. This captures the picture one finds in the classical expositions: free and equal individuals in a state of nature agree to surrender certain freedoms for the sake of security and the rule of law. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is presented by the situation of every person born into an existing political society. The child born in London, or in Edinburgh, or in any settled polity, has consented to nothing. She has not been consulted. She has entered no agreement. She has exchanged none of her natural liberties for anything. She simply finds herself, from the first moment of her existence, subject to the laws, customs, and demands of a society whose arrangements were established before she was born. If the social contract requires the voluntary consent of all parties, no person born into an existing order is a party to it. The concept then applies, at most, to the original founders — and even their consent is largely mythical. What the definition overlooks is the need for an account of how subsequent generations, who have never consented, come to be bound. Without some notion of tacit consent, hypothetical consent, or constructive consent, the social contract cannot perform the work demanded of it.

definition: A group of persons G constitutes a community if and only if (i) the members of G share a common language, (ii) the members of G participate in shared institutions — churches, schools, markets, or the like, and (iii) the members of G recognise a shared identity that distinguishes them from outsiders.
conditions: Members share a common language
Members participate in shared institutions
Members recognise a shared identity distinguishing them from outsiders
counterexample: Consider the community of deaf persons in a large city. The deaf community shares a sign language, participates in shared institutions — deaf clubs, schools for the deaf, advocacy organisations — and recognises a shared identity. All three conditions are met. But now consider an immigrant neighbourhood in the same city, where families from a dozen different countries live side by side, speak different native languages, and attend different churches or none at all, yet have come to form a community through the shared experience of displacement, the daily interactions of the market and the schoolyard, and a growing sense of solidarity. The first condition — a common language — fails, for the neighbours speak many languages and communicate through a rough pidgin or through bilingual children. Yet they are a community in every meaningful sense.
missing_condition: The definition requires a common language, but communities may form across linguistic boundaries through shared experience, mutual dependence, and the gradual development of solidarity, so that linguistic commonality is a frequent feature of communities rather than a necessary condition.
passage: One might propose that a group constitutes a community if its members share a common language, participate in shared institutions, and recognise a shared identity. The definition captures many familiar communities. A village in rural England shares the English language, participates in the church, the school, and the public house, and recognises itself as a distinct community with its own customs and character. The definition yields the correct verdict. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by the immigrant neighbourhoods of large cities. Consider a neighbourhood in which families from Turkey, Pakistan, Somalia, and Poland live side by side. They do not share a common language — they speak Turkish, Urdu, Somali, Polish, and a smattering of the local tongue. They do not all attend the same church, for they belong to different faiths, and some attend no place of worship at all. The first condition of the definition fails. Yet over the years, through the daily interactions of the market, the school gate, and the launderette, through the shared experience of being newcomers in a foreign city, through the practical necessities of mutual aid — minding one another's children, sharing information about employment — these families have formed a community. They recognise one another, they look after one another, they feel a solidarity born of shared circumstance. They have developed a sense of identity as the people of this particular street, this particular neighbourhood, distinct from the wider city around them. One might observe that the definition, by requiring a common language, has modelled the concept upon the linguistically homogeneous village and has overlooked the possibility that communities may form across linguistic boundaries, bound together not by a shared tongue but by shared experience, mutual dependence, and the practical solidarities of daily life.

definition: A person A has authority over a group G if and only if (i) A occupies a recognised office or role that carries the power of command, (ii) the members of G habitually obey A's directives, and (iii) A's directives are backed by the possibility of sanctions against those who disobey.
conditions: A occupies a recognised office or role with the power of command
Members of G habitually obey A's directives
A's directives are backed by the possibility of sanctions
counterexample: Consider an elderly woman in a rural village who holds no office, commands no institutional power, and has no ability to impose sanctions on anyone. Yet when a dispute arises among her neighbours, they come to her for counsel. Her judgement is sought, her advice is followed, and her displeasure is feared — not because she can punish anyone, but because her character, her experience, and her manifest wisdom have earned her the respect of the entire village. She has authority in a sense that is immediately recognisable, yet none of the three conditions of the definition are satisfied.
missing_condition: The definition models authority upon the coercive, institutional variety — the authority of the magistrate, the officer, the official — and neglects the authority that arises from personal qualities such as wisdom, character, and experience, which commands obedience without office or sanction.
passage: One might hold that a person has authority over a group if he occupies a recognised office carrying the power of command, the group habitually obeys his directives, and those directives are backed by the possibility of sanctions. This definition captures the authority of the magistrate, the military officer, and the head of a government department. The magistrate occupies an office, his orders are obeyed, and those who defy them face the sanctions of the law. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be found in the unofficial authority wielded by certain persons in every society. Consider an elderly woman in a rural village who has never held any office. She is not a magistrate, not a councillor, not the head of any committee. She has no power to impose sanctions — no fines, no imprisonments, no deprivations of privilege lie at her disposal. Yet when a dispute arises between neighbours, both parties seek her out and submit the matter to her judgement. When a young person contemplates a rash course of action, it is her disapproval that gives him pause. The entire village defers to her, not from fear of punishment but from a recognition that her judgement is sound, her character beyond reproach, and her experience of life vast. She has authority in a sense that every member of the village would acknowledge. One might observe that the definition has been built upon the model of institutional authority — the authority of offices, laws, and sanctions — and has thereby excluded the personal authority that arises from wisdom, integrity, and long experience. This personal authority may be the more fundamental variety, for it does not depend upon the accidents of institutional position but upon qualities that command respect in their own right.

definition: A distribution of goods among a group of persons is just if and only if (i) each person receives a share proportional to their contribution, (ii) the distribution is carried out according to rules that were established in advance and known to all, and (iii) no person is excluded from the distribution on grounds of personal characteristics unrelated to contribution.
conditions: Each person receives a share proportional to contribution
The distribution follows pre-established and publicly known rules
No person is excluded on grounds unrelated to contribution
counterexample: Consider the distribution of food to the survivors of a natural disaster. Relief workers distribute bread, water, and blankets to those who have lost their homes. The distribution is not proportional to contribution — the survivors have contributed nothing to the production of the relief supplies. The rules of distribution were not established in advance; they are improvised on the spot according to the urgency of need. Yet most people would regard the distribution as just, provided that it is carried out fairly and reaches those in greatest need. The first and second conditions both fail, yet the distribution answers to our sense of justice.
missing_condition: The definition models justice upon the distribution of earned rewards and neglects the dimension of need, which becomes paramount in situations of crisis or deprivation where persons may have contributed nothing yet are entitled to assistance by virtue of their humanity and their suffering.
passage: One might propose that a distribution of goods is just if each person receives a share proportional to his contribution, the distribution follows rules established in advance, and no one is excluded on grounds unrelated to contribution. This definition captures an important dimension of justice — the justice of earned reward. A group of labourers who have worked together to bring in a harvest may justly divide the proceeds in proportion to the work each has done, according to rules agreed upon before the harvest began. The definition yields the correct verdict. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by the distribution of relief in the aftermath of a natural disaster. A flood has destroyed a village. Relief workers arrive with food, water, medicine, and blankets. They distribute these goods to the survivors according to need: the injured receive medical attention first, the children receive food before the adults, the elderly receive blankets before the young. The distribution is not proportional to contribution — the survivors have contributed nothing to the production of the relief supplies, and the relief workers do not enquire into the past labours of those they are helping. The rules of distribution are not established in advance; they are improvised in the moment, adapted to the circumstances as they unfold. Both the first and the second conditions of the definition fail. Yet the distribution is just — indeed, it would be unjust to distribute disaster relief in proportion to contribution, for this would mean that those who had contributed most to the local economy would receive the most aid, regardless of their current need. One might observe that the definition has modelled justice upon a single paradigm — the distribution of earned rewards — and has overlooked the equally compelling paradigm of distribution according to need, which governs our judgement in situations where the relevant consideration is not what a person has done but what a person requires.

definition: A person A is free with respect to an action X if and only if (i) no external agent prevents A from performing X, (ii) no law or regulation prohibits A from performing X, and (iii) A faces no threat of punishment for performing X.
conditions: No external agent prevents A from performing X
No law prohibits A from performing X
A faces no threat of punishment for performing X
counterexample: Consider a person living in a society with no legal prohibitions on education, no external agents preventing him from attending a university, and no threat of punishment for seeking an education. Yet he is desperately poor, cannot afford tuition, books, or transport, and must work from dawn to dusk to feed his family. No one prevents him from attending university; no law prohibits it; no punishment awaits him if he tries. All three conditions are met. Yet one might hesitate to say he is free to attend university, for his poverty imposes constraints as effective as any lock or prohibition.
missing_condition: The definition recognises only the absence of external, coercive obstacles — physical prevention, legal prohibition, and threatened punishment — but neglects the material conditions that enable the exercise of freedom, so that a person may be formally free yet practically unable to act.
passage: One might hold that a person is free to perform an action if no external agent prevents him, no law prohibits the action, and no punishment is threatened. This is a definition of freedom as the absence of external interference, and it captures an important class of cases. A person who walks freely in a park, unimpeded by barriers, unprohibited by law, and unthreatened by sanctions, is free in the relevant sense. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by the situation of the materially deprived. Consider a young man in a society where education is legally open to all, where no person or authority prevents him from entering a university, and where no punishment awaits the educated. All three conditions of the definition are met: he is, by the definition's lights, free to seek an education. But the young man is destitute. He cannot pay tuition. He cannot buy books. He cannot afford the journey to the nearest university town. He must work sixteen hours a day in order to eat, and there is no surplus of time, money, or energy that would permit him to attend a single lecture. The barriers he faces are not legal or coercive but economic and material. No one prevents him; no one threatens him; no law stands in his way. Yet his poverty is as effective a constraint upon his freedom as a barred gate. One might observe that the definition has identified freedom with the absence of coercion and has overlooked the enabling conditions — health, education, material sufficiency — without which the mere absence of interference is an empty formality. A person who is free in law but unable in fact to exercise his freedom is free only in a sense so attenuated as to border upon the meaningless.

definition: A practice P constitutes a living tradition if and only if (i) P has been continuously performed for at least two generations, (ii) P is valued by its practitioners as a link to the past, and (iii) P is performed in substantially the same manner as it was by the earliest known practitioners.
conditions: P has been continuously performed for at least two generations
P is valued as a link to the past
P is performed in substantially the same manner as by the earliest practitioners
counterexample: Consider the tradition of English common law. The common law has been practised for centuries, and its practitioners — barristers, judges, legal scholars — value it explicitly as a link to the past; they cite precedents from hundreds of years ago with reverence. The first two conditions are met. But the common law is not performed in substantially the same manner as by its earliest practitioners. The procedures, the rules of evidence, the doctrines, and the remedies available have changed enormously since the time of Henry II. The common law of the twentieth century differs from that of the twelfth century in countless respects. The third condition fails. Yet the common law is universally recognised as one of the great living traditions of English civilisation.
missing_condition: The definition requires that a tradition be performed in substantially unchanged form, but many of the most vital traditions — legal, artistic, intellectual — evolve continuously while preserving a recognisable continuity of principle and purpose, so that unchanging form is characteristic of fossilised customs rather than of living traditions.
passage: One might define a living tradition as a practice that has been continuously performed for at least two generations, is valued by its practitioners as a link to the past, and is performed in substantially the same manner as by the earliest known practitioners. This definition captures the traditions that seem most ancient and most stable — the liturgy of a church, the ceremonies of a university, the rituals of a guild. In each case the practice is old, it is valued as a connection to the past, and it is performed in a recognisable continuity with its original form. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by the tradition of the English common law. The common law has been practised continuously since the twelfth century. Its practitioners venerate it explicitly as a tradition: barristers cite ancient precedents, judges invoke the wisdom of their predecessors, and the entire system rests upon a conscious sense of continuity with the past. The first two conditions are met with ease. But the third condition — that the practice be performed in substantially the same manner as by its earliest practitioners — fails dramatically. The common law of the twentieth century differs from that of the twelfth century in almost every particular: the procedures, the rules of evidence, the substantive doctrines, the remedies available, the role of the jury, the relationship between law and equity — all have been transformed beyond recognition. Yet the common law is a tradition in the fullest sense, and its practitioners would be the first to insist upon this. One might observe that the definition has confused living tradition with static repetition. A living tradition is precisely one that changes — that adapts, develops, and responds to new circumstances whilst preserving a recognisable continuity of spirit and principle. The requirement of substantially unchanged performance describes a fossil, not a tradition.

definition: A person A has a right to X if and only if (i) there exists a law or social rule that grants A a claim to X, (ii) others have a corresponding obligation to respect A's claim, and (iii) there exists an institutional mechanism by which A can enforce the claim if it is violated.
conditions: A law or social rule grants A a claim to X
Others have a corresponding obligation to respect the claim
An institutional mechanism exists for enforcement
counterexample: Consider a dissident writer in a country where the law does not recognise freedom of expression, where no social rule protects the writer's speech, and where no institutional mechanism exists to enforce any such claim. The writer publishes a tract criticising the government and is imprisoned. We would say, and many philosophers have said, that the writer's right to free expression has been violated — that he had a right to speak even though no law, no social rule, and no institutional mechanism recognised or protected that right. All three conditions fail, yet we affirm the existence of the right.
missing_condition: The definition ties rights to positive law and institutional enforcement, but there appear to be rights — the right to speak freely, the right not to be tortured — that exist independently of any legal system and that can be violated even where no law recognises them, so that a purely positivist account of rights is insufficient.
passage: One might hold that a person has a right to something if and only if a law or social rule grants the claim, others have a corresponding obligation to respect it, and an institutional mechanism exists for its enforcement. This definition captures the rights that are most securely held in well-ordered societies: the right to property, which is granted by law, generates obligations upon others, and can be enforced through the courts; the right to bodily safety, which is similarly grounded. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by the predicament of the dissident in an authoritarian state. Consider a writer who publishes a tract criticising the government of a country in which freedom of expression is not recognised by any law, in which no social rule protects the critic, and in which no institutional mechanism exists for the vindication of such a claim. The writer is arrested, tried in a summary proceeding, and imprisoned. We would say — and not merely as a figure of speech — that the writer's right to free expression has been violated. We would say this even though no law, no social rule, and no institutional mechanism granted, recognised, or protected the right. The definition, which grounds rights entirely in positive law and institutional enforcement, cannot account for this judgement. One might observe that the most urgent assertions of rights in the history of political thought have been made precisely in circumstances where no positive law recognised the right asserted — the right of the slave to his freedom, the right of the heretic to his conscience, the right of the colonised people to self-determination. If rights exist only where law and institutions recognise them, then the slave had no right to his freedom until the law changed, which is a conclusion that many would find repugnant.

definition: A person A has an obligation to perform action X if and only if (i) A has voluntarily undertaken to perform X, (ii) another person B has a legitimate expectation that A will perform X, and (iii) failure to perform X would cause harm to B.
conditions: A has voluntarily undertaken to perform X
Another person has a legitimate expectation that A will perform X
Failure to perform X would cause harm to that person
counterexample: Consider a person who comes upon a child drowning in a shallow pond. He has not voluntarily undertaken to rescue the child; he has made no promise, signed no contract, assumed no role. No one has a legitimate expectation that this particular person will rescue this particular child — the encounter is entirely accidental. Yet most people would say without hesitation that he has an obligation to wade in and pull the child out, an obligation so strong that failure to act would be a moral enormity. All three conditions of the definition fail, yet the obligation is manifest.
missing_condition: The definition grounds obligation entirely in voluntary undertaking and the expectations of others, but there appear to be obligations — such as the obligation to rescue a person in immediate peril — that arise from the circumstances themselves, independently of any voluntary commitment or pre-existing expectation.
passage: One might propose that a person has an obligation to perform an action if he has voluntarily undertaken it, another person has a legitimate expectation that he will perform it, and failure would cause harm to that person. This captures the obligations that arise from promises, contracts, and voluntary commitments. A builder who has agreed to construct a house has voluntarily undertaken the task, the client has a legitimate expectation that the house will be built, and failure to build it would cause the client harm. The three conditions are met, and the obligation is clear. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by the obligation to rescue. Consider a man walking alongside a shallow pond who sees a young child fall into the water and begin to drown. He has made no promise to rescue the child. He has no relationship with the child or the child's family. He has not voluntarily undertaken any commitment of aid. No person has a legitimate expectation directed specifically at him that he will perform a rescue — the encounter is entirely accidental. All three conditions of the definition fail. Yet the obligation to wade into the pond and pull the child out is, to most moral sensibilities, overwhelming. The man who walks past, leaving the child to drown because he has not volunteered to save it, has failed in an obligation that seems as weighty as any contractual duty. One might observe that the definition has modelled obligation upon the voluntary, contractual variety and has overlooked the obligations that arise simply from the circumstances in which one finds oneself — obligations that are imposed by the situation rather than assumed by choice, and that bind us regardless of whether we have consented to be bound.

definition: A government G is legitimate if and only if (i) G was established through procedures accepted as valid by the governed population, (ii) G governs with the ongoing consent of the majority of the governed, and (iii) G provides basic security and order to those it governs.
conditions: G was established through accepted procedures
G governs with ongoing majority consent
G provides basic security and order
counterexample: Consider a government that came to power through a military coup — a procedure manifestly not accepted as valid by the governed population. In the years following the coup, the new government did not seek or receive the consent of the majority. Yet it established order after a period of civil chaos, built roads and schools, provided security from bandits and invaders, and governed with a competence that its predecessor conspicuously lacked. After several decades, the population has come to accept the government not because its origins were legitimate but because its conduct has been beneficial. The first and second conditions fail — the establishment was illegitimate and consent was never sought — yet many inhabitants would call the government legitimate, on the ground that it has earned legitimacy through decades of good governance.
missing_condition: The definition grounds legitimacy in procedural origin and ongoing consent but neglects the possibility that a government may acquire legitimacy through sustained effective governance, so that a regime with illegitimate origins may become legitimate in the eyes of the governed through its conduct over time.
passage: One might hold that a government is legitimate if it was established through accepted procedures, governs with the ongoing consent of the majority, and provides basic security. This definition captures the legitimacy of democratic governments with admirable clarity. A government elected in a free and fair election, enjoying the continued support of the citizenry, and maintaining public order is legitimate by any standard. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by governments that came to power through irregular means but have since governed well. Consider a government that seized power in a military coup after a period of civil disorder. The coup was not sanctioned by any accepted procedure; it was carried out by force, without the consent of the population. In the years that followed, the new government did not submit itself to popular approval. The first and second conditions of the definition fail. Yet the government proved competent and beneficial. It restored order, built infrastructure, provided education and medical care, protected the population from external threats, and administered justice with reasonable impartiality. After thirty years of effective governance, the population has come to regard the government as legitimate — not because of how it came to power, but because of how it has governed. One might observe that the definition has made procedural origin and ongoing consent necessary conditions, but that legitimacy may also be acquired through sustained good governance. A regime whose origins are stained by force may, by decades of competent and beneficial rule, earn the acquiescence and even the loyalty of the population it governs. The definition cannot account for this form of earned legitimacy, which has been common in the history of political life.

definition: An action taken against a person A constitutes punishment if and only if (i) A has been found to have violated a rule or law, (ii) the action is imposed by an authority with the recognised power to impose it, and (iii) the action involves the deliberate infliction of suffering or deprivation upon A.
conditions: A has been found to have violated a rule or law
The action is imposed by a recognised authority
The action involves deliberate infliction of suffering or deprivation
counterexample: Consider a parent who, upon discovering that her young child has drawn on the walls with crayons, removes the crayons and requires the child to help clean the wall. The child has violated a household rule. The parent has the recognised authority to respond. But the response — removing the crayons and requiring the child to clean — does not involve the deliberate infliction of suffering or deprivation in any straightforward sense. The child is not made to suffer; she is required to participate in the remedy. Yet most people would call this a punishment — a mild punishment, certainly, but a punishment nonetheless. The third condition, which requires deliberate suffering, seems too strong.
missing_condition: The definition requires the deliberate infliction of suffering, but many punishments — especially those aimed at education or restoration — involve the imposition of a burden or a corrective task rather than the infliction of suffering per se, so that the definition is too narrow in its characterisation of what punishment involves.
passage: One might define punishment as an action against a person who has violated a rule, imposed by a recognised authority, involving the deliberate infliction of suffering or deprivation. The definition captures the severe punishments of the criminal law: imprisonment, which deprives the offender of liberty; fines, which deprive him of property; and corporal punishment, which inflicts physical suffering. These are paradigm cases, and the definition accommodates them without difficulty. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by the milder, corrective punishments that are characteristic of family life and of educational institutions.
