Consider a mother who discovers that her young daughter has drawn on the sitting-room wall with crayons. The mother removes the crayons from the child's possession and requires the child to help scrub the wall clean. The child has violated a household rule — she has been told not to draw on the walls. The mother has the recognised authority to respond — she is the parent. But does the response involve the deliberate infliction of suffering or deprivation? The child is not made to suffer; she is required to participate in undoing the damage she has caused. The removal of the crayons is temporary, and the scrubbing is a task, not a torment. One might observe that the definition has taken the punishments of the criminal law as its model — imprisonment, the lash, the fine — and has made the infliction of suffering or deprivation a necessary condition, overlooking the vast category of punishments that are corrective and educative rather than afflictive. The mother's response is recognisably a punishment, yet it aims not at suffering but at instruction, and the definition, by insisting upon suffering, cannot accommodate it.

definition: A person A has consented to an arrangement X if and only if (i) A has been informed of the nature and consequences of X, (ii) A has expressed agreement to X by an overt act — a signature, a spoken word, a gesture, and (iii) A's expression of agreement was not obtained by coercion or deception.
conditions: A has been informed of the nature and consequences of X
A has expressed agreement by an overt act
A's expression was not obtained by coercion or deception
counterexample: Consider a person who moves to a new country, settles there, takes employment, uses the public roads and hospitals, pays taxes, and lives peaceably under the local laws for twenty years, but has never performed any overt act of agreement to the political and legal arrangements of that country — has never signed a declaration, never spoken an oath of allegiance, never made any gesture of formal assent. He has been informed of the laws; he is aware of the consequences of his residence. No coercion or deception has been employed. But the second condition — the requirement of an overt act of agreement — is not met. Yet many would say he has consented to the arrangements under which he lives, tacitly if not expressly, by his long and voluntary residence.
missing_condition: The definition requires an overt expression of agreement, but much of the consent upon which social and political life rests is tacit — inferred from voluntary participation, continued residence, and the acceptance of benefits — rather than formally expressed.
passage: One might hold that a person has consented to an arrangement if he has been informed of its nature and consequences, has expressed agreement by an overt act, and his expression was not obtained by coercion or deception. This definition captures the consent of contracts and treaties: the parties are informed, they sign a document, and the signatures are freely given. It captures the consent of the patient in a hospital, who is told of the risks and signs a form. The three conditions are met, and the consent is genuine. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis concerns the consent that is alleged to underlie political obligation. Consider a person who emigrates to a foreign country and lives there for twenty years. He uses the roads, sends his children to the public schools, avails himself of the hospitals, pays his taxes, and obeys the laws. He has been informed of the nature of the political arrangements — he knows the laws and understands their consequences. No coercion or deception has been employed; he came voluntarily and stays voluntarily. But he has never performed any overt act of agreement. He has never signed a declaration of allegiance, never spoken an oath, never raised his hand in a ceremony of citizenship. The second condition of the definition is not met. Yet many would say that by his twenty years of voluntary residence, by his willing participation in the institutions of the country, by his acceptance of the benefits those institutions provide, he has consented to the arrangements under which he lives. His consent is tacit, expressed not by a signature or a spoken word but by a pattern of voluntary conduct sustained over decades. One might observe that the definition has modelled consent upon the explicit, contractual variety and has overlooked the tacit consent that is inferred from conduct — a form of consent that, whatever its philosophical difficulties, is indispensable to our understanding of political and social life.

definition: A group of persons G constitutes a community if and only if (i) the members of G share a common geographical territory, (ii) the members of G interact with one another on a regular basis, and (iii) the members of G share a set of norms or values that regulate their interactions.
conditions: The members share a common geographical territory
The members interact with one another on a regular basis
The members share norms or values that regulate their interactions
counterexample: Consider a diaspora community — Jewish communities in mediaeval Europe, for instance — whose members are scattered across many countries and share no common territory. They interact with one another, when they do, through letters, travelling merchants, and occasional pilgrimages. They share a rich set of norms, values, and practices. The second and third conditions are satisfied, but the first — the requirement of shared territory — fails. Yet one would not hesitate to describe these scattered groups as forming a community, bound together by shared religion, language, law, and custom across vast distances.
missing_condition: The definition requires geographical co-location, but fails to recognise that community may be constituted by shared identity, culture, or practice rather than by territorial proximity, and that some of the most enduring communities in human history have been dispersed across wide areas.
passage: One might propose that a community is a group of persons who share a geographical territory, interact regularly, and hold in common a set of norms or values. This characterisation captures the village, the neighbourhood, the small town — groups bound together by proximity, daily contact, and shared standards of behaviour. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is the diaspora community. Consider the Jewish communities of mediaeval Europe — groups of people dispersed across many kingdoms and principalities, separated by hundreds of miles, sharing no common territory. They were united not by geography but by religion, by the Torah and the Talmud, by shared law and custom, by a common language of scholarship, and by a profound sense of shared identity. Their interactions were intermittent — a travelling merchant, a letter carried along trade routes, a scholarly disputation conducted by correspondence — but their sense of community was deep and enduring. One might observe that the first condition of the definition is plainly unsatisfied: these communities shared no territory. Yet to deny them the name of community would be to misunderstand the very concept. The definition, by making territorial co-location essential, excludes communities constituted by shared practice, faith, and identity rather than by physical proximity.

definition: A person P has authority over a group G if and only if (i) P occupies a recognised position or office within an institutional structure, (ii) the members of G acknowledge P's right to issue directives, and (iii) P's directives are backed by the power to impose sanctions for non-compliance.
conditions: P occupies a recognised position within an institutional structure
The members of G acknowledge P's right to issue directives
P's directives are backed by the power to impose sanctions
counterexample: Consider a village elder in a traditional society who holds no formal office and belongs to no institutional structure. The villagers defer to her judgement in disputes, follow her counsel on matters of planting and harvest, and seek her guidance in times of crisis. She has no power to impose sanctions — she cannot fine, imprison, or punish anyone. Yet her authority is real and effective; her word carries weight that no one else's does. The first and third conditions fail, but her authority is undeniable.
missing_condition: The definition requires institutional position and coercive power, but fails to accommodate the authority that arises from personal qualities — wisdom, experience, moral character — and that operates through deference and respect rather than through institutional mechanisms or the threat of sanctions.
passage: A natural analysis of authority holds that a person has authority over a group if and only if she occupies a recognised institutional position, the group acknowledges her right to issue directives, and her directives are backed by the power of sanctions. This analysis fits the paradigmatic cases of political and bureaucratic authority: the magistrate, the commanding officer, the headmaster. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is the authority of the informal leader in a traditional community. Consider a village elder who holds no formal office. There is no constitution that establishes her position, no written charter of her powers, no institutional structure within which she operates. She is simply a woman of advanced years whose wisdom, experience, and strength of character have earned the deep respect of her neighbours. The villagers consult her in disputes, follow her counsel on practical matters, and defer to her judgement in times of difficulty. She has no power to impose sanctions — she cannot fine or punish anyone. If a villager disregards her advice, she can do nothing beyond expressing her disapproval. Yet her authority is perfectly real. One might observe that the first and third conditions of the definition fail in this case: there is no institutional position and no power of sanction. But the elder's authority is, in practice, more effective than that of many officeholders, because it rests upon voluntary deference rather than coercion. The definition, by requiring institutional position and coercive backing, excludes an ancient and important form of authority.

definition: A distribution of goods D is just if and only if (i) D allocates goods according to a principle that all members of the community have agreed upon, (ii) D treats like cases alike — persons in relevantly similar circumstances receive relevantly similar shares, and (iii) D is the product of a procedure that is free from bias, corruption, and arbitrary interference.
conditions: D allocates goods according to a principle the community has agreed upon
D treats like cases alike
D is produced by a procedure free from bias, corruption, and arbitrary interference
counterexample: Consider a society that has unanimously agreed upon the principle that goods shall be distributed according to the eldest son's right of primogeniture. The distribution treats like cases alike — all eldest sons receive equal treatment. The procedure is scrupulously administered without bias or corruption. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that the distribution is unjust, for it excludes daughters, younger sons, and all who fall outside the favoured category from any share of the inheritance, regardless of their need or merit.
missing_condition: The definition fails to impose any substantive constraint on the content of the distributive principle, so that a community might unanimously agree upon a principle that is manifestly unfair — discriminatory, exclusionary, or indifferent to need — and the definition would pronounce the resulting distribution just.
passage: One might propose that a distribution of goods is just if and only if it accords with a principle the community has agreed upon, treats like cases alike, and is administered through an unbiased procedure. This proposal captures much of what we mean by procedural justice: a fair process, impartially applied, yielding consistent results. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when the agreed-upon principle is itself unjust in its substance. Consider a community that has unanimously adopted the rule that all property shall pass by primogeniture to the eldest son. The rule is applied consistently — every eldest son receives his inheritance. The procedure is free from corruption — no official takes bribes, no case is decided arbitrarily. Like cases are treated alike. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Yet it appears that the distribution is unjust. Daughters receive nothing. Younger sons receive nothing. Those whose need is greatest may receive least, whilst those whose need is slight may receive most. One might observe that the definition, by attending only to the procedural aspects of distribution — agreement, consistency, and impartiality — takes no account of the substantive fairness of the principle itself. A community might agree upon a principle that is manifestly discriminatory, and the definition would pronounce the resulting distribution just, which is a conclusion we should be reluctant to accept.

definition: A person S is free with respect to an action A if and only if (i) no external agent prevents S from performing A, (ii) no law or regulation prohibits S from performing A, and (iii) S is not subject to coercion or threats that would make performing A perilous.
conditions: No external agent prevents S from performing A
No law or regulation prohibits S from performing A
S is not subject to coercion or threats regarding A
counterexample: Consider a person who wishes to study at a university. No one prevents him from applying; there is no law prohibiting his admission; and no one threatens him with harm if he attempts to enrol. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the man is desperately poor, and the university charges fees he cannot possibly afford. He is, on the definition's terms, free to study, but this freedom is entirely notional — he cannot exercise it. One might observe that a freedom one lacks the material means to exercise is a freedom in name only.
missing_condition: The definition concerns itself exclusively with the absence of external impediments — physical, legal, and coercive — but fails to recognise that a person may lack the material or economic resources necessary to exercise a freedom, rendering it purely formal and practically empty.
passage: A common analysis of freedom holds that a person is free with respect to an action if no external agent prevents her from performing it, no law prohibits it, and she faces no coercion or threats. This negative conception of freedom — freedom as the absence of interference — captures important cases. A person locked in a cell is unfree because an external agent prevents her from leaving; a person in a police state is unfree because the law prohibits dissent; a person under threat of violence is unfree because coercion constrains her choices. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when one considers the relation between freedom and material capacity. A young man wishes to study medicine at a university. No one physically prevents him from applying. No law bars his admission. No one threatens him. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied, and the definition pronounces him free to study. But the man comes from a family of extreme poverty. The university charges fees he cannot afford, and he must work to support his family. His freedom to study is, in practical terms, empty. One might observe that there is something deeply unsatisfactory about a definition that declares him free whilst he is manifestly unable to act upon that freedom. The definition, by conceiving freedom solely as the absence of external impediment, neglects the positive conditions — material resources, education, opportunity — without which freedom is formal rather than real.

definition: A practice P constitutes a tradition if and only if (i) P has been transmitted from one generation to the next over an extended period, (ii) the participants in P regard it as inherited from the past and value it for that reason, and (iii) P has remained substantially unchanged throughout its transmission.
conditions: P has been transmitted across generations over an extended period
Participants regard P as inherited from the past and value it for that reason
P has remained substantially unchanged throughout its transmission
counterexample: Consider the tradition of Christmas celebrations in England. The practice has been transmitted across many generations and is valued as an inheritance from the past. But the substance of the celebration has changed enormously: the Victorian era introduced the Christmas tree, the Christmas card, and the figure of Father Christmas as we know him; the twentieth century added the wireless broadcast, the television special, and the exchange of manufactured gifts on a commercial scale. The celebration of 1750 would be scarcely recognisable to a participant in 2023. The third condition fails. Yet one would not hesitate to call Christmas a tradition, and indeed it is one of the most celebrated traditions in English life.
missing_condition: The definition requires substantial continuity of content, but fails to recognise that traditions characteristically evolve, accumulate new elements, and shed old ones, and that what makes a practice a tradition is its perceived continuity with the past, not its actual invariance.
passage: One might characterise a tradition as a practice that has been transmitted across generations, is valued by its participants as an inheritance from the past, and has remained substantially unchanged through the process of transmission. This definition captures the idealized tradition — the unchanging ritual handed down through the centuries in fixed form. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is provided by any tradition whose history one examines with care. Consider the English celebration of Christmas. It is undeniably a tradition: families observe it each year, regarding it as an inheritance from their forebears, and valuing it precisely because it connects them to the past. But the practice has changed profoundly over the centuries. The mediaeval Christmas feast bore little resemblance to the Victorian celebration, which introduced the Christmas tree, the exchange of cards, and the modern figure of Father Christmas. The twentieth century added further transformations — the royal broadcast, the television special, the commercial gift-giving that now dominates the season. One might observe that the third condition of the definition is not satisfied: the celebration has not remained substantially unchanged. Yet Christmas is as much a tradition as any practice in English life. The difficulty is that the definition confuses the participants' perception of continuity with actual invariance. What makes a practice a tradition is not that it has remained the same but that it is experienced as a continuation of something received from the past.

definition: A person S has an obligation to perform an action A if and only if (i) S has explicitly consented to perform A, (ii) the performance of A would benefit someone to whom S stands in a recognised relationship, and (iii) failure to perform A would constitute a violation of a norm accepted by S's community.
conditions: S has explicitly consented to perform A
The performance of A would benefit someone to whom S stands in a recognised relationship
Failure to perform A would violate a community norm
counterexample: Consider a person who witnesses a child drowning in a shallow pond. She has not consented to rescue anyone; there is no relationship between her and the child. Yet it appears that she has an obligation to wade in and save the child, an obligation that is among the most compelling we can imagine. The first and second conditions fail, but the obligation is undeniable.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate obligations that arise not from consent or relationship but from the sheer moral urgency of a situation, in which the ease of rendering aid and the gravity of the harm combine to generate an obligation irrespective of any prior agreement or social bond.
passage: One might propose that a person has an obligation to perform an action if and only if she has explicitly consented to do so, the action would benefit someone to whom she stands in a recognised relationship, and failure to act would violate a community norm. This proposal captures many obligations well: the employee who has agreed to her terms of employment, whose work benefits her employer, and whose failure to work would violate the norms of the workplace. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is the obligation of the bystander. A woman walks past a shallow pond and sees a small child who has fallen in and is drowning. She has never met the child; there is no relationship between them. She has consented to nothing. The first two conditions of the definition fail entirely. Yet it appears that she has an obligation to rescue the child — an obligation of the most urgent and compelling kind. The rescue would cost her almost nothing: she need only wade into the shallow water. The harm to the child is catastrophic: he will die without assistance. One might observe that the obligation arises not from consent or relationship but from the moral character of the situation itself — the combination of grave need and easy remedy. The definition, by insisting upon consent and recognised relationship, cannot account for obligations that spring from the sheer urgency of a human situation.

definition: A government G is legitimate if and only if (i) G came to power through a process recognised as lawful by the existing constitutional order, (ii) the majority of the governed consent to G's rule, and (iii) G governs in accordance with established legal norms.
conditions: G came to power through a lawful constitutional process
The majority of the governed consent to G's rule
G governs in accordance with established legal norms
counterexample: Consider a revolutionary government that comes to power by overthrowing a tyrannical regime. The revolution violates the existing constitutional order; the process by which the new government assumes power is not recognised as lawful by the old regime's laws. The first condition fails. Yet if the previous government was itself oppressive and the new government institutes just laws, protects the rights of the people, and governs with their broad support, one might observe that it would be perverse to deny the new government's legitimacy merely because it came to power through irregular means.
missing_condition: The definition ties legitimacy exclusively to constitutional legality, but fails to recognise that legitimacy may be conferred by the justice and beneficence of a government's conduct, independently of the process by which it came to power, especially when the prior constitutional order was itself illegitimate or oppressive.
passage: A common account of political legitimacy holds that a government is legitimate if and only if it came to power through constitutional processes, enjoys the consent of the majority, and governs according to legal norms. This account captures the legitimacy of the well-established democracy: the government wins an election, the citizens accept its authority, and it governs within the bounds of law. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is the revolutionary government that overthrows a tyranny. Consider a colonial regime that governs an unwilling population through force and exploitation. A revolutionary movement overthrows this regime and establishes a new government that institutes democratic processes, protects individual rights, and governs with the broad support of the population. The new government satisfies the second and third conditions — it has popular consent and governs according to (new) legal norms. But it did not come to power through the constitutional processes of the old regime; it came to power by violating them. The first condition fails. Yet one might observe that to deny legitimacy to the revolutionary government is to grant a kind of permanent authority to whatever constitutional order happens to exist, regardless of how unjust it may be. The definition, by requiring constitutional legality, cannot distinguish between the overthrow of a just order — which might indeed undermine legitimacy — and the overthrow of an unjust one — which might be a precondition for legitimacy.

definition: A person S has consented to an arrangement X if and only if (i) S has been informed of the terms and consequences of X, (ii) S has expressed agreement to X through an overt act — a signature, a verbal affirmation, or the like, and (iii) S's expression of agreement was made freely, without duress or manipulation.
conditions: S has been informed of the terms and consequences of X
S has expressed agreement through an overt act
S's expression of agreement was free from duress or manipulation
counterexample: Consider a patient who signs a consent form before surgery. She has been informed of the risks and benefits; she signs the form without duress. But the medical terminology in the form is so technical that she does not understand what she has been told, despite the information having been provided. She has been informed in the sense that information was presented to her, but she has not understood it. The first condition is satisfied in the letter but not in the spirit. Yet the definition pronounces her consent valid.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the person be informed, but fails to distinguish between the provision of information and the comprehension of information, so that a person who has been given information they do not understand is counted as having been adequately informed.
passage: One might define consent as the condition in which a person, having been informed of the terms and consequences of an arrangement, expresses her agreement through an overt act made freely and without duress. This definition captures the paradigmatic case of consent: a buyer who reads the terms of a contract, understands them, and signs. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises in the domain of medical practice. A patient is presented with a consent form before surgery. The form describes the procedure, its risks, and its likely benefits. A physician explains the contents of the form. The patient signs freely, without any pressure. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. But the medical terminology in the form and in the physician's explanation is so technical, so laden with specialist vocabulary, that the patient does not in fact understand what she has agreed to. She has been informed in the narrow sense that information has been conveyed to her, but she has not comprehended it. One might observe that informed consent presupposes not merely the transmission of information but its reception and understanding. A patient who signs a form she cannot understand has been informed in the letter of the definition but not in its spirit. The definition, by requiring only that the person be informed rather than that she understand what she has been informed of, fails to capture the essential element of genuine consent.

definition: A rule R is a social norm if and only if (i) R is widely followed within a community, (ii) deviation from R is met with social disapproval or sanction, and (iii) the members of the community are aware of R and can articulate it when asked.
conditions: R is widely followed within the community
Deviation from R is met with social disapproval or sanction
Members are aware of R and can articulate it
counterexample: Consider the norm governing the physical distance at which people stand during conversation. In most English-speaking societies, conversational partners maintain a distance of roughly an arm's length. This norm is widely followed, and deviation from it — standing too close — provokes discomfort and is met with subtle but unmistakable social disapproval. Yet most people cannot articulate the norm when asked. They follow it automatically, without reflection, and would be hard pressed to state the rule they are following. The third condition fails. Yet the norm is as real and as effectively enforced as any that can be consciously stated.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the norm be consciously accessible and articulable, but fails to accommodate the vast class of tacit norms — norms that govern behaviour effectively without being explicitly represented in the consciousness of those who follow them.
passage: A plausible account of social norms holds that a norm is a rule that is widely followed within a community, whose violation is met with social disapproval, and which the members of the community can articulate when asked. This account captures many explicit norms: the rule against queue-jumping in England, the norm of removing one's hat indoors, the expectation that one will reciprocate a greeting. Each of these is widely followed, sanctioned when violated, and easily articulated. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is the norm of conversational distance. In English-speaking societies, persons who are not intimately acquainted maintain a distance of roughly three to four feet when conversing. This norm is widely followed — one can observe its operation in any public setting. Deviation from it is sanctioned — a person who stands too close is met with visible discomfort, a step backward, a subtle social rebuke. But the third condition is not satisfied. Most people cannot articulate the norm. If asked how far apart they stand during conversation, they would be uncertain; if asked to state the rule they are following, they would struggle to formulate one. The norm operates beneath the level of conscious articulation, governing behaviour through habit and bodily disposition rather than through explicit representation. One might observe that many of the most powerful social norms are of precisely this character — tacit, embodied, and effective without being consciously held — and that a definition which requires articulability excludes them.

definition: An interaction between persons A and B constitutes cooperation if and only if (i) A and B act together toward a shared goal, (ii) A and B each contribute effort or resources to the joint endeavour, and (iii) A and B have mutually agreed to pursue the goal together.
conditions: A and B act together toward a shared goal
A and B each contribute effort or resources
A and B have mutually agreed to pursue the goal together
counterexample: Consider two strangers who independently push a stalled car off a busy road. Each sees the car blocking traffic; each begins pushing from a different position. They have not communicated, have not agreed to work together, and may not even be aware that the other is pushing. Yet they act toward the same goal — clearing the car from the road — and each contributes effort. The third condition fails, for there has been no mutual agreement. Yet one might observe that what the two strangers are doing is cooperating, and that to deny this is to impose a requirement of deliberate coordination that the concept does not demand.
missing_condition: The definition requires mutual agreement, but fails to recognise that cooperation may arise spontaneously from the convergence of individual actions upon a shared goal, without any prior communication, negotiation, or explicit agreement between the participants.
passage: One might define cooperation as an interaction in which two or more persons act together toward a shared goal, each contributing effort or resources, having mutually agreed to pursue the goal jointly. This definition captures the paradigmatic case: two colleagues who discuss a problem, agree upon a strategy, and work together to implement it. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises in the domain of spontaneous collective action. Consider two strangers who encounter a car that has stalled on a busy road, blocking the flow of traffic. Each independently decides to push the car to the roadside. They begin pushing from different positions — one at the boot, the other at the driver's door. They have not spoken to each other; they have not agreed to work together; one may not even have noticed the other at first. Yet they are acting toward the same goal, and each is contributing physical effort to its achievement. One might observe that what these two strangers are doing is cooperating, despite the absence of any mutual agreement. Their actions converge upon a shared end and their efforts combine to achieve what neither could achieve alone. The definition, by requiring mutual agreement, excludes this perfectly natural and important form of cooperation — the spontaneous convergence of individual efforts upon a common purpose.

definition: A group of persons G constitutes a community if and only if (i) the members of G share a common geographical territory, (ii) the members of G interact with one another on a regular basis, and (iii) the members of G share a set of customs, values, or norms that distinguish them from outsiders.
conditions: The members of G share a common geographical territory
The members of G interact with one another on a regular basis
The members of G share a set of customs, values, or norms that distinguish them from outsiders
counterexample: Consider a group of mathematicians scattered across a dozen countries who correspond regularly, attend the same conferences, collaborate on papers, and share a distinctive set of professional norms — standards of proof, conventions of publication, a particular aesthetic regarding elegance. They interact regularly and share distinguishing norms. Yet they share no common geographical territory; they are dispersed across the globe. Nevertheless, one might observe that they constitute a genuine community — the mathematical community — and that their lack of territorial co-presence does not diminish the reality of their communal bonds.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for communities constituted by shared practice, intellectual tradition, or professional commitment rather than by geographical co-location, so that dispersed communities of practice are excluded.
passage: One might define a community as a group of persons sharing a common territory, interacting regularly, and bound by shared customs or norms. This characterisation fits the village, the neighbourhood, and the small town — the paradigmatic communities of the sociological tradition. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises with the phenomenon of professional and intellectual communities. Consider a group of mathematicians dispersed across a dozen countries. They correspond frequently, collaborate on research, attend the same conferences, and submit papers to the same journals. They share a distinctive set of norms — standards of rigour in proof, conventions of notation, a particular aesthetic that prizes elegance and generality. They interact more regularly with one another than with their geographical neighbours. Yet they share no common territory; they are scattered across continents. One might observe that the mathematical community is no less real for being dispersed, and that the bonds of shared practice and intellectual commitment are no less binding than the bonds of territorial proximity. It appears that the definition, by insisting upon geographical co-location, reflects a conception of community that is too narrow for the modern world, in which communities of practice may be constituted by shared engagement rather than by shared place.

definition: A practice or set of practices T constitutes a tradition if and only if (i) T has been transmitted from generation to generation over a substantial period, (ii) the participants in T regard it as having been handed down from the past, and (iii) T has remained substantially unchanged throughout its transmission.
conditions: T has been transmitted from generation to generation over a substantial period
The participants in T regard it as having been handed down from the past
T has remained substantially unchanged throughout its transmission
counterexample: Consider the tradition of the British coronation ceremony. It has been transmitted over many centuries. Participants regard it as an ancient custom. Yet historical investigation reveals that the ceremony has been substantially modified at nearly every coronation — new elements have been added, old ones dropped, and the symbolic meaning of many rites has been repeatedly reinterpreted. The third condition fails: the practice has not remained substantially unchanged. Yet no one would deny that the coronation is a tradition.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate the fact that traditions characteristically evolve through incremental modification and reinterpretation while preserving a continuous thread of self-conscious connexion to the past, so that traditions which have changed substantially are excluded.
passage: A natural characterisation of tradition holds that it is a practice transmitted across generations, regarded by participants as ancient, and substantially unchanged over time. This captures the intuitive picture: the traditional recipe handed down from grandmother, the traditional dance performed at harvest. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be found in the most conspicuous of British traditions. The coronation ceremony of the British monarch has been performed for over a thousand years. Each generation of participants regards it as a venerable custom handed down from the remote past. Yet historical scholarship reveals that the ceremony has been substantially modified at nearly every coronation. Elements have been added, others dropped. The order of proceedings has been altered. The symbolic meaning of the anointing, the investiture, and the oath has been reinterpreted in light of changing political circumstances. One might observe that the coronation is, by universal recognition, a tradition — perhaps the tradition of British public life. Yet it has not remained substantially unchanged. It appears that the definition, by requiring constancy, misunderstands the nature of living traditions, which characteristically evolve through cumulative modification while maintaining a continuous thread of self-conscious connexion to the past.

definition: A pattern of behaviour B is a custom if and only if (i) B is performed by the majority of members of a social group, (ii) B is performed out of a sense that it is the appropriate or expected thing to do, and (iii) deviation from B elicits social disapproval from other members of the group.
conditions: B is performed by the majority of members of a social group
B is performed out of a sense that it is the appropriate or expected thing to do
Deviation from B elicits social disapproval from other members of the group
counterexample: Consider the custom of applauding at the conclusion of a concert. It is performed by the majority of the audience. It is performed out of a sense that applause is the appropriate response. Yet deviation from it — sitting in silence while others applaud — typically elicits not social disapproval but indifference. No one censures the person who fails to clap. The third condition fails. Yet applauding at concerts is a custom by any ordinary reckoning.
missing_condition: The definition fails to include customs that are sustained by positive social reinforcement and habituation rather than by the threat of disapproval for deviation, so that customs which are followed willingly but whose violation carries no sanction are excluded.
passage: One might define a custom as a pattern of behaviour performed by the majority of a social group out of a sense of appropriateness and sustained by the disapproval that attaches to deviation. This characterisation captures customs such as removing one's hat indoors, shaking hands upon introduction, and queuing in orderly fashion — customs whose breach does attract censure. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises with the gentler customs of social life. Consider the custom of applauding at the conclusion of a concert. The vast majority of the audience applauds. Each person claps out of a sense that applause is the appropriate response to a performance — it would feel wrong to sit in stony silence. Yet if someone does sit in silence, the response of those around him is typically one of indifference rather than disapproval. No one glares at the non-clapper; no one whispers reproachfully. The custom is sustained not by the threat of sanction but by the positive satisfaction of expressing appreciation and by the force of social habit. One might observe that the absence of disapproval for deviation does not prevent us from regarding applause as a custom. It appears that the definition, by making disapproval a necessary condition, cannot accommodate customs that are maintained by positive reinforcement and habitual practice rather than by negative sanction.

definition: A social arrangement I is an institution if and only if (i) I consists of a system of rules that assigns roles, rights, and obligations to participants, (ii) I has been formally codified in written statutes, charters, or constitutions, and (iii) I is administered by designated officials or officers.
conditions: I consists of a system of rules that assigns roles, rights, and obligations to participants
I has been formally codified in written statutes, charters, or constitutions
I is administered by designated officials or officers
counterexample: Consider the institution of marriage as it existed in pre-literate societies. Marriage consisted of a system of rules assigning roles, rights, and obligations to the spouses and their families. It was administered, in a sense, by elders or religious figures who presided over the ceremonies. Yet in a pre-literate society, the rules of marriage were not formally codified in written documents — they were transmitted orally and sustained by communal memory. The second condition fails. Yet marriage in such societies was no less an institution than marriage in societies with written legal codes.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for institutions whose rules are transmitted orally and sustained by communal practice rather than by formal codification, so that the institutions of pre-literate and customary societies are excluded.
passage: One might propose that an institution is a social arrangement consisting of a rule-system that assigns roles, rights, and obligations, formally codified in written documents and administered by designated officials. This characterisation fits the modern state, the university, the corporation, and the court of law. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be drawn from the anthropological record. Consider the institution of marriage as it existed — and in many societies continues to exist — without written codification. In a pre-literate society, marriage consisted of an elaborate system of rules governing the selection of partners, the exchange of gifts, the allocation of domestic responsibilities, and the rights of offspring. These rules were as binding and as precisely articulated as any written statute. Elders presided over the ceremonies and adjudicated disputes. Yet the rules were transmitted orally and sustained by communal memory; they were never committed to writing. The second condition of the definition fails entirely. One might observe that the institution of marriage in such societies was no less real, no less structured, and no less consequential than its codified counterpart in modern legal systems. It appears that the definition, by requiring formal written codification, reflects a literate and bureaucratic conception of social order that cannot accommodate the institutions of customary and oral societies.

definition: A group G possesses a culture if and only if (i) the members of G share a common language, (ii) the members of G share a body of artistic and literary works that they regard as part of their heritage, and (iii) the members of G share a set of religious or philosophical beliefs that inform their way of life.
conditions: The members of G share a common language
The members of G share a body of artistic and literary works that they regard as part of their heritage
The members of G share a set of religious or philosophical beliefs that inform their way of life
counterexample: Consider the Deaf community in a given country. Its members share a common sign language. They share a body of storytelling traditions, poetry in sign, and visual art that they regard as their heritage. Yet the members of the Deaf community do not, as a community, share a set of religious or philosophical beliefs that distinguishes them from the hearing population; their religious affiliations are as various as those of the general public. The third condition fails. Yet the Deaf community possesses a rich and distinctive culture that is widely recognised by sociologists and by its own members.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for cultures constituted by shared practices, communicative traditions, and collective identity rather than by shared religious or philosophical commitments, so that communities whose cultural distinctiveness lies in their language and social practices rather than their beliefs are excluded.
passage: A natural characterisation of culture holds that a group possesses a culture when its members share a common language, a body of artistic and literary heritage, and a set of religious or philosophical beliefs that inform their way of life. This characterisation captures much of what we mean when we speak of French culture, Chinese culture, or the culture of ancient Greece. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises with the phenomenon of Deaf culture. The Deaf community in a given country shares a common sign language — a fully developed linguistic system with its own grammar, syntax, and expressive capacity. Its members share a rich body of storytelling traditions, poetry composed in sign, and visual art that they regard as part of their collective heritage. Yet the Deaf community does not, as a community, share a distinctive set of religious or philosophical beliefs; its members hold the same variety of religious and philosophical convictions as the hearing population. The third condition fails. One might observe that the Deaf community nonetheless possesses a genuine and distinctive culture — recognised by its members and by the sociological literature — grounded in shared language, shared practices of communication, and a powerful sense of collective identity. It appears that the definition, by requiring shared religious or philosophical beliefs, adopts too narrow a conception of what constitutes a culture.

definition: A rule R is a social norm if and only if (i) R prescribes or proscribes a type of behaviour within a social group, (ii) R is accepted by the majority of the group's members as a standard of conduct, and (iii) R has been explicitly formulated and communicated to the group's members.
conditions: R prescribes or proscribes a type of behaviour within a social group
R is accepted by the majority of the group's members as a standard of conduct
R has been explicitly formulated and communicated to the group's members
counterexample: Consider the norm of maintaining an appropriate distance from strangers in a queue. This norm prescribes a type of behaviour — standing at a certain distance. It is accepted by the majority as a standard of conduct — people who stand too close are regarded as behaving improperly. Yet the norm has never been explicitly formulated or communicated; no sign instructs people to stand at arm's length, and no one is formally taught the rule. It is absorbed through socialisation and imitation. The third condition fails. Yet the norm is real and operative.
missing_condition: The definition fails to include norms that are tacit and acquired through socialisation, imitation, and habitual practice rather than through explicit formulation, so that the vast body of unspoken social norms governing everyday conduct is excluded.
passage: One might define a social norm as a rule prescribing or proscribing behaviour, accepted by the majority as a standard, and explicitly formulated and communicated. This definition captures the norms embodied in formal codes of conduct, written rules, and publicly announced expectations. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises with the norms that govern the most ordinary interactions of daily life. Consider the norm of maintaining an appropriate distance from strangers in a queue. In Britain, one is expected to stand at roughly arm's length from the person ahead. This norm prescribes a type of behaviour. It is accepted by the majority — anyone who stands too close will be regarded as behaving improperly, and those nearby will shift uncomfortably. Yet the norm has never been explicitly formulated. No sign in the post office reads 'Stand at arm's length.' No parent sits a child down and articulates the rule. The norm is absorbed through years of socialisation, through imitation of others, and through the subtle corrective responses — the slight step forward, the pointed glance — that signal a breach. One might observe that this tacit, unarticulated norm is no less real, no less binding, and no less consequential than any written regulation. It appears that the definition, by requiring explicit formulation, cannot accommodate the vast body of tacit norms that structure everyday social life.

definition: A joint activity J constitutes cooperation between agents A1 and A2 if and only if (i) A1 and A2 act together towards a shared goal, (ii) A1 and A2 are each aware that the other is participating in J, and (iii) A1 and A2 have explicitly agreed to participate in J.
conditions: A1 and A2 act together towards a shared goal
A1 and A2 are each aware that the other is participating in J
A1 and A2 have explicitly agreed to participate in J
counterexample: Consider two strangers who arrive simultaneously at a narrow doorway, each carrying a heavy box. Without a word, one steps back and holds the door while the other passes through; then the first follows. They act together towards a shared goal — getting through the doorway efficiently. Each is aware that the other is participating. Yet they have made no explicit agreement; the entire interaction is conducted through glances and gestures. The third condition fails. Yet one might observe that this is a paradigmatic instance of everyday cooperation.
missing_condition: The definition fails to include spontaneous, tacit cooperation in which agents coordinate their actions through mutual adjustment and non-verbal cues rather than through explicit prior agreement.
passage: One might propose that cooperation consists in joint action towards a shared goal, with mutual awareness and explicit agreement to participate. This characterisation captures the cooperation involved in a business partnership, a scientific collaboration, or a military alliance — cases in which the parties have clearly articulated their commitment to a common end. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this proposal may be found in the most mundane circumstances. Consider two strangers who arrive at a narrow doorway at the same moment, each laden with a heavy box. Without a word of discussion, one steps back and holds the door open; the other passes through with a nod of gratitude; the first then follows. The two have acted together towards a shared goal — the efficient navigation of the doorway. Each was aware of the other's participation. Yet there was no explicit agreement: no words were spoken, no plan formulated, no commitment articulated. The entire episode was conducted through glances, gestures, and the kind of mutual adjustment that characterises the most fluid forms of social interaction. One might observe that this spontaneous coordination is cooperation in its purest form. It appears that the definition, by requiring explicit agreement, is too restrictive and cannot accommodate the tacit, improvised cooperation that pervades everyday social life.

definition: A person P trusts another person Q with respect to a matter M if and only if (i) P believes that Q will act in P's interest with respect to M, (ii) P is vulnerable to Q — that is, P would be harmed if Q failed to act as expected, and (iii) P has evidence that Q has acted reliably in the past with respect to similar matters.
conditions: P believes that Q will act in P's interest with respect to M
P is vulnerable to Q — that is, P would be harmed if Q failed to act as expected
P has evidence that Q has acted reliably in the past with respect to similar matters
counterexample: Consider a patient who, upon arriving in a foreign city, falls seriously ill and is taken to a hospital where she has never been and where she has no acquaintance with any member of the medical staff. She allows the surgeon to operate. She believes the surgeon will act in her interest. She is deeply vulnerable — her life depends upon the surgeon's competence. Yet she has no evidence whatever that this particular surgeon has acted reliably in the past; she has never met him and has no information about his record. The third condition fails. Yet one might observe that the patient trusts the surgeon — indeed, she must.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for trust that is grounded in the authority of institutional roles, professional credentials, or social structures rather than in the truster's personal evidence of the trusted party's past reliability.
passage: A natural account of trust holds that one person trusts another when she believes he will act in her interest, is vulnerable to his failure, and has evidence of his past reliability. This account captures many familiar instances of trust: we trust the friend who has proved dependable, the colleague who has delivered on past commitments, the merchant who has always dealt honestly. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account arises in circumstances where past evidence is unavailable. Consider a patient who falls seriously ill while visiting a foreign city. She is taken to a hospital she has never visited, and a surgeon she has never met prepares to operate. The patient believes the surgeon will act in her interest — she submits to the procedure. She is profoundly vulnerable — her life depends on his skill. But she has no evidence whatever of this particular surgeon's past reliability; she does not know his name, his record, or his reputation. The third condition fails entirely. Yet one might observe that the patient trusts the surgeon, and that this trust is not irrational — it is grounded in the institutional authority of the hospital, the professional credentials signified by the surgeon's qualifications, and the regulatory structures that govern medical practice. It appears that the definition, by requiring personal evidence of past reliability, cannot accommodate the trust that individuals routinely place in institutional roles and professional structures.

definition: A person P is alienated from an activity A if and only if (i) P engages in A out of external compulsion rather than intrinsic motivation, (ii) P derives no satisfaction or fulfilment from A, and (iii) P has no control over the manner in which A is performed.
conditions: P engages in A out of external compulsion rather than intrinsic motivation
P derives no satisfaction or fulfilment from A
P has no control over the manner in which A is performed
counterexample: Consider a highly paid corporate lawyer who freely chose her profession, exercises considerable autonomy in how she conducts her cases, and earns substantial rewards. She is not externally compelled; she chose the career and could leave it. She has extensive control over her work. Yet she feels a deep sense of estrangement from her daily activities — she finds the work meaningless and feels that it does not express her true capacities or values. The first and third conditions fail. Yet one might observe that the lawyer is alienated in a recognisable and important sense.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for alienation that arises from a felt disconnexion between one's activity and one's deeper values, capacities, or sense of self, even when external compulsion is absent and practical autonomy is present.
passage: One might define alienation as engagement in an activity under external compulsion, without satisfaction, and without control over the manner of performance. This characterisation captures the alienation of the factory worker who is compelled by economic necessity to perform repetitive tasks over which he has no say and from which he derives no satisfaction. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be found in the professional classes. Consider a highly paid corporate lawyer who freely chose her profession, exercises considerable autonomy in the conduct of her cases, and is remunerated handsomely. She is under no external compulsion — she chose the career after careful deliberation and could leave it at any time. She has extensive control over her work — she selects her cases, determines her strategies, and manages her schedule. Yet she feels a pervasive sense of estrangement from her professional life. The work strikes her as meaningless; it does not engage her deepest capacities or express her most cherished values. She performs it competently and even brilliantly, but with an inner detachment that colours her entire existence. One might observe that the lawyer is alienated in a perfectly recognisable sense, despite the absence of compulsion and the presence of autonomy. It appears that the definition, by locating alienation in external constraint and lack of control, cannot accommodate the subtler alienation that arises from a disconnexion between activity and selfhood.

definition: A political arrangement P is legitimated by a social contract if and only if (i) the members of the society have consented to P, (ii) P secures certain advantages — such as peace, security, or the protection of property — that would be unavailable in its absence, and (iii) the terms of P are known to and understood by all members of the society.
conditions: The members of the society have consented to P
P secures certain advantages — such as peace, security, or the protection of property — that would be unavailable in its absence
The terms of P are known to and understood by all members of the society
counterexample: Consider a stable political arrangement in a modern democracy. The arrangement secures peace, security, and the protection of property. Its terms are publicly available in a written constitution. Yet the vast majority of citizens have never explicitly consented to it — they were born into it and have simply continued to live under it. Some may not even be aware of the constitution's provisions. The first and third conditions fail for a large portion of the population. Yet one might observe that the arrangement is widely regarded as legitimate.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for tacit, hypothetical, or constructive consent — the kind of consent that may be imputed to rational agents who benefit from an arrangement they have not explicitly endorsed — so that political arrangements to which no one has expressly consented cannot be legitimated.
passage: A natural account of the social contract holds that a political arrangement is legitimate when its members have consented to it, when it secures advantages unavailable without it, and when its terms are known and understood by all. This account captures the contractarian ideal: government by the consent of the governed, for the benefit of all, on terms that are transparent and publicly accessible. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when one considers the actual circumstances of modern political life. In any modern democracy, the political arrangement secures peace, security, and the protection of property. The terms of the arrangement are set out in a written constitution that is, in principle, available to all citizens. Yet the overwhelming majority of citizens have never explicitly consented to the arrangement. They were born into it. They continue to live under it not because they have affirmed it but because they have never seriously contemplated the alternative. Many citizens have never read the constitution and could not state its principal provisions. The first and third conditions fail for a substantial portion of the population. Yet one might observe that the political arrangement is widely regarded as legitimate — indeed, it is the paradigm of legitimate government. It appears that the definition, by requiring explicit consent and universal understanding, sets a standard that no actual political arrangement could satisfy, and that a more adequate account must accommodate the tacit or hypothetical consent of those who benefit from an arrangement they have never expressly endorsed.

definition: A set of practices, beliefs, and artefacts C constitutes a culture if and only if (i) C is shared by the members of a distinct social group, (ii) C is transmitted from one generation to the next through learning rather than biological inheritance, and (iii) C forms a coherent and unified whole whose elements are systematically related to one another.
conditions: C is shared by members of a distinct social group
C is transmitted across generations through learning
C forms a coherent and unified whole
counterexample: Consider a modern cosmopolitan city in which residents of diverse origins live side by side. The city possesses a distinctive set of practices — particular forms of greeting, culinary habits, architectural styles, and modes of public conduct — that are shared among residents regardless of origin and transmitted to newcomers through daily interaction. These practices satisfy the first two conditions. But they do not form a coherent and unified whole; they are an eclectic assemblage drawn from many sources, often contradictory in spirit, and lacking the systematic integration the definition requires. Yet the residents themselves speak of their city's culture, and visitors recognise it as distinctive. The definition, by requiring coherent unity, excludes hybrid and syncretic cultures that are real and recognisable despite their internal diversity.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for cultures that are internally diverse, hybrid, or syncretic — assemblages of practices drawn from multiple sources that do not form a tightly integrated system yet are recognised as a distinctive cultural formation by participants and observers alike.
passage: One might propose that a culture is a set of shared practices, beliefs, and artefacts, transmitted across generations through learning, and forming a coherent unified whole. This characterisation captures the image of culture as an integrated system — the customs, beliefs, and material productions of a people, all of a piece and mutually reinforcing. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by the culture of a modern cosmopolitan city. Consider a great port city whose population has been drawn from many lands over many centuries. The city has developed its own distinctive practices — particular forms of greeting, a characteristic cuisine blending several traditions, a style of architecture that borrows freely from diverse sources, and modes of public conduct that residents recognise as 'the way things are done here.' These practices are shared among the city's inhabitants and transmitted to newcomers through daily interaction. The first two conditions are met. But the practices do not form a coherent and unified whole. The cuisine is eclectic, the architecture is hybrid, and the customs are drawn from traditions that are, in their original settings, quite incompatible with one another. The definition, by requiring systematic unity, would deny that the city possesses a culture — a conclusion that both its residents and its visitors would reject. It appears that cultures need not be internally unified to be genuine; they may be hybrid, syncretic, and internally diverse while remaining distinctive and recognisable.

definition: A person or body P possesses authority over a group G if and only if (i) P issues directives that the members of G are expected to follow, (ii) P's position is recognised as legitimate by the members of G, and (iii) P has the power to enforce compliance through sanctions.
conditions: P issues directives that G is expected to follow
P's position is recognised as legitimate by G
P has the power to enforce compliance through sanctions
counterexample: Consider a highly respected elder in a small village who is consulted on all important matters and whose advice is invariably followed. The elder issues no formal directives, possesses no power of enforcement, and could impose no sanctions upon anyone. Yet the villagers defer to her judgement on disputes, follow her guidance in communal decisions, and would feel it improper to act against her counsel. She exercises authority of the most genuine kind — moral and customary authority — without meeting conditions (i) or (iii).
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate authority exercised through moral influence, customary deference, or personal prestige rather than through formal directives and enforceable sanctions, thereby restricting authority to its coercive and institutional forms.
passage: One might characterise authority as a relation in which a person or body issues directives, is recognised as legitimate, and has the power to enforce compliance. This analysis captures the authority of the magistrate, the military commander, and the regulatory body. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is furnished by the phenomenon of moral authority as exercised in traditional communities. Consider a highly respected elder in a small village — a woman of great experience and good judgement who is consulted on all important matters. She issues no formal directives; she gives advice. She has no power of enforcement; she commands no police and can impose no fines. Yet her counsel is followed with remarkable consistency. The villagers defer to her in disputes, seek her guidance in communal decisions, and would feel it improper — indeed, something close to shameful — to act against her advice. She possesses authority in the fullest sense, yet her authority is moral and customary rather than coercive. The definition, by requiring directives and sanctions, captures only institutional authority and excludes the moral and customary forms. It appears that authority is a broader phenomenon than the definition allows, encompassing not only the power to command and punish but also the capacity to guide through respect, wisdom, and the weight of custom.

definition: A distribution of goods in a society is just if and only if (i) each person receives a share proportional to that person's contribution, (ii) the distribution is determined by a procedure to which all affected parties have consented, and (iii) no person's share falls below the minimum necessary for subsistence.
conditions: Each person receives a share proportional to contribution
The distribution is determined by a consented procedure
No person's share falls below subsistence
counterexample: Consider a society in which a procedure for distributing the harvest has been agreed upon by all members. Each person receives a share proportional to the labour contributed. No one falls below subsistence. All three conditions are met. Yet suppose a member of the society is born with a severe disability that prevents her from contributing any labour. Under the distribution, she receives nothing proportional, since her contribution is nil. The subsistence floor prevents her from starving, but she receives only the barest minimum while those who labour receive abundantly. One might question whether a distribution that consigns the disabled to bare subsistence while the able-bodied flourish is truly just.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for persons whose inability to contribute is involuntary — owing to disability, illness, or circumstance beyond their control — and whose receipt of mere subsistence rather than a fair share may constitute an injustice that contribution-based distribution cannot remedy.
passage: A natural account of distributive justice holds that a distribution is just when each person receives a share proportional to contribution, the distribution follows a procedure to which all have consented, and no one falls below subsistence. This captures the intuition that effort and productivity ought to be rewarded, that legitimate distributions require consent, and that no one should be left to perish. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is drawn from the circumstances of involuntary incapacity. Suppose a society has agreed upon a procedure for distributing the annual harvest in proportion to each member's labour. The procedure is consented to by all. No one falls below the subsistence threshold. All three conditions are met. Now suppose a member of the society is born with a severe physical disability that prevents her from contributing any labour whatever. Under the proportional rule, her share is nil — she has contributed nothing. The subsistence condition prevents outright starvation, but she receives only the bare minimum while those who labour enjoy abundance. One might observe that this distribution, though it satisfies every condition in the definition, strikes one as unjust. The disabled member's inability to contribute is not her fault; it is a misfortune that befell her without any choice or responsibility on her part. A definition that rewards contribution alone cannot address the claims of those whose incapacity is involuntary.

definition: A person P is free with respect to an action A if and only if (i) no external agent prevents P from performing A, (ii) no law or regulation prohibits P from performing A, and (iii) P is aware that A is available as an option.
conditions: No external agent prevents P from performing A
No law or regulation prohibits A
P is aware that A is available
counterexample: Consider a man living in a society with no laws against travel and no physical barriers at the borders. He is aware that emigration is an available option. No external agent prevents him from leaving. All three conditions are met. Yet the man is desperately poor — he cannot afford a ticket, he has no savings, he lacks the resources to sustain himself during the journey. He is free in the sense the definition specifies, but he is utterly unable to exercise that freedom. One might question whether a freedom that cannot be exercised for want of material means is freedom in any robust sense.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that P possess the material means or effective capacity to perform A, so that a person who is formally free but practically unable to act owing to poverty or deprivation is classified as free despite the practical emptiness of that freedom.
passage: One might propose that a person is free with respect to an action if no external agent prevents the action, no law prohibits it, and the person is aware it is available. This negative characterisation of freedom captures the absence of overt constraint and the absence of legal prohibition — the two most visible forms of unfreedom. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is provided by the phenomenon of poverty. Consider a man living in a society that places no legal restrictions upon travel and erects no physical barriers at its borders. He is aware that emigration is an option. No one physically prevents him from leaving. All three conditions are met. Yet the man is destitute. He cannot afford passage on a ship or a railway ticket. He has no savings and no resources with which to sustain himself during a journey. His freedom to emigrate is, in practical terms, entirely empty — he possesses it in name but cannot exercise it in fact. One might observe that the definition, by attending only to the absence of external prevention and legal prohibition, captures what might be called formal freedom while ignoring the material conditions that determine whether formal freedom can be translated into effective action. A more adequate account would need to acknowledge that freedom has a positive as well as a negative dimension.

definition: A government G is legitimate if and only if (i) G was established through a process that the governed population accepted, (ii) G governs in accordance with established laws, and (iii) G maintains the consent of the governed through regular elections.
conditions: G was established through an accepted process
G governs in accordance with established laws
G maintains consent through regular elections
counterexample: Consider a benevolent monarch who assumed the throne by hereditary succession — a process accepted by the population in accordance with ancient custom. The monarch governs in strict accordance with established laws and is widely regarded as just and wise. All of the first two conditions are satisfied. Yet the monarch holds no elections; consent is maintained not through the ballot but through custom, tradition, and the manifest quality of the ruler's governance. The third condition is not met, yet the population regards the government as entirely legitimate, and no serious movement for reform has arisen.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for forms of political legitimacy that rest upon custom, tradition, or the manifest justice of governance rather than upon elections, thereby excluding hereditary monarchies and traditional polities that are regarded as legitimate by their own populations.
passage: One might characterise governmental legitimacy as requiring an accepted founding process, governance in accordance with law, and the maintenance of consent through regular elections. This analysis fits the modern democratic state admirably: established by constitution, governed by law, and sustained by periodic elections in which the citizenry expresses its continued consent. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation is furnished by the institution of hereditary monarchy. Consider a kingdom whose monarch assumed the throne by hereditary succession — a process accepted by the population in accordance with a tradition stretching back many centuries. The monarch governs in strict accordance with the established laws of the realm and is widely regarded as just, wise, and devoted to the welfare of the people. The first two conditions are plainly met. But the third is not: no elections are held. Consent is maintained not through the ballot box but through the force of custom, the satisfaction of the governed, and a tradition of deference that the population regards as natural and proper. Yet this government is legitimate by any reasonable standard — the population accepts it, obeys its laws willingly, and shows no inclination to replace it. The definition, by requiring elections, imposes a specifically modern and democratic mechanism of consent upon a concept that admits of other forms.

definition: A person P has a moral obligation to perform an act A if and only if (i) P has voluntarily undertaken a commitment to perform A, (ii) the performance of A would benefit another person or the community, and (iii) P is capable of performing A.
conditions: P has voluntarily undertaken a commitment to perform A
Performance of A would benefit another or the community
P is capable of performing A
counterexample: Consider a bystander who comes upon a child drowning in a shallow pond. The bystander has made no commitment to rescue anyone. Yet the bystander is capable of performing the rescue, and performing it would benefit the child. Most would hold that the bystander has a moral obligation to act, despite having undertaken no voluntary commitment. The first condition is unmet, yet the obligation seems undeniable.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for moral obligations that arise from circumstances rather than from voluntary commitment — obligations of rescue, of aid to those in distress, and of basic human decency that are imposed by the situation itself rather than by any prior undertaking.
passage: One might propose that a person has a moral obligation to perform an act only if the person has voluntarily undertaken a commitment to perform it, the act would benefit others, and the person is capable of performing it. This analysis captures obligations arising from promises, contracts, and voluntary associations — the promiser must keep his word, the physician must treat her patients. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation is provided by the ethics of rescue. Suppose a man walking beside a shallow pond sees a small child fall in and begin to struggle. The child will drown without immediate assistance. The man has made no promise to rescue anyone. He has entered into no commitment. He does not know the child. Yet he is capable of wading in and lifting the child to safety at trivial cost to himself. Most persons would hold, with considerable conviction, that the man has a moral obligation to act. The source of this obligation is not voluntary commitment but the gravity of the situation — the immediacy of the danger, the ease of the rescue, and the helplessness of the child. The definition, by requiring voluntary commitment as a necessary condition, excludes obligations that arise from circumstance alone. A more adequate account would need to recognise that some obligations are imposed by the situation itself.

definition: A rule R is a social norm in a community C if and only if (i) the members of C regularly conform to R, (ii) the members of C expect one another to conform to R, and (iii) the members of C sanction deviations from R through expressions of disapproval.
conditions: Members regularly conform to R
Members expect conformity from one another
Members sanction deviations through disapproval
counterexample: Consider the norm against queue-jumping in a British town. The residents regularly form orderly queues, expect one another to do so, and express disapproval when someone pushes ahead. All three conditions are met. Now suppose a tourist, unfamiliar with the custom, walks directly to the front of a queue. The residents disapprove, but the tourist is unaware of the norm and does not regard herself as having violated any rule. Is the norm operative with respect to her? The definition says nothing about whether the norm applies to those who are ignorant of it, yet the residents plainly feel that it does.
