The definition characterises the norm from the perspective of those who share it but cannot specify its scope — whether it binds only insiders or also those who enter the community without awareness of its customs.
missing_condition: The definition fails to specify the conditions under which a social norm extends to newcomers and outsiders who have not participated in the practice of conformity, expectation, and sanction, leaving the scope of the norm indeterminate.
passage: One might define a social norm as a rule to which the members of a community regularly conform, which they expect one another to observe, and deviations from which they sanction through disapproval. This analysis captures the familiar norms of everyday social life — norms of politeness, dress, and orderly conduct. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when one considers the boundaries of the norm's applicability. Take the British custom of queuing. In a given town, the residents regularly form orderly queues, expect one another to do the same, and express disapproval — ranging from pointed looks to verbal reproach — when someone pushes ahead. All three conditions are satisfied. Now suppose a tourist, unfamiliar with the convention, walks directly to the front of a queue. The residents disapprove, and their disapproval is genuine. Yet the tourist is unaware of the rule and does not recognise herself as having violated anything. She has not participated in the practice of regular conformity. She has formed no expectation that others will queue. She is, in short, outside the circle of mutual conformity and expectation that the definition describes. Yet the residents feel, with some conviction, that the norm applies to her. The definition cannot resolve this question because it characterises the norm entirely in terms of the behaviour and attitudes of those who already participate in it, without specifying when the norm extends to those who do not.

definition: A person P has consented to an arrangement X if and only if (i) P has been informed of the nature and consequences of X, (ii) P has expressed agreement to X through a voluntary act, and (iii) P was free from coercion or undue pressure at the time of agreement.
conditions: P has been informed of the nature and consequences of X
P has expressed voluntary agreement to X
P was free from coercion or undue pressure
counterexample: Consider a sailor who signs a contract to serve aboard a merchant vessel. He has been informed of the terms. He signs voluntarily. No one coerces him. All three conditions are met. Yet the sailor signs because he is in a port town with no money, no employment, and no means of reaching home. The only alternative to signing is destitution. While no person coerces him, his circumstances leave him no genuine choice. One might question whether agreement reached under such duress of circumstance constitutes genuine consent.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that P possess a reasonable alternative to agreeing, so that consent extracted by the pressure of desperate circumstances — rather than by the coercion of another agent — may satisfy all three conditions while lacking the voluntariness that genuine consent requires.
passage: One might analyse consent as an informed, voluntary expression of agreement made free from coercion. This analysis captures the conditions that render agreements binding in both moral and legal contexts. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is drawn from the circumstances of economic desperation. Consider a sailor stranded in a foreign port, without money, employment, or means of return. A shipmaster offers him a contract to serve aboard a vessel bound for a distant colony on terms that are harsh but not unlawful. The sailor is informed of the terms. He signs the contract without physical compulsion. No one threatens him. All three conditions are met. Yet the sailor's alternative to signing is destitution — sleeping in doorways, begging for food, and eventually perishing from exposure. His agreement is voluntary in the sense that no human agent forces his hand, yet it is not voluntary in the richer sense that he had a genuine choice between acceptable alternatives. One might observe that the definition, by requiring only the absence of coercion by another agent, cannot distinguish consent freely given from consent wrung from a person by the pressure of desperate circumstances. A more adequate account would need to attend not only to the absence of external compulsion but also to the presence of reasonable alternatives.

definition: A person A trusts a person B with respect to a matter M if and only if (i) A believes that B will act competently and in good faith regarding M, (ii) A is willing to make themselves vulnerable to B's actions regarding M, and (iii) A's belief in B is based on evidence of B's past reliability.
conditions: A believes B will act competently and in good faith
A is willing to be vulnerable to B's actions
A's belief is based on evidence of past reliability
counterexample: Consider a patient who, upon arriving in a foreign country, is rushed to hospital in a medical emergency and must place herself in the care of a surgeon she has never met. She believes the surgeon will act competently — the hospital is reputable, the staff are professional. She makes herself vulnerable by submitting to surgery. But she has no evidence of this particular surgeon's past reliability; she has never seen him before and knows nothing of his record. The third condition is not met. Yet she trusts him — indeed, she has no choice but to trust him, and her trust is genuine.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate trust that is placed not on the basis of personal evidence of past reliability but on the basis of institutional guarantees, professional credentials, or the structure of a situation that warrants confidence in the absence of direct experience.
passage: One might propose that trust is a relation in which one person believes another will act competently and in good faith, is willing to be vulnerable to the other's actions, and bases this belief on evidence of the other's past reliability. This analysis captures trust as it develops between friends, colleagues, and long-standing associates — persons who have had the opportunity to observe one another's conduct and to form judgements accordingly. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises in circumstances where past evidence is unavailable. Suppose a traveller, taken ill in a foreign country, is brought to a hospital and informed that emergency surgery is required. She has never met the surgeon. She knows nothing of his personal record. She has no evidence of his past reliability beyond the fact that he is employed by what appears to be a reputable institution. Yet she trusts him — she consents to the surgery, places her life in his hands, and believes he will act competently. Her trust is genuine. The definition, by requiring evidence of past reliability, excludes this case. It appears that trust can be grounded not only in direct evidence of a particular person's track record but also in institutional assurances, professional credentials, and the structure of the situation. A more adequate account would need to accommodate these indirect grounds for trust.

definition: Two or more persons are engaged in cooperation if and only if (i) they act jointly towards a shared goal, (ii) each participant voluntarily contributes effort towards the achievement of that goal, and (iii) each participant expects to benefit from the joint activity.
conditions: They act jointly towards a shared goal
Each voluntarily contributes effort
Each expects to benefit from the joint activity
counterexample: Consider a group of villagers who work together to repair a bridge that has been damaged by a flood. Each villager contributes labour voluntarily. They act jointly towards the shared goal of restoring the bridge. All three conditions appear met. Yet suppose one of the villagers lives on the far side of the river and will not personally benefit from the bridge's repair, since he rarely crosses it; he joins the effort because he feels it would be wrong to stand by while others labour for the common good. He does not expect to benefit, yet his participation is cooperative in every sense. The third condition is not met, yet no one would deny that he is cooperating.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for cooperative participation motivated by a sense of duty, solidarity, or regard for others' welfare rather than by an expectation of personal benefit, thereby excluding altruistic contributions to joint endeavours from the category of cooperation.
passage: One might characterise cooperation as a relation among persons who act jointly towards a shared goal, each contributing effort voluntarily and each expecting to benefit. This analysis captures the collaborative enterprise in its typical form — business partnerships, team sports, and communal projects in which all participants stand to gain. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation is furnished by an instance of communal repair. Suppose a bridge in a village is damaged by a flood, and the villagers organise a joint effort to restore it. Each villager contributes labour willingly. They act towards the shared goal of rebuilding the bridge. Most of the villagers live on the near side and use the bridge daily; they expect to benefit directly. But one villager lives on the far side of the river and rarely crosses the bridge. He joins the work not because he expects to benefit but because he feels it would be shameful to stand idle while his neighbours labour. His contribution is as cooperative as anyone else's — he works alongside the others, follows the same plan, and shares the same purpose. Yet he does not expect personal benefit. The definition, by requiring that each participant expect to benefit, excludes his participation from the category of cooperation. A more adequate account would need to recognise that cooperation may be motivated by solidarity, duty, or regard for others' welfare, and not solely by the expectation of personal advantage.

definition: A person or body P possesses authority over a group G if and only if (i) P issues directives that G is expected to follow, (ii) P's position is recognised by G as conferring the right to issue such directives, and (iii) P's directives are generally obeyed by members of G.
conditions: P issues directives that G is expected to follow
P's position is recognised by G as conferring the right to issue such directives
P's directives are generally obeyed by members of G
counterexample: Consider a charlatan who, during a fire at a crowded theatre, takes the stage and issues calm, authoritative instructions for evacuation. The crowd, taking him for the theatre manager, recognises his apparent position as conferring the right to direct them. His directives are generally obeyed. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the charlatan possesses no genuine authority; his position is fraudulent, and the crowd's recognition is based on a misapprehension.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that P's position be legitimately held — that the recognition by G be based on accurate information about P's actual standing, rather than on deception or misunderstanding.
passage: One might characterise authority as follows: a person or body possesses authority over a group if that person issues directives the group is expected to follow, the group recognises the person's position as conferring the right to issue such directives, and the directives are generally obeyed. This proposal captures the elements of directiveness, recognition, and compliance that we associate with authority. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from an emergency situation. Suppose a fire breaks out at a crowded theatre. A man — in fact a charlatan with no connection to the theatre — calmly takes the stage and, in a commanding voice, issues detailed instructions for an orderly evacuation. The crowd, taking him for the theatre manager, recognises his apparent position as conferring the right to issue such directives. His instructions are clear and sensible, and they are generally obeyed. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Yet one might observe that the charlatan possesses no genuine authority. His position is fraudulent; the crowd's recognition is based on a misapprehension of his identity and standing. The definition, by requiring only that the group recognise the position as conferring the right to issue directives, without requiring that this recognition be based on accurate information, cannot distinguish genuine authority from the mere appearance of authority produced by imposture or deception.

definition: A person A has consented to an arrangement X if and only if (i) A has been informed of the essential features of X, (ii) A has expressed agreement to X either verbally or by conduct, and (iii) A was free from coercion at the time of expressing agreement.
conditions: A has been informed of the essential features of X
A has expressed agreement to X either verbally or by conduct
A was free from coercion at the time of expressing agreement
counterexample: Consider a tenant who signs a lease containing, buried in dense legal prose on page forty-seven, a clause permitting the landlord to enter the premises at any time without notice. The tenant has been informed of the essential features of the lease — or at least, the document was provided to him. He expressed agreement by signing. And he was free from coercion. All three conditions are met. Yet we should hesitate to say the tenant consented to unrestricted entry, for the clause was practically invisible amid the complexity of the document, and no reasonable person would have found it without legal training.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the information provided be presented in a manner that a reasonable person could be expected to understand and attend to — that is, it does not distinguish between the formal provision of information and its effective communication.
passage: One might define consent as follows: a person has consented to an arrangement if he has been informed of its essential features, has expressed agreement verbally or by conduct, and was free from coercion at the time. This characterisation captures the informational, expressive, and voluntariness conditions that we ordinarily associate with consent. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this proposal may be drawn from the practice of residential tenancy. Suppose a tenant is presented with a fifty-page lease drafted in dense legal prose. Buried on page forty-seven, in a sub-clause of a sub-clause, is a provision permitting the landlord to enter the premises at any time without prior notice. The tenant signs the lease. He has been informed of the essential features of the arrangement — the document containing them was placed before him, and he had the opportunity to read it. He expressed agreement by signing. And he was free from coercion; the landlord applied no threats or undue pressure. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that the tenant did not genuinely consent to unrestricted entry. The critical clause was buried in a manner that no reasonable person without legal training could be expected to discover. The definition, by requiring only that the person be informed without specifying that the information be presented in a manner that a reasonable person could be expected to understand and attend to, conflates the formal provision of information with its effective communication.

definition: An activity A engaged in jointly by persons P1 and P2 constitutes cooperation if and only if (i) P1 and P2 each contribute effort toward a shared outcome, (ii) P1 and P2 are aware of each other's contributions, and (iii) the shared outcome could not be achieved by either party acting alone.
conditions: P1 and P2 each contribute effort toward a shared outcome
P1 and P2 are aware of each other's contributions
The shared outcome could not be achieved by either party acting alone
counterexample: Consider two rival shopkeepers on the same street who each place attractive displays in their windows, drawing customers to the street who then visit both shops. Each contributes effort that results in a shared outcome — increased foot traffic. Each is aware of the other's contributions. And the outcome — a lively shopping street — could not be achieved by either alone. All three conditions are met. Yet the shopkeepers are not cooperating; they are competing, and the shared benefit is an unintended by-product of their rivalry.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that P1 and P2 intend the shared outcome as a joint goal — that they coordinate their efforts deliberately toward a common purpose, rather than each pursuing private ends that happen to produce a mutually beneficial result.
passage: One might characterise cooperation as a joint activity in which two parties each contribute effort toward a shared outcome, are aware of each other's contributions, and could not achieve the outcome alone. This proposal captures the contributory, awareness, and interdependence conditions that seem essential to cooperation. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the world of commerce. Consider two rival shopkeepers who occupy adjacent premises on the same high street. Each places attractive displays in his shop window, and each is aware that the other does the same. The combined effect of their efforts is to draw a great many customers to the street — more than either could attract alone. Each contributes effort toward a shared outcome. Each is aware of the other's contributions. And the outcome could not be achieved by either party acting alone. All three conditions appear to be met. Yet one might observe that the shopkeepers are not cooperating in any genuine sense. They are competing — each seeking to attract customers to his own shop at the expense of the other. The shared benefit of increased foot traffic is an unintended by-product of their rivalry, not a joint goal. The definition, by failing to require that the parties intend the shared outcome as a deliberate joint purpose, cannot distinguish genuine cooperation from competitive situations that happen to produce mutually beneficial side-effects.

definition: A set of practices S constitutes a culture if and only if (i) S is shared by a group of persons who regard themselves as a community, (ii) S is transmitted from one generation to the next, and (iii) S includes customs, rituals, or modes of expression that distinguish the group from other groups.
conditions: S is shared by a group of persons who regard themselves as a community
S is transmitted from one generation to the next
S includes customs, rituals, or modes of expression that distinguish the group from other groups
counterexample: Consider a regiment in the British Army with a distinctive set of mess customs, regimental songs, initiation rituals, and modes of address. These practices are shared by the members, who regard themselves as a community. They are transmitted from one generation of officers and soldiers to the next. And they include customs and rituals that distinguish the regiment from every other. All three conditions are met. Yet we should hesitate to call the regiment's practices a culture in the full sense; they are a set of institutional traditions maintained within a hierarchically imposed framework, lacking the organic, evolving character of a genuine culture.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the practices emerge organically from the shared life of the community, rather than being imposed or maintained by institutional authority — that they arise from the voluntary, creative activity of the group rather than from regulation and command.
passage: One might define a culture as a set of practices shared by a community, transmitted across generations, and including customs or rituals that distinguish the group from others. This proposal captures the communal, intergenerational, and distinctive features we associate with culture. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from military life. Consider a regiment of the British Army — say, a guards regiment with a history stretching back several centuries. The regiment has a distinctive set of mess customs, regimental songs, initiation ceremonies, modes of address, and traditions of dress. These practices are shared by the members of the regiment, who regard themselves as a community with a strong esprit de corps. They are transmitted from one generation of officers and soldiers to the next through training, mentorship, and social pressure. And they include customs and rituals that unmistakably distinguish the regiment from every other unit in the army. All three conditions appear to be met. Yet one might observe that a regiment's customs do not constitute a culture in the fullest sense. They are institutional traditions, maintained by military regulation and hierarchical authority rather than by the organic, creative activity of a community living freely together. The definition, by failing to require that the practices emerge organically from the shared life of the community, cannot distinguish a genuine culture from an imposed set of institutional conventions.

definition: A person P is free with respect to an action A if and only if (i) no external agent prevents P from performing A, (ii) no law or regulation prohibits P from performing A, and (iii) P possesses the physical and mental capacity to perform A.
conditions: No external agent prevents P from performing A
No law or regulation prohibits P from performing A
P possesses the physical and mental capacity to perform A
counterexample: Consider a person who has been raised from infancy in a religious community that has instilled in him an overwhelming terror of music. No external agent prevents him from attending a concert. No law prohibits it. He has the physical and mental capacity to walk to the concert hall and listen. All three conditions are met. Yet the man is not free to attend a concert in any meaningful sense, for his conditioning has rendered the act psychologically impossible for him — he cannot bring himself to do it, though nothing external stands in his way.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that P be free from internal psychological compulsions or conditioning so severe as to render the action effectively impossible — that is, it does not account for the way in which deeply ingrained fears, compulsions, or indoctrination can restrict freedom as effectively as external barriers.
passage: A familiar characterisation of freedom holds that a person is free with respect to an action if no external agent prevents him from performing it, no law prohibits it, and he possesses the physical and mental capacity to perform it. This proposal attends to external, legal, and capacity conditions, and it covers many paradigmatic cases of freedom and unfreedom. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the sphere of psychological conditioning. Consider a person who has been raised from earliest infancy in a severely restrictive religious community. Through years of intense conditioning, an overwhelming terror of music has been instilled in him — a terror so profound that the mere sound of a melody induces paralysing anxiety. No external agent now prevents him from attending a concert. No law or regulation prohibits it. He has the physical capacity to walk to the concert hall and the mental capacity, in the narrow sense, to understand what a concert is. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that the man is not free to attend a concert in any meaningful sense. His conditioning has rendered the act psychologically impossible for him. The definition, by requiring only the absence of external prevention, legal prohibition, and incapacity, cannot account for the way in which deeply ingrained psychological compulsions — the products of indoctrination or conditioning — may restrict freedom as effectively as any external barrier.

definition: A distribution D of goods among members of a group G is just if and only if (i) each member of G receives a share proportional to their or their contribution to the production of those goods, (ii) the distribution is carried out according to a publicly known rule, and (iii) no member of G is excluded from the distribution without cause.
conditions: Each member of G receives a share proportional to their or their contribution to the production of those goods
The distribution is carried out according to a publicly known rule
No member of G is excluded from the distribution without cause
counterexample: Consider a village in which the harvest is distributed according to a publicly known rule that assigns shares proportional to each villager's contribution to the planting and reaping. No one is excluded without cause. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose one villager is an elderly widow who can no longer work in the fields. By the rule, she receives nothing — her contribution is nil. The distribution is unjust, for it leaves a vulnerable member of the community destitute despite her decades of past contribution and her manifest need.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the distribution take account of the needs of members who are unable to contribute — that justice in distribution involves not only proportionality to contribution but also a minimum provision for those who, through no fault of their own, cannot participate in production.
passage: One might propose that a distribution of goods is just if each member receives a share proportional to his contribution, the distribution follows a publicly known rule, and no one is excluded without cause. This characterisation captures the meritocratic, procedural, and inclusionary features that we often associate with justice. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this proposal may be drawn from the life of a small agricultural community. Suppose a village distributes its harvest according to a publicly known rule that assigns each villager a share proportional to his or her contribution to the planting and reaping. No one is excluded without cause. An elderly widow, however, is no longer able to work in the fields. By the terms of the rule, her contribution is nil, and she receives nothing. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied: shares are proportional to contribution, the rule is publicly known, and no one is excluded without cause — for the widow is not excluded; she simply receives a share proportional to her zero contribution. Yet the distribution is plainly unjust, for it leaves a vulnerable member of the community destitute despite her decades of past service and her manifest need. The definition, by making proportionality to contribution the sole principle of allocation, cannot accommodate the claims of those who, through age, illness, or misfortune, are unable to contribute to production but whose needs justice surely requires us to recognise.

definition: A governing body B is legitimate if and only if (i) B was established through a process accepted by the governed population, (ii) B governs in accordance with a set of established rules or a constitution, and (iii) the governed population does not actively resist B's authority.
conditions: B was established through a process accepted by the governed population
B governs in accordance with a set of established rules or a constitution
The governed population does not actively resist B's authority
counterexample: Consider a colonial administration established in a territory where the indigenous population, exhausted by decades of military defeat, has ceased to resist. The administration was established through a process — military conquest followed by a treaty signed under duress — that the population accepted only in the sense of acquiescing. It governs according to a written constitution. And there is no active resistance. All three conditions are met. Yet we should hesitate to call the colonial government legitimate; the absence of resistance reflects not consent but resignation, and the process of establishment was one of coercion, not genuine acceptance.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish between genuine acceptance and mere acquiescence born of exhaustion or powerlessness — that is, it does not require that the process of establishment be one to which the governed population freely agreed, rather than one they ceased to oppose because resistance had become futile.
passage: One might characterise the legitimacy of a governing body as follows: a governing body is legitimate if it was established through a process accepted by the governed, governs according to established rules, and faces no active resistance from the population. This proposal captures the elements of procedural origin, rule-governance, and popular acquiescence that are often associated with legitimacy. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the history of colonialism. Consider an imperial power that, after decades of military campaigns, establishes an administration over a conquered territory. The indigenous population, exhausted by war and deprived of the means of further resistance, has ceased to oppose the administration. A treaty — signed by local leaders under conditions of extreme duress — may be pointed to as a process of acceptance. The administration governs according to a written constitution imposed by the imperial power. And no active resistance is offered. All three conditions appear to be satisfied. Yet one might observe that the colonial government is not legitimate in any morally serious sense. The acceptance was not genuine but coerced. The absence of resistance reflects not contentment but exhaustion. The definition, by failing to distinguish between genuine acceptance and mere acquiescence born of powerlessness, cannot separate legitimate governance from the quiet submission of a subjugated people.

definition: A regularity of behaviour R within a group G constitutes a social norm if and only if (i) the majority of members of G conform to R, (ii) members of G expect other members to conform to R, and (iii) deviation from R is met with disapproval by members of G.
conditions: The majority of members of G conform to R
Members of G expect other members to conform to R
Deviation from R is met with disapproval by members of G
counterexample: Consider a factory in which most workers take an unofficial fifteen-minute tea break at ten o'clock. The majority conform. Each worker expects the others to do the same. And a worker who conspicuously refuses to join the tea break is met with disapproval — she is thought to be currying favour with management. All three conditions are met. Yet the regularity is not a social norm in the fullest sense; it is a convention that arose by accident and persists through inertia and mild social pressure, lacking the prescriptive force and moral seriousness we associate with genuine norms.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that conformity to R be regarded by members of G as in some sense right or obligatory — that the expectation of conformity carry prescriptive force rooted in the values or moral commitments of the group, rather than reflecting mere habitual practice sustained by peer pressure.
passage: One might propose that a social norm is a regularity of behaviour to which the majority of a group conforms, which members expect one another to follow, and from which deviation is met with disapproval. This characterisation captures the behavioural, expectational, and sanctioning features commonly associated with norms. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this proposal may be drawn from the life of a factory floor. Suppose that in a particular factory, most workers take an unofficial fifteen-minute tea break at ten o'clock each morning. The majority conform to this practice. Each worker expects the others to observe it. And a worker who conspicuously refuses to join the tea break — preferring to continue working — is met with disapproval: she is suspected of currying favour with management, and her colleagues express mild hostility. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that this regularity is not a social norm in the fullest sense. It is a convention that arose by accident — someone once began making tea at ten, and others followed — and it persists through inertia and mild social pressure. It lacks the prescriptive force and moral weight that we associate with genuine norms, such as norms of honesty or fairness. The definition, by requiring only majority conformity, mutual expectation, and disapproval of deviation, cannot distinguish norms rooted in the values and moral commitments of a community from mere habitual practices sustained by peer pressure.

definition: A person P has an obligation to perform an action A if and only if (i) P has voluntarily entered into an arrangement that requires P to perform A, (ii) another person Q has a corresponding right to demand that P perform A, and (iii) failure to perform A would cause harm to Q.
conditions: P has voluntarily entered into an arrangement that requires P to perform A
Another person Q has a corresponding right to demand that P perform A
Failure to perform A would cause harm to Q
counterexample: Consider a passerby who comes upon a child drowning in a shallow pond. The passerby has not voluntarily entered into any arrangement with the child or the child's parents. No specific person has a corresponding right — in the strict sense of a right arising from a prior arrangement — to demand that the passerby rescue the child. Yet most people would say the passerby has an obligation to save the child if he can do so without danger to himself. All three conditions fail, yet the obligation appears to exist.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for obligations that arise not from voluntary arrangements but from the mere fact of being in a position to prevent serious harm — that is, it cannot accommodate duties of rescue or beneficence that are grounded in the moral significance of the situation rather than in a prior contractual or promissory relation.
passage: One might characterise obligation as follows: a person has an obligation to perform an action if he has voluntarily entered into an arrangement requiring it, another person has a corresponding right to demand it, and failure to perform it would cause harm. This proposal captures the contractual and rights-based features we often associate with obligation. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from ordinary moral experience. Suppose a man walking through a park comes upon a small child drowning in a shallow pond. The man has not entered into any arrangement — voluntary or otherwise — with the child or the child's parents. No specific person has a right, in the strict sense arising from a prior arrangement, to demand that the man wade into the pond. The child is a stranger. Yet most thoughtful persons would say without hesitation that the man has an obligation to rescue the child, provided he can do so without danger to himself. The obligation arises not from any contract, promise, or prior arrangement, but from the bare fact that a human being is in danger and the passerby is in a position to help. The definition, by grounding obligation exclusively in voluntary arrangements and corresponding rights, cannot accommodate obligations that arise from the moral significance of the circumstances alone — duties of rescue, beneficence, and common humanity that exist independently of any prior transaction between the parties.

definition: Person A trusts person B with respect to a matter M if and only if (i) A believes that B will act competently and in good faith regarding M, (ii) A is willing to make themselves vulnerable to B's actions regarding M, and (iii) A has evidence from past interactions that B is reliable regarding matters of the type M.
conditions: A believes that B will act competently and in good faith regarding M
A is willing to make themselves vulnerable to B's actions regarding M
A has evidence from past interactions that B is reliable regarding matters of the type M
counterexample: Consider a child who trusts her new schoolteacher on the first day of school. The child believes the teacher will act competently and in good faith. She is willing to make herself vulnerable — she follows the teacher's instructions, accepts the teacher's authority, and confides her worries. Yet the child has no evidence from past interactions that this teacher is reliable; she has never met the teacher before. All three conditions are not satisfied — the third fails. But the child plainly trusts the teacher; her trust is based on the general authority of the role, not on any history of interaction.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for trust that is grounded in the perceived role, office, or social position of the trusted party, rather than in a personal history of reliable interaction — it cannot accommodate the trust we extend to strangers who occupy positions of recognised responsibility.
passage: One might define trust by stipulating that a person trusts another with respect to some matter if she believes the other will act competently and in good faith, is willing to make herself vulnerable to the other's actions, and has evidence from past interactions that the other is reliable in such matters. This proposal captures the belief, vulnerability, and evidential conditions that often characterise trust between persons with an established relationship. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the experience of childhood. Consider a young child who arrives at a new school and is introduced to a teacher she has never met. The child believes the teacher will act competently and in good faith — she assumes, as children do, that teachers are benevolent and capable. She is willing to make herself vulnerable: she follows the teacher's instructions, accepts her authority, and confides her anxieties about the new school. Yet the child has no evidence whatsoever from past interactions that this particular teacher is reliable. She has never seen the teacher before today. The third condition fails. By the terms of the definition, the child does not trust the teacher. Yet she plainly does. Her trust is grounded not in personal experience but in the perceived authority of the role — the social institution of teaching, the assumption that persons in positions of responsibility will discharge their duties faithfully. The definition, by requiring evidence from past interactions, cannot accommodate the trust we routinely extend to strangers who occupy positions of recognised responsibility.

definition: A group of persons G constitutes a community if and only if (i) the members of G share a geographical location, (ii) the members interact regularly with one another, and (iii) the members recognise a common identity or sense of belonging.
conditions: The members of G share a geographical location
The members interact regularly with one another
The members recognise a common identity or sense of belonging
counterexample: Consider a diaspora community — say, a community of emigrants from a particular region who are scattered across several continents. They do not share a geographical location; they may be separated by thousands of miles. Yet they interact regularly through correspondence, periodic gatherings, and shared cultural practices. They recognise a powerful common identity rooted in shared origin, language, and tradition. The first condition is violated, yet the group is a community in the fullest sociological sense.
missing_condition: The definition's geographical requirement fails to accommodate communities constituted by shared identity, culture, or purpose rather than by physical proximity — it confuses one historically common vehicle of community with a necessary condition of community itself.
passage: One might propose that a community is a geographically co-located group whose members interact regularly and share a common identity. This characterisation captures the village, the urban neighbourhood, and the small town with considerable accuracy. Yet a case which tells against the geographical condition may be drawn from the phenomenon of diaspora. Consider a community of emigrants from a particular region — say, a group dispersed across several continents by the circumstances of history. The members do not share a geographical location; they may be separated by thousands of miles of ocean. Yet they interact regularly through correspondence, periodic gatherings, the circulation of newspapers and periodicals, and the maintenance of shared cultural practices — religious observances, culinary traditions, linguistic habits. They recognise a powerful common identity rooted in their shared origin, their shared language, and their shared experience of displacement. The first condition is plainly violated, yet the group is a community in the fullest and most robust sociological sense. It appears that the definition, by requiring geographical co-location, confuses one historically common vehicle of community formation with a necessary condition of community itself. Communities, one might observe, can be constituted by shared identity, culture, or purpose as readily as by physical proximity.

definition: A practice P constitutes a tradition if and only if (i) P has been transmitted across at least three generations, (ii) P is valued by its practitioners for its antiquity, and (iii) P remains substantially unchanged in form from one generation to the next.
conditions: P has been transmitted across at least three generations
P is valued by its practitioners for its antiquity
P remains substantially unchanged in form
counterexample: Consider the tradition of jazz improvisation in New Orleans. Jazz has been practised for several generations, and its practitioners value the continuity with earlier masters. But its form has changed substantially from generation to generation — from Dixieland to swing to bebop to free jazz. Each generation transforms the tradition whilst maintaining continuity with what came before. The third condition is violated, yet jazz is unquestionably a tradition — indeed, a tradition whose vitality derives precisely from its capacity for transformation.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that living traditions change, adapt, and develop across generations, and that what constitutes continuity is not unchanging form but a recognisable thread of practice, value, and reference to predecessors that persists through transformation.
passage: One might define a tradition as a practice transmitted across at least three generations, valued for its antiquity, and remaining substantially unchanged in form. This characterisation captures certain paradigmatic cases — the unchanged liturgy of a religious community, the inherited recipes of a family, the perpetuated customs of a village festival. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of the immutability requirement may be drawn from the tradition of jazz. Jazz has been practised in New Orleans and beyond for several generations. Its practitioners value the continuity with earlier masters — they study the recordings of Armstrong, Ellington, Parker, and Coltrane with the devotion that classical musicians bring to the study of Bach. Yet the form of jazz has changed substantially from generation to generation: from Dixieland to swing, from swing to bebop, from bebop to free jazz, and through countless further transformations. Each generation inherits, reinterprets, and extends the tradition. The third condition is plainly violated. Yet jazz is unquestionably a tradition — indeed, a tradition whose vitality derives precisely from its capacity for creative transformation. It appears that the definition, by requiring substantial unchanged form, mistakes preservation for continuity and cannot accommodate the living traditions whose identity persists through ongoing development.

definition: A behavioural pattern B is a custom if and only if (i) B is widely practised within a community, (ii) B is transmitted through imitation or instruction rather than through formal legislation, and (iii) deviation from B incurs social disapproval.
conditions: B is widely practised within a community
B is transmitted through imitation or instruction
Deviation from B incurs social disapproval
counterexample: Consider the custom of tipping waiters in restaurants. In many communities this is widely practised and learned through imitation. Yet in a community where tipping is customary, a person who fails to tip may suffer no social disapproval at all — the waiter may be displeased, but fellow diners may not even notice. The third condition is violated in many contexts, yet tipping remains a custom.
missing_condition: The definition makes social disapproval a necessary feature, but many customs persist through habit, convention, and a generalised sense of propriety rather than through any mechanism of active censure — the definition confuses the enforcement mechanism of some customs with a necessary condition of customariness itself.
passage: One might define a custom as a widely practised behavioural pattern transmitted through imitation or instruction and enforced by social disapproval. This characterisation captures many familiar customs — the removal of hats indoors, the shaking of hands upon meeting, the observance of mourning periods. Yet a case which tells against the requirement of social disapproval may be drawn from the practice of tipping. In many communities, the tipping of waiters in restaurants is customary: it is widely practised, learned through observation and parental instruction, and performed as a matter of course. But in many of these same communities, a person who fails to tip may suffer no active social disapproval from fellow diners. The waiter may be silently displeased; other diners may be unaware of the omission. The third condition is violated, or at best doubtfully satisfied, yet tipping remains a custom. It appears that many customs persist not through any mechanism of active censure but through habit, convention, and a generalised sense of what is appropriate. The definition, by making social disapproval a necessary condition, confuses the enforcement mechanism of some customs with a constitutive feature of customariness itself. A custom, one might observe, is a pattern that persists because it is done rather than because failure to do it is punished.

definition: A social arrangement S constitutes an institution if and only if (i) S involves a set of rules or norms governing the behaviour of participants, (ii) S serves a recognised social function, and (iii) S is formally established by an act of legislation or founding.
conditions: S involves a set of rules or norms governing behaviour
S serves a recognised social function
S is formally established by an act of legislation or founding
counterexample: Consider the institution of marriage as it exists in many pre-literate societies. It involves norms governing the conduct of spouses, serves the recognised social functions of regulating kinship and child-rearing, and is treated by the community as an institution of the first importance. Yet it was never formally established by an act of legislation or founding — it emerged gradually through custom and practice over generations. The third condition is violated, yet marriage in such societies is as much an institution as any parliament or university.
missing_condition: The definition restricts institutions to those formally established by deliberate act, failing to recognise that many of the most fundamental social institutions — language, marriage, property, customary law — arise spontaneously through the accumulated practices of communities without any formal act of creation.
passage: One might define an institution as a social arrangement involving rules or norms, serving a social function, and formally established by legislation or founding. This characterisation captures the parliament, the university, the central bank, and the established church. Yet a case which tells against the requirement of formal establishment may be drawn from the comparative study of societies. Consider the institution of marriage as it exists in many pre-literate societies. It involves a complex set of norms governing the conduct of spouses — duties of fidelity, obligations of mutual support, rules of inheritance. It serves recognised social functions — the regulation of kinship, the organisation of child-rearing, the structuring of economic cooperation. The community treats it as an institution of the first importance. Yet it was never formally established by an act of legislation or founding. It emerged gradually, over generations, through custom and practice. No legislator decreed it; no founding document authorised it. The third condition is violated, yet marriage in such societies is as much an institution as any parliament. It appears that the definition, by requiring formal establishment, captures only those institutions that are the products of deliberate design and excludes the vast class of institutions that arise spontaneously through accumulated social practice.

definition: A rule R is a social norm if and only if (i) R prescribes or proscribes a type of behaviour, (ii) R is followed by a majority of the members of the relevant group, and (iii) violation of R is met with punishment or sanction.
conditions: R prescribes or proscribes a type of behaviour
R is followed by a majority of the relevant group
Violation of R is met with punishment or sanction
counterexample: Consider the norm of queuing — waiting one's turn in an orderly line. In many societies this norm prescribes a type of behaviour and is followed by the majority. Yet violation is rarely met with punishment or formal sanction. A person who pushes to the front of a queue may receive a disapproving glance or a muttered comment, but there is no punishment in any robust sense. The third condition is at best weakly satisfied, yet queuing is a genuine social norm that structures behaviour extensively.
missing_condition: The definition overstates the role of sanctions in sustaining norms, failing to recognise that many norms are maintained by internalised habits, mutual expectations, and a generalised sense of propriety rather than by any systematic practice of punishment.
passage: One might define a social norm as a rule prescribing or proscribing behaviour, followed by a majority of the group, and enforced by punishment or sanction. This characterisation captures many norms effectively — the prohibition against theft, the expectation of honesty in contracts, the requirement of deference to elders in certain cultures. Yet a case which tells against the sanction requirement may be drawn from the norm of queuing. In many societies, the practice of waiting one's turn in an orderly line is a well-established social norm. It prescribes a type of behaviour — taking one's place at the back of the queue and advancing in order. It is followed by the great majority of the relevant population. But violation is rarely met with punishment in any robust sense. A person who pushes to the front of a queue may receive a disapproving glance, a quiet tut, or an irritated remark; but there is no formal sanction, no fine, and typically no confrontation. The third condition is at best weakly satisfied. Yet queuing is a genuine and powerful social norm — one whose violation, whilst rarely punished, is universally noticed and produces a sense of affront. It appears that many norms are sustained not by punishment but by internalised habit, mutual expectation, and a diffuse sense of what is appropriate.

definition: An interaction I between agents A₁ and A₂ constitutes cooperation if and only if (i) A₁ and A₂ share a common goal, (ii) A₁ and A₂ coordinate their actions to achieve that goal, and (iii) both A₁ and A₂ benefit from the achievement of the goal.
conditions: A₁ and A₂ share a common goal
A₁ and A₂ coordinate their actions to achieve that goal
Both A₁ and A₂ benefit from the achievement of the goal
counterexample: Consider two rival merchants who both wish to prevent a bridge from collapsing because each uses it to transport goods to market. They coordinate their actions — one provides timber, the other provides labour — to repair the bridge. They share the goal of a functioning bridge and both benefit. All three conditions are met. Yet each acts solely from self-interest, and each would gladly see the other's business fail. Their interaction is cooperative in outcome but not in spirit — it is a convergence of self-interested strategies rather than genuine cooperation.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish between mere strategic convergence — where self-interested agents happen to coordinate towards a shared outcome — and genuine cooperation, which involves a willingness to subordinate individual advantage to the joint enterprise and a disposition to maintain the cooperative relation even when defection would be individually profitable.
passage: One might define cooperation as an interaction in which agents share a goal, coordinate their actions, and mutually benefit. This analysis captures many paradigmatic cases of cooperation: the barn-raising, the joint research project, the military alliance. Yet a case which tells against it may be constructed from the world of commerce. Consider two rival merchants, each of whom uses a certain bridge to transport goods to market. The bridge is in danger of collapse. Both merchants wish to prevent this — not from any mutual goodwill, but because each depends upon the bridge for his livelihood. They coordinate their actions: one provides timber, the other provides labour. The bridge is repaired. Both benefit. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the interaction may not constitute cooperation in any robust sense. Each merchant acts solely from self-interest; each would gladly see the other's business fail; neither would have contributed to the repair had it not served his own advantage. The interaction is a convergence of self-interested strategies, not a joint endeavour in which the parties subordinate individual advantage to a shared purpose. It appears that the definition cannot distinguish between strategic convergence and genuine cooperation — between agents who happen to coordinate and agents who cooperate.

definition: An agent A trusts an agent B if and only if (i) A has a positive expectation that B will perform a specified action, (ii) A is willing to make themselves vulnerable to B's non-performance, and (iii) A's expectation is based upon evidence of B's past reliability.
conditions: A has a positive expectation that B will perform a specified action
A is willing to make themselves vulnerable to B's non-performance
A's expectation is based upon evidence of B's past reliability
counterexample: Consider a young child who trusts her parent completely — relying upon the parent for food, safety, and comfort. The child has a positive expectation that the parent will provide care and is wholly vulnerable to the parent's failure to do so. Yet the child's trust is not based upon evidence of past reliability in any reflective sense; the infant trusts before she has accumulated evidence, before she can evaluate reliability, and indeed before she has any concept of evidence at all. The third condition is violated, yet the child's trust is as genuine as any.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate basic or non-evidential trust — the trust of the child, the trust of the stranger who relies upon the good faith of a community — which arises not from assessed evidence of past reliability but from a more fundamental disposition to rely upon others.
passage: One might define trust as a positive expectation of another's performance, a willingness to be vulnerable to non-performance, and an evidence-based assessment of past reliability. This analysis captures the trust of the experienced merchant, the seasoned employer, or the long-standing friend — cases in which trust has been earned through repeated demonstration of reliability. Yet a case which tells against the evidential requirement may be drawn from the earliest human relationships. Consider a young child who trusts her parent completely. She relies upon the parent for food, warmth, safety, and comfort, and she is wholly vulnerable to the parent's failure to provide these things. She has a positive expectation that care will be forthcoming. Yet her trust is not based upon evidence of past reliability in any reflective or evaluative sense. The infant trusts before she has accumulated evidence, before she can assess reliability, and before she possesses any concept of evidence at all. Her trust is immediate, pre-reflective, and unconditional. The third condition is violated, yet the child's trust is as genuine and as profound as any trust known to human experience. It appears that the definition, by requiring an evidential basis, captures only a sophisticated species of trust and fails to accommodate the more fundamental variety — basic trust — upon which, one might argue, all subsequent trust depends.

definition: A person P is alienated from an activity A if and only if (i) P engages in A involuntarily or under compulsion, (ii) P derives no satisfaction from A, and (iii) the product of A is appropriated by someone other than P.
conditions: P engages in A involuntarily or under compulsion
P derives no satisfaction from A
The product of A is appropriated by someone other than P
counterexample: Consider a wealthy artist who paints voluntarily, derives considerable satisfaction from the creative process, and retains ownership of every canvas. Yet she paints exclusively for a market she despises, producing works she regards as artistically worthless, in a style dictated by commercial demand rather than personal vision. She is, by her own account, profoundly alienated from her work. All three conditions of the definition are violated, yet her alienation is real and deeply felt.
missing_condition: The definition restricts alienation to conditions of external compulsion and expropriation, failing to accommodate forms of alienation that arise from the subjective experience of meaninglessness, inauthenticity, or disconnection from the deeper purposes of one's activity, even when the activity is voluntary and its products are retained.
passage: One might define alienation as the condition of engaging in an activity involuntarily, deriving no satisfaction from it, and having its product appropriated by another. This characterisation captures the paradigmatic case of the factory worker compelled by economic necessity to perform repetitive labour whose products are claimed by the factory owner. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from a different social position. Consider a wealthy artist who paints entirely voluntarily — no one compels her to pick up a brush. She derives considerable satisfaction from the physical act of painting, from the mastery of technique and the play of colour. And she retains ownership of every canvas, selling only when and to whom she pleases. All three conditions are violated. Yet she paints exclusively for a commercial market she despises, producing works she regards as artistically worthless, in a style dictated by the tastes of collectors rather than by her own creative vision. She experiences her activity as a betrayal of her artistic identity. She is, by her own account, profoundly alienated from her work. It appears that the definition, by focusing on external compulsion and appropriation, cannot accommodate the forms of alienation that arise from meaninglessness, inauthenticity, and disconnection from one's deeper purposes, even when the activity is voluntary and its products are one's own.

definition: A group G possesses a distinct culture if and only if (i) the members of G share a common language, (ii) G has distinctive artistic, religious, and culinary practices, and (iii) these shared features are transmitted from one generation to the next within G.
conditions: The members of G share a common language
G has distinctive artistic, religious, and culinary practices
These shared features are transmitted from one generation to the next
counterexample: Consider the scientific community of the early twentieth century. Its members did not share a single common language — they communicated in English, French, German, and other tongues. Their artistic, religious, and culinary practices were diverse. Yet they shared a distinctive culture: a set of norms governing inquiry, standards of evidence, modes of publication and peer review, and a shared ethos of disinterested pursuit of truth, transmitted from established researchers to apprentices through a recognisable process of training. The first two conditions are violated, yet the group possesses a culture.
missing_condition: The definition reduces culture to a particular set of shared features — language, art, religion, cuisine — and thereby fails to accommodate cultures constituted by shared norms, practices, values, and modes of inquiry that do not conform to the traditional anthropological checklist.
passage: One might define a distinct culture as a group whose members share a common language, distinctive artistic, religious, and culinary practices, and transmit these features across generations. This characterisation captures the culture of a traditional village or an ethnic group with considerable accuracy. Yet a case which tells against this definition may be drawn from the culture of professional communities. Consider the international scientific community of the early twentieth century. Its members did not share a single common language; they communicated in English, French, German, Russian, and other tongues. Their artistic practices were diverse — some were accomplished musicians, others indifferent to the arts. Their religious commitments ranged from devout faith to principled atheism. Their culinary habits reflected their national origins rather than any shared scientific cuisine. The first two conditions are plainly violated. Yet the scientific community possessed a distinctive culture: shared norms of inquiry, shared standards of evidence, shared modes of publication and peer critique, a shared ethos of disinterested investigation, and a shared process of training by which these features were transmitted from established researchers to apprentices. It appears that the definition, by identifying culture with a particular checklist of shared features, cannot accommodate cultures constituted by shared norms, values, and practices of a different kind.

definition: A political arrangement P is legitimate by virtue of a social contract if and only if (i) the members of the society have explicitly agreed to P, (ii) the agreement was entered into freely by all parties, and (iii) P serves the common interest of the contracting parties.
conditions: The members of the society have explicitly agreed to P
The agreement was entered into freely by all parties
P serves the common interest of the contracting parties
counterexample: Consider any modern citizen born into an existing state. She has never explicitly agreed to the political arrangement under which she lives; she was born into it. She did not enter freely into any contract — she had no choice in the matter. Yet if the state is just and well-governed, it may serve the common interest. The first and second conditions are violated, yet many would hold that the political arrangement is legitimate. The social contract, as actually invoked in political argument, functions as a hypothetical justification — what rational agents would agree to — rather than as a historical agreement that was actually made.
missing_condition: The definition treats the social contract as a historical event requiring explicit, free agreement, failing to recognise that the social contract as employed in political philosophy is typically a hypothetical construct — a device for testing the legitimacy of political arrangements against what rational agents could or would accept, rather than a real agreement actually entered into.
passage: One might characterise the legitimacy conferred by a social contract as requiring explicit agreement by all members of society, freely entered into, and serving the common interest. This analysis takes the social contract at its most literal: a real agreement, actually made, by identifiable parties. Yet a case which tells against this literal reading may be drawn from the condition of any modern citizen. Consider a person born into an existing state — say, a child born in London in 1930. She has never explicitly agreed to the political arrangement under which she lives. She did not enter freely into any contract; she was born into a pre-existing system of laws and institutions that she had no part in creating. The first and second conditions are violated. Yet if the state is just, its laws reasonable, and its institutions devoted to the common good, many would hold that the political arrangement is legitimate. It appears that the definition, by requiring explicit and free agreement, mischaracterises the social contract as a historical event. The social contract, as employed by political philosophers, typically functions as a hypothetical device — a test of legitimacy based upon what rational agents would agree to under specified conditions, rather than a real agreement that was actually entered into by identifiable persons at an identifiable moment.

definition: A group of persons G constitutes a community if and only if (i) the members of G share a common geographical territory, (ii) the members of G interact with one another on a regular basis, and (iii) the members of G share a set of norms or values.
conditions: The members of G share a common geographical territory
The members of G interact with one another on a regular basis
The members of G share a set of norms or values
counterexample: Consider the members of a dispersed religious diaspora — say, a group of adherents to a particular faith scattered across a dozen countries who have never met one another but who share a rich set of religious norms, values, and practices, and who interact regularly through correspondence, shared liturgical texts, and mutual aid sent across great distances. They interact regularly and share norms, but they do not share a common geographical territory. The first condition is not met. Yet it would be difficult to deny that they constitute a community.
passage: It is commonly supposed that a community is a group of persons who share a territory, interact regularly, and hold a set of norms or values in common. This analysis is attractive in its simplicity and appears to capture many paradigmatic cases: the village, the town, the neighbourhood — all are communities in which geographical proximity, regular interaction, and shared values are manifestly present. Yet a case which tells against the sufficiency of this analysis may be constructed by considering groups whose communal bonds transcend territorial boundaries. Suppose there exists a dispersed religious diaspora — a body of adherents to a particular faith scattered across a dozen countries, many of whom have never met face to face. These individuals share a rich and articulate set of religious norms, values, and practices. They interact regularly through correspondence, through the circulation of liturgical texts, and through the provision of mutual aid sent across vast distances. They recognise one another as members of a common fellowship, celebrate the same festivals, observe the same dietary laws, and mourn the same losses. The second and third conditions of the proposed definition are met: there is regular interaction and a shared normative framework. But the first condition — the sharing of a common geographical territory — is not satisfied, for the members are dispersed across the globe. Yet it would be exceedingly difficult to deny that this group constitutes a community. The bonds that unite its members are as strong as, and in many cases stronger than, those that unite the residents of a single street. The definition, by insisting upon shared territory, mistakes a common feature of local communities for a necessary condition of community as such.

definition: A practice P constitutes a tradition if and only if (i) P has been transmitted from one generation to the next, (ii) P has been practised without interruption for a significant period, and (iii) the practitioners of P regard it as inherited from the past.
conditions: P has been transmitted from one generation to the next
P has been practised without interruption for a significant period
The practitioners of P regard it as inherited from the past
counterexample: Consider a ceremony that was invented wholesale in the nineteenth century by a group of nationalists who deliberately fabricated a historical pedigree for it, claiming it had been practised for centuries. The ceremony has now been transmitted across several generations; it has been practised continuously since its invention; and its practitioners sincerely regard it as a custom inherited from their distant ancestors. All three conditions are met. Yet the ceremony is, in fact, an invention of recent origin, and its supposed antiquity is a fiction. One might question whether something founded upon a deliberate falsehood about its own history can properly be called a tradition.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the claimed historical transmission be genuine — that the practice actually originate in the past from which it purports to descend, rather than being a modern fabrication presented under a false genealogy.
passage: One might analyse the concept of a tradition by stipulating that a practice is a tradition when it has been transmitted from generation to generation, practised without interruption for a significant period, and regarded by its practitioners as inherited from the past. This analysis captures a wide range of familiar traditions: the harvest festival, the annual pilgrimage, the recitation of ancient prayers. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the well-documented phenomenon of invented traditions. Consider a ceremony that was fabricated in its entirety during the nineteenth century by a group of nationalists who, seeking to bolster a sense of national identity, deliberately created a ritual and furnished it with a fictitious historical pedigree, claiming it had been observed for many centuries. The ceremony has now been transmitted from its inventors through several subsequent generations. It has been practised without interruption since its creation. And its current practitioners sincerely regard it as a venerable custom inherited from their distant ancestors. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Yet the ceremony is a modern invention, and its purported antiquity is a deliberate fabrication. One might observe that a definition which classifies manufactured rituals resting upon historical falsehoods as genuine traditions fails to capture what is distinctive about tradition — namely, an authentic connexion to the past from which the practice claims to descend. The definition, by requiring only that the practitioners regard the practice as inherited, without demanding that this regard be well-founded, admits as traditions what are in truth elaborate impostures.

definition: A social arrangement I is an institution if and only if (i) I consists of a system of rules that govern the behaviour of a group, (ii) I is recognised as authoritative by the members of the group, and (iii) I persists beyond the tenure of any particular individual within it.
conditions: I consists of a system of rules that govern the behaviour of a group
I is recognised as authoritative by the members of the group
I persists beyond the tenure of any particular individual within it
counterexample: Consider an organised criminal syndicate that has operated in a particular city for several decades. It has a detailed system of rules governing the conduct of its members — rules about territory, tribute, hierarchy, and punishment. Its members recognise these rules as authoritative and submit to them on pain of severe penalty. And the syndicate persists beyond the tenure of any individual member; leaders are replaced, foot-soldiers retire or perish, and the organisation endures. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet we might hesitate to call a criminal syndicate an institution in the sense intended by social theorists, for it lacks the legitimacy and public accountability that ordinarily accompany the concept.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the social arrangement possess some form of legitimacy or normative standing within the broader society — that it be recognised not merely by its own members but as a proper part of the social order by the community in which it operates.
passage: A familiar analysis of the concept of a social institution holds that an institution is a system of rules governing the behaviour of a group, recognised as authoritative by the members of that group, and persisting beyond the tenure of any particular individual. This characterisation captures many paradigmatic institutions — the legislature, the court, the university, the church — each of which consists of rules, commands the allegiance of its members, and endures across generations. Yet a case which tells against the sufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the less reputable precincts of social life. Consider an organised criminal syndicate that has operated in a particular city for several decades. The syndicate possesses a detailed system of rules governing the conduct of its members: rules about territorial boundaries, the payment of tribute, the hierarchy of authority, and the punishment of disloyalty. Its members recognise these rules as authoritative — indeed, they submit to them with a discipline that many legitimate organisations might envy. And the syndicate persists beyond the tenure of any particular individual; its leaders are succeeded by new leaders, its members are replaced, and the organisation itself endures. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Yet one might observe that a criminal syndicate is not an institution in the sense that a parliament or a university is. It lacks the legitimacy, the public accountability, and the normative standing within the broader social order that we ordinarily associate with institutions. The definition, by omitting any requirement of legitimacy, admits organisations whose authority rests upon fear and coercion rather than upon any recognised normative foundation.

definition: A rule R is a social norm if and only if (i) R prescribes or prohibits a type of behaviour within a group, (ii) the members of the group generally conform to R, and (iii) deviation from R is met with social disapproval or sanction.
conditions: R prescribes or prohibits a type of behaviour within a group
The members of the group generally conform to R
Deviation from R is met with social disapproval or sanction
counterexample: Consider a society in which there is a widely acknowledged rule that one ought not to accept bribes. The rule prescribes behaviour — the refusal of bribes. Yet suppose that in this society bribery is in fact pervasive: virtually every official accepts bribes, and those who refuse are not praised but rather regarded with suspicion and treated as uncooperative. The rule exists; its violation is nominally disapproved of in public discourse; but actual conformity is rare and actual deviance is met not with sanction but with social acceptance. The second condition is not satisfied in practice, yet many theorists would still regard the anti-bribery rule as a genuine, if widely flouted, social norm.
passage: It has been proposed that a social norm is a rule that prescribes or prohibits a type of behaviour within a group, to which the members of the group generally conform, and whose violation is met with disapproval or sanction. This characterisation captures many familiar cases — the norm of queuing in an orderly fashion, the norm of greeting one's acquaintances, the norm of dressing appropriately for formal occasions. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed from the observation of societies in which norms and practice diverge sharply. Consider a society in which there is a widely acknowledged rule that one ought not to accept bribes. The rule prescribes a type of behaviour — the refusal of corrupt payments. It is publicly endorsed, taught to children, and invoked in official discourse. Yet suppose that in practice bribery is pervasive: nearly every official accepts bribes, and those rare individuals who refuse are regarded not with admiration but with suspicion, treated as uncooperative, and effectively excluded from the networks of patronage upon which careers depend. The second condition of the proposed definition — general conformity — is not met; indeed, what is general is non-conformity. Yet many theorists would hesitate to deny that the anti-bribery rule is a genuine social norm. It retains its prescriptive force in public discourse; it is the standard against which behaviour is officially judged, even if that judgement is rarely enforced. The definition, by requiring general conformity, conflates the existence of a norm with its effective observance and thereby fails to account for the important class of norms that are widely acknowledged yet widely violated.

definition: An interaction between agents A and B constitutes cooperation if and only if (i) A and B each perform an action directed toward a shared goal, (ii) A and B are aware of each other's contribution, and (iii) A and B voluntarily choose to participate.
conditions: A and B each perform an action directed toward a shared goal
A and B are aware of each other's contribution
A and B voluntarily choose to participate
counterexample: Consider two prisoners in adjacent cells who are each digging an escape tunnel. By coincidence, their tunnels converge and meet, creating a single viable escape route. Each prisoner has performed an action directed toward a shared goal — escape — and each becomes aware of the other's contribution once the tunnels meet. Each chose to dig voluntarily. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the prisoners did not cooperate; they acted independently, each pursuing his own plan, and the convergence of their efforts was entirely accidental. The resulting escape route is a product of coincidence, not of cooperative action.
