missing_condition: The definition fails to require that A and B coordinate their actions in light of each other's contribution — that each adjust their behaviour to complement the other's, rather than merely pursuing an independently conceived plan that happens to converge on the same outcome.
passage: One might define cooperation as an interaction in which two or more agents each perform an action directed toward a shared goal, each being aware of the other's contribution, with both participating voluntarily. This analysis has the merit of identifying the core features of cooperative activity: the shared objective, the mutual awareness, and the element of choice. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be constructed from a simple scenario of convergent but independent action. Suppose two prisoners are confined in adjacent cells, and each, unknown to the other, begins to dig an escape tunnel. By coincidence, their tunnels converge and meet, creating a single usable escape route. Each prisoner has performed an action directed toward the shared goal of escape. Upon the tunnels meeting, each becomes aware of the other's contribution. And each chose to dig voluntarily, without compulsion. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Yet it would be very odd indeed to say that the prisoners cooperated. Each acted entirely on his own initiative, pursuing his own independently conceived plan, with no thought of coordinating his efforts with those of another. The convergence of their tunnels is a matter of pure coincidence, not of cooperative design. The definition, by failing to require that the agents coordinate their actions — that each adjust his contribution in the light of what the other is doing — admits as cooperation what is in fact nothing more than the accidental alignment of independent endeavours.

definition: Agent A trusts agent B if and only if (i) A holds a positive expectation that B will act in a manner favourable to A's interests, (ii) A is willing to make themselves vulnerable to B's actions, and (iii) A's expectation is based on past experience with B.
conditions: A holds a positive expectation that B will act favourably toward A's interests
A is willing to make themselves vulnerable to B's actions
A's expectation is based on past experience with B
counterexample: Consider a traveller who, arriving in a foreign city for the first time, entrusts her luggage to a hotel porter she has never met and about whom she has no past experience whatsoever. She holds a positive expectation that the porter will deliver her luggage safely to her room. She makes herself vulnerable by surrendering her belongings. Yet her expectation is based not on any past experience with this porter but on general social conventions, the reputation of the hotel, and the visible uniform of the porter. All three conditions are not jointly met, since the third requires past experience with B specifically. Yet the traveller plainly trusts the porter.
passage: A common account of trust holds that one agent trusts another when the first holds a positive expectation that the second will act favourably, makes herself vulnerable to the second's actions, and bases her expectation upon past experience with that particular individual. This analysis captures many familiar cases of trust: the employer who trusts a long-serving employee, the patient who trusts a physician she has consulted for years. Yet a case which tells against the necessity of the third condition may be constructed from the experience of travel. Suppose a traveller arrives for the first time in a foreign city and, at the hotel entrance, entrusts her luggage to a porter she has never met and about whom she possesses not the slightest trace of past experience. She holds a positive expectation that the porter will convey her luggage safely to her room. She makes herself vulnerable by surrendering her belongings to a stranger. Yet the basis of her expectation is not past experience with this individual — she has none — but a constellation of more general considerations: the reputation of the hotel, the visible uniform and professional demeanour of the porter, and the general social conventions governing the hotel-guest relationship. The third condition of the proposed definition is not satisfied, for her expectation rests upon no prior acquaintance with the person she trusts. Nevertheless, the traveller plainly trusts the porter. The definition, by insisting that trust be grounded in past experience with the trusted party specifically, fails to accommodate the pervasive phenomenon of trust extended to strangers on the basis of social role, institutional affiliation, and general convention.

definition: A person P is alienated from an activity A if and only if (i) P engages in A, (ii) P derives no satisfaction or fulfilment from A, and (iii) P would prefer not to engage in A.
conditions: P engages in A
P derives no satisfaction or fulfilment from A
P would prefer not to engage in A
counterexample: Consider an assembly-line worker who finds his work monotonous and repetitive, who has no control over the pace or nature of his labour, and whose product is immediately taken from him. He is, by any reasonable assessment, alienated from his work. Yet suppose that he has, through long habituation, come to derive a quiet satisfaction from the rhythm of the assembly line, and that he does not prefer to be doing anything else — he has, in effect, adapted his preferences to his circumstances. The second and third conditions are not met, since he derives some satisfaction and does not prefer otherwise. Yet his alienation, far from being resolved, might be said to be deepened by this very adaptation.
missing_condition: The definition fails to capture the structural dimension of alienation — the objective separation of the worker from the product of their labour, from the process of production, and from their own creative capacities, which may persist even when subjective dissatisfaction is absent.
passage: It has been proposed that a person is alienated from an activity when he engages in it, derives no satisfaction from it, and would prefer not to be doing it. This analysis captures the phenomenology of alienation as it is often experienced: the factory worker who dreads the morning whistle, the clerk who watches the clock, the student who endures lectures without interest. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the subtler forms of estrangement. Consider an assembly-line worker whose task is to affix a single bolt to a component that passes before him on a conveyor belt, hundreds of times each day. He has no control over the pace of work, no say in the design of the product, and no connexion to the finished article, which is immediately removed and sold by an enterprise in whose profits he has no share. By any serious theoretical account, this worker is alienated from his labour. Yet suppose that through long habituation he has come to derive a quiet, almost mechanical satisfaction from the repetitive rhythm of his task. He does not prefer to be elsewhere; he has accommodated his expectations to his situation. The second and third conditions of the proposed definition are not met. Yet his alienation, far from having been overcome, might be said to have been deepened by this very adaptation, for he has lost not merely the fulfilment that creative labour might bring but even the capacity to recognise its absence. The definition, by attending only to subjective states — satisfaction and preference — overlooks the structural and objective dimensions of alienation.

definition: A pattern of behaviour B is a custom if and only if (i) B is widely practised within a particular society, (ii) B has been practised over a prolonged period, and (iii) the members of the society are aware that B is a common practice.
conditions: B is widely practised within a particular society
B has been practised over a prolonged period
The members of the society are aware that B is a common practice
counterexample: Consider a new fashion in greeting that sweeps through a society within a single season — say, the practice of touching elbows instead of shaking hands, adopted widely and rapidly during a period of public health concern. The practice is widely observed; the members of the society are fully aware that it is common; yet it has been in existence for only a few months and thus fails the requirement of prolonged practice. Nevertheless, speakers would readily describe the elbow-touch as a new custom, and it functions in every social respect as a custom does.
passage: One might characterise a custom as a pattern of behaviour that is widely practised within a society, has been observed over a prolonged period, and is recognised by the members of the society as a common practice. This analysis captures many of the customs with which we are familiar: the exchange of gifts at particular festivals, the observance of mourning rituals, the conventions governing polite address. Yet a case which tells against the necessity of the second condition may be drawn from the rapid emergence of new social practices. Suppose that during a period of widespread public health concern, a novel greeting — the touching of elbows in place of the handshake — is adopted almost overnight by a large proportion of the population. Within weeks, the practice is ubiquitous; people perform it without deliberation, expect others to do likewise, and regard its omission as mildly uncouth. The members of the society are fully aware that the elbow-touch is a common practice; it is remarked upon in conversation, observed in public places, and described in periodicals. The first and third conditions of the proposed definition are met. But the practice has been in existence for only a matter of weeks or months, far short of the prolonged period that the second condition demands. Yet ordinary speakers would not hesitate to describe the elbow-touch as a new custom, and it performs every social function that customs characteristically perform — it regulates behaviour, expresses social solidarity, and marks membership in a community. The definition, by insisting upon prolonged practice, is unable to accommodate the important class of customs that emerge rapidly in response to novel circumstances.

definition: A group G possesses a culture if and only if (i) the members of G share a common language, (ii) the members of G share a body of customs and practices, and (iii) the members of G transmit these shared elements to subsequent generations through deliberate instruction.
conditions: The members of G share a common language
The members of G share a body of customs and practices
The members of G transmit these shared elements to subsequent generations through deliberate instruction
counterexample: Consider a group of deaf individuals in a hearing society who share no common spoken language with one another — some communicate through signing, others through written notes, still others through lip-reading — but who share a rich body of customs, practices, artistic traditions, and social norms that they transmit to younger members not through deliberate instruction but through immersive participation in communal life. The first condition is not met, since they do not all share a common language; and the third condition is partially unsatisfied, since transmission occurs through immersion rather than deliberate instruction. Yet the group manifestly possesses a culture.
passage: It has been proposed that a group possesses a culture when its members share a common language, share a body of customs and practices, and transmit these shared elements to subsequent generations through deliberate instruction. This analysis captures many recognisable features of cultural life and has the merit of identifying language, custom, and transmission as central to the concept. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the experience of communities whose cultural bonds do not depend upon a shared spoken language or upon formal instruction. Consider a group of deaf individuals dispersed across a hearing society. Some among them communicate through a formal sign language; others rely upon written notes; still others employ lip-reading. They do not all share a single language. Yet they possess a rich and distinctive body of customs, artistic traditions, social practices, and shared understandings. Younger members absorb these not through deliberate instruction in any formal sense but through immersive participation in the communal life of the group — by attending gatherings, observing the behaviour of elders, and gradually internalising the norms that govern social interaction. The first condition of the proposed definition is not met, for there is no single common language; and the third condition is imperfectly satisfied, since the transmission is largely implicit rather than deliberately instructional. Yet it would be extraordinary to deny that this group possesses a culture. The definition, by insisting upon a common language and deliberate instruction, mistakes contingent features of certain cultures for necessary conditions of culture itself.

definition: An arrangement A between a governing authority and its subjects constitutes a social contract if and only if (i) the subjects have consented to the authority's rule, (ii) the authority undertakes to protect the fundamental interests of the subjects, and (iii) the arrangement is entered into freely by all parties.
conditions: The subjects have consented to the authority's rule
The authority undertakes to protect the fundamental interests of the subjects
The arrangement is entered into freely by all parties
counterexample: Consider an occupying military power that, after conquering a territory, establishes a regime that scrupulously protects the fundamental interests of the inhabitants — their safety, their property, their livelihoods. Over time, the inhabitants come to accept the regime and even to express their consent through participation in its political processes. Yet the arrangement was not entered into freely; it was imposed by conquest. The third condition is not met. Some theorists would nonetheless describe the resulting arrangement as having the character of a social contract, since consent has been given and interests are protected.
passage: One might analyse the social contract as an arrangement in which the subjects of a governing authority have consented to its rule, the authority undertakes to protect their fundamental interests, and the arrangement is entered into freely by all parties. This analysis captures the essential elements of the contractarian tradition: consent, mutual obligation, and voluntariness. Yet a case which tells against the necessity of the third condition may be constructed from the history of political occupation. Suppose that a military power conquers a territory and establishes a governing regime. The conquest is, of course, involuntary — the inhabitants did not choose to be conquered. Yet suppose that the new regime proves remarkably attentive to the interests of the inhabitants. It protects their property, ensures their safety, maintains their livelihoods, and respects their customs. Over a generation, the inhabitants come to accept the regime. They participate in its political processes, express their consent through lawful channels, and regard its authority as legitimate. The first and second conditions of the proposed definition are met: the subjects have consented, and the authority protects their interests. But the third condition — that the arrangement be entered into freely — is not satisfied, for the original imposition was the product of conquest. Nevertheless, one might observe that the resulting arrangement has acquired all the features of a social contract: consent, mutual obligation, and a stable system of governance. The definition, by requiring that the arrangement be entered into freely from the outset, is unable to accommodate the important class of political settlements that originate in coercion but evolve into genuinely consensual relationships.

definition: A group of persons G constitutes a community if and only if (i) the members of G share a common geographical location, (ii) the members of G interact with one another on a regular basis, and (iii) the members of G recognise themselves as belonging to a collective with shared interests.
conditions: The members of G share a common geographical location
The members of G interact with one another on a regular basis
The members of G recognise themselves as belonging to a collective with shared interests
counterexample: Consider the passengers who ride the same commuter train every morning. They share a common geographical location — the train carriage — and interact with one another on a regular basis, exchanging greetings, sharing newspapers, and observing the informal courtesies of daily travel. Over time, many of them come to recognise themselves as belonging to a kind of collective, referring to 'our carriage' and expressing a sense of shared interest in the punctuality and comfort of the service. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet we should hesitate to call this group a community in any robust sense. The association is thin, circumstantial, and easily dissolved; if the train schedule changes, the group disperses without residue.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the members be bound by substantive shared values, mutual obligations, or a common history that gives the group a depth and stability beyond the mere convergence of location, routine interaction, and self-recognition.
passage: One might propose that a community consists of persons who share a common geographical location, interact regularly, and recognise themselves as a collective with shared interests. This analysis captures the spatial, interactive, and self-reflective dimensions of communal life. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the routines of everyday urban existence. Consider the passengers who ride the same commuter train every weekday morning. They share a common geographical location — the interior of the same carriage. They interact on a regular basis, exchanging greetings, sharing the morning newspaper, and observing the informal courtesies of daily travel. Over time, many of them come to recognise themselves as belonging to a kind of collective: they speak of 'our carriage,' express a shared interest in the punctuality of the service, and feel a mild sense of solidarity with their fellow commuters. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that we should be reluctant to call this group a community in any philosophically robust sense. The association is thin and circumstantial; it rests upon the accident of a shared schedule rather than upon substantive shared values, mutual obligations, or a common history. If the train timetable changes, the group disperses without residue. It appears that the definition, by requiring only shared location, regular interaction, and collective self-recognition, fails to demand the depth of shared commitment, mutual obligation, and historical continuity that distinguishes a genuine community from a merely recurrent aggregation of persons.

definition: A practice P is a tradition if and only if (i) P has been transmitted from one generation to the next, (ii) P is performed in a manner substantially similar to its earlier instances, and (iii) the practitioners of P regard it as having been inherited from their predecessors.
conditions: P has been transmitted from one generation to the next
P is performed in a manner substantially similar to its earlier instances
The practitioners of P regard it as having been inherited from their predecessors
counterexample: Suppose a group of villagers performs an annual harvest festival that they believe to be an ancient custom handed down through many generations. The festival is conducted in a manner substantially similar to earlier performances, and the villagers regard it as an inheritance from their ancestors. All three conditions are met. Yet suppose that historical research reveals the festival to have been invented only forty years ago by a local school teacher who fabricated a story about its ancient origins to foster community spirit. The practitioners were deceived about the history of the practice. It appears that the practice satisfies the proposed conditions — it has been transmitted across at least one generation, it is performed in a substantially similar manner, and the practitioners regard it as inherited — yet its status as a genuine tradition is called into question by its fabricated provenance.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the practice actually have the historical depth and continuity that its practitioners attribute to it, rather than merely being believed to have such a history; it thus classifies as traditions practices that rest upon fabricated or mistaken genealogies.
passage: One might define a tradition as a practice transmitted from one generation to the next, performed in a manner substantially similar to its earlier instances, and regarded by its practitioners as inherited from their predecessors. This formulation captures the intergenerational transmission, continuity of form, and self-conscious historical identification that we associate with traditional practices. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the phenomenon of invented traditions. Suppose a group of villagers performs an annual harvest festival that they firmly believe to be an ancient custom handed down through many generations. The festival is conducted in a manner substantially similar to its earlier performances; the same songs are sung, the same rituals enacted, and the same foods prepared. The villagers regard the festival as a cherished inheritance from their ancestors. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Yet suppose that careful historical research reveals the festival to have been invented only forty years ago by a local school teacher, who fabricated a narrative of ancient origins in order to foster a sense of community. The practitioners were deceived about the provenance of the practice. One might observe that the practice satisfies all the stated conditions — it has been transmitted across at least one generation, is performed in a substantially similar manner, and is regarded as inherited — and yet its claim to be a genuine tradition is severely undermined by the discovery of its fabricated genealogy. It appears that the definition, by resting upon transmission, similarity, and the practitioners' own understanding, fails to require that the practice actually possess the historical depth that its practitioners attribute to it.

definition: A pattern of behaviour B is a custom if and only if (i) B is regularly practised by the members of a social group, (ii) B is transmitted through socialisation rather than formal instruction, and (iii) deviation from B is met with social disapproval.
conditions: B is regularly practised by the members of a social group
B is transmitted through socialisation rather than formal instruction
Deviation from B is met with social disapproval
counterexample: Consider the practice of driving on the left-hand side of the road in a certain country. This behaviour is regularly practised by all members of the society. It is, in large part, transmitted through socialisation — children observe their parents and neighbours driving on the left long before they receive formal driving instruction. And deviation from the practice is met with severe social (and indeed legal) disapproval. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet driving on a particular side of the road is not ordinarily regarded as a custom; it is a legal regulation, codified in statute and enforced by the state. Its origin lies in legislative enactment rather than in the gradual, organic emergence from social life that characterises customs.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the practice have arisen spontaneously from the organic social life of the group, rather than having been imposed by formal authority or legislative decree, thereby conflating customs with legally mandated behaviours that happen to be widely observed and socially reinforced.
passage: One might characterise a custom as a pattern of behaviour regularly practised by members of a social group, transmitted through socialisation rather than formal instruction, and accompanied by social disapproval of those who deviate. This analysis captures the regularity, informality of transmission, and normative pressure that we associate with customary behaviour. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the domain of traffic regulation. Consider the practice of driving on the left-hand side of the road in a certain country. The behaviour is regularly practised by all members of the society who drive. It is transmitted in large part through socialisation: children observe their parents and neighbours driving on the left long before they undergo formal driving lessons. And deviation from the practice is met with social disapproval — indeed, with alarm, anger, and legal penalty. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that driving on a particular side of the road is not ordinarily regarded as a custom. It is a legal regulation, codified in statute and enforced by the apparatus of the state. Its origin lies not in the gradual, organic emergence from social life that characterises genuine customs — the practices of greeting, dining, mourning, and celebration that arise from the lived texture of communal existence — but in a legislative decision. It appears that the definition, by requiring only regular practice, socialised transmission, and social disapproval of deviation, fails to require that the behaviour have arisen spontaneously from the social life of the group rather than having been imposed by formal authority.

definition: A social arrangement I is an institution if and only if (i) I consists of a set of rules, roles, and procedures that are recognised by the members of a society, (ii) I serves a function that the members regard as essential to the maintenance of social order, and (iii) I persists across changes in the individuals who occupy its roles.
conditions: I consists of a set of rules, roles, and procedures that are recognised by the members of a society
I serves a function that the members regard as essential to the maintenance of social order
I persists across changes in the individuals who occupy its roles
counterexample: Suppose a neighbourhood establishes an informal arrangement whereby the eldest resident serves as an arbiter of local disputes, with recognised procedures for bringing grievances, hearing testimony, and rendering judgement. The members of the neighbourhood regard this arrangement as essential to maintaining peace, and as successive eldest residents assume the role, the arrangement persists across changes in personnel. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet this arrangement is an informal social practice, not an institution in any robust sense. It has no charter, no formal existence independent of the particular neighbourhood, and no enforcement mechanism beyond the willingness of residents to comply. If the neighbourhood disperses, the arrangement vanishes entirely.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the arrangement possess a formal or quasi-formal structure that gives it an existence and authority independent of the particular individuals and particular social setting in which it operates, distinguishing institutions from informal social arrangements that merely happen to exhibit recognised rules, perceived essentiality, and continuity of personnel.
passage: One might define an institution as a social arrangement consisting of recognised rules, roles, and procedures, serving a function regarded as essential to social order, and persisting across changes in the individuals who occupy its roles. This characterisation captures the structured, functional, and enduring character of institutions. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from informal social life. Suppose the residents of a small neighbourhood establish an arrangement whereby the eldest among them serves as an arbiter of local disputes. Recognised procedures are developed: a grievance is brought before the arbiter, testimony is heard from both sides, and a judgement is rendered. The members of the neighbourhood regard this arrangement as essential to the maintenance of peace and harmony. And as successive eldest residents assume the role over the years, the arrangement persists across changes in personnel. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that this arrangement is an informal social practice, not an institution in the philosophically robust sense. It has no charter, no formal existence independent of the particular neighbourhood, and no enforcement mechanism beyond the voluntary compliance of the residents. If the neighbourhood disperses or the residents lose interest, the arrangement vanishes without residue. It appears that the definition, by requiring only recognised rules, perceived essentiality, and continuity across personnel, fails to demand that the arrangement possess a formal or quasi-formal structure giving it an existence and authority independent of the particular individuals and setting in which it operates.

definition: A system of shared meanings M constitutes a culture if and only if (i) M includes a common language, a body of symbols, and a set of interpretive practices, (ii) M is transmitted across generations through education, ritual, and daily interaction, and (iii) M distinguishes the group that possesses it from other groups.
conditions: M includes a common language, a body of symbols, and a set of interpretive practices
M is transmitted across generations through education, ritual, and daily interaction
M distinguishes the group that possesses it from other groups
counterexample: Consider the system of meanings shared by professional mathematicians worldwide. They possess a common symbolic language — the notation and conventions of modern mathematics. They share a body of symbols and interpretive practices for reading proofs, evaluating conjectures, and recognising elegance. This system is transmitted across generations through education and daily interaction in universities and research institutes. And it distinguishes mathematicians from other groups. All three conditions are met. Yet one might hesitate to call the shared meanings of professional mathematics a culture in the full sense; it is a professional practice or disciplinary tradition, not a culture in the way that one speaks of French culture or Japanese culture.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the system of shared meanings encompass the full breadth of human experience — including moral norms, aesthetic sensibilities, modes of daily life, and conceptions of the sacred or the significant — rather than being confined to the specialised practices of a professional or technical community.
passage: One might propose that a culture is a system of shared meanings that includes a common language, symbols, and interpretive practices, is transmitted across generations, and distinguishes its possessors from other groups. This formulation attempts to capture the symbolic, intergenerational, and identity-constituting character of culture. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the world of professional practice. Consider the system of shared meanings possessed by professional mathematicians worldwide. They employ a common symbolic language — the notation and conventions of modern mathematics — and share a body of symbols and interpretive practices for reading proofs, assessing conjectures, and recognising elegance. This system is transmitted across generations through university education, apprenticeship in research groups, and the daily interaction of conferences and seminars. And it distinguishes mathematicians from other professional groups with considerable clarity. All three conditions of the proposed definition appear to be satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that we should hesitate to call the shared meanings of professional mathematics a culture in the full sense of the term. It is a professional practice, a disciplinary tradition, or a specialised community of inquiry, but not a culture in the way one speaks of French culture or Japanese culture. It appears that the definition, by requiring only shared symbolic resources, intergenerational transmission, and group distinctiveness, fails to demand that the system of meanings encompass the full breadth of human experience — moral norms, aesthetic sensibilities, modes of daily life, and conceptions of the sacred — rather than being limited to the specialised practices of a professional community.

definition: A rule R is a social norm if and only if (i) R prescribes or proscribes a particular type of behaviour within a social group, (ii) the members of the group generally comply with R, and (iii) violations of R are met with informal sanctions such as criticism, ridicule, or ostracism.
conditions: R prescribes or proscribes a particular type of behaviour within a social group
The members of the group generally comply with R
Violations of R are met with informal sanctions such as criticism, ridicule, or ostracism
counterexample: Consider a newly enacted workplace directive requiring employees to submit weekly progress reports. The directive prescribes a particular type of behaviour. The employees generally comply with it. And violations are met with informal sanctions — supervisors express displeasure, colleagues remark unfavourably on the non-compliant employee's attitude. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet this is a formal organisational policy, not a social norm. It originated in a management decision, was communicated through official channels, and would be recognised by all parties as an administrative requirement rather than a norm arising from the organic social life of the group.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the rule have emerged organically from the repeated interactions and shared expectations of the group, rather than having been deliberately promulgated by an authority, thereby conflating social norms with formally imposed regulations that happen to attract informal sanctions.
passage: One might characterise a social norm as a rule that prescribes or proscribes behaviour, is generally complied with by the members of a group, and whose violation attracts informal sanctions such as criticism, ridicule, or ostracism. This analysis captures the prescriptive force, widespread observance, and informal enforcement that we associate with social norms. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed from the domain of organisational life. Suppose a workplace enacts a new directive requiring every employee to submit a weekly progress report. The directive prescribes a particular type of behaviour within the group. The employees generally comply with it. And violations are met with informal sanctions: supervisors express displeasure, and colleagues remark unfavourably on the non-compliant employee's attitude and commitment. All three conditions of the proposed definition appear to be satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that this directive is not a social norm. It is a formal organisational policy, originated in a management decision, communicated through official channels, and recognised by all concerned as an administrative requirement. It did not emerge from the organic social life of the group — from the gradual crystallisation of shared expectations through repeated interaction — but was imposed from above. It appears that the definition, by requiring only prescription, general compliance, and informal sanction, fails to require that the rule have arisen organically from the group's own interactions and shared expectations. The distinction between a social norm and a formally imposed rule that happens to attract informal enforcement is thereby lost.

definition: An interaction between agents A and B constitutes cooperation if and only if (i) A and B act jointly toward a shared goal, (ii) each agent contributes effort or resources that complement those of the other, and (iii) each agent benefits from the interaction.
conditions: A and B act jointly toward a shared goal
Each agent contributes effort or resources that complement those of the other
Each agent benefits from the interaction
counterexample: Suppose two prisoners, each confined to a separate cell, simultaneously and independently decide to remain silent during interrogation, knowing that mutual silence will result in lighter sentences for both. Each benefits from the interaction, and their actions produce an outcome equivalent to that of a shared goal — mutual advantage. Yet the prisoners do not act jointly; they act independently, without communication or coordination. One might argue that each contributes something (silence) that complements the other's contribution. But there is no joint action, no mutual awareness of collaboration, and no coordination. The conditions appear to be satisfied in form, yet the interaction lacks the character of genuine cooperation.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the agents be aware of each other's participation and intentionally coordinate their actions, rather than merely happening to act in parallel ways that produce mutual benefit; genuine cooperation requires mutual awareness and intentional coordination, not merely convergent individual actions.
passage: One might define cooperation as a joint interaction in which agents act toward a shared goal, contribute complementary efforts, and each benefit from the outcome. This formulation captures the goal-directed, complementary, and mutually beneficial character of cooperative endeavours. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from a familiar sort of strategic scenario. Suppose two prisoners, each confined to a separate cell and unable to communicate with the other, simultaneously decide to remain silent during interrogation. Each knows that if both remain silent, both will receive lighter sentences. The outcome is mutually beneficial: each prisoner gains from the other's silence. The actions are in some sense complementary, since each person's silence is effective only if the other also remains silent. And the outcome realises what might be described as a shared goal — mutual advantage. The conditions of the proposed definition appear to be satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that this interaction lacks the character of genuine cooperation. The prisoners do not act jointly; they act independently, without communication, coordination, or mutual awareness of each other's participation at the moment of action. Their convergent behaviour is the product of parallel individual reasoning, not of shared deliberation or intentional collaboration. It appears that the definition, by requiring only joint action toward a shared goal, complementary contribution, and mutual benefit, fails to require that the agents be aware of each other's participation and intentionally coordinate their efforts. Without this requirement, convergent individual actions that happen to produce mutual benefit are indistinguishable from genuine cooperation.

definition: Agent A trusts agent B with respect to a matter M if and only if (i) A believes that B will act in a manner favourable to A's interests regarding M, (ii) A is willing to make themselves vulnerable to B's actions regarding M, and (iii) A's belief and willingness are based on A's assessment of B's past behaviour.
conditions: A believes that B will act in a manner favourable to A's interests regarding M
A is willing to make themselves vulnerable to B's actions regarding M
A's belief and willingness are based on A's assessment of B's past behaviour
counterexample: Suppose a young child trusts her newly appointed guardian — a person she has never met before — to care for her welfare. The child believes the guardian will act favourably regarding her care, and she is willing to make herself vulnerable by depending upon the guardian for food, shelter, and comfort. Yet the child's belief and willingness are not based upon any assessment of the guardian's past behaviour, for she has no acquaintance with the guardian's history whatsoever. Her trust rests instead upon a general disposition to depend upon those placed in a caregiving role. All three conditions are not jointly satisfied, and yet the child plainly trusts the guardian.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that trust may arise from sources other than the assessment of past behaviour — including general dispositions, institutional roles, social recommendations, or the very vulnerability of the trusting party — and that confining the basis of trust to past behaviour excludes many paradigmatic instances of trust.
passage: One might define trust as the belief that another will act favourably toward one's interests, combined with a willingness to make oneself vulnerable, where both are grounded in an assessment of the other's past behaviour. This analysis captures the predictive, dispositional, and evidential dimensions of trust. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the relationship between a child and a newly appointed guardian. Suppose a young child, following the death of her parents, is placed in the care of a guardian whom she has never previously met. The child believes that the guardian will care for her — will provide food, shelter, and comfort. She is willing to make herself vulnerable by depending entirely upon this stranger for her daily needs. Yet her belief and willingness are not based upon any assessment of the guardian's past behaviour, for she has no acquaintance whatever with the guardian's history. Her trust rests upon a general disposition to depend upon those placed in a caregiving role, reinforced perhaps by the assurances of other adults she does know. The third condition of the proposed definition is not satisfied, and yet one might observe that the child plainly trusts the guardian. It appears that the definition, by requiring that trust be based upon an assessment of past behaviour, is too restrictive. Trust may arise from many sources — from general dispositions, from institutional roles, from the recommendations of others, or from the sheer vulnerability of the trusting party. A definition that confines the basis of trust to past behavioural evidence excludes many of the most fundamental and paradigmatic instances of trust in human life.

definition: A person P is alienated from an activity or product X if and only if (i) P is engaged in or has produced X, (ii) P experiences X as foreign, meaningless, or imposed rather than as an expression of P's own purposes, and (iii) P's engagement with X is compelled by external circumstances rather than freely chosen.
conditions: P is engaged in or has produced X
P experiences X as foreign, meaningless, or imposed rather than as an expression of P's own purposes
P's engagement with X is compelled by external circumstances rather than freely chosen
counterexample: Suppose an artist freely chooses to undertake a large commissioned mural for a public building. No external compulsion is involved; the artist sought the commission and negotiated its terms. Yet as the work progresses, the artist finds that the mural, shaped by the client's requirements and public expectations, no longer expresses her own artistic vision. She experiences the work as foreign and meaningless — as something imposed by the demands of the commission rather than as an expression of her creative purposes. She is engaged in the activity, and she experiences it as alien. But her engagement is freely chosen. Condition (iii) is not met, yet the artist is plainly alienated from her work.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that alienation may arise even in the absence of external compulsion, when the intrinsic character of the activity or product becomes disconnected from the agent's own purposes and self-understanding, regardless of whether participation was freely chosen.
passage: One might propose that alienation consists in being engaged in an activity or having produced something that one experiences as foreign or meaningless, where one's engagement is compelled by external circumstances. This analysis attempts to capture the phenomenological, productive, and coercive dimensions of alienation. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the experience of artistic creation. Suppose an artist freely chooses to undertake a large commissioned mural for a public building. No external compulsion is involved: the artist sought the commission, negotiated its terms, and embarked upon the project with enthusiasm. Yet as the work progresses, the artist discovers that the mural, shaped increasingly by the client's requirements and the expectations of the public, no longer expresses her own artistic vision. She experiences the work as foreign and meaningless — as something imposed by the logic of the commission rather than as an emanation of her creative purposes. She is engaged in the activity, and she experiences it as alien. Yet her engagement was freely chosen; no external force compelled her participation. The third condition of the proposed definition is not satisfied, and yet one might observe that the artist is plainly alienated from her work. It appears that the definition, by requiring external compulsion as a necessary condition, fails to recognise that alienation may arise even where participation is voluntary, whenever the intrinsic character of the activity becomes disconnected from the agent's own purposes and self-understanding. Alienation, it seems, is a matter of the relation between the agent and the activity, not merely of the circumstances under which the agent came to be engaged in it.

definition: A political arrangement A is grounded in a social contract if and only if (i) the members of the society have consented, either explicitly or tacitly, to the terms of A, (ii) A assigns rights and obligations to the members in exchange for the benefits of social cooperation, and (iii) A is the product of rational deliberation among free and equal persons.
conditions: The members of the society have consented, either explicitly or tacitly, to the terms of A
A assigns rights and obligations to the members in exchange for the benefits of social cooperation
A is the product of rational deliberation among free and equal persons
counterexample: Consider a feudal society in which the serfs, having been born into their condition and knowing no alternative, acquiesce in the existing political arrangement. Over time, a certain stability develops: the serfs accept the terms of their labour in exchange for the lord's protection, and a system of rights and obligations emerges. One might characterise this acquiescence as tacit consent, the exchange of labour for protection as an assignment of rights and obligations in return for the benefits of cooperation, and the gradual codification of the arrangement as a product of deliberation among participants. All three conditions could be construed as satisfied. Yet we should hesitate to say that this feudal arrangement is grounded in a social contract, for the serfs were never free and equal parties to any deliberation, and their 'consent' was the product of coercion and the absence of alternatives.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the consent be given under conditions of genuine freedom and equality — that the parties have real alternatives and are not coerced, ignorant, or structurally disadvantaged — thereby allowing arrangements grounded in acquiescence under coercion to be classified as social contracts.
passage: One might characterise a social contract as a political arrangement to which the members of a society have consented, which assigns rights and obligations in exchange for the benefits of cooperation, and which is the product of rational deliberation among free and equal persons. This formulation captures the consensual, reciprocal, and deliberative dimensions of the social contract tradition. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the domain of feudal political organisation. Consider a feudal society in which serfs, born into their condition and knowing no alternative mode of life, gradually acquiesce in the existing arrangement. A system of rights and obligations develops: the serf provides labour in exchange for the lord's protection and the use of land. Over generations, this exchange becomes codified and accepted. One might construe the serfs' acquiescence as tacit consent to the terms of the arrangement. One might describe the exchange of labour for protection as an assignment of rights and obligations in return for the benefits of social cooperation. And one might regard the gradual codification as a product of deliberation among the parties involved. All three conditions of the proposed definition could, with some latitude, be regarded as satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that we should be most reluctant to say that this feudal arrangement is grounded in a social contract. The serfs were never free and equal parties to any deliberation; their so-called consent was extracted under conditions of profound structural inequality and the complete absence of alternatives. It appears that the definition, by allowing tacit consent and by failing to specify the conditions under which consent must be given, cannot distinguish genuine contractual foundations from acquiescence under coercion.

definition: A group G of persons constitutes a community if and only if (i) the members of G share a common geographical locality, (ii) the members of G interact with one another on a regular basis, and (iii) the members of G share a sense of belonging and mutual identification.
conditions: The members of G share a common geographical locality
The members of G interact with one another on a regular basis
The members of G share a sense of belonging and mutual identification
counterexample: Consider a case in which the members of a religious diaspora, scattered across several continents, maintain regular contact through correspondence, observe the same rituals, celebrate the same festivals, and possess a profound sense of belonging to and identification with one another. They interact regularly — through letters, communal gatherings when travel permits, and the circulation of religious texts — and they share a sense of mutual identification that is deeper than many geographically concentrated groups ever achieve. Yet they do not share a common geographical locality; they are dispersed across the globe. Condition (i) fails, and yet it seems plainly correct to say that they constitute a community.
missing_condition: The definition must allow for communities constituted by shared practices, beliefs, or identities rather than requiring geographical co-location
passage: The concept of community has often been analysed in terms of three jointly sufficient conditions: shared locality, regular interaction, and a common sense of belonging. This analysis reflects a long tradition in social thought according to which communities are essentially local phenomena — groups of persons living in proximity, encountering one another in the ordinary course of life, and recognising one another as fellows. The village, the parish, the neighbourhood — these are the paradigmatic communities, and the proposed definition captures their essential features well. Yet one may observe that our use of the word 'community' extends well beyond the boundaries of shared geographical locality. Let us consider the case of a religious diaspora whose members are scattered across several continents. They do not live in the same town, the same country, or even the same hemisphere. Yet they maintain regular and intimate contact through correspondence and through communal gatherings convened whenever travel permits. They observe the same rituals, celebrate the same festivals, study the same sacred texts, and share a sense of mutual identification that is, by all accounts, deeper and more enduring than anything found in many geographically concentrated groups. One would not hesitate to call this dispersed body a community — indeed, its members insist upon the designation with particular fervour. But condition (i) is unsatisfied: they share no common locality. The case suggests that geographical proximity, however natural it may seem as a condition of community, is not in fact necessary. What constitutes a community, it appears, is not where its members happen to reside but what they share — practices, beliefs, identities, and a mutual recognition that binds them together across distance. A satisfactory analysis must accommodate this possibility.

definition: A practice P is a tradition if and only if (i) P has been transmitted from one generation to the next over an extended period, (ii) P is regarded by its practitioners as having been inherited from the past, and (iii) P is performed in a manner that preserves continuity with its earlier instances.
conditions: P has been transmitted from one generation to the next over an extended period
P is regarded by its practitioners as having been inherited from the past
P is performed in a manner that preserves continuity with its earlier instances
counterexample: Consider a case in which a village festival, believed by the inhabitants to be of ancient origin, is in fact a recent invention — inaugurated some thirty years ago by the local council as a tourist attraction. The participants sincerely believe the festival to have been handed down from their grandparents; they perform it with careful attention to what they take to be its traditional form. They satisfy conditions (ii) and (iii) entirely. Condition (i), however, is not met, for the practice has not in fact been transmitted across generations — it was devised within living memory. And yet the participants and outside observers alike call it a tradition without the slightest hesitation.
missing_condition: The definition must address whether a practice's status as a tradition depends upon actual historical transmission or upon the sincere belief of practitioners that such transmission has occurred
passage: It is natural to suppose that a tradition is a practice transmitted across generations, regarded by its practitioners as inherited from the past, and performed in a manner continuous with its earlier instances. These conditions seem to mark off traditions from mere habits, fashions, and innovations. A tradition, on this view, is something with a pedigree — a practice whose roots extend deep into the past and whose present form reflects the accumulated modifications of successive generations. Yet one may observe that certain practices that we unhesitatingly call traditions turn out, upon closer investigation, to be nothing of the sort. Suppose that a village in the English countryside celebrates an annual harvest festival which the inhabitants believe to date from time immemorial. They process through the streets with sheaves of corn, sing songs that they take to be of great antiquity, and perform a pageant depicting the labours of their forebears. The festival is a source of considerable local pride, and the participants devote much care to preserving what they regard as its traditional character. Yet the festival was in fact inaugurated some thirty years ago by the local council, partly as a means of attracting tourists and partly as an exercise in community spirit. The songs were composed by the village schoolmaster; the pageant was adapted from a play in a popular anthology. No earlier generation practised anything of the kind. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are satisfied in full: the practitioners sincerely believe the festival to be inherited from the past, and they perform it in a manner they take to be continuous with earlier instances. But condition (i) fails: there has been no actual transmission across generations. The practice is, as a matter of historical fact, a recent invention. And yet it is called a tradition by all who know of it. The case raises a pointed question for the analysis: does the status of a tradition depend upon the reality of historical transmission, or merely upon the belief that such transmission has occurred?

definition: A pattern of behaviour B practised within a group G is a custom if and only if (i) B is performed regularly and repeatedly by the members of G, (ii) B is sustained by social expectation rather than by formal law, and (iii) deviation from B is met with informal social disapproval.
conditions: B is performed regularly and repeatedly by the members of G
B is sustained by social expectation rather than by formal law
Deviation from B is met with informal social disapproval
counterexample: Consider a case in which a small academic department observes the practice of bringing biscuits to the weekly meeting. The practice is performed regularly; it is sustained by social expectation rather than by any formal regulation; and yet deviation from it — a week in which no one brings biscuits — is met not with disapproval but with cheerful indifference. Nobody minds when the biscuits fail to appear. All conditions except (iii) are satisfied, and yet most members of the department would describe the practice as a custom. The analysis appears to overstate the role of social censure.
missing_condition: The definition must allow for customs that are maintained by positive expectation and habit rather than by the sanction of disapproval
passage: The notion of a custom would seem to be captured by three conditions: the behaviour in question must be regularly practised, it must be sustained by social expectation rather than formal law, and deviation from it must attract informal social disapproval. This analysis has a good deal of plausibility. The custom of removing one's hat indoors, the custom of shaking hands upon introduction, the custom of bringing a gift when invited to dinner — in each case, the behaviour is regular, socially expected, and its omission is liable to be noticed and frowned upon. Yet one may reflect upon cases in which a practice that we should wish to call a custom is maintained without any trace of disapproval attending its neglect. Suppose that in a small university department it has become the practice for one member to bring biscuits to the weekly meeting. This practice is performed with great regularity — nearly every week for several years. It is sustained by social expectation in the sense that those present look forward to the biscuits and would notice their absence. But when, on some particular Friday, no one brings biscuits, the response is not disapproval but cheerful indifference. No one is criticised, no one is thought to have fallen short, no one is made to feel that a duty has been neglected. The omission passes without remark. Yet if one were to ask the members of the department whether the bringing of biscuits is a custom, most would say that it is — and would say so quite naturally. The case suggests that the third condition of the analysis is too strong. Not all customs are sustained by the sanction of disapproval; some are maintained by nothing more than positive expectation, habit, and the mild pleasure that their observance affords. A definition that insists upon social censure as a necessary condition will exclude practices that are, by any ordinary reckoning, customs.

definition: A social arrangement I is an institution if and only if (i) I consists of a system of rules that govern the behaviour of individuals occupying defined roles, (ii) I persists over time independently of the particular individuals who participate in it, and (iii) I is formally established by an act of deliberate creation.
conditions: I consists of a system of rules that govern the behaviour of individuals occupying defined roles
I persists over time independently of the particular individuals who participate in it
I is formally established by an act of deliberate creation
counterexample: Consider the institution of marriage as it has existed in countless societies throughout history. Marriage consists of rules governing the behaviour of those who enter into it; it persists over time, surviving the deaths and replacements of all its participants. Yet in many societies marriage was not formally established by any act of deliberate creation — it emerged gradually through custom, religious practice, and social convention, without any identifiable moment of founding. Condition (iii) is unsatisfied, and yet marriage is among the most widely cited examples of a social institution.
missing_condition: The definition must allow for institutions that arise through gradual social evolution rather than requiring a discrete founding act
passage: An institution, one might say, is a system of rules defining certain roles and governing the behaviour of those who occupy them, which persists through changes in personnel and which was established by a deliberate act of creation. This definition captures many familiar cases: a university is founded by charter, a parliament by constitution, a corporation by articles of incorporation. In each instance, there are defined roles, persistent rules, and a discrete founding event. Yet one may reflect upon what is perhaps the most venerable of all social institutions — marriage — and find that the analysis is inadequate. Marriage plainly satisfies conditions (i) and (ii). There are rules, some formal and some customary, that govern the conduct of married persons; the institution of marriage persists across centuries and millennia, surviving the deaths of all who participate in it at any given time. But condition (iii) is another matter. Marriage, in most societies, was not established by any deliberate act of creation. No one sat down on a particular day and resolved to invent marriage. It emerged gradually, over vast stretches of time, through the slow accumulation of customs, religious rites, and social expectations. There is no founding charter, no constitutional article, no identifiable moment at which marriage came into existence. The same may be said of other institutions — the family, the marketplace, the practice of promising. These are institutions by any reasonable standard, yet they arose through social evolution rather than through formal acts of establishment. The proposed definition, by insisting upon deliberate creation, is therefore too narrow. A satisfactory analysis must recognise that institutions may come into being gradually, through custom and convention, no less than through formal founding acts.

definition: A group G possesses a culture if and only if (i) the members of G share a set of beliefs, values, and practices, (ii) these beliefs, values, and practices are transmitted through learning rather than biological inheritance, and (iii) the shared elements form a coherent and integrated system.
conditions: The members of G share a set of beliefs, values, and practices
These beliefs, values, and practices are transmitted through learning rather than biological inheritance
The shared elements form a coherent and integrated system
counterexample: Consider a case in which a large and diverse nation harbours within itself numerous sub-groups whose beliefs, values, and practices are in open tension with one another — rural and urban populations disagree on fundamental matters of morality; religious and secular factions hold irreconcilable positions on the nature of authority; regional customs vary so widely as to be mutually unintelligible. These elements are transmitted through learning, and the members share certain practices at a superficial level, but the shared elements do not form a coherent and integrated system — they are fractured, contested, and in many respects contradictory. Condition (iii) fails, yet we commonly speak of this nation as possessing a culture.
missing_condition: The definition must accommodate cultures that are internally heterogeneous, contested, and marked by tension rather than requiring coherence and integration
passage: The concept of culture has been analysed in terms of shared beliefs, values, and practices that are transmitted through learning and that form a coherent system. This definition has much to recommend it: it distinguishes cultural phenomena from biological ones, it emphasises the role of social transmission, and it captures the sense in which a culture is more than a mere collection of random habits — it is an integrated way of life. Yet one may observe that the third condition — the requirement of coherence and integration — is more demanding than it may first appear. Consider a large and diverse nation in which rural and urban populations hold deeply divergent views on matters of morality; in which religious conservatives and secular progressives are locked in what can only be described as a protracted cultural struggle; and in which regional customs, dialects, and social conventions vary so widely as to be, in certain cases, mutually unintelligible. The members of this nation share certain practices at a broad level — they observe the same public holidays, recognise the same governmental authority, and communicate in a common language — yet their beliefs and values are fractured, contested, and in many important respects contradictory. The shared elements do not form a coherent and integrated system; they form a turbulent and internally divided one. Condition (iii) is not satisfied, at least not in the way the definition seems to require. And yet we should not hesitate to say that this nation possesses a culture — indeed, we should say it possesses a particularly rich and complex one. The case suggests that coherence and integration, while characteristic of some cultures, are not necessary features of culture as such. A satisfactory definition must allow for cultures that are internally heterogeneous, marked by disagreement, and held together by something looser than systematic integration.

definition: A rule R operative within a group G is a social norm if and only if (i) the majority of members of G conform to R, (ii) the members of G believe that conformity to R is expected, and (iii) deviation from R is met with sanctions — formal or informal — imposed by other members of G.
conditions: The majority of members of G conform to R
The members of G believe that conformity to R is expected
Deviation from R is met with sanctions imposed by other members of G
counterexample: Consider a case in which the members of a society widely acknowledge that bribery of public officials is wrong and believe that refraining from bribery is expected. Yet the majority of the population, in practice, regularly offers bribes to obtain services. The norm against bribery is recognised, the expectation of conformity is sincerely held, and deviation — that is, the offering of bribes — is, in principle, subject to legal sanctions. But in practice, the majority does not conform, and the sanctions are rarely enforced. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are nominally satisfied, yet condition (i) fails. Nevertheless, it seems correct to say that there is a norm against bribery in this society — a norm that is widely violated but that remains a norm for all that.
missing_condition: The definition must distinguish between the existence of a norm and its observance, allowing for norms that persist despite widespread non-compliance
passage: A natural analysis of social norms holds that a norm exists within a group when the majority conforms to the relevant rule, when the members believe conformity to be expected, and when deviation is met with sanctions. These conditions seem jointly to capture what is meant by a social norm as opposed to a mere regularity of behaviour or a private preference. The norm of queuing, the norm of returning a greeting, the norm of dressing appropriately for a funeral — in each case, the conditions appear to be satisfied. Yet reflection upon a certain class of cases suggests that the analysis may be too closely tied to actual conformity. Suppose that in a given society there exists a widely recognised prohibition against the bribery of public officials. The members of the society are unanimous in declaring bribery to be wrong; they believe, sincerely, that refraining from bribery is expected of every citizen; and the legal code prescribes punishments for those who engage in it. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are thus at least nominally satisfied. But in practice, the majority of the population regularly offers bribes to obtain governmental services, and the prescribed sanctions are only sporadically enforced. Condition (i) fails: the majority does not conform. Must we then say that there is no norm against bribery in this society? Such a conclusion seems clearly mistaken. The norm exists; it is recognised, invoked in public discourse, and appeals to it are made constantly. What has failed is not the norm itself but the observance of the norm. The case reveals that the proposed analysis conflates the existence of a norm with its effectiveness. A norm may persist as a genuine social rule — structuring expectations, guiding moral judgement, and providing grounds for criticism — even when the majority fails to comply with it. A more adequate definition must separate the question of a norm's existence from the question of its observance.

definition: An interaction between agents A and B is an instance of cooperation if and only if (i) A and B act jointly toward a shared goal, (ii) each agent contributes effort that is necessary for the achievement of the goal, and (iii) each agent acts with the intention of benefiting both parties.
conditions: A and B act jointly toward a shared goal
Each agent contributes effort that is necessary for the achievement of the goal
Each agent acts with the intention of benefiting both parties
counterexample: Consider a case in which two rival merchants independently lower their prices in order to drive a common competitor out of the market. Their actions converge upon a shared goal — the elimination of the competitor — and each merchant's price reduction contributes effort that is necessary for achieving that goal, since neither alone could undercut the competitor sufficiently. Yet neither merchant acts with the intention of benefiting the other; each acts purely out of self-interest, and each would be quite pleased if the other were driven out of business in turn. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied, but condition (iii) fails. Yet the two merchants are, in a recognisable sense, cooperating — they are working toward the same end, and their joint action is more effective than either could have managed alone.
missing_condition: The definition must allow for cooperation that arises from convergent self-interest rather than requiring mutual benevolent intention
passage: Cooperation is commonly analysed as joint action directed toward a shared goal, in which each participant contributes necessary effort and acts with the intention of benefiting all parties involved. This analysis captures the spirit of cooperation as it is ordinarily understood: two persons working together, each contributing something indispensable, each desiring that the other should share in the benefits of their joint endeavour. The neighbours who help one another build a fence, the colleagues who collaborate on a report — these are paradigmatic cases of cooperation, and the definition fits them well. Yet one may construct a case which satisfies the structural conditions of cooperation while lacking the benevolent intentions that the analysis demands. Suppose two rival merchants in the same street each decide, independently but with awareness of the other's action, to lower their prices significantly. Their shared goal is the elimination of a third merchant whose shop has recently opened nearby and who threatens to capture their customers. Each merchant's price reduction is necessary for achieving the goal: neither alone could undercut the newcomer sufficiently to drive him away, but their combined reductions make the market untenable for the third party. They are, in effect, acting jointly toward a shared end, and each contributes indispensable effort. Yet neither merchant intends to benefit the other. Each acts purely out of self-interest, and each privately hopes that once the common rival has been dispatched, the other will be the next to fall. Condition (iii) is plainly unsatisfied. And yet there is a recognisable sense in which the two merchants are cooperating — they are pursuing a shared objective through complementary actions, and their joint effort is more effective than either individual effort would have been. The case suggests that cooperation can arise from convergent self-interest no less than from mutual goodwill, and that the definition, by insisting upon benevolent intention, excludes a significant and familiar form of cooperative behaviour.

definition: Agent A trusts agent B with respect to action φ if and only if (i) A believes that B will perform φ, (ii) A is vulnerable to harm if B fails to perform φ, and (iii) A refrains from taking precautionary measures against B's failure to perform φ.
conditions: A believes that B will perform φ
A is vulnerable to harm if B fails to perform φ
A refrains from taking precautionary measures against B's failure to perform φ
counterexample: Consider a case in which a prudent businessman believes that his partner will repay a substantial loan, is vulnerable to serious financial harm if the partner defaults, and yet — precisely because the sum is so large — insists upon a written contract and a guarantor before advancing the money. He trusts his partner; his belief in the partner's reliability is genuine and well-founded. But he does not refrain from taking precautionary measures — the contract and the guarantor are precautions against default. Condition (iii) fails, and yet it seems perfectly natural to say that the businessman trusts his partner. The precautions do not negate the trust; they supplement it.
missing_condition: The definition must distinguish between trust and reliance, allowing for trust that is accompanied by reasonable precautionary measures rather than requiring their absence
passage: Trust, in its simplest analysis, would seem to consist in a belief that another will act as expected, a vulnerability to harm should that expectation be disappointed, and an absence of precautionary measures against the other's failure. When one trusts a friend to keep a secret, one believes he will do so, one stands to suffer embarrassment if he does not, and one does not take steps to prevent his speaking — one simply relies upon his discretion. The three conditions appear to capture the essence of the matter. Yet one may observe that in the sphere of practical affairs trust is often accompanied by precautions without ceasing, for all that, to be trust. Consider a businessman who lends a substantial sum to his partner. He believes, sincerely and on good grounds, that the partner will repay him. He is acutely aware of his vulnerability — the sum is large enough that its loss would cause him serious financial distress. And yet he insists upon a written contract and the provision of a guarantor before he advances the money. These are precautionary measures of precisely the kind that condition (iii) requires him to forgo. If the definition is taken strictly, the businessman does not trust his partner. But this seems wrong. He does trust his partner — he is lending him money, after all, and his belief in the partner's reliability is genuine. The contract and the guarantor are not substitutes for trust but supplements to it; they are the prudent accompaniments of a trust that is real but not reckless. The case suggests that condition (iii) draws the boundary in the wrong place. Trust and precaution are not mutually exclusive; a person may trust another and yet take reasonable steps to protect himself against the possibility, however remote, that his trust may be disappointed. An adequate analysis must accommodate this common and perfectly rational combination.

definition: An individual A is alienated from an activity or product X if and only if (i) A engages in or produces X, (ii) A experiences X as foreign or meaningless, and (iii) A's relation to X is mediated by external compulsion rather than by A's own freely chosen purposes.
conditions: A engages in or produces X
A experiences X as foreign or meaningless
A's relation to X is mediated by external compulsion rather than by A's own freely chosen purposes
counterexample: Consider a case in which a talented painter, under no external compulsion whatever, freely chooses to produce commercial illustrations for an advertising firm. She finds the work thoroughly uninspiring and experiences the finished illustrations as foreign to her artistic sensibility — they express nothing that she cares about and embody no vision that she recognises as her own. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied: she engages in the activity and experiences it as meaningless. But condition (iii) fails: there is no external compulsion. She chose this work freely, motivated by the desire for financial security, and could leave at any time. Yet she describes herself as profoundly alienated from her commercial work, and this description seems apt.
missing_condition: The definition must recognise that alienation can arise from internal conflicts between an agent's values and activities, not solely from external compulsion
passage: The concept of alienation has been analysed as a condition in which an individual engages in an activity or produces something that he experiences as foreign and meaningless, under the pressure of external compulsion rather than in pursuit of his own freely chosen ends. This analysis has deep roots in social thought and captures a great deal of what is meant when workers, for instance, are said to be alienated from their labour. The factory hand who performs the same monotonous operation eight hours a day, who has no say in what is produced or how, and who works only because starvation is the alternative — he is alienated in the fullest sense. But let us consider a rather different case. Suppose a talented painter, possessed of genuine artistic ability and under no external compulsion of any kind, takes a position at an advertising firm producing commercial illustrations. She is free to leave at any time; she chose the work of her own volition, motivated by the desire for a reliable income. Yet she finds the work thoroughly uninspiring.
