The illustrations she produces — bright, cheerful depictions of consumer products — seem to her utterly foreign to her artistic sensibility. They express nothing she cares about; they embody no vision she recognises as her own. She describes her relation to her commercial output as one of profound alienation. Conditions (i) and (ii) of the proposed analysis are clearly satisfied: she engages in the activity and experiences its products as foreign and meaningless. But condition (iii) is not met: there is no external compulsion. She chose this work freely, and she remains free to abandon it at any time. The definition would therefore deny that she is alienated. Yet her own testimony and the testimony of anyone who observes her suggest otherwise. The case reveals that alienation can arise from an internal conflict between an agent's deepest values and the activity in which she is engaged, and that external compulsion, while a common source of alienation, is not the only one. A more adequate analysis must allow for alienation that is self-imposed or that springs from the failure of an activity to express the agent's authentic purposes.

definition: A political arrangement P is grounded in a social contract if and only if (i) the members of the relevant society have consented, explicitly or tacitly, to the terms of P, (ii) the consent was given under conditions of freedom and equality, and (iii) P specifies mutual obligations between the governed and the governing authority.
conditions: The members of the relevant society have consented, explicitly or tacitly, to the terms of P
The consent was given under conditions of freedom and equality
P specifies mutual obligations between the governed and the governing authority
counterexample: Consider a case in which a man is born into a well-ordered society whose laws he has never explicitly endorsed. He has never signed any document of political agreement, never participated in a founding convention, and never been asked whether he accepts the arrangement under which he lives. It is said that by remaining in the territory and enjoying the benefits of the political order, he has tacitly consented. But suppose this man has no realistic means of emigration — he lacks the resources, and no other state will receive him. His 'consent' is tacit only in the sense that he has not left a place he cannot leave. Condition (i) is dubiously satisfied at best, and condition (ii) fails altogether if the man's situation is one of economic constraint rather than freedom. Yet the political arrangement under which he lives may be perfectly just by other standards.
missing_condition: The definition must address the problem of non-voluntary membership in political societies, where the absence of genuine alternatives renders the notion of tacit consent vacuous
passage: The doctrine of the social contract holds that political authority is legitimate only in so far as it rests upon the consent of those who are governed. A standard formulation requires that the members of the society have consented — whether explicitly or by some form of tacit acceptance — to the political arrangement, that this consent was given under conditions of freedom and equality, and that the arrangement establishes mutual obligations between rulers and ruled. This analysis has exercised enormous influence upon political thought, and it captures something important about the idea that government ought to serve the interests of those it governs. Yet it faces a difficulty that has been remarked upon by many critics. Consider a person who is born into a well-ordered political society. He has never signed a charter of political agreement; he has never been consulted about the laws under which he lives; he has never been presented with the terms of the arrangement and asked to accept or reject them. The standard response is that by remaining within the territory and enjoying the benefits of the social order, he has tacitly consented to its terms. But suppose this man has no realistic option of departing. He lacks the financial means to emigrate; no other state is willing to accept him; his family, his livelihood, and his entire way of life are bound up with the place of his birth. Under these circumstances, his continued residence can scarcely be regarded as a free choice. He stays not because he has consented but because he has no alternative. Condition (i) is satisfied only on the most attenuated reading of tacit consent, and condition (ii) is not satisfied at all, for the man's situation is one of constraint rather than freedom. The case suggests that the notion of tacit consent becomes vacuous when applied to persons who have no genuine alternative to remaining where they are, and that a satisfactory account of political legitimacy must confront the problem of non-voluntary membership in political societies.

definition: A group of persons constitutes a community if and only if (i) they share a common geographical location, (ii) they engage in regular face-to-face interaction, and (iii) they possess a sense of shared identity or belonging.
conditions: The members share a common geographical location
They engage in regular face-to-face interaction
They possess a sense of shared identity or belonging
counterexample: Suppose that a diaspora community of persons scattered across many nations — say, a persecuted religious minority — shares no common geographical location and seldom if ever meets face to face. Yet its members possess a profound sense of shared identity and belonging, maintain traditions, correspond by letter, observe common rituals on the same dates, and regard themselves as bound together by ties as strong as any neighbourhood. The first two conditions fail entirely, yet we unhesitatingly call this group a community.
missing_condition: The definition erroneously requires geographical co-location and face-to-face interaction, when a community may be constituted by shared identity, mutual recognition, and sustained practices of solidarity, regardless of physical proximity.
passage: The concept of community has been invoked by social philosophers to designate a form of human association that differs from both the impersonal aggregate and the merely contractual arrangement. A natural analysis holds that a community is a group sharing a common geographical location, engaging in regular face-to-face interaction, and possessing a sense of shared identity. One might observe that this captures the village, the urban neighbourhood, and the small town with considerable accuracy. Yet we should hesitate to regard these conditions as jointly necessary. Consider the case of a diaspora community: a persecuted religious minority whose members have been dispersed across many nations over the course of centuries. These persons share no common geographical location; they reside on different continents and in different climates. They seldom if ever meet face to face, their interactions conducted by letter, through intermediaries, or in the pages of shared publications. Yet their sense of shared identity is profound and abiding. They maintain common traditions, observe the same rituals on appointed dates, and regard themselves as bound together by ties of obligation and affection as strong as any that obtain among neighbours. We unhesitatingly call this group a community, and to deny it that designation would be to distort the concept beyond recognition. This case reveals the insufficiency of the proposed analysis, for it shows that community may be constituted by shared identity and mutual recognition in the absence of geographical proximity or face-to-face interaction.

definition: A practice constitutes a tradition if and only if (i) it has been transmitted across at least three generations, (ii) it is performed in a manner substantially similar to its original form, and (iii) its practitioners regard it as authoritative because of its antiquity.
conditions: The practice has been transmitted across at least three generations
It is performed in a manner substantially similar to its original form
Its practitioners regard it as authoritative because of its antiquity
counterexample: Consider a case in which a new nation, established only twenty years ago, institutes an annual independence day ceremony. The ceremony has been performed for fewer than one generation and thus fails condition (i). Yet suppose it is performed each year in a manner substantially similar to the inaugural ceremony, and its citizens already regard it as authoritative on account of its association with the founding moment of the state. Within these twenty years, the ceremony has acquired the solemnity, the resistance to alteration, and the normative force characteristic of a tradition. We are inclined to say that a tradition is forming, indeed has already formed, despite the fact that three generations have not yet elapsed.
missing_condition: The definition imposes an arbitrary generational threshold, failing to recognise that what makes a practice a tradition is not mere temporal duration but the normative attitude of continuity and the resistance to arbitrary alteration that practitioners adopt toward it.
passage: The concept of tradition plays a central role in social philosophy, serving to distinguish those practices that carry the weight of the past from those that are merely habitual or fashionable. A common analysis holds that a tradition is a practice transmitted across at least three generations, performed in a manner substantially similar to its original form, and regarded as authoritative on account of its antiquity. One might observe that this analysis captures many paradigm cases: religious rituals passed down over centuries, craft techniques taught from master to apprentice, and seasonal festivals observed since time immemorial. Yet we should hesitate to accept the generational threshold as a necessary condition. Suppose a new nation is established and institutes an annual independence day ceremony in its founding year. The ceremony has existed for fewer than twenty years and has not been transmitted across even a single generational boundary. Yet each year it is performed in a manner closely resembling the inaugural observance, and the citizens already regard it as carrying normative weight, viewing any proposed alteration as an affront to the solemnity of the founding. The ceremony has acquired the resistance to change and the gravity that we associate with traditions. We are strongly inclined to say that this is, or is rapidly becoming, a tradition, despite the failure of the generational condition. This case reveals the insufficiency of the proposed analysis.

definition: A pattern of behaviour constitutes a custom if and only if (i) it is practised by a majority of members of a social group, (ii) it is transmitted through social learning rather than formal instruction, and (iii) deviation from it is met with informal social disapproval.
conditions: The pattern is practised by a majority of members of the social group
It is transmitted through social learning rather than formal instruction
Deviation from it is met with informal social disapproval
counterexample: Suppose that in a particular society, the custom of removing one's shoes before entering a home is observed by only a minority of households — perhaps thirty per cent. The practice is transmitted through social learning within those households, and deviation (wearing shoes indoors) is met with disapproval from those who observe the custom. Yet because only a minority practises it, the first condition is not satisfied. Nevertheless, within the relevant social circles, this is plainly recognised as a custom: those who observe it speak of it as such, expect compliance from guests, and experience a sense of violated norms when it is not followed.
missing_condition: The definition erroneously requires majority observance, when a custom may be sustained by a sub-group within a larger society, its normative force deriving from the expectations of the practising group rather than from sheer numerical prevalence.
passage: The notion of custom occupies an important place in social philosophy, marking out those regularities of behaviour that carry a normative dimension without being codified in law or formal regulation. One proposal holds that a custom is a pattern of behaviour practised by a majority of a social group, transmitted through social learning, and enforced by informal disapproval of deviation. One might observe that this analysis accounts for many familiar cases: the custom of shaking hands upon greeting, of saying grace before meals, or of dressing in a particular manner for particular occasions. Yet we should hesitate to accept the majority condition as necessary. Consider a society in which the practice of removing one's shoes before entering a home is observed by only a minority of households — perhaps thirty per cent. Within these households, the practice is transmitted through social learning: children acquire it by observation and imitation, not through formal instruction. And deviation from it is met with genuine disapproval: a guest who wears shoes into such a home is regarded as having committed a social fault. All the marks of custom are present except majority observance. Yet those who practise shoe-removal speak of it as a custom, expect compliance, and feel a sense of normative violation when it is disregarded. This case reveals the insufficiency of the analysis, for it shows that a custom may be sustained by a sub-group and derive its force from the expectations of that group.

definition: A social arrangement constitutes an institution if and only if (i) it consists of formalised rules and procedures, (ii) it assigns specific roles to individuals within the arrangement, and (iii) it is established by deliberate collective decision.
conditions: The arrangement consists of formalised rules and procedures
It assigns specific roles to individuals
It is established by deliberate collective decision
counterexample: Consider a case in which the institution of language is examined. Language consists of formalised rules (grammar, syntax, phonological rules) and assigns specific roles to individuals (speaker, hearer, author, reader). Yet language was not established by any deliberate collective decision; no committee convened to create English or Mandarin. Language emerged gradually through centuries of uncoordinated usage, drift, and convention. The third condition fails. Yet language is one of the most fundamental social institutions, underlying and enabling virtually all others.
missing_condition: The definition erroneously requires deliberate establishment, failing to recognise that many of the most fundamental institutions arise through gradual, unplanned processes of social evolution rather than through any act of collective decision.
passage: Social philosophers have long sought to identify the features that distinguish institutions from mere patterns of behaviour or informal social arrangements. A prominent analysis holds that an institution consists of formalised rules and procedures, assigns specific roles to individuals, and is established by deliberate collective decision. One might observe that this analysis captures many important cases: a parliament, a university, or a court of law is established by a definite act of collective will, operates according to formalised procedures, and distributes specific roles among its members. Yet we should hesitate to accept the third condition as genuinely necessary. Suppose we consider the institution of language. Language plainly consists of formalised rules: grammars, syntactic principles, and phonological conventions that can be explicitly stated and taught. It assigns specific roles to individuals, distinguishing speakers from hearers, authors from readers, and so forth. Yet language was never established by a deliberate collective decision. No assembly of early humans voted to create a language; no committee designed the rules of English or Mandarin. Rather, language emerged through centuries of uncoordinated usage, gradual drift, and the slow crystallisation of convention. The third condition is therefore unsatisfied. Nevertheless, language is perhaps the most fundamental social institution of all, underlying and enabling every other. This case reveals the insufficiency of the proposed analysis.

definition: A body of practices and beliefs constitutes a culture if and only if (i) it is shared by a geographically bounded population, (ii) it is transmitted from one generation to the next through socialisation, and (iii) it provides its members with a distinctive worldview that differs from those of other cultures.
conditions: The practices and beliefs are shared by a geographically bounded population
They are transmitted from one generation to the next through socialisation
They provide members with a distinctive worldview differing from those of other cultures
counterexample: Suppose that a scientific research community spread across dozens of countries shares a body of practices (peer review, replication, formal publication) and beliefs (commitment to empirical evidence, fallibilism, methodological naturalism). These are transmitted through socialisation in laboratories, departments, and conferences, and they provide members with a distinctive worldview that differs markedly from that of non-scientific communities. Yet this community is not geographically bounded; its members reside on every inhabited continent. The first condition fails. Nevertheless, we commonly speak of 'scientific culture' and recognise it as a genuine culture.
missing_condition: The definition erroneously requires geographical boundedness, failing to account for cultures constituted by shared practices, beliefs, and socialisation within communities that transcend geographical boundaries.
passage: The attempt to analyse the concept of culture has been a persistent concern of social philosophy, and a widespread proposal holds that a culture is a body of practices and beliefs shared by a geographically bounded population, transmitted through socialisation across generations, and productive of a distinctive worldview. One might observe that this characterisation fits many familiar cases: the culture of a remote island people, the culture of a medieval European nation, or the culture of an isolated Amazonian tribe. In each case the population is geographically circumscribed, socialisation transmits the relevant practices and beliefs, and a distinctive worldview emerges. Yet we should hesitate to accept geographical boundedness as a necessary condition. Consider the global community of scientific researchers. This community shares a rich body of practices — peer review, controlled experimentation, formal publication — and a set of deeply held beliefs concerning the authority of empirical evidence and the fallibility of established theories. These practices and beliefs are transmitted through socialisation in laboratories, graduate programmes, and professional conferences. And they yield a worldview that differs markedly from that of communities not engaged in scientific inquiry. Yet this community is not geographically bounded in any meaningful sense; its members are distributed across every inhabited continent. The first condition is thus unsatisfied. Nevertheless, we speak readily and intelligibly of scientific culture. This case reveals the insufficiency of the proposed analysis.

definition: A rule is a social norm if and only if (i) it prescribes or proscribes a type of behaviour, (ii) a majority of the relevant group conforms to it, and (iii) deviation from it is sanctioned by other members of the group.
conditions: The rule prescribes or proscribes a type of behaviour
A majority of the relevant group conforms to it
Deviation from it is sanctioned by other members of the group
counterexample: Consider a case in which a society widely acknowledges a norm against bribery of public officials. The norm prescribes that one should not offer bribes. However, due to systemic corruption, a majority of citizens in fact regularly engage in bribery to obtain basic services. Deviation from bribery — that is, refusing to offer a bribe — is not sanctioned; indeed, those who refuse are regarded with puzzlement rather than approval. Yet the norm against bribery persists: citizens invoke it in public discourse, feel guilt when they violate it, and regard the prevalence of bribery as a lamentable departure from how things ought to be. The norm exists even though majority conformity and sanctioning of deviation are both absent.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for the normative attitude — the belief that the prescribed behaviour ought to be followed — which can sustain a norm even when compliance is low and sanctions are absent.
passage: The concept of a social norm has been central to social philosophy and to the explanation of human behaviour in group settings. A straightforward analysis proposes that a social norm is a rule that prescribes or proscribes a type of behaviour, is conformed to by a majority of the relevant group, and whose violation is sanctioned by other members. One might observe that this captures many familiar norms: the norm of queuing in an orderly fashion, the norm of reciprocating a greeting, and the norm of keeping one's word. In each case the behaviour is prescribed, widely followed, and sanctioned when breached. Yet we should hesitate to regard majority conformity and sanctioning as necessary conditions. Suppose a society widely acknowledges a norm against the bribery of public officials. Citizens invoke this norm in public discourse, express shame when they violate it, and regard the prevalence of corruption as a deplorable departure from how things ought to be conducted. Yet due to systemic corruption, a majority of citizens regularly engage in bribery to obtain basic services, and those who refuse are met not with approval but with bewilderment. Both the majority-conformity condition and the sanctioning condition fail. Nevertheless, the norm persists as a living feature of the society's moral landscape. This case reveals the insufficiency of the proposed analysis, for it shows that a norm may exist in virtue of the normative attitudes of its members even when compliance and enforcement are lacking.

definition: Two or more agents cooperate if and only if (i) they act together toward a shared goal, (ii) each agent is aware of the others' participation, and (iii) each agent voluntarily chooses to contribute.
conditions: The agents act together toward a shared goal
Each agent is aware of the others' participation
Each agent voluntarily chooses to contribute
counterexample: Suppose that two neighbouring farmers, unknown to each other, each clear their respective sides of a shared drainage ditch, motivated solely by self-interest. The result is a functional ditch that benefits both. They have acted toward a shared goal (a clear ditch), but neither is aware of the other's participation; each believes she is acting alone. The second condition fails. Yet the outcome is one of genuine cooperation in the functional sense: the ditch works because both contributed, and an observer would describe them as having cooperated to maintain it.
missing_condition: The definition requires mutual awareness, failing to account for cases of functional cooperation in which agents contribute to a shared outcome without awareness of one another's participation, as when independent actions converge to produce a collectively beneficial result.
passage: The concept of cooperation has received sustained attention from social philosophers concerned with the foundations of collective action and the emergence of social order. A familiar analysis holds that agents cooperate when they act together toward a shared goal, each being aware of the others' participation and each voluntarily choosing to contribute. One might observe that this captures the paradigm cases well: friends who coordinate to move a heavy piece of furniture, or villagers who jointly build a bridge, each knowing of and consenting to the others' efforts. Yet we should hesitate to regard mutual awareness as strictly necessary. Let us imagine two neighbouring farmers who share a drainage ditch running along their common boundary. Each farmer, acting from purely self-interested motives and without any knowledge of the other's activities, clears her own side of the ditch of debris and overgrowth. The result is a fully functional ditch that serves both properties. The farmers have acted toward a shared goal — a clear and operative ditch — and each has voluntarily contributed. But neither is aware that the other has participated; each believes she has acted entirely alone. An observer surveying the outcome would naturally describe the farmers as having cooperated to maintain the ditch, for the beneficial result depends upon both contributions. This case reveals the insufficiency of the proposed analysis, for it shows that cooperation may obtain functionally even in the absence of mutual awareness.

definition: Person A trusts person B if and only if (i) A believes that B will perform some expected action, (ii) A is vulnerable to harm if B fails to perform that action, and (iii) A has evidence from past interactions that B is reliable.
conditions: A believes that B will perform the expected action
A is vulnerable to harm if B fails to perform that action
A has evidence from past interactions that B is reliable
counterexample: Suppose that a patient about to undergo surgery trusts the surgeon who will operate on her. She believes the surgeon will perform the operation competently and is plainly vulnerable to grave harm if the surgeon fails. Yet she has never met this surgeon before and has no evidence from past interactions of the surgeon's reliability; she was assigned the surgeon by the hospital. The third condition is unsatisfied. Yet she genuinely trusts the surgeon, her trust grounded in the institutional credentials, the professional norms of medicine, and the recommendations of others rather than in any personal history of interaction.
missing_condition: The definition erroneously requires evidence from past personal interactions, failing to account for trust grounded in institutional frameworks, professional credentials, testimonial evidence, or social reputation.
passage: The phenomenon of trust has attracted growing philosophical attention, particularly as it bears upon the possibility of social cooperation and the functioning of institutions. A natural analysis proposes that A trusts B when A believes B will perform an expected action, A is vulnerable to harm should B fail, and A possesses evidence from past interactions establishing B's reliability. One might observe that this captures many familiar cases: one trusts a friend to keep a secret on the basis of years of reliable confidence-keeping, or trusts a business partner on the strength of a long and successful commercial relationship. Yet we should hesitate to accept the evidential condition as necessary. Consider a patient who is about to undergo a serious surgical procedure. She believes the surgeon will perform the operation competently, and she is acutely vulnerable to harm should the surgeon fail. Yet she has never met this surgeon before today; she was assigned to his care by the hospital administration and possesses no evidence whatsoever from past personal interactions regarding his reliability. Nevertheless, she trusts him. Her trust is grounded in the institutional framework of the hospital, the professional qualifications displayed on the wall, the norms of medical practice, and the assurances of the referring physician. This case reveals the insufficiency of the proposed analysis, for it shows that trust may rest upon institutional and testimonial foundations rather than upon personal experience of reliability.

definition: A person is alienated if and only if (i) the person is separated from something to which they were formerly connected, (ii) the person experiences a subjective sense of estrangement or loss, and (iii) the separation was imposed by external social or economic forces.
conditions: The person is separated from something to which they were formerly connected
The person experiences a subjective sense of estrangement or loss
The separation was imposed by external social or economic forces
counterexample: Suppose that a monk voluntarily enters a contemplative order, renouncing all worldly possessions, social ties, and former occupations. He is separated from things to which he was formerly connected — family, profession, community — and the separation is not imposed by any external force but freely chosen as a spiritual discipline. Yet over the years, despite the voluntary character of his withdrawal, he comes to experience a profound sense of estrangement from human life and from his own former self. He is separated and experiences estrangement, satisfying the first two conditions, but the third condition fails: no external social or economic force imposed the separation. Nevertheless, we would recognise his condition as a genuine form of alienation.
missing_condition: The definition erroneously requires that the separation be externally imposed, failing to recognise that alienation may arise from voluntary choices whose consequences produce an estrangement the agent did not foresee or intend.
passage: The concept of alienation has been a central preoccupation of social philosophy since at least the middle of the nineteenth century. A familiar analysis holds that a person is alienated when they are separated from something to which they were formerly connected, experience a subjective sense of estrangement, and have had this separation imposed upon them by external social or economic forces. One might observe that this characterisation captures the paradigm case admirably: the factory worker separated from the product of his labour by the structure of capitalist production, experiencing a profound sense of loss and disconnection. Yet we should hesitate to accept the condition of external imposition as necessary. Let us imagine a monk who voluntarily enters a strict contemplative order, freely renouncing all worldly ties, possessions, and former occupations as a spiritual discipline. No external force compels him; the decision is entirely his own. Yet over the course of years, despite the voluntary character of his withdrawal, he comes to experience a deep and pervasive sense of estrangement from human life and from his own former self. He is separated from things to which he was connected, and he feels this separation as a genuine loss. The first two conditions are satisfied, but the third is not, for the separation was freely chosen. We would nevertheless recognise his condition as a form of alienation. This case reveals the insufficiency of the proposed analysis.

definition: A political arrangement constitutes a social contract if and only if (i) it is an agreement among individuals to form a political society, (ii) each party consents to the arrangement, and (iii) the agreement specifies the rights and obligations of each party.
conditions: The arrangement is an agreement among individuals to form a political society
Each party consents to the arrangement
The agreement specifies the rights and obligations of each party
counterexample: Suppose that a group of shipwreck survivors washes ashore on an uninhabited island. Without any explicit discussion or negotiation, they gradually fall into a pattern of cooperation: certain individuals take on the role of gathering food, others build shelter, and a system of mutual aid and shared decision-making emerges through practice. No one consents to an agreement, for no agreement is articulated; no document or explicit understanding specifies rights and obligations. Yet the arrangement functions precisely as a social contract: it constitutes a political society, distributes roles, and sustains cooperation. The second and third conditions fail, yet what obtains is, in functional terms, a social contract.
missing_condition: The definition requires explicit consent and an articulated specification of rights and obligations, failing to recognise that social contracts may emerge tacitly through patterns of behaviour and mutual expectation without any express act of agreement.
passage: The concept of the social contract has been among the most influential in the history of political philosophy. A standard analysis holds that a social contract is an agreement among individuals to form a political society, entered into by the consent of each party, with the rights and obligations of each party explicitly specified. One might observe that this analysis captures the theoretical models of the classical contractarians admirably: the idea of free and rational individuals coming together to negotiate the terms of their collective life. Yet we should hesitate to require explicit consent and articulated terms as necessary conditions. Consider a group of shipwreck survivors cast upon an uninhabited island. Without any formal discussion, negotiation, or explicit agreement, they gradually fall into a pattern of mutual cooperation. Certain individuals assume the role of procuring food, others construct shelters, and a system of shared decision-making and reciprocal assistance emerges through practice and precedent. No one consents to an agreement, for none is articulated; no document or verbal understanding specifies the rights and obligations of each member. Yet the arrangement functions precisely as a social contract in every meaningful respect: it constitutes a rudimentary political society, distributes roles and responsibilities, and sustains collective life. This case reveals the insufficiency of the proposed analysis, for it shows that social contracts may emerge tacitly through patterns of cooperative behaviour rather than through any express act of agreement.
